Renewable/short-term business: abandonment means not renewing at the end of the term.

subsections.title: Market Perception
bullets: Poorly handled withdrawal causes brand damage, media attention, and increased lapse rates. Existing customers may fear financial instability, question value for money, or feel trapped (especially on policies with small/no surrender values).
For long-term products (CI, IP, LTCI), fallout is relatively limited since the insurer must honour existing contracts; distributor concern is more likely than policyholder concern.

For short-term renewable products, closure is more disruptive. If withdrawal is due to losses, the replacement product will likely offer poorer value and the transition must be managed carefully. PMI insurers often use incentives to encourage transfer rather than forced switches.

subsections.title: Implications of a Declining Portfolio
bullets: Critical mass for premium review: a shrinking portfolio may become statistically unsustainable for experience analysis; the new line's experience may not be directly applicable.

Selective deterioration: healthier lives who can obtain cover elsewhere will leave; those unable to switch due to poor health remain, causing a spiral of higher claims → premium increases → more lapses → worse experience.

subsections.title: Reinsurer Reaction
bullets: If a product is loss-making, the reinsurer will generally support profitability improvement strategies or cancel the treaty for new business.
The insurer may need to provide the reinsurer an opportunity to recover losses via cession of new/similar lines; reinsurance treaty terms must be considered when deciding whether to abandon.
Lessons from the failed product must be applied to any successor.

subsections.title: Reasons for Changing the Contract
bullets: Competitive pressure: other insurers offer better benefit packages, prompting upgrades to maintain attractiveness and avoid lapse-and-re-entry. Lapse-and-re-entry is most damaging early in the policy term when the earned asset share is negative.
Financial performance: the product is not behaving as expected; reviewability clauses are used to adjust premiums up or down.
Correcting errors: errors in policy wording need to be rectified.

subsections.title: Legality
bullets: The insurer may improve (but not worsen) a guaranteed contract without invalidating it; increasing premiums on guaranteed contracts is generally not permissible.
For reviewable contracts, the reviewability clause must be carefully worded to preserve legality post-amendment.
For annually renewable policies, the contract technically renews each year so changes are legally possible — but must be marketed and worded clearly to avoid regulatory or court challenge.

subsections.title: Market Perception
bullets: Market view of contract changes is influenced by what other insurers are doing — acting out of step invites suspicion or disapproval.
Radical changes can alternatively be seen as innovation and generate market share gains before competitors catch up.

UK examples: BUPA's individually priced PMI (lifestyle factors) was initially sceptical but has since become standard; Norwich Union Healthcare's NHS cash benefit product attracted political criticism but remains in operation.

subsections.title: Treating Customers Fairly (TCF)
bullets: Policyholders' reasonable expectations under reviewable contracts are difficult to gauge — a key TCF consideration.
Customers must understand from the outset the distinction between guaranteed and reviewable contracts; some companies annually remind customers of the reviewability.
TCF requirements and their interpretation vary significantly by jurisdiction.

subsections.title: Administration
bullets: Changed policies require systems to retain a historical record of cover applicable at each date — needed for future claims validation.

Administrative burden is reduced if existing and new business conditions are changed simultaneously, so all in-force policies share the same benefit structure at any given time, simplifying claims assessment.
Precise historical records are also important as evidence in disputed claims.

subsections.title: Choice or Compulsion
bullets: Under reviewable contracts, if the insurer exercises the right to increase premiums, the policyholder faces three options: accept, reduce benefits, or lapse.
Those more likely to accept increases tend to be higher-risk individuals (e.g. with a new medical condition, or currently claiming) — the insurer must account for selective lapsing.
Contract alterations are harder to impose where health cover is a rider to a larger product (e.g. whole of life or endowment policy).

subsections.title: Projected Volumes and Profitability
bullets: Any changes require the actuary to update the model office with new assumptions, including volume forecasts for new business and business retention rates.
In healthcare, given potential for outcome variability, the actuary should revise stochastic assessments of likely outcomes.
Even seemingly cost-neutral benefit additions should be statistically evaluated for potential adverse impact on profitability and solvency.

subsections.title: Reinsurance
bullets: Any premium/benefit change affects reinsurance arrangements and must be discussed with the reinsurer in advance.
Most reinsurance treaties permit premium variation within certain limits without requiring full renegotiation.

subsections.title: Overview
bullets: The goal is to make claims less likely or less severe.
Each initiative should be subject to a cost/benefit analysis before implementation, and its impact monitored against expectation.

subsections.title: Free Health Checks
bullets: Benefits to insurer: early identification of impairments at a less severe (cheaper) stage; encouragement of healthier lifestyles.

For employer-sponsored business: reinforces employer care for staff wellbeing and enhances product value. For individually-purchased business: attracts health-conscious individuals, though may also attract hypochondriacs.
Can be used as a customer loyalty tool (e.g. offered at each annual PMI renewal).

Risk: free health checks may increase claims by identifying previously undetected illnesses — this must be weighed against earlier, cheaper treatment. Incentives like discounted gym memberships and wearable technology are also used in some markets.

subsections.title: Group Risks
bullets: For insurer covering a workforce at limited sites, there is a unique opportunity to influence the health risk environment through advising on workplace accident/infection risks and claims acceptance practices.
The insurer may employ a risk management expert to visit, assess, and collaborate (not dictate) with the employer on risk reduction — most cost-effective for larger schemes.

Employers co-operate because: sickness absence reduces productivity; work-related illness may create employer liability; many group schemes are experience rated (lower claims = lower cost); and paternalistic employer motivation.

Where a broker intermediates: the insurer has less direct access to the employer; the broker may be reluctant to impose unpopular measures. The insurer should help the broker appreciate the advantages to the employer of controlling risks.

subsections.title: Overview
bullets: Experience analysis may reveal problems addressable through changes to how distributors are paid, with the aim of cutting costs, increasing business, or improving persistency.
Potential issues identified by analysis of profitability by source: expenses too high; sales volumes too low; persistency too low; claims costs too high.

subsections.title: Reducing Remuneration
bullets: Possible approaches: freezing basic salaries or increasing below inflation; not promoting people; reducing non-contractual pay (bonuses, commission); cutting headcount or outsourcing to cheaper staff.

Problems: staff morale and performance deteriorate; trade union resistance and possible industrial action; loss of skilled staff and difficulty recruiting replacements; negative press reaction; long-established bonuses may have set legal precedent; contractual constraints on existing staff.

subsections.title: Rewarding Better Business
bullets: Pay higher commission for business that adds most to profitability. Where persistency improvement is the goal, shift the commission balance from initial to renewal commission.

Optimisation principle: increasing commission raises expenses but may boost volumes — the optimal commission level maximises overall insurer profit, considering the long-term profitability (allowing for lapse rates) of the product line and the competitive market commission rate.

subsections.title: Penalising Worse Experience
bullets: Tactics: reducing commission for distributors with significantly worse persistency; analysing distributors generating low proportions of non-standard business (may indicate non-disclosure); terminating arrangements with distributors providing no net positive contribution to profit.
Further tactics: increasing commission clawback levies for early lapsing policies; deferring initial commission until the policy has been in force for specified periods.

subsections.title: Improving Persistency (Non-Commission Tactics)
bullets: Loyalty bonuses or discounts for long-standing policyholders, or subtler measures such as free health checks at renewal.
Better sales training and supervised interviews to identify and attract the most profitable customer types; more prescribed client dialogue.
Attendance at sales conventions based on in-force business rather than new sales — a perk and incentive for maintaining a persistent book.

subsections.title: Changing Mix of Business
bullets: The insurer sets underwriting criteria for cases: accepted at standard rates / loaded premiums / with exclusions / declined. Loading is common for long-term business; exclusions are more common for PMI.

Underwriting information includes: application form (occupation, residence, travel, pastimes, personal/family medical history, lifestyle); supplementary medical/occupation questionnaires; GP report; Medical Examiner's report; medical test results.

Post-experience-analysis options: relax criteria to expand portfolio (only viable if sufficient margin; relaxing standards increases claims costs); or tighten criteria if risk premiums are not covering claims (improves average health of new entrants but increases underwriting costs).
Reinsurers have significant expertise in underwriting strategy and are well-placed to advise.

subsections.title: Improving Questions
bullets: Better/more detailed questions improve risk selection, but excess questions lose customer goodwill, create delays, and add admin cost — balance is needed.
Questions should be tailored to the sales channel (face-to-face selling supports more questions than off-the-page advertising).

Internet sales raise particular concerns: buyer is likely higher-risk and aware of health needs; easy price comparison attracts adverse selection; easier to test different answers before submitting; potentially easier to misrepresent to a computer than to a human interviewer.

subsections.title: Internal Control Levels
bullets: Tightening: require higher management authority to approve policies that were previously standard.

Loosening: allow point-of-sale acceptance for straightforward cases, with referral of complex cases to head office. A computerised decision system handles clear-cut responses; complex cases still go to underwriters. The quality of overall underwriting should not be compromised.

subsections.title: Automation
bullets: Computerised underwriting systems offer: speed, consistency, reduced human processing, and simpler admin linkage.

Disadvantages: does not utilise human underwriting experience; in health insurance, loadings may be generated from statistics that are not relevant or credible; experienced underwriters deal only with complex referrals and lose skill in routine cases.
Periodic sampling should compare automated decisions against what a human underwriter would decide.

Tele-underwriting (telephone-based supplemental questioning by a trained interviewer, often third-party) is an increasingly popular alternative, allowing additional information gathering without front-loading the application form.

subsections.title: The Claims Process
bullets: A sound claims procedure should be: smooth, efficient, and cost-effective in administration; compliant with policy conditions and exclusions; fast for valid claims with minimal inconvenience; sensitive and knowledgeable in handling (even for declined claims); aligned with customer expectations.
High customer satisfaction must be achieved without compromising underwriting standards, profitability, or reinsurer satisfaction. The process should be monitored regularly.

subsections.title: Training
bullets: Poor training is a common cause of claims deterioration and customer dissatisfaction. Other causes: high staff turnover, rapidly changing environment.
Remedies: enhanced initial and ongoing training; participation in industry forums and conferences (e.g. AMEI claims forums); review of claims staff remuneration and conditions to reduce turnover.

Examples of inexperienced assessor errors — IP: wrong disability definition used; fraudulent claims accepted; benefit paid when claimant can return to work; benefit exceeds salary cap. CI: claims accepted below policy trigger threshold; insufficient evidence for Terminal Illness or Total Permanent Disability. LTCI: claims accepted without ADL trigger; benefit level too high relative to ADL count. PMI: claims accepted for excluded/pre-existing conditions; chronic conditions paid beyond exclusion period; treatment fees exceed benefit limits. All products: greater potential for fraud, exaggerated claims, or missed non-disclosure.

subsections.title: More Accurate Information
bullets: Medical evidence: enhanced claims acceptance can involve additional questions on claim forms or supplementary tests by the claimant's GP, an independent doctor, or an insurer employee. Periodic checks (random or targeted at unusual behaviour) can correct ambiguities and feed improvements back into underwriting/claims.

Financial evidence: primarily relevant to IP claims; also CI and LTCI for high benefit amounts. IP policies contain benefit limitation clauses (usually a % of pre-disability salary) — earnings evidence is required to enforce these. Financial evidence also helps assess motivation to return to work; unwillingness to provide it can indicate fraud.

subsections.title: Involving All Parties in the Claims Process
bullets: Claims process design should involve product developers, pricers, and medical underwriters.

The liaison between underwriters and claims managers is critical to ensure: consistent application of exclusions at underwriting and claims stages; a claim is not paid for an illness that would have been excluded (or loaded) had it been disclosed at proposal.

subsections.title: Controlling Claims Costs (PMI)
bullets: PMI claims control has two dimensions: validity (is the claim covered under the policy?) and amount (is the quantum of the claim appropriate?).
Even where the policyholder is entitled to treatment, the insurer may negotiate provider charges (pre-event via schedules with hospital chains, or post-event by querying specific cost items).

Pre-authorisation requirements can also manage claims costs. The insurer may review clinical protocols with providers to confirm procedures are medically appropriate and necessary — keeping claims frequency and severity closer to the pricing basis.

subsections.title: Market Analysis
bullets: Compare actual returns against standard market benchmarks for the same period. If underperformance is in line with the market, that may be an acceptable explanation — though many will want to go further.

subsections.title: Comparing with Product Needs
bullets: Assess what investment return the product needed to achieve required profitability (the 'interest on reserves' pricing assumption).

Check whether liabilities are matched by corresponding assets — greater matching reduces the impact of investment underperformance. Matching is conceptually difficult for short-term health insurance; more formally attempted for long-term products, especially LTCI.
If the shortfall cannot be resolved by matching, investment return assumptions in the pricing basis may need revision, leading to premium rate review.

subsections.title: Selecting Investment Managers
bullets: A change of investment manager may improve returns. For health and care insurance, investment return is a relatively minor profit driver; any investment change is considered in the context of the wider company investment strategy.

subsections.title: Changing Investment Strategy
bullets: Consider alternative asset classes bearing in mind: security, term, currency, type, liquidity, and risk. Higher-risk strategies require the actuary to justify the additional return against the risk of non-achievement.

Insurers with large free assets can support more volatile investment strategies. Factors that reduce free assets include: large volumes of new business (new business strain); shareholder distributions; with-profits bonus distributions; adverse claims/investment experience; changes to reinsurance strategy (e.g. reducing financial reinsurance); strengthening of reserving basis.

subsections.title: Requirements and Considerations
bullets: From the actuary's perspective, the required IT service should: capture and retain policy data; analyse data and import from other sources as needed; interface with the insurer's existing IT infrastructure; bear a cost supportable by product IT expense margins; be flexible to accommodate anticipated future changes.
Key considerations: in-house vs external; mainframe vs PC-server; improved monitoring/pricing software; time and expense to implement changes, and prioritisation.
There is an increasing trend towards IT outsourcing, particularly advantageous for newer/rapidly expanding insurers not constrained by legacy system compatibility.

sections.title: Changing Office Personnel and Competence Balance

subsections.title: Recruiting or Promoting
bullets: Analysis may reveal decisions being made by staff who are too senior (unnecessarily expensive) where junior staff could adequately decide, or decisions requiring more experienced staff than currently available.

Common examples arise in medical underwriting and claims control. Certain processes may benefit from specialist expertise (e.g. clinical expertise for specialist health products; local market knowledge for overseas operations).

subsections.title: Training
bullets: Existing staff can be trained for more expert roles — less costly than recruitment if urgency is not critical. External companies often provide this training.

subsections.title: External Expertise
bullets: External sources (consultants, reinsurers) provide one-off specialist support.
The choice between consultant and reinsurer depends on: explicit vs implicit cost; expertise and value offered.
The actuary should be able to identify situations where each might be used and the criteria for choosing between them.

22.summary:
Introducing new products
• May arise from saturated market for existing products or too few customers buying existing products for them to make financial sense.

Abandoning a product
• Consider the implications of abandoning a product, eg: can create negative customer sentiment and ‘brand damage’; the portfolio may reduce, leaving less data for analysis and a deterioration in experience due to selective lapsing; there will be some reinsurer reaction, that will depend on the nature of the relationship with the insurer and the expected length of the treaty.
• Impacts more significant on short-term or renewable products, existing long-term contracts must continue cover to the end of the term.

Changing policy conditions
• Contract may be changed to offer a better benefit package (eg to bring it in line with the rest of the market) or to improve profitability (eg by increasing premiums on reviewable contracts).
• The legality of the policy change must be checked, terms cannot be worsened and requirements to treat customers fairly must be met. Policyholders’ reasonable expectations under reviewable contracts can be difficult to gauge. Policyholders should be reminded of the possibility of change. The reinsurer should be involved.
• A step out-of-line with other insurers may be viewed with suspicion. Industry bodies can help to enable change for the whole market.
• Changing existing policies for consistency with new business helps reduce administrative complexity.
• The impact of any change (eg on profitability or future solvency) should be modelled using revised assumptions. A stochastic approach may be particularly appropriate for health insurance.

Improving the risk environment
• Methods of improving the risk environment to reduce claims include: offering free health checks; managing group risks, eg working with the employer to reduce the potential for accident and disease in the workplace.
• The insurer will have less direct influence on an employer where there is a broker involved.

Changing distributor remuneration structures
• Remuneration may be reviewed to cut costs or increase new business sales. Salaries could be reduced (eg in real terms) or frozen. Commission could be increased on more profitable or persistent business.
• Persistency may also be improved by ‘loyalty’ bonuses or discounts, better sales procedures and rewarding sales staff based on persistency and risk measures.

Changing medical underwriting
• Changing underwriting standards will change the mix of business by those: accepted at ordinary rates, loaded premiums or exclusions and declined.
• Consider the number and level of detail of the questions asked, the standards of underwriting (eg the level of underwriter dealing with each case) and point-of-sales systems used to accept cases automatically.
• A computerised process to accept all but the most complex applications would be quick, consistent, cheaper and tie in with admin systems. It will not make use of human experience and the data used to make decisions may be irrelevant, out-of-date or non-credible. Regular monitoring would be required.

Changing claims management
• Should achieve a high level of customer satisfaction without compromising underwriting standards or profitability.
• Could be improved by: increasing training and sharing experience; gathering more, or more accurate, evidence; involving all parties in the claim process (eg underwriters and policy developers); checking the quality of information gathered.

Changing investment policy or managers
• If the investment performance has not met expectations, could consider: performance against the rest of the market; the return required to achieve profitability criteria, the extent of matching to liabilities and whether premium rates need to be reviewed; changing the investment manager; changing the investment strategy.

Changing IT structures
• Requirements include to capture, retain and analyse the data, link with existing systems and flexibility to meet future changes.
• Consider the expenses of implementing changes and maintaining the system.

Changing office personnel and competence balance
• Addressed concerns about whether appropriate staff members are making decisions by recruiting or restructuring to ensure the right level of expertise is utilised in making decisions, training existing staff or making increased use of external expertise (consultant or reinsurer).

1.title: Introduction to Subject SA1

subsections.title: Chapter Overview
bullets: This introductory chapter has four sections: (1) Advice to help you pass Subject SA1; (2) Links with Subject SP1; (3) Control and product cycles; (4) Suggested further reading.
No syllabus objectives are specifically covered in this introductory chapter.

sections.title: Advice to Help You Pass Subject SA1

subsections.title: Overview
bullets: SA1 requires demonstration of higher-order skills: analysing specific details of the problem, applying judgement, making a recommendation, and communicating the answer.
Understanding, analysing, and judging are more important than learning and recalling — SA subjects test application, not memory.
Candidates should try to understand the reasoning behind statements in the course notes, criticise where appropriate, and decide the extent to which they agree.
Practice under exam conditions is strongly recommended — in SA1, more than any other subject, practice makes perfect.
Further reading is advised; candidates should keep abreast of latest developments in health and care insurance beyond the ActEd course notes.

subsections.title: Higher Skills
bullets: Examiners set questions that rely less on bookwork and more on candidates’ ability to apply four higher skills: analysis, synthesis, critical judgement, and communication.

ANALYSIS: Candidates are expected to analyse complex and/or unusual situations. The purpose of analysis is to identify issues that need addressing and investigations required. Good analytical skills involve a picky approach — poring over question details carefully to discover all relevant points. Analysis often precedes synthesis.

SYNTHESIS: Described as a creative activity, in contrast to the ‘destructive’ nature of analysis. Examples: having identified issues (analysis), suggest methods of addressing them (synthesis); having identified a problem, suggest a solution; having stated investigations needed, describe how results would be used.

Synthesis requires selectivity: many ideas arising in the analytical stage that are irrelevant or minor must be filtered out. Main points should be covered in sufficient detail; minor issues very briefly; truly irrelevant issues not at all.

When analysis and synthesis are in the same part of the question (e.g. ‘state any problems with this course of action and suggest solutions’), candidates must give examiners evidence of both the analysis (structured statement of problems) and the synthesis (solutions).

CRITICAL JUDGEMENT: Exam questions often require a decision or recommendation. If the question indicates judgement is needed, exercise it. Where insufficient information is given, state a provisional view and indicate what further information is needed.

In judgement questions, it is not the decision that gains marks but the quality of supporting argument — equivalent to method marks in a mathematical question. Always show how a conclusion was reached.

COMMUNICATION: Spending time planning an answer helps demonstrate understanding. Effective communication involves adopting a clear logical structure and meeting the needs of the audience (e.g. explaining an issue to a marketing manager, making a recommendation to a director).

key_terms.definition: A higher skill involving breaking down a complex or unusual situation to identify issues needing attention and investigations required. Described as a ‘destructive’ activity — the precursor to synthesis.
A higher creative skill involving proposing solutions or methods after analysis. Requires filtering out irrelevant points and focusing on what is material to the problem.
The ability to make a decision or recommendation based on available information, showing the reasoning behind the conclusion. Marks are awarded for the quality of argument, not the decision itself.
Demonstrating understanding through clear logical structure and audience-appropriate language. Requires planning answers before writing.

subsections.title: Problem Solving
bullets: The four higher skills (analysis, synthesis, critical judgement, communication) underpin problem solving throughout the SA1 course.
These skills are revisited in Chapter 26 when looking at ‘solving complex issues’.

subsections.title: SP1 as Foundation for SA1
bullets: SA1 revisits some but not all of the topics covered in SP1. Some SA1 sections provide more detailed explanations of SP1 topics to enable deeper situational analysis.
Knowledge of the whole SP1 course is required for the SA1 examination. Candidates should review the SP1 course to remind themselves of the underlying principles of health and care insurance.
SA1 builds on SP1 by requiring candidates to apply SP1 knowledge in more complex and judgement-heavy scenarios rather than simply recalling it.

subsections.title: Overview
bullets: Considering the stages of a control or product cycle provides a useful framework for structuring answers and as a prompt for areas/topics to consider in a solution.
Two cycles are particularly relevant to SA1 (especially risk management): the actuarial control cycle and the product cycle.

subsections.title: The Actuarial Control Cycle
bullets: Introduced in Subject CP1. In SA1, it is of particular relevance in risk management.
Three core stages in a continuous loop: (1) Specifying the problem → (2) Developing the solution → (3) Monitoring the experience → back to (1).
Two additional inputs that cut across all stages: the general commercial and economic environment, and professionalism.
The models used in risk management are dynamic — they change in light of experience or changes in the environment.

GENERAL COMMERCIAL AND ECONOMIC ENVIRONMENT: Sets the overall scene. Not strictly part of the control cycle but essential context for understanding the risks and potential problems. Evolves over time.

SPECIFYING THE PROBLEM: Involves specification of the targeted outcome and assessment of the risks faced and how they can be handled. Also covers analysis of options for plan design and pricing, and strategic courses of action to handle particular risks.

DEVELOPING THE SOLUTION: Involves examination of major actuarial models in use and how they may be adjusted for the specific problem; selection of the most appropriate model or construction of a new one; consideration and selection of assumptions (critical — actuary must understand their sensitivities); interpretation of modelling results; consideration of implications for all stakeholders; determining a proposed solution; considering alternative solutions; and formalising a proposal.

MONITORING THE EXPERIENCE: Identifies the causes of any departure from the targeted outcome and assesses whether such departures are likely to recur. Enables the actuary to identify sources of surplus or deficit and modify future assumptions. Allows the company to rectify the source of the problem and review strategic decisions continually.
PROFESSIONALISM: Must be demonstrated throughout all work that an actuary does. Guided by relevant local actuarial associations and/or more formal regulatory requirements.

key_terms.definition: A dynamic framework with three core stages — Specifying the problem, Developing the solution, Monitoring the experience — plus inputs from the commercial/economic environment and professionalism. Used especially in risk management.
Stage of the actuarial control cycle involving defining the targeted outcome, assessing risks, analysing plan design options and pricing, and identifying strategic courses of action.
Stage involving model selection/construction, assumption setting, result interpretation, stakeholder consideration, and formalising a proposal to address the identified problem.
Stage involving identifying departures from the targeted outcome, assessing whether they are likely to recur, updating assumptions, and enabling continual review of strategic decisions.

subsections.title: The Product Cycle
bullets: Introduced in Subject SP1. The product cycle shows the interconnected stages of an insurance product’s life.
Main elements (cyclical): Product design → Pricing → Marketing/Sales → Underwriting → Claims management → Experience monitoring → Valuation → back to Product design.
Each stage informs the next; experience monitoring and valuation feed back into product design and pricing decisions, making the cycle continuous.
Useful as a checklist when structuring answers to questions about product management, risk, or strategy.

key_terms.definition: A cyclical framework showing the main stages of an insurance product’s life: product design, pricing, marketing/sales, underwriting, claims management, experience monitoring, and valuation — each feeding into the next.

subsections.title: Introduction
bullets: Three categories of additional reading: (1) reading mentioned in the SA1 Core Reading; (2) further reading recommended by ActEd; (3) other possibilities.
None of the additional reading is directly examinable. Read selectively — breadth across a wide range of health product issues is preferable to depth on a single topic.
Approach additional papers actively: stop to think about issues raised, develop own solutions to highlighted problems, then compare critically with the paper’s ideas.
Exam questions are often set on topical issues that currently concern health and care actuaries — broader reading helps anticipate these.

subsections.title: Further Reading Mentioned in the Core Reading
bullets: Candidates are recommended to read about all current developments in global health and care markets.
A change in the rules relating to another branch of insurance could open up opportunities in the health insurance sector.

Sources of additional reading include: financial press and weekend supplements, trade press (health and care specialists), publications/websites of reinsurers and actuarial consultancies, information from ABI, PRA and FCA websites, IFoA and SIAS papers (British Actuarial Journal), the Longevity Bulletin, output from IFoA working parties, Continuous Mortality Investigation bulletins, Health and Care Conference presentations, health seminars by IFoA.
Most items are available from the Actuarial Profession’s website (actuaries.org.uk). SIAS papers are available at sias.org.uk/resources.

subsections.title: Example Papers
bullets: A non-exhaustive list of papers of interest for specific areas of the course is available on the IFoA’s Subject SA1 Resources page: actuaries.org.uk/qualify/curriculum/health-and-care/resources-for-sa1.
SIAS resources can be found at sias.org.uk/resources.
TASs are available on the FRC website (frc.org.uk); professional guidance is available at actuaries.org.uk/standards/standards-and-guidance.

subsections.title: Some Other Possibilities
bullets: A book of potential interest: ‘Individual Health Insurance’, Leida & Bluhm, 2nd ed, Actex Publications, 2015.
UK online and regular magazines include Cover Magazine (covermagazine.co.uk) for intermediaries in protection and health insurance.
The Actuary magazine (theactuary.com) regularly contains articles relating to healthcare and health insurance.
Other useful websites: local regulator (PRA, FCA in UK; APRA in Australia), local industry body (ABI in UK), and the IFRS Foundation (ifrs.org) for international accounting standard developments.
Candidates are also encouraged to look at various health insurers’, consultancies’, and reinsurers’ websites for practical and current perspectives.

1.summary: Chapter 1 introduces the SA1 exam approach and essential frameworks. SA1 tests four higher skills — analysis, synthesis, critical judgement, and communication — rather than bookwork recall. Full SP1 knowledge is assumed. Two key structural frameworks are introduced: the actuarial control cycle (Specify → Develop → Monitor, with inputs from commercial environment and professionalism) and the product cycle (design → pricing → sales → underwriting → claims → monitoring → valuation). Both serve as answer-structuring tools throughout the course. Broad further reading across current health and care topics is advised.

5.title: General Business Environment (1)

subsections.title: Overview of Chapter 5
bullets: The general business environment for a health and care company can be complex and can include a wide range of often inter-related factors. This chapter and the next highlight those aspects that particularly affect the management of health and care insurance companies.
This chapter covers issues to do with selling, including the roles of the State and of employers in the provision of health and care benefits.
Section 1: overview of the market for health insurance products including the impact of data science and climate change.
Section 2: main channels used to distribute health insurance and the impact of regulation and technology on these channels.
Section 3: potential for mis-selling of health and care products.
Section 4: the role of the State in healthcare provision.
Section 5: the role of employers in healthcare provision.

subsections.title: Introduction
bullets: This section gives a brief overview of the current market for the main types of health insurance, covering: key drivers; current demand for each main product line; customer perception of the insurance industry; competition; lessons from observing other markets; impact of technology and data science on health and care insurers.
Some of these areas have been covered in Subject SP1 and earlier in the SA1 course, so may be treated as revision.

subsections.title: Key Drivers
bullets: Level of wealth and sophistication of the general population — in many countries, increasing wealth means individuals expect better provision of healthcare and are prepared to pay for it.
Demographic factors — people are living longer, especially with disabilities or chronic conditions; comorbidity (multiple chronic conditions in one individual) is increasingly common.
Economic conditions — in some countries, historically low interest rates have decreased demand for insurance-based savings vehicles, leading to more consumer attention on protection products.
Level and form of State health care provision — e.g. the UK's comprehensive NHS reduces demand for PMI compared to the USA where State provision is very limited.
Employer-provided benefits — in some territories, employers provide or contribute to employees' medical insurance as part of the employment package.

Impact of technology trends — aggregator/comparison websites enable customers to fill in one form and obtain quotes from multiple insurers; mobile and wearable devices can measure activity levels and are being harnessed in product design.
Most of these drivers should, in theory, increase demand for health insurance products, though State provision limits potential sales.

key_terms.definition: A situation where multiple diseases or conditions are present simultaneously in one individual.
A comparison website that enables customers to complete a single form and obtain quotes from a range of different insurers; purchase can often be completed through the website.

exam_qa.q: Outline other factors (beyond the key drivers listed) that will influence demand for health insurance products.
exam_qa.a: Level of competition and range of products available; pricing levels and perceived value for money; the state of the economy (unemployment, investment performance); willingness and ability of brokers to sell; appropriateness of distribution methods; public attitude to risk; public level of awareness of health issues and products (driven by media, advertising, government campaigns); overall perception of the insurance industry; quality of customer service; ability of products to meet customer needs (innovative design); perception of State healthcare quality; other political drivers (uncertainty over State provision or legislative changes).

subsections.title: Demand for Health and Care Insurance
bullets: The main health and care insurance products have been extensively described in the first five chapters of Subject SP1.

IP INSURANCE: Intended to cover lost income due to illness or injury. Has been around for over 100 years. Purchasers of individual IP policies are commonly: those for whom an occupational scheme does not exist (e.g. self-employed); and particularly informed middle-earners who see the product's advantages on top of State welfare. Products have to be 'sold' rather than 'purchased'. Individuals in employment may have IP cover through an employer-sponsored scheme. In the UK, group schemes account for around three quarters of those with IP cover.

IP INSURANCE — Factors reducing group IP demand: greater financial constraints on employers; employers becoming less paternalistic; lack of employer understanding of the product; employees preferring additional salary over benefits; lack of competition to drive sales or product design; changes in tax incentives for employers.

IP INSURANCE — Customer perception issue: in some markets there is a perception that the insurer holds the power when admitting or declining claims. Declined claims are often published in the press. IP claims are made at a difficult time — when the policyholder has been off work and may be in financial difficulty.

CI INSURANCE: Provides a large lump sum on diagnosis of a covered condition; in some territories, benefit paid may vary by type of condition and severity. A particular challenge is the complexity of the contract, which may deter purchase. CI insurance has been prohibited in some US States due to concerns that consumers do not fully understand the limited nature of coverage. In the UK, Canada, Singapore and Malaysia, standardised policy definitions have been developed.

CI INSURANCE — Why CI demand may exceed IP demand: CI gives more flexibility (lump sums can be used more flexibly than income); the benefit may appear greater for a given premium; CI is simpler to understand than IP (IP pricing involves occupation, deferred periods etc.); CI can be easily sold as a rider to life cover; consumers tend to fear critical illness more than inability to work due to incapacity; CI may offer better broker commission than IP.

LONG-TERM CARE INSURANCE (LTCI): Pre-funded LTCI is the health insurance product that everyone needs but very few purchase. It is expensive when the need is perceived; at young ages when it is cheap, other priorities dominate; and there is often an expectation that government will look after the elderly. In the UK, LTCI sales have been disappointingly low — the immediate needs annuity market is only ~£100 million pa. In the USA, around 7.2 million had LTCI cover in 2014 (annual premiums ~$12bn); the market has since declined. Pension benefit legislation in each country is likely to have implications for LTCI demand.

PMI: Demand is influenced greatly by the national healthcare system and government policy towards State provision. In the UK, consumers weigh up whether the perceived cost is worth the 'double pay' element (paying both for private care and for the NHS via taxation). In the USA, the lack of State provision creates a natural market for PMI. PMI sales tend to be linked to economic cycles. Individuals in employment may get PMI cover through an employer-sponsored scheme. In jurisdictions with comprehensive State provision, it is more financially aware and better-off customers who are more likely to purchase PMI.

ALL PRODUCTS: Health and care insurance ranks low in consumer priorities — after basics (food, clothing, shelter), household durables, transport, general household upkeep, and leisure. It is therefore one of the first discretionary items to be cut when employment becomes uncertain or a major capital expenditure appears. Demand is closely linked to the economic cycle: high demand when disposable incomes are high; low demand in times of unemployment and low incomes.

In many European countries with high State healthcare spending, the market for insurance may only change if credible political parties offer a viable alternative to State funding with tax-based recognition of the decision to opt out.

In China, health expenditure has increased but public healthcare systems are becoming inadequate for an increasingly ageing and urbanised population — demand for private health care insurance has increased.

key_terms.definition: The ratio of IP benefit to pre-disability income; typically kept below 100% (after tax adjustments) to maintain financial incentive to return to work.
Where customers have not answered underwriting questions correctly (deliberately or innocently), e.g. failing to disclose a pre-existing condition.
Where even though all questions are answered factually, the customers who buy a product are those more likely to claim, and the price charged does not fully reflect this increased risk.
An annuity purchased for someone who already requires long-term care, providing income to fund care costs.
The situation in PMI markets where consumers with comprehensive State provision must pay both for private care and for the State system via taxation.

exam_qa.q: Suggest factors that may lead to a reduction in demand for group IP insurance.
exam_qa.a: Greater financial constraints on employers; employers becoming less paternalistic and more focused on profit; lack of understanding of the product's benefits due to complexity; employees preferring additional salary over benefits; lack of competition to drive sales or product design; changes in tax incentives for employers.

exam_qa.q: Suggest why demand for CI insurance may be much greater than that for IP insurance in an individual market.
exam_qa.a: CI gives more flexibility than IP — lump sums can be used more flexibly than income; the benefit may appear greater for a given premium; CI is simpler to understand than IP (occupation, deferred periods, 'unable to follow any occupation' definitions make IP complex); CI can be sold as a simple rider to life cover when taking a mortgage; consumers tend to fear critical illness more than inability to work; CI may offer better commission to brokers.

exam_qa.q: Explain the difference between non-disclosure and anti-selection and how each might lead to higher claims.
exam_qa.a: Non-disclosure: customers have not answered underwriting questions correctly (deliberately or negligently), e.g. failing to disclose a pre-existing medical condition. These customers are more likely to claim; unless wilful non-disclosure can be proved, the insurer must pay in full. Anti-selection: even though all questions are answered factually, customers who buy the product are those more likely to claim and the price does not fully reflect this. This may be because not all relevant questions are asked on the proposal form. Customers who think they are more likely to claim will be more likely to buy, increasing claims.

exam_qa.q: Outline the changes a future government could make to stimulate demand for health insurance products.
exam_qa.a: All products: introduce tax relief / reduce tax on premiums; reduce taxation payable by insurers (who should then reduce premiums); make insurance compulsory; introduce an awareness scheme of the values of having health insurance. IP insurance: reduce State disability benefits. LTCI: reduce State subsidies towards long-term care. PMI: reduce funding to the NHS; introduce co-payments for certain NHS treatments.

subsections.title: Customer Perception of the Industry
bullets: The public's perception and understanding of health and care insurance products in a particular jurisdiction can be an important factor in demand for these products.
Customer perception is influenced by: the public's understanding of health and care products; tax incentives for policyholders; adverse media coverage.

PUBLIC UNDERSTANDING: In developed economies, the general understanding of financial products may be quite good, though socioeconomic and geographic divides exist. In emerging markets, many new potential consumers have little experience with formal financial products — improved consumer awareness may be necessary. Financial literacy is viewed as important in reducing economic inequalities and information asymmetries between customers and providers.

TAX INCENTIVES: A consumer's inclination to purchase a health and care product may be increased if tax incentives exist. In the UK, PMI premiums are subject to Insurance Premium Tax (IPT) at 12% and are not eligible for tax relief. In contrast, Ireland and Germany allow at least partial tax relief on PMI premiums.

ADVERSE MEDIA COVERAGE: In some jurisdictions, the health and care insurance industry has come in for severe criticism. Examples include: corporate failures; mis-selling; compliance breaches. Adverse media exposure can have a negative impact on public perception of the industry in a jurisdiction.

key_terms.definition: A tax levied on general insurance premiums; in the UK PMI premiums are subject to IPT (currently 12%) rather than being eligible for tax relief.
The ability to understand and effectively use various financial skills; viewed as important in reducing information asymmetries between providers and consumers.

exam_qa.q: Suggest how adverse media coverage could arise.
exam_qa.a: High profile scandals involving insurance companies; corporate failures; mis-selling; compliance breaches; poor investment return on insurance products; lack of transparency or unreasonably high fund management fees; association through the financial sector with banks hit by the credit crunch.

subsections.title: Competition
bullets: The level of competition between health and care insurers for new business can have a significant impact on an insurer's ability to attract new business.

Due to the specialist nature of health and care insurance, in many developed markets there is often a relatively small number of existing specialist health and care insurers, meaning some competition as these players may be looking to attract the same customers.

A number of factors will be important in terms of whether a health and care insurer's product proposition remains attractive, including whether products are competitive in terms of price and product features.
In developed markets, market dynamics could lead to consolidation — a limited number of large insurers dominating the local market.
In less developed countries, there may be fewer local competitors, or the market could be dominated by one or two major players.
In some countries, State-sponsored insurers offering products within the market can cause a barrier to entry for commercial insurers.

The level of competition in a jurisdiction may also be directly impacted by local legislation — e.g. an insurer based outside a country may be required to partner with a local insurer to sell business in a particular jurisdiction.

Example (UK PMI market): dominated by a small number of insurers; the UK competition authorities commenced an investigation into the wider private healthcare market in 2012, finding high barriers to entry and weak competitive constraints on private hospitals in many local markets, leading to higher prices for treatments.

Example (China): private health insurance market dominated by three largest domestic health insurers, with international groups having acquired stakes in existing companies; most private plans provide supplementary insurance to complement public health insurance plans.

subsections.title: Lessons from Other Markets
bullets: Insurance products in a territory will generally have arisen as a complement to the State benefits on offer in that territory.

An actuary advising on health and care insurance should be aware of other systems of State welfare provision and products being developed in other territories. Concepts from other markets could potentially be developed for the domestic market. Regulatory environments in a country can change over time, and knowledge of other territories can help understand the impact of the change.

Example — Critical illness insurance: originally developed in South Africa as 'dread disease' product designed to provide funds that could be used to purchase treatment or deal with financial consequences following treatment. Since then, CI cover has been successfully adopted into many insurance markets worldwide — also referred to as 'trauma insurance', 'serious illness insurance', and 'living assurance' in some countries.

Other innovations need careful analysis and possible market testing before development. Products covering major medical expenses (lump sums in a tiered fashion to cover treatment costs on a non-indemnity basis) were also developed in South Africa but have not had the same success as CI insurance — possibly because the absence of indemnity coverage was too great a negative, relative cheapness, and because customers can have low awareness of the typical costs of medical treatment.

Major Medical Expense (MME) insurance — big negatives of non-indemnity cover: no guarantee that the costs of treatment will be covered, so the customer must find any extra from somewhere else or go through the State; if the treatment required is not listed in the cover, there will be no payout; benefit levels may not keep pace with medical inflation, so the cover may fall short over time of meeting full treatment costs.

key_terms.definition: Original South African form of critical illness insurance — designed to provide funds to purchase treatment or deal with financial consequences following a serious diagnosis.

A PMI product variant that pays a fixed amount (from a schedule relating to severity) to policyholders who undergo surgery of a non-investigative and non-cosmetic nature; pays on a non-indemnity basis.

exam_qa.q: Explain in more detail why the absence of indemnity coverage on major medical expenses (MME) cover is a big negative.
exam_qa.a: MME provides a specified lump sum when the insured undergoes a listed medical procedure. The level of payout depends on the actual procedure (allowing for its expected cost). A big negative is the lack of peace of mind for the customer — there is no guarantee that the costs of the treatment will be covered. The customer must find any extra from somewhere else or go through the State (and hence potentially wait for treatment). If the customer falls ill requiring treatment for a procedure not in the cover, no payout is made. Benefit levels may not keep pace with medical inflation, so over time the cover will fall short of meeting the full cost of treatment.

subsections.title: Technology
bullets: Technology is increasingly driving change for health and care insurers globally, impacting: products sold; how business is distributed; underwriting and back office operations.

New business acquisition: AI can be used to qualify leads and identify prospects, then pass these on to actual sales people. Some insurers target customers with simple on-demand products online, using different adverts to appeal to younger and older customers.

Underwriting: some insurers are considering new data sources (relatively unproven in health and care insurance) to improve the underwriting process, e.g. using data from third parties to help assess health risks. Motor insurers already use mobile technology to monitor driving factors; life insurers could similarly use mobile devices to monitor physical exercise.

Claims: insurers can use modern technology to read handwriting and speed up claims processing — data can be extracted from handwritten and typed forms at high levels of accuracy. Machine learning and humans can validate the results. Voice risk analysis has been used in many insurance industries to try to identify potentially false claims.

Consumer access: technology gives consumers access via the internet to basic details about insurers and products. Insurers have added additional sources of customer service — website self-service, e-mail, live chat, text messaging, and social media. Interactive tools include: making product amendments online; switching between funds; applying for and monitoring health and care insurance products through smartphones and tablets.

Wearable technology: health and care insurers in a number of jurisdictions have tried to integrate wearable devices into their products. Consumers who provide insurers with data on physical activity can receive incentives and possibly discounts on their policy premiums. However, research on the impact of increased physical activity on insurance claims has been inconclusive. It is likely that products would attract those already keen to maintain a healthy lifestyle, so there may be a benefit from selective applications. It has not always been clear how scientific the data and pricing models are that underlie discount schemes.

Price comparison sites: in many markets, consumers are increasingly able to compare insurers' products through price comparison sites. These allow consumers to compare products, receive policy quotes, and often complete sales. Consumers in the UK are the most active users but there is evidence in other parts of Europe, Asia and Latin America of their use.

Providing consumers with a better service through technology will benefit the insurer — strengthening customer relationships, improving retention, and mitigating competition. It also opens up the possibility of gathering data on physical activity of policyholders through monitors on mobile devices.

key_terms.definition: The combination of 'insurance' and 'technology' — predominantly US-based start-ups, often partnering with existing insurers to upgrade aspects of the distribution process such as finding efficiencies in claims and policy management.

subsections.title: Data Science
bullets: Health and care insurers have long gathered data to understand the nature of the risks of the business they are writing. Advances in computer power, cloud storage, and the increase in digital data available online mean a health and care insurer can now collect, store and analyse data much more quickly. Increased digitalisation also offers new data sources which can be combined with traditional types of data.

Data science is generally associated with the collection of large amounts of data and the advanced computational methods used to analyse and gain insight into any trends, patterns and associations in the data. 'Big data' refers to very large datasets that create additional challenges in terms of analysis and use.

EIOPA (2018) thematic review findings: strong trend towards data-driven business models throughout the insurance value chain; traditional demographic/exposure data increasingly combined with online media data or telematics; biggest use of data science was in pricing and underwriting; significant risks arising from data science techniques — including ethical issues, concerns around accuracy, transparency, auditability and explainability of AI.

ADVANTAGES: Data science techniques offer health and care insurers a step-change in risk analysis, allowing risks to be seen in much more detail and on a continuous basis. Benefits include: processing and drawing useful insights; supporting new products and services; reducing market frictions and inefficiencies. Used responsibly, data science can offer fairer outcomes for consumers — increased inclusivity and improved customer experience.

POTENTIAL ISSUES — Data: data from new sources is unlikely to always be perfect; incorrect, incomplete or biased data could invalidate results; determining whether appropriate consumer consent has been obtained can be challenging (e.g. social media); harmonising, cleaning and validating data from different sources; as datasets grow larger, ensuring data quality becomes more difficult (though data science techniques themselves can be used to identify data quality issues).

POTENTIAL ISSUES — Mis-selling: data science techniques could be used to influence consumer behaviour, e.g. 'nudging' to provide consumers with a light push to buy a product they do not actually want or need.

POTENTIAL ISSUES — Data security: as health and care insurers collect larger amounts of detailed and sometimes sensitive customer data, the risk that this data could be lost, corrupted or stolen increases. The costs of a cyber-attack include lost/corrupted data, potential damage to reputation, and business interruption costs. Health and care insurers will need to develop processes with appropriate controls against hacking and other unauthorised access.

POTENTIAL ISSUES — Legacy systems: companies with large existing legacy business may face barriers to using data science techniques efficiently, due to poor quality of existing data and high cost of updating legacy computer systems.
POTENTIAL ISSUES — Staff skills: a further challenge is difficulty in attracting staff with the necessary skills to develop and manage complex data science related projects.

ETHICAL USE: Innovation in new types of data and data science techniques can create new ethical challenges. Potential areas of tension: collection and use of new types of data; use of opaque third party data based on algorithms (e.g. credit scores); use of data science to influence consumer behaviour. As health and care insurers see risks in finer detail, cross-subsidy between policyholders could decline, potentially making it unaffordable for less healthy policyholders to find health and care insurance. The IFoA has issued a guide for ethical data science.

REGULATION: Insurance regulators expect compliance with existing regulatory and supervisory frameworks on data science. Regulators will want to balance benefits of developments against risks of adverse outcomes for consumers (reduced access to insurance, poor transparency, lack of fairness, unethical practice). Under Solvency II, data risks are considered within the operational risk component of the SCR calculation, with data quality requirements under Pillar 2.

Conditions under which health and care insurers may process personal sensitive data (at least one must be satisfied): explicit consent of the data subject; required by law for employment purposes; needed to protect the vital interests of the individual or another person; needed in connection with the administration of justice or legal proceedings.

key_terms.definition: The collection of large amounts of data and the advanced computational methods used to analyse and gain insight into trends, patterns and associations in the data.
Very large datasets that create additional challenges in terms of analysis and use.
European Insurance and Occupational Pensions Authority — launched a thematic review in 2018 on the use of data science techniques specifically in European motor and health insurance.

Technology that combines telecommunications and informatics to monitor and transmit data about consumer behaviour (e.g. driving habits, physical activity levels) for use in insurance underwriting and pricing.
Using data science techniques to provide consumers with a light push to buy a product which the consumer does not actually want or need — a potential mis-selling issue.

A policy that covers: specific roles and responsibilities of individuals within the insurer with regard to data; how the insurer captures, analyses and processes data; issues with respect to data security and privacy; the controls put in place to ensure required data standards are applied; and how the adequacy of controls will be monitored on an ongoing basis.

exam_qa.q: Suggest the conditions that may have to be satisfied by a health and care insurer around the processing of personal sensitive data.
exam_qa.a: At least one of the following conditions must be satisfied: (1) the data subject has given explicit consent; (2) it is required by law for employment purposes; (3) it is needed to protect the vital interests of the individual or another person (e.g. disclosing a medical condition to treating medical staff after a work accident); (4) it is needed in connection with the administration of justice or legal proceedings.

exam_qa.q: Outline the areas that a health and care insurer's data governance policy should cover.
exam_qa.a: The specific roles and responsibilities of individuals within the insurer with regards to data; how the insurer will capture, analyse and process data; issues with respect to data security and privacy; the controls put in place to ensure that the required data standards are applied; how the adequacy of the controls will be monitored on an ongoing basis with respect to data usability, accessibility, integrity and security.

subsections.title: Climate Change
bullets: The scientific consensus is that climate change associated with global warming is in progress (IPCC, 2014), though the scale and timing of its impacts are uncertain.

Climate change could have a wide-ranging impact on health and mortality, physical assets, and financial markets. The future actions of society in response to climate change, and their ultimate effectiveness in mitigating the impacts, are also uncertain.

Climate change could have significant implications for health and care insurers, impacting many areas of actuarial work: product design; pricing; reserving; capital management; risk management; investment.

These impacts are driven by potential changes in: mortality and morbidity rates (e.g. implications for food and water availability and the spread of diseases); asset values (e.g. poor performance of equity holdings in companies reliant on fossil fuels); economic growth rates (influencing demand for and pricing of insurance products); the level of uncertainty about future trends and outcomes; regulation.

The IFoA Risk Alert on Climate-Related Risks (2017) states that actuaries should ensure they understand, and are clear in communicating, the extent to which they have taken account of climate-related risks in any relevant decisions, calculations or advice. All institutions should consider the potential implications of climate-related risks on their invested assets; insurers should evaluate and manage the impact of changing patterns of temperature and disease on mortality; insurance providers may need to make alterations to pricing or other assumptions in relation to mortality and morbidity risks.
Boards of financial institutions will need to consider the potential impact of climate risks more in future business decision making and strategic planning.
Climate change is covered further in the Core Reading — particularly in Chapters 8 (product design) and 10 (regulation).

key_terms.definition: Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change — a United Nations body created to provide regular scientific assessments on climate change and its related risks.

subsections.title: Pandemics
bullets: Recently, pandemics have had a significant impact on global health.
Pandemics can have a significant impact on health and care insurers.
This topic is considered further in the next chapter (Chapter 6 — General Business Environment (2)).

subsections.title: The Main Channels
bullets: The main distribution channels and their effects on sales of health and care insurance business were discussed in the distribution channels chapter of Subject SP1. This section features some examples of factors that could impact the sale of health and care insurance business.

Main channels for distributing health and care insurance products: (1) Insurance intermediaries (brokers) — remunerated via commission from the insurance company and/or fees from clients; (2) Tied agents (Appointed Representatives) — remunerated via commission from the company/companies to which they are tied; (3) Own salesforce (Direct Sales Force, DSF) — remunerated via commission and/or salary; (4) Direct marketing — policies sold direct, no remuneration involved (includes mailshots, telephone selling, press advertising, internet advertising and comparison websites); (5) Worksite marketing — remuneration depends on whether it is a broker or a representative of the insurance company doing the selling.

The complexity of the products sold varies by sales channel. Experience can vary between channels across: demographic experience (as a result of different classes of lives or target markets and different levels of underwriting); product pricing (to reflect different demographic assumptions and competitiveness); withdrawal rates (will depend on level of financial sophistication and who initiated the sale).

key_terms.definition: An insurer's own salesforce — employees of the insurance company who are remunerated via commission and/or salary.
Selling insurance products at the employer's worksite, where the seller may be a broker or a representative of the insurance company.
An agent tied to one or more insurance companies, remunerated via commission from those companies.

exam_qa.q: List the different areas in which experience can vary between distribution channels.
exam_qa.a: Demographic experience — as a result of different classes of lives or target markets and different levels of underwriting; product pricing — to reflect the different demographic assumptions and competitiveness; withdrawal rates — will depend on level of financial sophistication and who initiated the sale.

subsections.title: Form of Remuneration
bullets: In some jurisdictions, to pursue a more regular client relationship, some intermediaries have moved away from commission-based remuneration to one of (possibly annual) fees. In general, this may only be practical for corporate clients and high net worth individuals.

By charging a fee rather than taking commission, the intermediary can more easily demonstrate that they are not influenced by the level of commission being offered and are giving 'best advice'. The initial commission will be 'rebated' back to the customer (effectively reducing the premium or charges); instead, the customer pays a fee.
Commission remains a common form of remuneration for intermediaries in many jurisdictions.

Financial services regulators in some countries have been concerned that commission would result in product bias and recommendations based on the level of commission an adviser would receive rather than on which product would be most suitable for the client — for this reason, some regulators have looked to ban commission in their markets, and may prefer financial advisers to operate on a fee basis (e.g. UK for savings products in 2013).

Services brokers may provide for corporate clients (beyond selling insurance): regular research of the health and care insurance market to recommend the best value and most appropriate products; administration of the client's records; employee assistance programmes, such as health information advice lines.

key_terms.definition: Where an intermediary operating on a fee basis returns the initial commission back to the customer (effectively reducing the premium or charges), with the customer paying an explicit fee instead.

exam_qa.q: Apart from selling them insurance products, suggest the services that brokers might provide for corporate clients.
exam_qa.a: Regular research of the health and care insurance market to recommend the best value and most appropriate products; administration of the client's records; employee assistance programmes, such as health information advice lines.

subsections.title: Regulation
bullets: Local regulation can have a significant impact on how a health and care insurance company sells business in a given jurisdiction.

Possible examples of areas where regulation can impact how business is sold: the types of product that can be sold by insurers; how advice is provided and paid for; the level and form of charges on products; caps on product charges; the requirements for distributors (e.g. certain products can only be sold via certain distributors); the extent to which a market is open to external competition; how mis-selling issues are dealt with.
Regulations that may affect distribution are also covered in Chapter 25.

sections.title: Mis-selling in Health and Care Insurance

subsections.title: Overview of Mis-selling
bullets: Consumer protection is an important issue for insurance regulators around the world. Regulators are concerned about protecting consumers from poor sales tactics within the health and care industry. Poor practice can include: inappropriate advice being given to consumers; or products being sold to consumers that were not appropriate for the consumer's needs.

Poor practice by an insurer can lead to: fines for the insurer; the regulator requiring the insurer to compensate policyholders; substantial operational costs in dealing with these issues. Poor practice can also lead regulators to proactively change regulation to provide greater protection for consumers.
Health and care insurers should therefore be mindful of the scope within their sales processes to create another mis-selling scandal.

The following sections outline a few (deliberately contentious) suggestions as to possible areas of concern that may emerge. The Subject SA1 candidate may wish to decide on their own views for examination purposes.

subsections.title: IP Insurance
bullets: Benefits that don’t protect all income: IP contracts typically do not provide benefits equalling or exceeding the level of income — the income replacement ratio is kept below 100% (after tax) to maintain a financial incentive for claimants to return to work. Additionally, the increase in the level of benefits built into the policy may fall short of any actual increase in the policyholder's income.

Resolution of income-benefit mismatch: benefits may be increased automatically in line with some index (price inflation or national earnings), often capped at a maximum rate to prevent the replacement ratio rising excessively. Alternatively, the policy may include a guaranteed insurability option (GIO) allowing the benefit level to be amended annually in line with income levels without requiring additional medical evidence.

Benefits that don’t protect income in the event of unemployment: the name 'Income Protection' is perhaps misleading if no cover is provided against unemployment — some commentators argue the name should be changed.

Restricting benefits even when premiums have been paid: the insurer has the right to restrict benefits under an IP insurance policy when the policyholder's income drops (e.g. due to a move to a less well-paid role), even though all premiums have been paid for the originally agreed benefit. The insurer’s insistence on this right could lead to litigation. Benefit levels need to be reduced to: allow for differences in taxation between pre- and post-sickness benefits; allow for other sources of income while sick (e.g. State benefits); maintain a financial incentive for claimants to return to work.

key_terms.definition: A policy feature allowing the insured to increase the benefit level (e.g. annually in line with income) without requiring additional medical evidence, thus avoiding re-underwriting.

exam_qa.q: Suggest reasons why the benefit in an IP policy may fall short of the actual increase in policyholder's income, and how this problem can be resolved.
exam_qa.a: The benefit could be level throughout the policy term, or may be increased automatically in line with some index (e.g. price inflation or national earnings) — often this rate is capped to prevent an increase in the replacement ratio, or at a fixed rate. However, the policyholder's income is likely to fluctuate over time and so not increase in line with the benefit (e.g. promotional increases). To resolve this: frequently review benefit levels and increase if required (though an increase would normally require re-underwriting, which may cause customer dissatisfaction); or IP contracts that offer the facility to amend the benefit level annually in line with income levels without requiring additional medical evidence (i.e. a guaranteed insurability option). Alternatively, the benefit escalation rate could be increased but the insurer would need to regularly check that the net replacement ratio is not increasing to an unacceptably high level.

subsections.title: CI Insurance
bullets: Covering all critical illnesses? The very nature of the CI insurance contract — only a limited number of diseases is covered — could lead to disappointed and litigious policyholders if a critical illness arises that is not on the list. The customer may think they are covered for any 'critical' illness, but they are only covered for conditions described in the policy and their illness must meet the insurer's definition of the condition (which may be more restrictive than a medical definition).

In the 1990s in the UK, marketing departments went to great lengths to include more and more conditions in CI plans. However, most of these additional conditions are very rare among CI insurance policyholders and are likely to result in a claim for terminal illness or TPD in any case — hence they are unlikely to add much to the policy in terms of real value.

Definitions not made clear at outset: a particular issue in CI insurance is that the policy wording can be complex and may not be fully understood by the policyholder. Example: many policyholders may believe they are covered for any heart attack, whereas the policy may only cover very severe heart attacks (according to specific medical criteria involving ECG changes and cardiac enzyme/Troponin levels). The industry may publish model definitions to address this, but these can be wrapped up in complex medical jargon.

Example UK CI definitions — heart attack: some insurers have amended definitions to be simpler (e.g. 'a definite diagnosis of an acute Myocardial Infarction by an attending UK consultant cardiologist as defined by either the current recognised International Standard'). The ABI standard definition requires death of heart muscle due to inadequate blood supply, with all of: typical clinical symptoms; new characteristic electrocardiographic changes; characteristic rise of cardiac enzymes or Troponins (T > 200 ng/L or I > 500 ng/L). Excluded from the ABI standard definition: other acute coronary syndromes; angina without myocardial infarction.
Best Practice around CI insurance claim definition wordings is discussed in more detail in Chapter 25.

key_terms.definition: A cardiac enzyme used as a medical marker in the diagnosis of acute myocardial infarction; Troponin T > 200 ng/L and Troponin I > 500 ng/L are threshold levels used in some CI definitions.

subsections.title: Long-Term Care Insurance (LTCI)
bullets: Selling cash not care: for many LTCI contracts, long-term care insurance is a misnomer — the contracts do not provide any care at all; they simply provide cash on a structured basis (usually income) to allow the policyholder to pay towards necessary costs when the tests for ADLs are failed. If LTCI policies provide a cash shortfall for care, there is the possibility of litigation and reputational damage.

ADLs (Activities of Daily Living): functional tests against which incapacity (disability) can be measured. Failure of a number (usually two or three) is usually used to trigger a claim payment under LTCI contracts. The six most commonly used ADLs are: Washing (maintaining an adequate level of cleanliness and personal hygiene); Dressing (putting on and taking off all necessary garments); Feeding (transferring food from plate to mouth); Toileting (managing bowel and bladder function); Mobility (moving indoors from room to room on level surfaces); Transferring (moving from a lying position in a bed to a sitting position in an upright chair or wheelchair and vice versa). The claimant must fail the ADL with or without the use of a device.

Additional LTCI claims trigger: mental or cognitive impairment — the policyholder must require care 'as a result of deterioration in, or loss of, mental capacity' and 'from an organic cause' (e.g. Alzheimer's disease).

In most cases, LTCI policies provide pre-determined cash amounts rather than indemnifying the policyholder for future long-term care costs.
