The benefit amount may be indexed in line with inflation; however, even if the level of benefit payable is guaranteed, it is not certain that this will be sufficient to cover the eventual care costs.
Poor investment performance would adversely impact unit-linked LTCI policies — additional contributions may be required to meet target benefits at a time when the policyholder can least afford them.

Mitigation: ensure the selling process is such that all customers fully understand that cash benefits are being provided, and that they may not provide the full cost of care required. Alternatively, change the policy design to indemnify the cost of care benefits in the future (but, given the uncertainty involved, this will result in a substantial increase in premium rates).

Other LTCI contracts have been structured better, offering to indemnify care costs up to a limit. Time will tell whether these limits will prove meaningful — if the true cost of care in old age exceeds these monetary amounts in a high proportion of cases, the protection afforded will be much diminished and litigation may arise.

key_terms.definition: Functional tests against which incapacity (disability) can be measured. The failure of a number of these (usually two or three) is usually used to trigger a claim payment under LTCI contracts. The six most commonly used are: Washing, Dressing, Feeding, Toileting, Mobility, and Transferring.

exam_qa.q: Explain what ADLs are and describe the main ones used for LTCI.
exam_qa.a: ADLs are activities of daily living — functional tests against which incapacity (disability) can be measured. The failure of a number of these (usually two or three) is usually used to trigger a claim payment under LTCI contracts. The six most commonly used ADLs are: Washing (ability to maintain an adequate level of cleanliness and personal hygiene); Dressing (ability to put on and take off all necessary garments, artificial limbs or other surgical appliances that are medically necessary); Feeding (ability to transfer food from a plate or bowl to the mouth once food has been prepared and made available); Toileting (ability to manage bowel and bladder function, maintaining an adequate and socially acceptable level of hygiene); Mobility (ability to move indoors from room to room on level surfaces at the normal place of residence); Transferring (ability to move from a lying position in a bed to a sitting position in an upright chair or wheelchair and vice versa). The claimant must fail the ADL with or without the use of a device.

exam_qa.q: Suggest how LTCI insurers can help mitigate the potential for complaints that cash benefits are insufficient to cover actual care costs.
exam_qa.a: Two suggestions: (1) Ensure the selling process is such that all customers fully understand that cash benefits are being provided and that they may not provide the full cost of care required. (2) Change the policy design to indemnify the cost of care benefits in the future (but, given the uncertainty involved, this will result in a substantial increase in premium rates).

subsections.title: PMI
bullets: Restrictions vs comprehensive: in some jurisdictions, PMI policies have been designed to meet a variety of needs and income levels. Some products, in order to reduce premiums, have limited applicability. The applicant may emerge from the sales process believing that they have bought cover that is sufficient to meet all their private medical needs, when in fact it is a restricted policy.

Example (UK): PMI policies may only provide benefits where NHS waiting lists exceed a specified number of weeks; they may have restrictions on indemnification or only pay for treatment in particular hospitals. The applicant may believe they have bought comprehensive cover.
Regulation in the UK is now in place for health products to ensure customers are clear as to the scope and type of product purchased and that it is suitable for their demands and needs.

The most frequent misunderstanding by PMI policyholders in the UK is what constitutes an acute as opposed to a chronic condition. Policyholders may receive treatment for acute (i.e. treatable) conditions covered by PMI, but then at some point the condition becomes chronic and PMI cover ceases. This withdrawal of cover, coupled with the news that their illness is not expected to be cured, can be extremely upsetting for the customer.

Introducing common industry-agreed definitions for acute and chronic has gone some way towards reducing this ambiguity. However, to avoid potential misunderstandings and subsequent litigation, insurers must handle such claims extremely carefully from the day the claim is first reported, making the customer fully aware of what acute and chronic actually mean, the potential for the condition to turn chronic, and the fact that cover will be withdrawn if the condition becomes chronic.

Some insurers will end up 'breaking the rules' and paying for chronic conditions, perhaps on 'moral grounds' — this can be seen as good customer service, but insurers must allow for the financial implications.

key_terms.definition: A disease, illness or injury that is likely to respond quickly to treatment which aims to return the patient to the state of health they were in immediately before suffering the disease, illness or injury, or which leads to the patient's full recovery.

A disease, illness or injury that has one or more of the following characteristics: it needs ongoing or long-term monitoring; it needs ongoing or long-term control or relief of symptoms; it requires rehabilitation or there is no known cure; it continues indefinitely; it comes back or is likely to come back.

subsections.title: Product Types Confused
bullets: Due to the complexity of health and care products, the policyholder may not be clear on what product they have purchased.
A survey in the UK in 2001 indicated that a significant number of people who had purchased CI insurance policies believed their product to be income protection insurance.

Potential problems arising from such misconceptions: customers will presumably believe they will receive benefits for long-term sickness, so problems arise when they try to claim if: the cause of sickness does not constitute a claim under the CI policy but is sufficient to prevent the insured from working normally (e.g. broken bones or infectious disease); the lump sum form of the benefit may not meet the customer's need (for regular income, though the lump sum could be converted into an income stream); the policy will cease on claim, leaving the policyholder without further cover; if these customers thought they had another type of policy, it is highly unlikely that they understand the detailed features of the cover (e.g. exclusions), resulting in further misunderstandings.

The above may result in lots of complaints (some well-publicised), lapses, lack of consumer confidence in the insurance provider, and consequently lack of sales. Claims will be contested and, if the action is successful, claims have to be paid that the insurer was not expecting.
This issue will least affect those insurers who have sold their customers the 'right' product.

exam_qa.q: What are the potential problems arising from policyholders confusing CI insurance with IP insurance?
exam_qa.a: Customers believe they will receive benefits for long-term sickness. Problems when claiming: (1) the cause of sickness may not constitute a CI claim but would prevent working normally; (2) the lump sum benefit may not meet the need for regular income; (3) the policy will cease on claim, leaving no further cover; (4) customers who thought they had a different policy type are highly unlikely to understand the detailed cover features or exclusions, resulting in further misunderstandings. Consequences: lots of (possibly well-publicised) complaints; lapses; lack of consumer confidence; contested claims that may be decided against the insurer.

subsections.title: Distribution Through Technology
bullets: Increasingly consumers are becoming more self-directed and are making use of many different sources and media to research, seek advice and ultimately purchase insurance.

A new development has been the rapid growth of insurtech start-ups. Insurtech (insurance + technology) is predominantly US-based and often partners with existing insurers to upgrade aspects of the distribution process, finding efficiencies in claims and policy management. Examples: an online provider of CI insurance which relies on computers to make a quick decision on policies up to a certain maximum sum assured through an online questionnaire and other online data sources; companies that make insurance policy recommendations based on individual's social media profiles; management tools for brokers and insurers to launch, distribute and monitor new products without using legacy systems.

There will potentially be greater e-commerce for health and care insurance in the future. However, some consumers may be less comfortable doing independent research and making online purchases without specialist advice — especially for the more complex health and care products.
There may be challenges selling via the internet to consumers in some developing markets as many consumers may not have online access, although this is changing in some jurisdictions.

Health and care insurers are also developing third party distribution models to connect with individuals who are neither financially connected to the banking system nor digitally connected. Significant mobile phone coverage in many developing countries means that insurers can use digital technology to reach a broader market at an affordable cost. Example: microinsurance in developing countries can be paid for via mobile phones.

sections.title: The Role of the State in Healthcare Provision

subsections.title: Complementary Roles of State and Insurers
bullets: The level and form of the State provision of health care is likely to have a significant impact on the benefits which can be provided by an insurer in a particular jurisdiction.

In the UK, significant State provision has been in place since the 1940s. For the most part, insurers try to provide coverage in areas where UK welfare benefits are not available or are deemed inadequate: CI insurance has no obvious counterpart in UK State healthcare benefits (CI provisions are solely done by health and care insurers in the UK); IP insurance provides benefits augmenting UK welfare provision (which is intended to provide only subsistence level); IP insurance benefits can continue to retirement if the disability persists (UK welfare payouts may run for a shorter duration); LTCI has a niche for those whose prospective wealth is above the expected means-tested threshold; PMI can be an alternative to the NHS for acute in-patient procedures and certain out-patient consultations or treatment, though it does not attempt to compete with accident and emergency.
Private healthcare provision can vary significantly between jurisdictions, ranging from primary coverage for particular population groups to a supporting role for public systems.

In countries where the State offers very limited healthcare provision, private healthcare can provide a significant role in healthcare financing. In the USA — the only country in the OECD where voluntary health insurance represents the main health financing and coverage system for most of the population — there is a risk that the poor are not able to find affordable healthcare; from the insurer's perspective, there is a risk of not being able to profitably sell business to poorer policyholders.

Where the State provides a wide range of generous benefits, then unless the State operates a monopoly on health provision, the benefits offered by an insurer are likely to be complimentary to the State benefits. The purchase of private healthcare could be motivated by: desire for reduced waiting times until treatment; cover for gaps in State treatments.

Reasons why there may still be a PMI market in a country with a comprehensive free healthcare system: faster access to treatment; perceived better quality treatment; higher standard of accommodation (e.g. private rooms); choice over who carries out the treatment; choice over where and when the treatment is carried out.

exam_qa.q: Outline reasons why there may still be a PMI market in a country with a comprehensive free healthcare system.
exam_qa.a: Patients may use private healthcare to offer: faster access to treatment; perceived better quality treatment; a higher standard of accommodation (e.g. private rooms); choice over who carries out the treatment; choice over where and when the treatment is carried out.

subsections.title: Means Testing
bullets: One possible solution to increasing social welfare cost in a jurisdiction is through means testing, as a way of targeting funds to those who need it most. Means testing could apply in a number of ways to reduce the cost of various types of health and care benefits such as long-term care provision and medical services.
Example (South Africa): individuals with very low incomes receive a subsidy for public sector healthcare; healthcare is only subsidised for those earning less than a specified threshold.

Introduction of means-tested co-payments for acute hospital service costs might increase demand for: PMI (if individuals need to make a significant co-payment, or if the level of co-payment makes private treatment a more attractive alternative to any State care); cash plans (offering fixed payments while in hospital); and slightly for CI and IP insurance (the level of perceived needs for some customers when ill and requiring hospital treatment may increase). There may also be a slight decrease in claim rates on some products (the need to make a co-payment may deter some people from having hospital treatment).
The introduction of means testing can be driven by: demographic change; a movement to a low tax environment; competing demands on public expenditure.
Co-payments are common in Ireland and were introduced in Germany in 2003.
Note: co-payments are a double-edged sword in the long run — not having medical conditions treated would probably lead to more problems and therefore more claims, later.

key_terms.definition: A way of targeting State welfare funds to those who need it most by assessing the individual's financial resources; individuals above a certain income or asset threshold receive reduced or no subsidy.
A contribution made by a consumer towards the cost of a healthcare service (e.g. a hospital treatment), with the State or insurer covering the remainder.

exam_qa.q: Suggest which insurance products might be affected by the introduction of means-tested co-payments towards acute hospital services costs. Describe the effect in each case.
exam_qa.a: PMI: demand may increase if individuals need to make a significant co-payment, or if the level of co-payment makes private treatment a more attractive alternative to State care. Cash plans: demand may increase as they offer fixed payments while in hospital. CI and IP: there may be a slight increase in demand as the level of perceived needs for some customers when ill and requiring hospital treatment may increase. Also a possible slight decrease in claim rates on some products as the need to make a co-payment may deter some people from having hospital treatment (though not having conditions treated would probably lead to more claims later).

subsections.title: Future Role of the State
bullets: Insurers need to consider that different political parties in a country may have very different views about the role of the State in healthcare provision. A change in government could lead to material changes in the approach a State takes to providing health and care benefits.

Government action can affect health insurance products in the following areas: regulation or legislation directly affecting insurance products (e.g. disclosure requirements or restrictions on rating factors used); taxation (on benefits, premiums or investments); changes to State healthcare benefits (e.g. State disability benefits); changes to the national healthcare system (e.g. to its funding or efficiency); actions leading to changes in the economy; communication (e.g. public statements criticising the actions of insurers).

The future role of the State in healthcare provision is crucial to the planning of insurance strategy. Changes to State health care provision may considerably impact the benefits which an insurer can offer. In particular, consideration needs to be given to expectations of future trends and any promises made by the State about future healthcare provision.

IP INSURANCE — future State role considerations: in many developed countries, the government may provide subsistence levels of income to individuals that cannot work; insurance is needed as a top-up; a State may see its role as investing in prevention rather than increasing the level of benefits (e.g. encouraging healthier practices at home and at work, and by rehabilitation); in a bid to manage costs, governments may introduce stricter qualification rules for State benefits and requirements aimed at getting claimants back to work; governments encouraging preventative measures bring benefits: reduced demand on State resources; reduced State benefits paid out on sickness and disability; increased taxation revenue from increased numbers able to work; increased consumer spending; wider economic productivity.

CI INSURANCE — future State role considerations: most countries do not provide State lump sum benefits on disability, and it is perhaps unlikely that they will intend to do so in the future. Therefore CI insurance is likely to continue to be provided by the private sector in many countries.

LTCI — future State role considerations: there has been considerable discussion in many developed countries about the extent to which governments fund long-term care costs, particularly as demand is increasing globally due to increased longevity, medical technology advances, and social change (e.g. higher divorce rates leading to more elderly people living alone). Different countries are at various stages of this demand curve. Countries where long-term care costs are currently heavily funded with public money may wish to consider increasing the use of private arrangements such as LTCI products — however, turning this into a possibility can be difficult (LTCI may be prohibitively expensive; lack of disposable funds; perception that the State supports older people so there should be no need for self-provision; insurance industry concerned about changing State cover in the future).

PMI — future State role considerations: governments in many developed countries with significant State health care systems have seen healthcare costs rise, particularly where the country has an ageing population and individual treatment costs have been rising. Governments may need to prioritise healthcare costs relative to other items of spending or raise tax rates; alternatively they may look to manage costs by targeting resources at specific groups — potentially private health care provision could meet some of the gaps.

exam_qa.q: Outline the areas in which government can affect health insurance products.
exam_qa.a: Regulation or legislation directly affecting insurance products (e.g. disclosure requirements or restrictions on rating factors used); taxation (on benefits, premiums or investments); changes to State healthcare benefits (e.g. State disability benefits); changes to the national healthcare system (e.g. to its funding or efficiency); actions leading to changes in the economy; communication (e.g. public statements criticising the actions of insurers).

exam_qa.q: Outline the benefits to a government of encouraging preventative measures to improve the nation's health.
exam_qa.a: Reduced demand on State resources; reduced State benefits paid out on sickness and disability; increased taxation revenue from the increased numbers able to work; increased consumer spending (as more people are physically able to and have the income to spend more); positive effects on the economy (e.g. greater productivity leading to more profitable companies); people who are healthier feel happier and are more likely to feel good about the current government (i.e. more likely to re-elect them).

exam_qa.q: Suggest reasons why it may be difficult for a country where the State heavily funds long-term care costs to increase use of LTCI.
exam_qa.a: LTCI may be prohibitively expensive for individuals to purchase; there may be a lack of disposable funds from those who need LTCI (e.g. money may be tied up in pension funds and property); there may be a perception (or requirement) that the State supports older people and so there should be no need for self-provision; the insurance industry may be concerned about changing State cover in the future (e.g. as another party comes into power and may change eligibility rules for State care, leading to a higher volume and value of claims than expected).

sections.title: The Role of Employers in the Provision of Health and Care Benefits

subsections.title: Overview
bullets: In some countries, the majority of both the private medical and income protection insurance markets is employer paid or sponsored (voluntary) cover.
The attitude of employers to such staff benefits is vital to the future insurance revenue streams for these two products.
Group health products provided by an employer for its staff — influences on demand for the two main products: group IP insurance and group PMI.

subsections.title: Valued Benefits
bullets: From an employer's point of view, disability or medical benefits may be a normal part of the total benefits package, with the advantage that: PMI may assist in an early return to work; IP demonstrates a continuing financial responsibility when an employee is ill for a prolonged period.
Such benefits within an employment package may also be a significant incentive in attracting and retaining staff, or to incentivise and reward individuals on promotion to higher grades.
Some schemes also cover dependants, although this has become less common due to increased cost and the economic climate.

subsections.title: Tax Efficiency
bullets: It may be tax efficient for the insurance premiums to be paid by the employer where these are then allowed as a business expense. However, the extent of any tax efficiency may vary by jurisdiction.

For example, an IP insurance claim benefit could be paid gross to the employer who then passes it on to the employee via the payroll system, so it could be subject to tax in the hands of the employee in the same way as was their pre-disability salary.
PMI could also be tax efficient for the employer.
Whilst tax efficiency for the employer will encourage groups to offer healthcare insurance to employees, the tax efficiency for employees will affect the take-up rates (where cover is optional).

subsections.title: Relative Cost
bullets: In general, due to economies of scale and greater pooling, group risks tend to be priced more cheaply per person than if the disability policy were bought on an individual basis. However, there are many examples where this is not the case (e.g. a young person in an elderly workforce where a single unit rate is calculated, or an office worker in a heavy engineering company may get a cheaper rate by applying to the insurer directly).

Group schemes are normally paid by the employer and membership is often compulsory for employees (often due to the preference of the insurer, who would be exposed to anti-selection if membership was voluntary). Therefore, opting out for cheaper individual cover is neither financially advantageous, nor is it possible.

The cost of cover for the scheme as a whole is likely to be less than the total cost of individually purchased products, for reasons beyond economies of scale and experience rating: less scope for anti-selection than in the individual market; the employer can take an active approach to claims management (it is in its interest to reduce claims); employees may be less aware that they have the cover and so may not make a claim when the need arises (this is much less likely with a considered purchase); a large employer is in a better position to negotiate a lower price with an insurer; initial expenses tend to be lower due to less underwriting and usually lower commission levels.

Lives that are in poor health may be covered under a group scheme up to the free cover level for IP insurance and may be eligible for some group PMI benefits, but may be unable to obtain suitable (or any) individual cover because of their poor health.

Some large group PMI schemes offer cover to employees on a Medical History Disregarded (MHD) basis — meaning any medical conditions existing before the cover started would be covered (rather than excluded). These schemes are usually heavily experience-rated or cost plus (i.e. self-insured), so most or all of the risk is borne by the employer.

key_terms.definition: The maximum benefit amount in a group scheme up to which individual underwriting is not required; lives in poor health may be covered up to this level even if they would not be eligible for individual cover.
A method of pricing group insurance where the premium is based on the actual claims experience of the group, rather than market averages.

An arrangement where the employer bears all or most of the risk of claims itself, typically paying an administration fee to the insurer rather than a risk premium; commonly used for large group PMI schemes on an MHD basis.

A basis for large group PMI schemes where any medical conditions that existed before the cover started would be covered (rather than excluded), usually in exchange for the scheme being heavily experience-rated or self-insured.

exam_qa.q: Economies of scale and experience rating are cited as two reasons why group business tends to be cheaper per head than individual cover. Suggest other factors that may contribute to this difference.
exam_qa.a: The cost of cover will be influenced by claims costs, which are generally lower on group business because: there is less scope for anti-selection than in the individual market; the employer can take an active approach to claims management — it is in its interest to reduce claims; employees may be less aware that they have the cover and so may not make a claim when the need arises (this is much less likely with a considered purchase). In addition, one could argue that a large employer is in a better position to negotiate a lower price with an insurer. Initial expenses also tend to be lower due to less underwriting and usually lower commission levels.

5.summary:
The healthcare marketplace

The key drivers of demand are:
• the increasing level of wealth and sophistication
• demographic trends (people living longer, especially in disability or with chronic conditions; comorbidity)
• economic conditions (reducing demand for savings and increasing demand for protection)
• the level and form of state health care provision
• employer provided benefits
• technology trends (aggregator websites, wearable devices).

Customer perception of the health insurance industry is influenced by:
• public understanding of health and care products (particularly an issue in developing markets)
• tax incentives (e.g. UK PMI subject to IPT at 12%, no relief; Ireland and Germany allow partial relief)
• adverse media coverage (e.g. corporate failures, mis-selling, compliance breaches).

Other factors affecting the health and care insurance market:
• competition — often niche markets with few providers; possible consolidation; State-sponsored insurers as barriers to entry; local legislation may require local partnerships
• lessons from overseas — CI insurance derived from South Africa's dread disease product; MME cover less successful (non-indemnity basis a big negative)
• technology — new business acquisition, underwriting (new data sources), claims management, consumer access tools, wearable devices, price comparison sites
• data science — step-change in risk analysis; potential issues around data quality, consent, mis-selling (nudging), data security, legacy systems, staff skills, ethical use and evolving regulation
• climate change — wide-ranging impact on health, mortality, physical assets and financial markets; actuaries must account for climate-related risks in decisions
• pandemics — significant threat to health and care insurers (see Chapter 6).

Methods of sale
• Main channels: brokers, tied agents, own salesforce, direct marketing, worksite marketing.
• Each channel attracts clients with particular demographic profiles, and needs different contract designs and pricing.
• Sales methods may be regulated: types of products sold; how advice provided/paid for; charge caps; requirements around who can distribute certain products; restrictions on competition; how mis-selling is dealt with.

Mis-selling
• IP: income replacement ratio kept below 100%; benefit escalation may not match income growth; name 'Income Protection' misleading if no unemployment cover; insurer's right to restrict benefits when income drops could lead to litigation.
• CI: limited number of diseases covered; complex and technical policy wording (e.g. heart attack definitions); standardised definitions help but can still be complex.
• LTCI: selling cash not care — ADL-based cash payments may not cover actual care costs; poor investment performance on unit-linked policies; mitigation is to make cash nature explicit at point of sale.
• PMI: restricted vs comprehensive confusion; acute/chronic condition misunderstanding is a major issue.
• Product type confusion — CI vs IP: could lead to complaints, lapses, and contested claims.
• Insurtech is reducing mis-selling risk in developed markets; less easily in developing markets.

The role of the State in healthcare provision
• Insurers try to complement the role of the State.
• Means testing certain State benefits creates a need for health insurance. Means testing may be driven by demographic change, commitment to a low tax environment, and competing demands on public expenditure.
• Introduction of co-payments increases demand for PMI and cash plans; may slightly reduce claims.
• The future role of the State in healthcare provision is crucial to an insurer's strategy. Indicators to watch: IP — levels and public awareness of State benefits, preventative and rehabilitation measures, changes to State benefits; LTCI — means-testing thresholds and how LTC will be funded; PMI — government commitment to State healthcare funding and tax culture; all — demographic changes.

The role of employers in provision of health and care benefits
• IP insurance and PMI mainly sold on a group basis. The attitude of employers to providing such benefits is key to the insurer's future revenue streams, influenced by:
  - value of benefits to the employer (perk, or enabling early return to work)
  - tax efficiency (premiums may be a business expense; benefits may be taxed as salary)
  - cost relative to individual purchase (group usually cheaper due to lower anti-selection, employer claims management, lower initial expenses, negotiating power).
• Group schemes may cover dependants; Medical History Disregarded (MHD) basis available for large schemes, usually heavily experience-rated or self-insured.

6.title: General Business Environment (2)

subsections.title: Overview of Chapter 6
bullets: This chapter continues to discuss aspects of the general business environment that a health and care insurer operates within.

Section 1: use of medical underwriting versus pre-existing condition (PEC) clauses, including front-end and back-end underwriting approaches, psychosocial factors in IP underwriting, and 'once only' underwriting on group IP.
Section 2: genetic testing — anti-selection issues, increased availability of testing, implications for insurance products, and regulatory restrictions.
Section 3: use of counterparties — credit/counterparty risk, third party outsourcers, brokers, hospitals/consultants, and independent investigators.
Section 4: external influences — demographic, medical, economic, political and social factors, pandemics, climate change, environmental factors, and mental health.
Section 5: key medical conditions affecting mortality and morbidity, specific diseases, and regional variations.

sections.title: Use of Medical Underwriting versus Pre-existing Condition Clauses

subsections.title: Introduction
bullets: Underwriting is an important feature of product design and the effective management of risk for a health insurer.

Medical underwriting can manage risk by: protecting the insurer from anti-selection; identifying substandard lives for special terms; determining the most suitable level/type of special terms; ensuring fair risk classification; keeping actual morbidity experience close to that assumed in pricing; and reducing over-insurance risk through financial underwriting.

This section is primarily in the context of individual health insurance business. Group business has particular underwriting features: the underwriter can take a global view of whether the group experience will approximate the premium basis; there is less scope for anti-selection if a high proportion of eligible members join at the first available opportunity.
For group business, the insurer typically requires all insured members to be 'actively at work' on the day cover commences and may insist on a waiting period before a claim is accepted.

A 'free cover limit' may be set for group business — a level of benefit available to all members without individual underwriting; only the largest cases need to provide medical information or undergo tests.

By underwriting, the insurer attempts to classify applicants into homogeneous risk groups so that an appropriate price can be placed on each group commensurate with the risks undertaken. The insurer aims to refuse cover to the fewest possible number of people, generally where the applicant is in such poor health that the risk becomes impossible to quantify.

Health and care risks are complex and the propensity to claim is a function of many factors: current state of health; personal medical history; family medical history; occupation; area of residence; pastimes/leisure activities; benefits covered.

The proposal form gathers personal details (date of birth, address, gender) used to calculate rating factors (age, location, gender). Financial underwriting (salary details) is also used to prevent over-insurance, fraudulent claims, and early lapses.

key_terms.definition: The process of assessing an applicant's health risk using medical information to classify them into appropriate risk groups and set suitable premiums or terms.

Assessment of an applicant's financial circumstances (e.g. salary details) to ensure the level of cover is in line with the person's normal financial needs, minimising the risk of over-insurance or fraudulent claims.
A level of benefit available to all members of a group scheme without individual underwriting; only cases above this limit require medical evidence.

Where customers who buy a product are those more likely to claim, and the price does not fully reflect this increased risk. Can arise where individuals know more about their own health risk than the insurer.

exam_qa.q: State six ways in which medical underwriting can be used to manage risk.
exam_qa.a: 1) Protect the insurer from anti-selection, especially from lives whose health is so seriously impaired that risk cannot be accurately assessed. 2) Identify lives with substandard health risk for whom special terms need to be quoted. 3) For substandard risks, identify the most suitable approach and level for special terms to be offered. 4) Ensure adequate risk classification so that all risks are rated fairly. 5) Ensure actual morbidity experience does not depart too far from that assumed in pricing. 6) For larger proposals, financial underwriting reduces the risk from over-insurance.

exam_qa.q: Describe the other information that may be requested on the proposal form.
exam_qa.a: Personal details (date of birth, address, gender) used to calculate rating factors. Also financial details such as salary to ensure the level of cover is in line with normal financial needs (financial underwriting), to minimise: people taking out large policies intending to claim fraudulently; adverse claims experience through anti-selection; cancellations and early lapses when people realise they cannot afford the cover.

subsections.title: Underwriting as a Deterrent
bullets: The number of questions required to elicit underwriting information can be daunting and can deter the consumer from applying for cover. A number of shortcuts have been devised, with a mixed reception from regulators and consumers.

subsections.title: Advantages and Disadvantages of Front-end and Back-end Underwriting
bullets: FRONT-END UNDERWRITING: At the proposal stage, the applicant completes a fact-find measuring healthcare needs against policy benefits and identifying continuing ability to pay premiums. Medical information is provided to allow the underwriter to allocate the applicant to a risk class — similar to life insurance underwriting.
Most long-term health insurance products involve some degree of front-end (or full medical) underwriting, including medical, lifestyle and financial underwriting.

Additional sources of medical evidence include: reports from medical doctors consulted; a medical examination on the applicant; specialist medical tests. Supplementary questionnaires may be used (e.g. for asthma, epilepsy, anxiety/depression). Questionnaires may also be sent to obtain occupational or financial information.

The major advantage of front-end underwriting to the insured is that all details are provided upfront and they know exactly what is and is not covered. The disadvantage is the length of the proposal form, the time taken, and the degree of personal disclosure.

Higher risks that are not acceptable at standard rates can be dealt with as follows: offered higher (loaded) premiums or a reduction in benefit; offered reassessment following a deferral period (if the higher risk is deemed temporary); declined; offered a different (less risk-intensive) type of policy; offered to a reinsurer facultatively with zero retention; have certain specific perils excluded.
For PMI, the most common approach is to exclude specific conditions — those the applicant has suffered previously (pre-existing condition exclusions).

BACK-END UNDERWRITING: The applicant is immediately covered for benefits relating to any new condition suffered (i.e. medical conditions arising after the application has been accepted). No checking of medical history is done at this stage, nor are specific exclusions applied. At point of claim, the company checks the medical condition against the claimant's medical record. If the condition, or an illness directly related to it, arose before the date of application, the claim may be refused.
In the UK, back-end (moratorium) underwriting is used on short-term ASU business, group CI insurance cover, and the majority of PMI business.

The advantage of back-end underwriting is the immediacy of cover without formal underwriting. The disadvantage is the lack of certainty of what is and is not covered, especially when the insurer has the right to rule on what illnesses are connected. In the UK in the 1990s, refusing claims for connected conditions prompted action from the insurance ombudsman.

It is more usual to disallow claims arising from medical conditions that have occurred in the five years before application, and that after a two-year period without treatment for these pre-existing conditions, future claims will be covered. This moratorium approach is most applicable to PMI.

The main drawback of back-end underwriting to the insurer (compared to front-end) is that it is much more difficult to establish what conditions existed at the time the policy was taken out, and in particular which ones the customer actually knew about.
Claims underwriting takes place even if a front-end approach is taken — the insurer must establish whether the actual claim is valid under policy conditions, and check for possible non-disclosure.

key_terms.definition: Underwriting carried out at the proposal stage — medical and other information is collected and assessed before the policy is issued, so the insured knows exactly what is and is not covered from the outset.

Underwriting whereby the applicant is immediately covered for new conditions without prior medical assessment; underwriting is carried out at the claims stage by checking whether the condition pre-existed the policy commencement date.

The period during which claims for pre-existing conditions will not be covered under a back-end underwritten policy; typically relates to conditions occurring in the five years before application, with coverage resuming after two years without treatment.
Where the policyholder did not declare all relevant information at the application stage, either deliberately or inadvertently.
A method of dealing with a high-risk applicant by offering the entire risk to a reinsurer, with the insurer retaining no liability.

exam_qa.q: State six actions that can be taken to deal with increased risks that are not acceptable on normal terms following front-end underwriting.
exam_qa.a: 1) Offer higher (loaded) premiums or a reduction in benefit. 2) Offer reassessment following a deferral period if the higher risk is deemed temporary. 3) Decline the application if the additional risk is too high. 4) Offer a different, less risk-intensive type of policy. 5) Offer to a reinsurer facultatively with zero retention. 6) Exclude certain specific perils from the cover.

exam_qa.q: Explain the main drawback to the insurer of underwriting at the claims stage as opposed to adopting a front-end approach.
exam_qa.a: The main drawback is that it will be much more difficult to establish what conditions existed at the time the policy was taken out, in particular which of these the customer actually knew about. Under the front-end approach, the insurer already knows what conditions are 'pre-existing' and therefore excluded from cover.

subsections.title: Pre-existing Condition (PEC) Exclusions
bullets: The use of PEC exclusions always requires transparent communication and careful explanation to policyholders.

Exclusions often include related conditions — e.g. someone with a history of back problems may have 'disorders of the back and associated conditions' excluded. Specific details of exclusions are given to the applicant in writing at outset.

In the UK, concerns have been raised over some products where the extent of exclusions is such that there is a very low likelihood of ever being able to make a claim (e.g. payment protection insurance, a type of ASU creditor insurance).

key_terms.definition: A policy exclusion that removes cover for conditions the applicant has suffered previously, including conditions related to those pre-existing ones; requires transparent communication to policyholders.

subsections.title: Size of Benefit
bullets: Under long-term health insurances, the applicant can choose whatever level of cover they want within reason. The degree of underwriting and medical information required increases with the sum insured/benefit level.

It is unlikely that a simple PEC exclusion will be used as a method of underwriting where, for example in the UK, a CI insurance sum insured exceeds £100,000 or an IP insurance benefit exceeds £20,000 pa.

These thresholds are known as 'non-medical limits'. At higher levels of cover, the underwriter will generally insist on further evidence of health — e.g. a medical attendant's report, an independent medical examination, or specific tests.
The limits depend on the degree of risk the insurer is taking on and often vary by age and certain benefit features (e.g. for income protection, limits decrease with age, reflecting higher risk).

key_terms.definition: Thresholds of benefit level below which a simple PEC exclusion suffices as the underwriting method; above these limits, more detailed medical evidence is required.

subsections.title: Psychosocial Factors in Underwriting IP Insurance
bullets: Understanding of the things that keep people away from their workplace has developed in recent years. Back pain and stress-related incapacity continue to be a significant part of any IP insurer's claims portfolio. The underlying, often unexpressed, factors that combine to influence a decision to stop working appear to play just as big a part as the reported medical condition itself.

Logically, someone with a history of back pain carries a higher-than-average risk of further incapacitating pain. Yet a significant group among back pain claimants have no previous back pain history at all.

The likelihood that a person will take prolonged time off work may be predicted to some extent by their pre-existing resilience to common but poorly explained health problems: fatigue, headaches, dizziness, muscular and abdominal pain. A combination of these conditions or symptoms increases the risk of future long-term sickness absence, even if not attributed to a mental and nervous disorder.

Clear predictive flags for long-term disability risk include: attendance at the doctor's surgery at a rate inconsistent with known medical problems; a poor work attendance record; changes in personal circumstances; adverse reactions to normal, albeit temporarily stressful, life events.

Some insurers have introduced new approaches that focus on how people have dealt with illnesses, rather than on what conditions they have had — e.g. asking 'How much time did you take off work for this illness?' rather than listing previous illnesses.

Having a medical history is only part of the incapacity story — applicants who have been told IP insurance is unavailable or unaffordable may, through demonstration of resilient reaction to their diagnosed conditions, get the cover they need.

key_terms.definition: The underlying, often unexpressed, behavioural and social factors (e.g. resilience, work attendance record, reaction to stressful life events) that influence the likelihood of a person making a long-term disability claim, beyond their medical history alone.

subsections.title: 'Once Only' Underwriting on Group IP Insurance
bullets: A relatively recent development on group IP insurance is the availability of increases in cover at the underwriting terms applied when a life was originally underwritten. This allows greater evidence gathering for initial underwriting, with the offset of no further underwriting on future increases in benefit.
This approach (also known as 'one time' or 'once and done' underwriting) applies only to benefits over the free cover level, up to the policy maximum, and has been adopted by most group IP insurers.

Other forms of underwriting increasingly utilised for group IP insurance include: tele-interviewing (collecting underwriting information via telephone, improving quality and reducing non-disclosure); face-to-face underwriting; use of company annual medical examination data.
These techniques reduce the need for supplementary information such as medical reports and external medical examinations, reducing overall cost.

Now that more accurate and cost-efficient methods of collecting initial underwriting evidence are being used, the need for underwriting on subsequent benefit increases is reduced — explaining the willingness of insurers to offer 'once only' underwriting.

key_terms.definition: A group IP underwriting approach where increases in cover are provided at the same underwriting terms as the original policy, with no further underwriting required on future benefit increases; applies to benefits above the free cover limit up to the policy maximum.

A method of collecting underwriting information via telephone interview, which can improve the quality of information gathered (e.g. reducing non-disclosure) and help speed up acceptance of the application.

subsections.title: Genetic Testing
bullets: Genetic testing is where a person's genetic characteristic (chromosome, DNA or RNA molecule) is tested to assess their disposition to certain diseases before any symptoms manifest themselves.

For example, one of the simplest tests is for Huntington's disease (HD) — if you have a copy of a certain gene, you will almost certainly develop HD. Most genetic tests are less straightforward with many more factors affecting the likelihood of developing a genetic disease.
Genetic testing technology is relatively new but is rapidly becoming more widely available and reducing in cost.

The advances in genetic sciences and the benefits in terms of prevention, detection and treatment of diseases are enormous and have only just begun. These advances potentially create problems for the health and care insurer and for the consumer.

key_terms.definition: Testing of a person's genetic characteristics (chromosomes, DNA or RNA molecules) to assess their disposition to certain diseases before any symptoms manifest themselves.

Medical conditions caused by mutations in a single gene; these are the conditions for which genetic test results are currently most predictive, though they are all fairly rare in the general population.

subsections.title: Anti-selection
bullets: Genetic testing can indicate a greater predisposition to particular diseases. An individual with this information will feel the need for insurance. However, where an insurance book becomes weighted with policyholders who have increased susceptibility to a particular condition and have selected higher-than-average levels of benefit, the insurer will probably not have enough in premiums to meet the ultimate claim outgo.

One of the principles of an insurance contract is that all factors material to risk are disclosed by the applicant so that risk can be properly assessed. A point of contention arises with information arising from a genetic test as this can cause an imbalance of information.

Many argue that an insurer has no right to additional insight into an applicant's potential future medical conditions and so should be restricted to traditional underwriting methods. The insurance argument is that the result of any genetic test may make a person aware of their predisposition to a particular disease and hence encourage them to find insurance, possibly at elevated levels.
Unfortunately, for some, the appropriate premium will be unaffordable.

Example: the existence of BRCA1 and BRCA2 genes might warrant a critical illness insurance premium some 40 times higher than standard, because of the significantly higher likelihood of breast or ovarian cancer. The danger is that some individuals who have undergone genetic tests will become virtually uninsurable if this information is shared with the underwriter, giving them an incentive not to disclose its findings.

key_terms.definition: Genetic markers associated with a significantly higher likelihood of breast or ovarian cancer; their existence might warrant a CI insurance premium some 40 times higher than standard.

subsections.title: Increased Availability and Cheapness of Testing
bullets: Genetic tests are used for many purposes and are increasingly accessible to consumers, though there are issues with the accuracy of direct-to-consumer genetic tests.

The solution for their acceptance within an underwriting framework is not easy to visualise. The dangers to the insurer are not always appreciated by the proposer, who may not see the difference in applying for insurance before and after the genetic test.

With cheap tests, the time might come when individuals with no adverse family history could request a genetic profile, just as many healthy individuals have regular medical check-ups. Whilst this would not change the mortality/morbidity of the typical individual, it would subdivide the large group into categories ranging from the super-fit to substandard, with a corresponding effect on the cost of insurance.

subsections.title: Implications for Insurance Products
bullets: If genetic testing were to become very widespread, one solution would be for the government to insure the population (social insurance) on a compulsory basis for a fixed benefit — this would mean the end of private insurance as we know it.
If significant selection against insurers resulted, they would have no choice but to withdraw from the market (or lose lots of money).

If genetic tests could save more in claims than they cost to administer, it could be in the insurer's financial interest to commission a test for applicants with no adverse family history — to identify a class of preferred lives for which they could gain commercial advantage by reducing premiums.

A more practical solution would be for contract terms to exclude all claim conditions connected to the identified genetic circumstances. There are potential ethical issues if all claim conditions connected to genetic circumstances were excluded, particularly as some aspects of the consumer's genetic circumstances cannot be changed whereas others may be more under the consumer's control.

In any case, pricing will need adjustment as soon as it is believed that the existence of genetic testing has a significant effect on the levels of risk within the portfolio. Some anti-selection is inevitable and this will probably mean an increase in health and care insurance premiums generally.

Regarding which product is most at risk from a ban on using genetic testing results: CI insurance is probably most at risk (genetic factors influence predisposition to covered diseases including the greatest number of claims — cancer and heart disease). Pre-funded LTCI carries some risk (a genetic test has been developed that detects genes accounting for over half of cases of Alzheimer's). Immediate needs LTCI is less affected as the impact of genetics can be allowed for in the price at the time of purchase. IP insurance is arguably less at risk (majority of claims are for reasons with a less obvious genetic link, e.g. stress or back problems; a lot of IP business is group-related where premiums/benefits can be adjusted if required). PMI is almost certainly the least at risk (premiums and policy conditions on existing policies can be reviewed on an annual basis if required to deal with the impact of new genetic tests).

It is possible that health and care insurers could also use tests to help the policyholder — providing 'actionable data' where medical intervention or lifestyle change could mitigate risk, benefiting both the policyholder's health and the insurer through lower future claims.

exam_qa.q: Discuss which of the four main types of health insurance (IP, CI, LTCI, PMI) is most at risk from a ban on the use of genetic testing results, and which is least at risk.
exam_qa.a: CI insurance is probably most at risk — genetic factors can influence predisposition to many of the diseases covered, including those generating the greatest number of claims (cancer and heart disease). Pre-funded LTCI is also at some risk — a genetic test has already been developed that detects genes accounting for over half of cases of Alzheimer's disease. Immediate needs LTCI will be less affected as there is no incidence risk and the impact of genetics can be allowed for in the price at time of purchase. IP insurance is arguably less at risk — the majority of claims are for reasons where there is a less obvious genetic link (e.g. stress, back problems) and a lot of IP business is group-related where premiums or benefits can be adjusted if required. PMI is almost certainly the least at risk — premiums and policy conditions on existing policies can be reviewed on an annual basis if required to deal with the impact of new genetic tests.

subsections.title: Regulatory Restrictions on Genetics and Insurance
bullets: The use of genetic testing by life and health and care insurers has been an area of considerable debate in many countries. There is a range of approaches to regulation including guidance, legislation, and moratorium.

UK insurers (at the time of writing, May 2023): have no right to use information from genetic tests in processing applications for IP, CI, and LTCI insurances. The only approved predictive test that insurers may use in determining premiums for high benefit life insurance (not health and care insurance) has been for Huntington's disease.
In the United States, health and care insurers are not allowed to use predictive genetic tests as a basis for determining insurance terms for an individual.
In other countries, limitations on use of predictive genetic tests by health and care insurance companies are down to insurers adhering to industry-agreed guidelines.

The prohibition on using predictive genetic tests is not universal — some countries do allow life and health and care insurers to ask for disclosure of predictive genetic tests for policies over a certain value.

In New Zealand there is a voluntary insurance code covering genetic tests which prevents insurers requiring tests to be taken, but insurers will ask applicants to disclose results of any tests taken and may use these to set exclusions or raise premiums.
In Australia, genetic tests cannot be required but any results known can be used to determine terms of cover and premiums charged.

subsections.title: Overview of Counterparty Risk
bullets: The insurer may involve a number of outside (not directly employed) parties to assist in the smooth flow of the relationship between company and insured. Each will have a different role and the failure of each to meet their obligations will have a different impact on the insurer. Assessment and management of credit or counterparty risk is therefore important.
Credit risk is the risk that a counterparty to an agreement will be unable or unwilling to fulfil its obligations.

Counterparty risks were identified in Subject SP1 — specifically those associated with reinsurers, distribution, managing the client relationship, controlling claims, and the provision of medical services.

The insurer must be aware of the risks, be able to quantify the extent of them, and take action to mitigate them. Usually there will be a legal contract between the insurer and the third party — a service level agreement (SLA).

The SLA should contain: dates the agreement is in force; details of parties, main contacts, who carries out the work, and where ultimate responsibility lies; details of what the third party will provide (including levels and frequency); details of payments (amounts, frequency, methods, conditions); details of any other obligations on the insurer's part; details of how and how often service standards will be monitored; details of what action will be taken if service standards are failed (e.g. penalties); details of cancellation agreements ('get out' clauses); details of dispute resolution procedures; details of the jurisdiction under which the agreement is written.

Main reasons for outsourcing include: greater flexibility (e.g. to deal with peaks and troughs in claim demands); access to expertise; allows the insurer to concentrate on strategy rather than day-to-day matters; may be cheaper due to economies of scale, competition for services, or cheaper labour overseas.

key_terms.definition: The risk that a counterparty to an agreement will be unable or unwilling to fulfil its obligations.

A legal contract between an insurer and a third party that sets out precisely the role each party agrees to undertake, monitoring procedures, penalties for failure, cancellation agreements, and dispute resolution procedures.

exam_qa.q: Outline the main counterparty risks associated with distribution.
exam_qa.a: 1) A distributor may be in a position, knowingly or otherwise, to commit the insurer to conditions that were not the original purpose of the contract. 2) A distributor handling policyholder money transactions may not return premiums received at the appropriate time, or may become bankrupt before passing them over — this is known as brokers' balances, and represents a credit risk. A similar problem may arise with claims. 3) A distributor, in dealing with clients on behalf of the insurer, may bring the insurer into disrepute.

exam_qa.q: Briefly suggest reasons why outsourcing has become so popular.
exam_qa.a: It gives the insurer more flexibility, e.g. to deal with peaks and troughs in claim demands; it gives access to expertise; it allows the insurer to concentrate on strategy, rather than day-to-day matters; it may be cheaper, due to economies of scale, competition for services, or cheaper labour overseas; other insurers are doing it.

subsections.title: Third Party Outsourcers
bullets: The insurer may enlist the support of outside parties to perform almost any function within the product control cycle: product design, pricing, marketing, sales, underwriting, claims management, experience monitoring, and valuation.

These services might be provided by: reinsurers; actuarial consultants; brokers (e.g. sales, early stages of underwriting); specialist consultants (e.g. specialist companies dealing with underwriting and/or claims management; marketing agencies); other insurance companies (taking a share of risk, perhaps under a coinsurance agreement).
Other services not directly related to the product control cycle that are often outsourced include: IT systems, training, administration, and investment.

Among the more usual services provided are those of medical evidence gatherers, asked to intervene at two stages: at the underwriting stage (obtaining evidence required to move an application forward after initial analysis — doctor's report, independent examination); and at the claim stage (obtaining evidence necessary to validate the degree of impairment and hence the claim).

To the extent that they deal directly with actual or potential clients in the insurer's name, there is a reputational (brand) risk. Full references and credentials should be checked before such parties are employed. This is particularly important for healthcare due to the sensitive nature of the information held.

People will tend to claim on these products at a time of distress. Requests for information at the claims stage that are not handled with sensitivity will reflect badly on the name of the insurer. Ideally the insurer should have some way of monitoring the standard of service provided by the third party, e.g. via customer surveys, detailed in the agreement between the two parties.

key_terms.definition: The full lifecycle of a product: product design, pricing, marketing, sales, underwriting, claims management, experience monitoring, and valuation — any stage of which may be outsourced to a third party.

Third-party specialists engaged by the insurer to obtain medical evidence either at the underwriting stage (to support the application process) or at the claim stage (to validate the degree of impairment and the claim).

exam_qa.q: Suggest what services within the product control cycle could be outsourced and who might provide such services.
exam_qa.a: The product control cycle has the following stages: product design, pricing, marketing, sales, underwriting, claims management, experience monitoring, and valuation. Any of these could be outsourced entirely or in part. For example, the collecting of medical evidence part of underwriting could be outsourced, leaving the insurance company to focus on the decision-making part. These services might be provided by: reinsurers; actuarial consultants; brokers (e.g. sales, early stages of underwriting); specialist consultants (e.g. specialist companies dealing with underwriting and/or claims management; marketing agencies); other insurance companies (under a coinsurance agreement).

subsections.title: Brokers
bullets: Brokers are independent of the insurer and as such there is no representational risk. However, for some lines of business, particularly short-term, the broker may hold sums of money from the insured in transition to the insurer — these are called brokers' balances.
There is thus a credit risk of default and a reduction in the insurer's ability to earn investment returns on these premiums. The insurer may hold capital to protect against this risk.
If the risk to the investment return is significant, this should be allowed for in the investment income assumption when the product is priced.

key_terms.definition: Sums of money held by brokers from the insured in transition to the insurer; these create a credit risk for the insurer if the broker defaults before passing the premiums over.

subsections.title: Hospitals and Consultants
bullets: Under many comprehensive PMI products in many countries, the insurer has a working practice (if not a legal policy condition) to indemnify the charges incurred by the insured within the terms of the policy. This means meeting fees charged by the surgeon, anaesthetist, consultant, other medical personnel, and accommodation costs of the hospital.

Many insurers have semi-official scales of charges that they are prepared to pay, outside of which they will negotiate with the hospital or consultant. Often the insured knows little of this method of cost control, although most insurers will have clauses in their policy wording that indicate fees will be paid only at a 'usual and customary level'.

Many PMI insurers require customers to obtain authorisation prior to treatment so that eligibility can be confirmed and any shortfall in the amount the insurer would pay relative to the actual likely cost is highlighted where known. However, due to the high volume of potential providers of treatment in some markets, it is not feasible to pre-agree all medical staff and hospital charges before treatment.

Any claim that exceeds the insurer's tolerance level (say more than 10% above the scale) would be challenged with the treatment provider, but in practice the insurer would normally still pay for the treatment in full rather than leave the customer to pay any shortfall.

If a treatment provider repeatedly charges unacceptably high amounts, the insurer may effectively 'black list' the provider, warning customers at the pre-authorisation stage that they may not get full indemnity of charges if they choose this provider.
One reason PMI insurers try to encourage use of preferred hospitals and hospital groups is that they have more control over the fees that are charged.
The 'usual and customary' or 'reasonable and customary' restriction on fees is to protect the insurer against the treatment provider charging excessive amounts.

key_terms.term: Usual and customary (reasonable and customary) fees
key_terms.definition: A standard built into PMI policy wording to protect the insurer against treatment providers charging excessive amounts — fees will only be paid up to the level that is usual and customary for the type of treatment in the relevant location.

A PMI process requiring the insured to obtain the insurer's approval prior to treatment, enabling eligibility to be confirmed and any shortfall in the amount payable relative to likely actual cost to be highlighted in advance.

subsections.title: Other Medical and Care Providers
bullets: To a lesser extent, an insurer is exposed when promising to indemnify the fees sought by other medical providers, including: general practitioners (in some overseas policies and increasingly as a limited benefit in the UK); dentists (scales of charges often agreed in advance); physiotherapists (a small part of the overall benefit package under private medical insurance).
Where these benefits are included, there are usually monetary limits (annual per treatment or a limit on the number of treatments) on the amount of benefit.

A growing number of PMI policies include cover for what were once called 'alternative or complementary treatments' — osteopathy, chiropractic, acupuncture, homeopathy, and herbal medicine. As for other treatments, these need to be 'medically necessary' and require a doctor's referral. Insurers will usually only allow treatment by 'approved' practitioners (qualified with the appropriate professional body and with a minimum time in practice).
Although LTCI is normally not indemnity, insurers may have reputational exposure to the quality of care delivered to policyholders by long-term care providers.

subsections.title: Independent Investigators and Assessors
bullets: Independent assessors are often utilised in IP insurance claims: (a) to validate the claim in the first place; (b) to assess the continuing state of health of the claimant periodically thereafter.
There is a reputational risk, where the investigator can be seen as the hard-nosed detective seeking to force a claimant back to work before the latter feels physically or psychologically prepared.
If properly and sympathetically handled, the process can aid both parties, with advice on rehabilitation programmes, State welfare benefits, and the availability of medical assistance.
Rehabilitation programmes need to be presented as being a help, rather than a hindrance, to claimants.

subsections.title: Overview of External Influences
bullets: External influences on health insurance business covered in this section: demographic influences; medical influences such as general medical advances, availability of surgical procedures, and new diseases; economic influences such as business optimism and employment security; political stability; social influences; pandemics; climate change; environmental factors; mental health.
We also consider the extent to which insurers can allow for uncertainty arising from external factors by making premiums and benefits reviewable.

subsections.title: Demographic Influences
bullets: In many countries in the developed world, the population is: getting older; living longer (with advances in medical science improving the health of the nation); having fewer children.

GETTING OLDER: The average age of the population is increasing. The problem for the State is that the number of retired people as a proportion of the number of working people (paying taxes) is increasing, so the State is having to pay out more benefits than the income it receives. The smaller proportionate workforce means that the cost of a pay-as-you-go welfare system is an increasing amount falling on relatively fewer shoulders. There is therefore demand for health and care insurance products to meet the shortfall in expectations.

LIVING LONGER: Medical science is advancing to increase life expectancy, coupled with growing interest in health and methods to sustain it. People are becoming more aware of their health and prepared to spend money to improve it. Advances in medical science can: reduce the number of people being ill; reduce the severity of illness or aid speedier recovery; increase life expectancy. This has many impacts including: reducing claim incidence on IP and CI insurances; delaying the incidence of long-term care; increasing chances of recovery from illness but may also mean claimants stay longer in claim (where they would formerly have died) under IP and LTCI; increase in PMI claims as people live longer but possibly with poor health.

HAVING FEWER CHILDREN: Lower fertility increases the wealth of individuals and their potential to buy insurance but adds to the ageing population problem with its draining on Treasury funds. Having children is one of the key life events that prompts people to buy health insurance — lower fertility decreases the need for insurance (even though it increases the income available to spend on insurance). In the long term, lower fertility decreases the number of potential customers.

Lower fertility in developed countries has been driven by: lower infant mortality rates; accessible birth control; socio-economic changes leading women to postpone having children; lack of affordable housing; lack of affordable/State-funded childcare.

A striking example is China: currently around 15.5% of China's population is older than 60 years old. By 2050, 25% of the population is expected to be 65 or over. Due to the one-child policy and rising cost of living, the fertility rate dropped from 29.2 per 1,000 in 2002 to 12.2 per 1,000 in 2011.

key_terms.definition: A system where current workers' taxes fund current retirees' benefits — as the ratio of retirees to workers increases, the system becomes more expensive to fund.
The fertility rate at which a population exactly replaces itself from one generation to the next — generally around 2.1 children per woman in developed countries.

exam_qa.q: Suggest why people having fewer children may be a disadvantage from a health insurer's point of view.
exam_qa.a: Having children is one of the key life events that prompts people to buy health insurance. Hence, lower fertility decreases the need for insurance (even though it increases the income available to spend on insurance). In the long term, lower fertility also decreases the number of potential customers.

subsections.title: Medical Influences
bullets: GENERAL MEDICAL ADVANCES — IP INSURANCE: Claims conditions are affected by medical advances. Incidence rates should fall as curative and preventive powers increase. Claimants who would formerly have died during the deferred period or early duration of claim may now survive longer, potentially increasing or decreasing the length of claim. On balance, a reduction is anticipated. However, medical progress will also enable people to survive longer in disability.
GENERAL MEDICAL ADVANCES — LTCI: Just surviving at older ages will increase the claim outgo for LTCI. These trends in costs have to be factored into pricing and reserving.

GENERAL MEDICAL ADVANCES — CI COVER: Advances in medical science may well reduce the probability of a disease occurring, through curative treatment of symptoms earlier in the process, and certain prescribed diseases may be rendered less than 'critical'. Improvements in medical science can lead to increased or earlier detection of some diseases. Earlier diagnosis of a disease can bring forward claims under CI policies, increasing cost to insurers. Some diseases may be diagnosed on a term policy that formerly would not have been diagnosed until after the policy had expired.

GENERAL MEDICAL ADVANCES — PMI: Medical technology advances can create newer, more expensive ways of diagnosing and treating a particular medical problem. This increases both costs (since the insurance is written on an indemnity basis) and frequency (as new treatments are covered, often in addition to existing treatments). Such increases in claims costs should be included in the periodic review of premiums charged, particularly where the rate of advance is accelerating.

AVAILABILITY OF SURGICAL PROCEDURES: Some medical procedures may become more readily available to the general public as they become safer and cheaper. The undertaking of certain major medical procedures where they are medically necessary is a claims trigger for certain CI benefits. The increase in numbers undergoing open-heart surgery or angioplasty in the UK are two common examples — unless the benefits are changed, this trend will lead to increased incidence of CI claims.

A definite fear among insurers is that some surgical procedures covered by their CI insurance policies will become so easily available to the point of becoming elective, with a lump sum policy benefit as a bonus. This is a particular problem on claim triggers that are based on a surgical procedure rather than a medical condition.

Policy wording can be tightened for new business such that the degree of bodily impairment is now well defined. The importance of reviewable charging is key — where the insurer does not have the option to change the claim definitions on existing business, premiums for guaranteed policies must have a sufficient margin built in.

It should also be noted that where operations are performed more readily and diseases are diagnosed more simply, there is increased scope for anti-selection — it is therefore important to consider underwriting as well as pricing impacts.

NEW DISEASES: Under IP insurance, any new disease that can potentially lead to periods of invalidity longer than the deferred period can create an upsurge in claimants. There may be similar impacts on PMI and LTCI, depending on the nature of the new disease. Remember it is not only the increasing incidence, but also the increasing length of sickness periods, that can cause concern to the insurer.

Under CI cover, there may be consumer pressure to add further new diseases (perceived to be 'critical') to existing policies. This creates a strain unless the insurer has the option of removing those diseases that have become easily treatable due to medical advances.

key_terms.definition: A surgical procedure to treat the narrowing of coronary arteries; its increasing availability as a routine procedure has increased the incidence of CI claims — many UK CI insurance providers have removed angioplasty cover for new policies.

A term used to describe a CI claim benefit where the payment received significantly exceeds the cost of the treatment and the size of any associated financial loss — typically arising on claim triggers based on a surgical procedure rather than a medical condition.

A public health programme enabling early detection of certain diseases; can increase the incidence of CI claims (by bringing forward diagnoses) but may also help reduce claims if earlier detection prevents conditions from reaching a 'critical' threshold.

exam_qa.q: Discuss what impact you would expect advances in medical science to have on claims experience under PMI.
exam_qa.a: Advances in medical science can affect both the incidence and severity of PMI claims. Generally, medical advances would reduce the number of people being ill, and therefore reduce claims incidence under PMI. However, medical advances may make new treatments available that were previously not, and may also increase the demand for medical treatment — these two factors will act to increase the number of claims (probably by more than the reduction due to the first factor). Similarly, people will recover more quickly and this should reduce many claims costs, such as hospital accommodation or physiotherapy. New technology may reduce the cost of treatment, but new treatments may often be more expensive, possibly becoming cheaper as they get more widely used. Therefore the impact on average claims costs depends on what the actual medical advance is.

exam_qa.q: Suggest how earlier diagnosis of disease could help reduce claims on CI insurance.
exam_qa.a: Early diagnosis of a disease may enable it to be treated before it becomes serious enough to trigger a CI claim — e.g. a malignant tumour may be discovered and treated before it is able to spread to adjacent tissue and hence trigger a CI claim.

exam_qa.q: Outline the issues around removing cover for conditions or treatments, such as 'angioplasty', which are no longer deemed to be 'critical'.
exam_qa.a: Removing cover is effectively a reduction in benefits. This may be illegal, certainly for existing policies with guaranteed benefits. If benefits are reviewable, cover could be removed, but this will need to be very carefully handled to ensure that customers' expectations are maintained — any change may lead to increased lapsing, customer complaints, regulatory action or legal challenges.
