It would be easier to remove the cover for new business, keeping existing policies unchanged. However, this is likely to result in reduced sales volumes, particularly if this move is not in line with the rest of the market. There may also be systems problems in having different benefits for new and existing products.

exam_qa.q: Suggest how medical advances could contribute to a reduction in PMI claims costs.
exam_qa.a: New treatments, such as laser surgery, might lead to quicker recoveries and therefore lower hospital accommodation costs. Better, more effective treatments might lead to a reduced requirement for follow-up treatment, such as physiotherapy. Longer term, better treatments should result in fewer subsequent claims for the same condition. Medical advances may enable existing procedures to be carried out more cheaply or efficiently — e.g. a procedure might be made more routine so that it can be carried out by less expensive staff, such as nurses rather than doctors. Improved technology in diagnostic and screening techniques should enable some conditions to be treated earlier before they become more serious.

subsections.title: Economic Influences
bullets: The two main economic factors affecting a health insurer's business are: general strength of the economy, including levels of disposable income (for individuals) and business optimism (affecting corporate sales); and unemployment trends and expectations regarding employment security.

BUSINESS OPTIMISM: In many countries, private medical and income protection insurance markets comprise employer-sponsored policies. The willingness of employers to introduce new schemes, their acceptance of renewal terms or their keenness to reduce cover (or withdraw completely) will be influenced by the economic cycle in their sphere of business.

At certain phases of the general economic cycle, the employer has to make decisions about reducing costs — optional staff benefits such as insurance covers may be the first to go, rather than salary cuts or redundancies. Equally, in phases of healthy economic outlook, such benefits may be introduced or increased.
In broad terms, the health and care insurer will need to predict the various phases of such business optimism when modelling the premium revenue flows.
Individuals, too, are generally happier to buy new or increased cover or renew policies when good times are expected, as they will expect to have more disposable income.

EMPLOYMENT SECURITY: The tendency of members to claim under PMI policies may be affected by their perception of their immediate employment security. Threats of redundancy may encourage an increase in claims of a more elective nature, such as private consultations and certain surgical procedures, whilst they are still employed and therefore covered — i.e. a 'claim whilst you still can' mentality.

In the same scenario, the impact on IP insurance can be complex. Inception rates could fall as a result of 'presenteeism', or they could increase due to increased stress. Rates of recovery for IP insurance claimants may fall, as individuals find it increasingly difficult to return to work and therefore attempt to persuade doctors to 'sign them off' for longer. This will apply especially in self-employed cases.

Also it is possible that some employers may try to use a group IP insurance contract for the 'early retirement' of employees, whereas the insurer will be looking to enable the claimant to return to work — difficulties may arise between the employer and the insurer.
Employment insecurity will force all consumers to question whether any form of insurance is a spending priority — thus new business levels will fall at times of economic downturn.

key_terms.definition: The willingness of employers to introduce, renew or expand group health insurance schemes — closely linked to the economic cycle and phases of general economic health.
The phenomenon of employees continuing to attend work whilst ill or otherwise not functioning at full capacity, often because of job insecurity; can reduce IP insurance inception rates.

Specialists who keep regular contact with IP insurance claimants, provide advice and help encourage them to return to work — advising on State disability benefits, rehabilitation treatment, and dealing with difficulties in returning to work or coping with disability.

exam_qa.q: Explain the role of disability counsellors in IP insurance.
exam_qa.a: Disability counsellors (sometimes called claims counsellors) keep regular contact with claimants, provide advice and help encourage them to return to work. For example, they may be able to advise on State disability benefits, provide access to rehabilitation treatment, and deal with any difficulties the claimant may be having in returning to work or coping with the disability. The insurer sometimes directly employs disability counsellors, but usually this service is outsourced to a specialist company.

subsections.title: Political Influences
bullets: The keenness of individuals to purchase insurance is influenced to some extent by government policies and the extent to which the government provides alternative forms of compensation. This is especially true of health and care insurance.
The generosity of the State in providing disability income benefits (and to some extent the employer too) will impact the customer's keenness to purchase IP insurance covers.
In countries where the government offers a 'cradle to grave' healthcare promise, this will influence the demand for PMI and LTCI products.

There is an obvious difficulty in continuing to finance such a strategy in a low-tax environment when the demographic trends and rising consumer expectations are acting to make this more expensive. There is evidence of this already in the UK, with the introduction of some means testing on care for the elderly.

A change in who runs the government of a particular country could lead to considerable uncertainty with regards to government policy surrounding the provision of heath and care insurance. This uncertainty could ultimately lead to changes which could have a significant impact on the consumers and providers of health and care insurance.

One example of increased uncertainty following a change of government has been the proposed intention by President Trump in the United States to repeal the Affordable Care Act (which had been introduced with the aim of improving access to health insurance).
The current and expected political stance towards healthcare is very important to the future prospects of health insurers. It is worthwhile keeping abreast of developments.

key_terms.definition: US legislation introduced by the Obama administration with the aim of improving access to health insurance for all Americans; proposed for repeal by the Trump administration, illustrating the significant political risk for health and care insurers.

subsections.title: Social Influences
bullets: Policyholders today tend to be more aware of their benefits and more likely to make a claim on sickness insurance. There is less of a stigma to admitting sickness and taking time off work, although this can vary depending on economic conditions.

In the past, some people were reluctant to claim on sickness policies, particularly those provided by group schemes — they felt that claiming demonstrated some sort of personal weakness. This attitude has changed nowadays: claims procedures have improved, and people would much rather get the money they deserve.

Previously many policies were provided by provident societies (not-for-profit organisations owned by the policyholders themselves). Today many policies are provided by proprietary insurers, against whom most people won't think twice about claiming.

For group schemes, the increased claim consciousness may apply to the employee's spouse as well. Equally, the attentive broker will be aware of the conditions within the policy and will be keen to encourage a client to claim when the circumstances are appropriate.

Of course, protection policies are there to provide peace of mind. But equally, it can be expected that the contract is more likely to be effected (taken out) if the applicant expects to make more in claims than they pay in premiums — in a word, this is anti-selection.
In an increasingly litigious society, policyholders are more likely to dispute claims that are refused.

Furthermore, growing client sophistication is likely to be increasingly evidenced by choice of cash over care in order to arrange one's own medical outcome, or of private treatment in preference to State provision where benefits (faster treatment, possibly more expert treatment, more comfortable surroundings) are seen as exceeding the costs (premiums).

Factors that have led health insurance policyholders to be generally more aware of the cover they have purchased: more detailed sales procedures ensuring customers fully understand their cover; greater disclosure requirements (e.g. Key Features Documents, Statements of Best Practice); generally more financial awareness, possibly due to there being more articles in the media about personal finance; potentially more competition, leading to more aggressive marketing.

key_terms.definition: The increasing awareness among policyholders of their policy benefits and their readiness to claim on health insurance when the opportunity arises — a trend associated with improved product disclosure, marketing, and reduced social stigma around claiming.

exam_qa.q: Suggest what influences have led to health insurance policyholders generally being more aware of the cover they have purchased nowadays.
exam_qa.a: More detailed sales procedures to ensure customers fully understand their cover; greater disclosure requirements, e.g. Key Features Documents, Statements of Best Practice; generally more financial awareness among customers, possibly due to there being more articles in the media about personal finance; potentially more competition, leading to more aggressive marketing, so that consumers are more aware of health insurance products generally.

exam_qa.q: Outline the influences that may have led to health insurance policyholders being more litigious.
exam_qa.a: Greater media attention being given to consumer issues; the ease with which complaints can be made (e.g. the complaints procedure may be clearly explained in policyholder literature); increasing activity among law firms to offer legal representation in disputes (e.g. no-win no-fee offers); increased consumer protection; awareness of the increasing number of disputed insurance claims being settled; influences from other countries, especially from the USA, where litigation is becoming more and more common.

subsections.title: Pandemics
bullets: Globally, the World Health Organisation (WHO) recognises that infectious disease in the form of a pandemic presents a huge threat to global health. The UK national risk register from 2017 considered that emergence of a pandemic influenza and/or a new infectious disease could lead to a civil emergency equal to or higher than that of extreme weather events, with considerable uncertainty about the timing of any such event.

The coronavirus pandemic is a recent example of a significant pandemic which has had a significant impact on the health of the population in many countries and more widely on the global economy. COVID-19 was declared a pandemic by the WHO in March 2020; in May 2023, the WHO declared that COVID-19 no longer represented a 'global health emergency'.

Many populations experienced excess mortality as a result of the pandemic, with the additional impact of increased morbidity likely to represent a long-tail event. 'Long COVID' is a term used to describe a range of symptoms that persist beyond 8 weeks from initial presentation.

There have been several significant pandemics in the last two hundred years, mostly from influenza ('flu) viruses — including the Spanish influenza of 1918-1920 with an estimated worldwide death toll of at least 50 million. The HIV/AIDS pandemic that was first recognised in the 1980s remains active — although now better managed through developed drugs, over 30 million have died from AIDS-related illnesses.

The risk of infectious diseases emerging has increased due to the human impact on the environment — the increased demand for meat due to a rising global population and deforestation leading to more intensive farming put humans and animals in closer contact, increasing the risk of pathogens spreading from animals to humans.

Pandemics have the potential to materially impact all aspects of a health and care insurer's business, including: mortality and morbidity rates; economic growth and therefore related factors such as asset values and demand for products; operational aspects, including staff sickness.

The potential impact of pandemics on health and care insurers is likely to be an important consideration for regulators. In particular, regulators are likely to want to ensure that health and care insurers have sufficient capital to meet policyholder liabilities in the event of a pandemic. Regulators may wish to see health and care insurers carry out stress testing to show that they are resilient to realistic pandemic scenarios.

key_terms.definition: An epidemic of an infectious disease that has spread across a large region, multiple continents, or worldwide — recognised by the WHO as presenting a huge threat to global health.

A term used to describe a range of symptoms that persist beyond 8 weeks from the initial presentation of COVID-19 infection — likely to represent a long-tail event for health and care insurers in terms of increased morbidity.

subsections.title: Climate Change
bullets: The scientific consensus is that climate change associated with global warming is in progress (IPCC, 2014), the scale and timing of its impacts are uncertain. The future actions of society in response to climate change, and their ultimate effectiveness in mitigating these impacts, are also unknown.

The effects of climate change could include impacts on health and mortality, physical assets, and financial markets. Climate change could therefore have significant implications for health insurers — affecting product design, pricing, reserving, risk management, and investment.
Climate risks for financial companies are categorised into: physical risks; transition risks; liability risks.

PHYSICAL RISKS: First-order effects of environmental changes such as greenhouse emissions, pollution and land use — may be chronic (global warming, sea level rise) or acute (instances of extreme weather). Examples of physical climate risks: higher rates of ill health (morbidity) due to climate change-related rise in wildfires and resulting air pollution, leading to higher health insurance claims; higher life insurance claims as climate change-induced rise in heat waves increases mortality/morbidity among elderly populations with pre-existing conditions; increased frequency and concentration of extreme weather events and natural disasters; chronic rise in temperatures and humidity as a breeding ground for vector-borne diseases, increasing the likelihood and severity of epidemics and pandemics; damage to coastal real estate due to sea level rise; failure of a crop harvest due to weather, pestilence or soil degradation, increasing ill health; increased morbidity in an insured population due to pollution.

TRANSITION RISKS: Economic, political and market changes as a result of efforts to mitigate climate change. Examples: policy changes designed to reduce fossil fuel consumption (taxes, subsidies, limitations) resulting in investments in fossil fuels and carbon-intensive industries losing value; trends in consumer preferences towards 'greener' products and companies; firms' investments in carbon-intensive industries resulting in reputational damage; technological innovation causing shifts in market supply and demand (e.g. renewable energy); transition to low-carbon economy reduces demand for life insurance products (e.g. occupational pension plans) where firms' customer base is heavily exposed to conventional carbon-intensive industries. From a health and care insurance company's perspective, transition risk principally relates to: changes in the values of assets held; changes in demand for certain health and care insurance products; adaptation of operational models.

LIABILITY RISKS: Arise from injured parties seeking compensation for the impacts of climate change. Examples: new links established between air pollution and adverse health conditions, resulting in a new class of latent claims; firms that do not take into account the impact of their investment decisions on climate change experience direct claims for damages. Health and care insurance companies would also be exposed in terms of: the impact on the market values of assets held in affected companies; counterparty risks arising from any relationships with affected companies; reputational risk resulting from poor investment decisions.

It is possible that some combinations of changes to physical risks could lead to decreases in morbidity and mortality — for example if lifestyle changes in response include a more active way of living. There are also operational risk impacts arising from physical climate risks, depending on geographical location.

key_terms.definition: First-order effects of environmental changes such as greenhouse emissions, pollution and land use — may be chronic (e.g. global warming, sea level rise) or acute (e.g. extreme weather events).

Risks arising from economic, political and market changes as a result of efforts to mitigate climate change — e.g. policy changes reducing fossil fuel consumption causing investments in carbon-intensive industries to lose value.

Risks arising from injured parties seeking compensation for the impacts of climate change — e.g. new links between air pollution and adverse health conditions resulting in a new class of latent claims against firms.

Infections transmitted through a living organism such as mosquitoes and ticks (e.g. malaria and dengue fever); climate change can impact the abundance of the vectors each season and their geographic spread.

subsections.title: Environmental Factors
bullets: Environmental conditions (other than climate change) could also be a factor on general health levels, for example air pollution, which has separate drivers from climate change.
It is estimated that each year in the UK, around 40,000 deaths are attributable to exposure to outdoor air pollution, with more linked also to exposure to indoor pollutants.
Exposure to air pollution can cause a range of health issues including exacerbation of asthma, respiratory conditions, cardiovascular disease and stroke.

Indoor air pollution can be caused by emissions from domestic appliances burning carbon containing fuel such as coal or wood. Cleaning and personal care products can also contain volatile organic compounds that can have adverse impacts on health. Tobacco smoke is also a form of air pollution which can increase the risk of lung cancer.

Other environmental factors that could impact health and care insurers include exposure to: ultraviolet (UV) rays — can lead to skin cancer and cause eye issues such as cataracts; noise — repeated exposure can lead to temporary or permanent hearing loss; lead — found in old paints and pipes which can pollute drinking water, causing anaemia and kidney and brain damage; radon in the environment — a radioactive gas found everywhere, higher levels found in buildings and some areas, can cause lung cancer particularly amongst smokers and ex-smokers.

subsections.title: Mental Health
bullets: It is estimated that each year, 1 in 4 adults experience a mental health problem. Mental health problems range from common problems such as depression and anxiety to rarer problems such as schizophrenia and bipolar disorder. Our understanding of the causes of mental health conditions, and how they are treated, has developed radically in the last century.

In the UK in 2017, mental health was the most common cause of claim on income protection insurance policies. As well as stress, mental health can also include post-natal depression, ADHD, eating disorders and addiction.

People with mental health problems can experience barriers in their everyday life, and these can be exacerbated when dealing with complex consumer products such as insurance policies. Health and care insurance companies therefore need to consider carefully how they engage with customers (and employees) about their mental health, in order to remove barriers and to meet the needs of these customers.

Mental health conditions can also be directly relevant to underwriting. The health and care insurance industry needs to ensure that all underwriting decisions are based on the best information available and seek to improve this information where possible. Mental health is also often associated with other issues, such as financial stress and comorbidities; the industry should seek to understand the effects that may confound with other factors. Health and care insurance companies should also seek to be as transparent as possible about underwriting decisions and pricing aspects of mental health.

In the UK, the Association of British Insurers (ABI) has launched its Mental Health and Insurance Standards — a set of voluntary guidelines which insurers can subscribe to in order to ensure they are meeting customers' needs in the area of mental health. It covers all areas of customer engagement from improving accessibility to communicating decisions.

Ways insurers could improve accessibility to health and care insurance for customers who may experience mental health concerns include: give customers a choice of how to communicate with them (e.g. telephone, email, text message); implement processes to support customers who may need assistance to complete the application process; implement training and staff awareness programmes (developing a basic understanding of mental health problems, showing compassion, using appropriate language).

key_terms.term: ABI Mental Health and Insurance Standards
key_terms.definition: A set of voluntary guidelines issued by the Association of British Insurers which insurers can subscribe to in order to ensure they are meeting customers' needs in the area of mental health, covering all areas of customer engagement from improving accessibility to communicating decisions.

exam_qa.q: Suggest ways insurers could improve accessibility to health and care insurance for customers who may experience mental health concerns.
exam_qa.a: Give customers a choice of how to communicate with them (e.g. telephone, email, text message); implement processes to support customers who may need assistance to complete the application process (e.g. have staff who will go through forms slowly with customers and explain all terms that may be confusing); implement training and staff awareness programmes to empower front-line staff, including: developing a basic understanding of mental health problems; showing compassion to customers and their concerns; using appropriate language.

subsections.title: Key Medical Conditions Affecting Mortality and Morbidity
bullets: The main causes of mortality and morbidity, and any specific diseases in a jurisdiction, may impact any health and care business written by an insurer.

MORTALITY: Of the 55.4 million deaths worldwide in 2019, more than half (55%) were due to the top ten causes. Heart disease and stroke are the world's biggest killers, accounting for a combined 15 million deaths in 2019 — these diseases have remained the leading causes of death globally in the last 20 years. Heart disease alone was responsible for 16% of the world's total deaths in 2019 (and includes angina, myocardial infarction, and sudden cardiac death).
Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease claimed 3.3 million lives in 2019, while lung cancer caused 1.8 million deaths, making it the 6th leading cause of death globally.
Diabetes killed 1.7 million people in 2019, but in reality this is likely to be much higher — the true prevalence of type 2 diabetes is not clear due to a significant proportion being undiagnosed.
Deaths due to dementia more than doubled between 2000 and 2019, making it the 7th leading cause of global deaths in 2019.

Lower respiratory infections caused 2.6 million deaths worldwide in 2019, representing a steady reduction since 2000. Tuberculosis is no longer in the global top 10, falling to thirteenth in 2019 with a 30% reduction in global deaths. Similarly, HIV/AIDS is no longer among the world's top 10 causes of death, deaths from this cause having fallen by 51% in the last 20 years.

Kidney disease is now the 10th leading cause of death globally — mortality has increased from 813,000 in 2000 to 1.3 million in 2019. Kidney disease is one of the main complications that arise from poorly controlled diabetes.

People living in a low-income country are far more likely to die of a communicable disease than a non-communicable disease. However, causes of death from non-communicable disease are increasing, and those from communicable diseases are decreasing in low-income countries. Non-communicable diseases (NCDs) caused 70% of deaths globally, ranging from 40% in low-income countries to 88% in high-income countries.
Globally, life expectancy has increased by more than six years between 2000 and 2019 — from 66.8 years in 2000 to 73.4 years in 2019.

In the UK, there is some evidence of improvements in life expectancy over time, mainly because of falls in the death rate from coronary heart disease, lung disease, and some cancers. However, mortality experience can vary not only between jurisdictions but also within regions of a jurisdiction.

MORBIDITY: The drivers of morbidity are similarly complex, with significant differences by region and jurisdiction. The top five causes of rates of disability adjusted life years lost (DALYs/100,000) globally in 2019 were: cardiovascular disease (1st); neoplasms (2nd); maternal and neonatal conditions (3rd); other non-communicable diseases (4th); respiratory infections and TB (5th).
For high-income countries, neoplasms are the leading cause of disability, followed by cardiovascular disease, musculoskeletal conditions, mental disorders, and neurological conditions.
There are some similarities between the main causes of death and ill health — for example, strokes are the third leading cause of death in England each year and the leading cause of disability.

Longer life expectancy and poorer health could have considerable implications for the overall population. A challenge for health and care insurers will be to come up with innovative products that will help individuals pay for their long-term care, at acceptable cost to the individual.

Changes in longevity and morbidity rates may make an insurer's historic data less credible — the insurer may need to access more/better data to properly price and reserve for its risks (e.g. from professional bodies, external consultancies, reinsurers, published research from cohort studies, medical experts, big data, wearable technology).

key_terms.definition: A measure of overall disease burden, expressed as the number of years lost due to ill-health, disability or early death; used to compare morbidity across regions and income levels.

Diseases that are not passed from person to person — e.g. cardiovascular disease, cancer, diabetes, chronic respiratory diseases; they caused 70% of deaths globally in 2019, with a higher proportion in high-income countries.

Activities that are not necessary for fundamental functioning but let an individual live independently in a community (e.g. shopping, doing laundry, cooking a hot meal); individuals typically fail IADLs before the standard ADLs and they can be used as early indicators of problems that may arise in the future.

Devices that can be worn by individuals to record data such as physical activity, heart rate, and sleep patterns; health and care insurers are exploring their use to obtain better data on individual health and to build correlations between health readings and the impact on mortality or morbidity.

exam_qa.q: Explain how an insurer could access more/better data when changes in longevity and morbidity rates make historic data less credible.
exam_qa.a: It could seek expert advice from professional bodies, such as the IFoA, who may have carried out data and modelling work on trends as they have been identified. External consultancies may also be able to provide assistance. Reinsurers may offer more data but whether it is better would need to be assessed as it will relate to the same historic period as the insurer's own data. Data from other countries is an alternative but great care would need to be taken when considering any demographic trends that have occurred in other countries and assessing whether the underlying causes are the same as in the insurer's own country. The insurer may need to consider different sources of data such as published research from cohort studies, talking to medical experts, big data, wearable technology etc.

subsections.title: Specific Diseases
bullets: HEART DISEASE: Main risk factors are: smoking; being overweight; having high blood pressure. Other risk factors include: high blood cholesterol; being physically inactive; having diabetes; a family history of heart disease; ethnic background; age and gender.

CANCER: Cancer, in its various forms, has become common in developed countries. Survival rates of cancer have been improving in many developed countries, particularly in respect of the most common cancers — more men are now surviving prostate and bowel cancer and more women are surviving breast cancer.

For example, in the UK, the 10-year survival rate for prostate cancer was around 25% in 1971-72 but by 2023 it was almost 80%. For breast cancer in women, the 10-year survival rate increased from around 40% in 1971-72 to almost 75% by 2023.

Unhealthy lifestyles are a significant contributing factor — it has been estimated that about a third of cancers are caused by smoking, diet, alcohol and obesity. In addition, many cancers are detected too late, despite there being national screening programmes for certain cancers.

The UK currently has three screening programmes: for bowel cancer in men and women; and breast and cervical cancers in women. There is a test available for prostate cancer but it is not yet reliable enough to use on a large scale.
Public awareness of symptoms and the need to seek help early is still too low in many countries.

Reasons why cancer may not be diagnosed early enough to receive optimal treatment include: low public awareness of symptoms (and symptoms having other causes); low public awareness of the seriousness of the illness; lack of national screening programmes; policyholder reluctance to admit there is an issue or to visit a doctor; long waiting times in seeing a doctor; long delays in being referred to a specialist.

The improvement in survival rates may have a significant impact on health and care insurers — policyholders that would have previously been expected to die prior to cancer could now survive and claim on their critical illness policy. Alternatively, policyholders that would have been expected to stop paying premiums on death may pay premiums longer than originally expected.
Where PMI covers cancer treatments, as more cancers become treatable, this may increase the costs to PMI providers.

In countries where there is no (or limited) State cover, receiving treatment for illnesses such as cancer can result in the individual becoming 'uninsurable' and having to remain with their existing insurer on whatever terms are offered.

STROKE: Main risk factors for a stroke (cerebrovascular disease) include: high blood pressure; diabetes; heart disease; smoking; age and gender. Whilst you can have a stroke at any age, it is more likely after age 55. The risk of a stroke is higher in women due to hormonal factors and increased risks associated with childbirth, among other factors. As outlined in Section 5.1, strokes are a major cause of death and ill health in middle-income and high-income countries such as the UK.

RESPIRATORY DISEASE: Lung disease is one of the most common causes of emergency admission to hospital and is expensive in terms of acute hospital care. The two main types of lung disease are asthma and Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease (COPD). The most important cause of COPD is smoking, but a proportion of cases are work-related, triggered by exposure to fumes, chemicals and dusts at work. Experience can vary significantly between jurisdictions.

LIVER DISEASE: Liver disease is the only major cause of mortality and morbidity that is on the increase in England while it is decreasing among European peers. All three major causes of liver disease — obesity, undiagnosed infection, and, increasingly, harmful levels of alcohol intake — are preventable.

key_terms.definition: A term given to a variety of illnesses including emphysema and chronic bronchitis; the most important cause is smoking, but a proportion of cases are work-related, triggered by exposure to fumes, chemicals and dusts at work.

exam_qa.q: State the main reasons why cancer may not be diagnosed early enough to receive optimal treatment.
exam_qa.a: Low public awareness of symptoms and symptoms having other causes; low public awareness of the seriousness of the illness; lack of national screening programmes; policyholder reluctance to admit there is an issue; policyholder reluctance to visit a doctor; long waiting times in seeing a doctor; long delays in being referred to a specialist.

subsections.title: Variations by Region
bullets: Local factors can mean that particular diseases are more prevalent in specific regions.
In Africa, HIV/AIDS, lower respiratory infections and diarrhoeal disease are leading causes of ill health.
In the Eastern Mediterranean and in South-East Asia, respiratory infections and diarrhoeal disease are the two leading causes.
Depression is one of the three leading causes of disease in the WHO regions of the Americas, Europe and the Western Pacific.
Violence is the second leading cause of disease in the Americas.
Heart disease is the third leading cause in the Western Pacific.

6.summary:
Medical underwriting and pre-existing condition clauses
• Used to obtain accurate risk classification so correct premium charged.
• Front-end underwriting – uses information from proposal form, medical report, medical examination / tests and supplementary questionnaires. Insured knows what events are / are not covered by the policy, underwriting decision may take some time.
• Back-end underwriting – provides immediate cover without formal underwriting. Underwriting performed at claims stage, conditions that were / are related to those pre-existing at application are excluded. May lead to uncertainty over what is covered. Can use ‘moratorium’ approach to back-end underwriting for PMI.
• IP insurance underwriting now involves considering psychosocial factors (how people behave towards illness and medical history). Group IP cover uses: ‘once only’ underwriting; tele-interviewing; face-to-face underwriting; company medical exam data.

Genetics
• Advances in genetic testing may lead to: anti-selection (unless all relevant information is declared); unaffordable premiums, leading to non-disclosure of relevant information; need for new product designs, revised pricing, updated underwriting procedures or withdrawal from market.
• Increased availability and cheapness of tests may increase the above impacts.
• May be regulatory restrictions around requiring genetic tests to be taken, or using the results of such tests as part of the underwriting process.

Use of counterparties
• Insurer must assess, quantify and mitigate credit and counterparty risks. Managed by carefully worded service level agreement and regular monitoring.
• Main counterparties for a health insurer include: third party outsourcers, involved in an increasing number of areas; brokers, who may hold back premiums due to the insurer (brokers’ balances); hospitals, consultants and other medical providers; independent investigators and assessors, eg on IP claims.

External influences
• Populations in the developed world are: getting older, placing pressure on State resources; living longer, changing claim rates; having fewer children, increasing individuals’ wealth, placing pressure on the State.
• Impact of medical advances varies by product (eg IP reduces incidence rates but prolongs survival, PMI increases incidence rates and claim costs).
• Greater availability of some surgical procedures and simpler diagnosis increases the scope for anti-selection (eg windfall payments) on CI insurance. Emergence of new diseases puts pressure on CI insurance providers to include these and can lead to increased claims on other health products.
• Insurers should consider economic factors, including: business optimism, eg on new business and renewals (particularly for groups); employment security, eg claims (IP insurance and PMI), use of IP insurance as an early retirement vehicle, new business and renewals.
• It should also allow for: political influences; social influences; pandemics; climate change – physical, transition and liability risks; other environment factors, such as air pollution; mental health conditions.

Medical conditions
• Main conditions affecting mortality and morbidity rates change over time.
• Life expectancy has improved, but people can expect to spend more years in ill health (impacting LTCI providers). Causes vary significantly by region.
• Five specific conditions affecting mortality rates are: heart disease – key risk factors are smoking, obesity and high blood pressure; cancer – recent improvements in survival rates will affect CI insurers, giving rise to more and potentially earlier claims; stroke; respiratory disease – the main risk factor is smoking but can also be work-related; liver disease – major causes are obesity, undiagnosed infection and harmful drinking, all of which are preventable.

7.title: Product Design and Pricing (1)

subsections.title: Overview
bullets: S1 covers policy conditions (policy wording/claim definitions) by product type.
S2 covers marketability factors: consumer demand, competition, distribution, loss leaders, cross-selling.
S3 covers how underwriting impacts product design and pricing.

subsections.title: Overview
bullets: Contract design and accurate wording are crucial to profitability — poor wording creates loopholes, claim disputes, and reputational damage.
Policy conditions can control claim amounts (e.g. self-retention limits), reducing both expected and uncertain claim costs.
The actuary must consider current environment and possible future external changes when drafting policy conditions.

exam_qa.q: Give three reasons why policy conditions are crucial to a health product's success.
exam_qa.a: (1) If terms don't meet policyholder needs, the product won't sell or reputation suffers. (2) Poorly worded conditions create loopholes for unintended claims, increasing claim costs. (3) Ambiguous wording leads to disputes, which are expensive and harm reputation.

subsections.title: Income Protection Insurance — Claim Definition
bullets: Definition of incapacity is key to a sustainable, profitable product and normally applies throughout the contract.

Main definitions: (i) Own occupation (own occ) — totally unable to follow own occupation(s) and not following any other; (ii) Any occupation (any occ) — totally unable to follow any occupation for which suited by training or education; (iii) USA hybrid — own occ for limited period (e.g. 2 years) then any occ.
Ability-based definitions: Functional Assessment Tests (FATs); Activities of Daily Living (ADLs); Activities of Daily Working (ADWs); Personal Capability Assessment (PCA).
Alternatives: amend definition after a claim period (usually 2 years); reserve right to apply alternative definition by occupation class.
Additional limits may apply — e.g. max 24 months benefit if incapacitated under a mental health definition.

Housepersons: Category 1 — inability to perform ADLs (equivalent to own occ); Category 2 — confinement to home/hospital or inability to perform household duties (equivalent to any occ). ADWs also used in UK broker market.
Exclusions by cause: aviation (non-passenger); criminal acts; drug/alcohol abuse; hazardous sports/pastimes; HIV/AIDS; self-inflicted injury; war/civil commotion; sometimes pregnancy complications.
Number and type of exclusions varies by country and provider; some can be specified in local legislation. Trend towards fewer standard exclusions in some jurisdictions.
Expiry age linked to expected retirement age; some policies written for fixed term (e.g. to match mortgage duration).
Claim notification periods: require prompt notification (e.g. within 13 weeks for a 26-week deferred policy) to allow insurer to aid recovery and reduce claim duration.

key_terms.definition: Incapacity definition requiring the insured to be totally unable to follow their own specific occupation(s) and not following any other occupation.
Incapacity definition requiring the insured to be totally unable to follow any occupation for which they are suited by training or education — stricter threshold than own occupation.
Activities of Daily Living — functional ability tests used to define incapacity: washing, dressing, feeding, personal hygiene, mobility (room to room), transferring (bed to chair).
Activities of Daily Working — ability tests related to work-based tasks, used as an alternative incapacity definition particularly in the UK broker market.
Functional Assessment Tests — ability-based tests used as an alternative definition of incapacity.
Personal Capability Assessment — an ability-based test used as an alternative definition of incapacity.
Waiting period at the start of incapacity before IP benefit payments commence; typical lengths are 4, 13, 26 or 52 weeks.

Policy condition requiring the policyholder to notify the insurer within a specified period after the onset of sickness; failure may delay benefit payments. Encourages early notification so insurer can assist recovery.
A person not in paid employment whose contribution to the family can be measured in financial terms; special incapacity definitions apply (ADLs or inability to perform household duties).

exam_qa.q: List four types of ability-based incapacity definitions.
exam_qa.a: Functional assessment tests (FATs); activities of daily living (ADLs); activities of daily working (ADWs); personal capability assessment (PCA).

exam_qa.q: Why might an insurer include a claim notification period in its IP policy conditions?
exam_qa.a: To encourage prompt notification, giving the company more opportunity to aid recovery and achieve earlier return to work, thus reducing overall claim costs.

subsections.title: Income Protection Insurance — Claims Management
bullets: Three key claims management components: benefit limitation; proportionate benefit; rehabilitation/partial benefit.

Benefit limitation: required so claimant retains financial incentive to return to work. Typical formula: [X% of gross pre-disability income] minus [all sources of continuing income], X<100. Limits include monetary maxima, percentage limitations, offsetting other income sources, stringent limits for high salaries, overall maximum. Difficult to enforce as claimants may earn undeclared informal income.

Proportionate benefit: if claimant takes up different employment, benefit is reduced in proportion to new earnings as a ratio of pre-disability earnings. Disability from original occupation must continue while proportionate benefit is paid.

Rehabilitation/partial benefit: payable when claimant returns to original occupation in reduced capacity; calculated same way as proportionate benefit. Rehabilitation via counselling (positive attitude, alternatives to full employment) yields significant cost reductions.
Telephone helplines also included as a claims management tool.

key_terms.definition: Policy condition capping the IP benefit to ensure the claimant retains a financial incentive to return to work; typically expressed as X% of gross pre-disability income less all sources of continuing income (X < 100).
Reduced IP benefit payable when a claimant takes up different employment; benefit is reduced in proportion to new earnings as a ratio of pre-disability earnings.
IP benefit payable when a claimant returns to their original occupation in a reduced capacity; calculated similarly to proportionate benefit.
Alternative term for rehabilitation benefit — payable when claimant returns to original occupation at reduced capacity.

exam_qa.q: Why may benefit limitation be difficult to impose?
exam_qa.a: Claimants may earn income from undeclared informal ('on the side') work which is very difficult for the insurer to prove, meaning full benefit continues to be paid even when the claimant has additional income.

exam_qa.q: A claimant's pre-disability salary was $20,000 pa; IP benefit is $11,000 pa. They take new employment at $6,000 pa. What is the proportionate benefit (ignoring inflation)?
exam_qa.a: Recovery ratio = 6,000/20,000 = 30%. Benefit reduces by 30% to 0.7 × $11,000 = $7,700 pa.

subsections.title: Critical Illness Insurance — Claim Definition
bullets: CI insurance may be personal/family protection or linked to a loan (e.g. mortgage).
Benefit paid on diagnosis of specific illness or undergoing specific treatment; may be linked to severity (e.g. 25–100% of sum insured across severity levels).
Windfall payments arise when policyholder has a covered condition with no significant financial impact — encourages anti-selection, misrepresentation, and exaggeration of conditions.
Medical advances accelerate the point of diagnosis, increasing windfall claim frequency; some advances eliminate covered procedures (e.g. coronary artery by-pass grafts), reducing claims.
Policy wordings must be updated in step with medical advances; non-guaranteed (reviewable) policy conditions may be used.
Policy wording for CI is vital: determines future claims riskiness; influences pricing assumptions and margins; affects product attractiveness and sales volumes.

key_terms.definition: A CI claim payment where the policyholder has a covered medical condition but suffers no significant financial impact — the benefit is disproportionate to the loss, encouraging anti-selection and misrepresentation.
Policy conditions (including benefit definitions) that the insurer reserves the right to update periodically, particularly to keep pace with medical advances.
CI benefit structure where the payout is proportional to the severity of the condition (e.g. 25–100% of sum insured across multiple severity levels).

exam_qa.q: Why does CI insurance face a direct threat to future profitability from medical advancements?
exam_qa.a: Proceeds are paid on diagnosis, not treatment or cure. Diagnostic improvements advance the point at which the condition is detected, accelerating when policy proceeds must be paid even if underlying sickness experience is unchanged. Early diagnosis also increases occasions where the insured's lifestyle or employment is unaffected, creating more windfall payments.

exam_qa.q: How would policyholders benefit if CI conditions were changed to remove windfall payments?
exam_qa.a: Premium rates would fall.

subsections.title: Long-Term Care Insurance — Claim Definition and Claims Management
bullets: LTCI comes in two forms: immediate needs annuities; pre-funded plans.

Immediate needs annuities: protect individuals currently needing care from uncertain survival duration; single premium, guaranteed lifetime income, no formal claim definition. Require careful individual underwriting to assess impaired mortality. High initial expenses (underwriting and intermediary costs) relative to standard life annuities.
Benefit may be level or escalate at fixed rate (often 5% pa) or linked to price index plus ~2% pa; care costs are primarily staff-based so linked to national average earnings.

Pre-funded LTCI — claim definition triggered by single or multiple events; single event may require specified level of disability for a minimum period; different benefits for different disability levels.

ADLs used as claims trigger in UK, USA, South Africa: washing; dressing; feeding; personal hygiene; mobility (room to room); transferring (bed to chair). Number of ADLs failed denotes dependency level.

Mental impairment trigger: need for care or supervision due to deterioration in memory, orientation, awareness of time/place, ability to solve problems — from organic cause (e.g. Alzheimer's, irreversible dementia) excluding depression or drug side-effects.
Claim trigger requires inability to perform ADLs alone without endangering health/well-being of self or others.
Benefit options: lump sum; annuity certain; lifetime benefit subject to ongoing disability; restricted benefit (maximum period or total amount) subject to ongoing disability.
Claims management for pre-funded LTCI: assessment period (similar to CI); telephone helplines.

key_terms.definition: LTCI product providing guaranteed lifetime income in exchange for a single premium, for individuals currently in need of care; protects against uncertain survival duration.
Long-term care insurance where premiums are paid in advance of benefits being needed (single or regular premium); benefit triggered by claim definition based on ADLs or mental impairment.
Activities of Daily Living — functional ability tests used to define incapacity: washing, dressing, feeding, personal hygiene, mobility (room to room), transferring (bed to chair).

LTCI claim trigger based on deterioration in or loss of mental capacity (memory, orientation, awareness of time/place, rational decision-making) from an organic cause such as Alzheimer's or irreversible dementia.
A period, analogous to a deferred period or waiting period, during which the claim definition must be satisfied before benefit payments commence under CI or LTCI contracts.

exam_qa.q: Why do initial expenses for immediate needs annuities tend to be significantly higher than for normal life annuities?
exam_qa.a: Immediate needs annuities require careful, detailed underwriting to assess impaired mortality for each applicant (over/under-estimating impairment causes losses or uncompetitiveness). Normal life annuities are bought by healthy lives with no underwriting needed. Additionally, the selling process is more complex (difficult decisions about future care) and intermediary costs (commission) are higher.

exam_qa.q: Why might depression not be covered by an LTCI mental impairment trigger?
exam_qa.a: It is extremely difficult to define depression unequivocally, making expected claim incidence very hard to predict; the margins required in premium rates to cover the uncertainty would make the product unaffordable and poor value for money.

subsections.title: Private Medical Insurance — Claim Definition and Claims Management
bullets: PMI is usually indemnity-based, compensating for cost of private medical treatment.
Scope of cover must be clearly defined: acute vs chronic conditions; primary, secondary or tertiary care only.

Acute condition: serious but treatable, resolves in relatively short time without long-term complications. Chronic condition: long treatment period, uncertain resolution, may be incurable; treatment only makes condition more tolerable.
PMI often restricted to acute conditions (where State covers chronic) — claim costs are limited and more predictable, enabling affordable premiums.

Primary care (PHC): first point of consultation — GP, physiotherapist. Secondary care: medical specialists, acute care for serious injury/illness. Tertiary care: specialised consultative, usually for inpatients, for advanced investigation/treatment.
Cancer treatment coverage varies considerably between providers.

Self-retention features: excess/deductible (insured pays first monetary amount per claim — reduces frequency and size); coinsurance/cost-sharing (insured pays percentage of costs); internal limits (ceiling on benefit per night/per annum); no claims discount (NCD — proxy for risk segmentation, operates like an excess); claims pre-authorisation (insurer approves treatment/provider before costs incurred); telephone helplines.
Interaction of excess and coinsurance: important to clarify whether deductible applies to whole claim or only to insurer's share.
NCD: insurer must ensure overall premium levels remain adequate — differentials are self-generated by experience rather than actuary-set.
Claims pre-authorisation: allows insurer to manage care provision, avoid inadmissible events and costs not covered.
Ability to adjust policy wording is important for PMI — external changes (new treatments, cost changes) can erode previously watertight definitions.

key_terms.definition: A medical condition that, while serious, is treatable by established procedures, improves or resolves in a relatively short time, and does not cause long-term ongoing complications.
A medical condition with a long treatment period, uncertain resolution and possible incurability; treatment manages but does not resolve the condition.
Self-retention feature whereby the insured is responsible for the first specified monetary amount of each claim; reduces both claim frequency and the size of claims paid by the insurer.
PMI policy condition whereby the policyholder is required to pay a proportion (e.g. 20%) of medical expenses incurred; also known as cost-sharing.
Monetary ceilings imposed on specific benefit components within the contract, e.g. maximum cost per night of hospital accommodation or maximum annual claim amount.

No Claims Discount — a premium reduction given to policyholders who have not claimed in a specified period; acts as a proxy for risk segmentation and operates similarly to an excess by creating a financial deterrent against small claims.

PMI procedure requiring the insurer to approve the intended course of treatment and/or treatment provider before costs are incurred; allows the insurer to control care provision and avoid inadmissible claims.
Healthcare provided by professionals acting as the first point of consultation for patients, e.g. GPs and physiotherapists.
Healthcare services provided by medical specialists and professionals who do not have first contact with patients; includes acute care for serious injury or illness.
Specialised consultative healthcare, usually for inpatients and on referral from primary or secondary professionals, in facilities with advanced medical investigation and treatment capabilities.

exam_qa.q: Explain why PMI may only cover acute conditions.
exam_qa.a: Where State cover exists for chronic conditions, acute conditions are more commonly the gap to fill. Acute conditions have a limited treatment period — costs are of limited size and more predictable than chronic conditions, enabling an affordable premium to be charged.

exam_qa.q: Explain how a NCD system is similar to an excess.
exam_qa.a: If a policyholder claims, they will pay more premium in future than if they had not claimed. The additional premium is effectively the policyholder's share of claim costs (paid in the future), deterring small claims — similar to an excess deterring small claims by making the insured pay part of each claim directly.

subsections.title: Cash Plans — Claim Definition and Claims Management
bullets: Also known as hospital cash plans. Pay a pre-specified cash sum on occurrence of certain medical events (hospital stays, physiotherapy, spectacles, etc.).
Benefits paid are typically low relative to actual cost — represent cash in hand, not indemnity.
Claims management: excess, coinsurance, internal limits, telephone helplines.

key_terms.definition: A health insurance product (also known as a hospital cash plan) that pays pre-specified cash sums on the occurrence of certain medical events; not indemnity-based.
Alternative name for a cash plan — pays fixed cash amounts for specified medical events such as hospital stays, physiotherapy or dental treatment.

subsections.title: Consumer Demand and Competition
bullets: Products should not be launched, redesigned or re-priced without a feasibility study testing customer demand and acceptable price levels.
Analysis of product design includes: likely target market size and expected future growth; fit with corporate image created by existing product range.
Where a product is already sold in a market, existing providers' experience should be analysed and adjusted for expected differences; policy design features and premium rates should be compared.
The market question: is the new product a revolutionary niche product or a copy of existing competition? If the latter, assess whether it can be priced competitively while remaining profitable.

subsections.title: Distribution
bullets: Survey existing intermediaries to ascertain willingness to sell; assess existing and new distribution methods.
Some health products only sell in quantity through specialist health insurance distribution channels.

Specialist health brokers: willing to explain product details, ensure customer needs are met, collect medical information for underwriting; focus on high net worth individuals or corporate clients; may cover all four main product areas.

If new product substitutes existing product: moral/legal restrictions may prevent forcing switches; health conditions developed post-inception cannot be re-underwritten; switching is harder if products are significantly different.
If existing policyholders cannot be moved to new products, both old and new must be maintained — causing systems and administrative complexity.
Additional costs of new distribution: persuading/adapting existing channels; establishing new appropriate channels.

subsections.title: Loss Leaders
bullets: A loss leader is a product priced below cost (sometimes below claims + variable expenses) to gain market entry and cross-sell opportunities.
Acceptable only in a reviewable premium scenario where there is clear intention to return to profitable pricing — must not be done by deliberate use of optimistic assumptions.
Must not conflict with treating customers fairly (TCF) obligations — initial premium levels create expectations; increases only permissible for general deterioration in experience.
Short-term products (PMI, cash plans) can increase premiums at renewal; long-term products (IP, CI) require reviewable policy conditions for this.

Strategic modelling essential: sufficient cashflow to meet liabilities; benefits (portfolio size/profit) must outweigh short-term losses. Selective lapsing effect must be modelled — customers who lapse on price increases are lower risk, worsening remaining portfolio.

key_terms.definition: A product deliberately priced below cost (sometimes below claims plus variable expenses) to gain market entry and cross-selling opportunities, with the intention of returning to profitable pricing over time.

The tendency for healthier, lower-risk policyholders to lapse their policies (e.g. in response to premium increases), leaving a worse-than-average risk portfolio and worsening future claims experience.

Treating Customers Fairly — regulatory principle requiring insurers to act in customers' best interests; initial premium levels create reasonable expectations that must not be violated without valid justification.

exam_qa.q: Explain why it is important to ensure sufficient cashflow when operating a loss-leader strategy.
exam_qa.a: Cashflow is required to meet liabilities as they fall due (e.g. to pay benefits and expenses). If insufficient cash is available, assets may need to be realised at an inopportune time.

subsections.title: Cross-Selling
bullets: Existing policyholders are 'warm' customers — brand awareness established, relationship built; selling process differs from cold customers.
Cross-selling prerequisites: customer must be satisfied with current product and service; assess satisfaction first (courtesy calls), resolve any issues before attempting to sell.

Sales synergies: promises at point of sale should be delivered; servicing should be smooth and friendly; cross-sell prospects are simpler and more cost-effective (e.g. may not require initial commission).

Product cost synergies: products designed to integrate with existing contracts can extract overlaps, making combined product cheaper than full standalone insurance (e.g. PMI sold to CI policyholders, covering only costs exceeding CI lump sum).
Administration savings: cross-selling is cheaper from initial administration and records perspective; subsequent servicing costs also reduced (especially if systems can link products per customer).
Customer data can identify characteristics predicting purchase of particular products — reduces marketing costs and increases success rate.

key_terms.definition: Selling additional insurance products to existing policyholders who are 'warm' customers — already aware of the brand and with a relationship established.
Cost savings arising when products are designed to integrate with existing contracts, extracting overlaps in coverage so the combined product is cheaper than buying each product separately.
Cost reductions achieved by cross-selling, as initial administration and records for existing customers are cheaper to set up and subsequent servicing costs are lower.

exam_qa.q: Discuss the two main advantages of a combined CI and PMI product.
exam_qa.a: (1) PMI typically covers only acute conditions; CI provides lump sum when condition becomes chronic, mitigating customer dissatisfaction at PMI refusing chronic claims — complementary coverage. (2) Cost saving: overlap in cover (curable conditions requiring expensive short-term private treatment) means combined product is cheaper than buying both separately, plus selling/admin cost savings.

subsections.title: Overview
bullets: Underwriting and pricing are inextricably linked: underwriting identifies which applicants get standard rates and which need special treatment.
The average claims experience of those accepted on standard terms must be reflected in the pricing basis assumptions.
The actuary sets underwriting guidelines as part of the design process; these are incorporated into the insurer's existing underwriting system.

subsections.title: Consistency
bullets: Underwriting decisions must be consistent over time — achieved only by ensuring underwriters are trained in the new product.

May be necessary to create a special team of underwriters to prevent 'contamination' of underwriting for other products (underwriters may unconsciously change their approach to existing products if different risk attitudes apply to the new one).

key_terms.definition: The risk that underwriters applying different risk assessment standards for a new product unconsciously change their approach to existing products, resulting in inconsistent underwriting across the portfolio.

subsections.title: Meeting Pricing Objectives
bullets: Underwriting guidelines are set to meet pricing objectives; conversely, pricing objectives must be set in line with existing company underwriting standards and culture.

It is unrealistic to expect tight underwriting guidelines if the insurer's other products use generous underwriting — three reasons: (i) underwriters cannot operate two very different risk assessment systems effectively; (ii) products with very different underwriting standards must target very different markets (different average risk profiles); (iii) difficult to offer both through the same sales channel without confusing brokers.

subsections.title: Linking with Claims
bullets: A monitoring process should feed back underwriting implications of claims experience in early and later years into underwriting guidelines.
Example: early CI cancer claims in UK exceeded pricing assumptions — cause was anti-selection combined with inadequate underwriting (high cancer risk persons accepted at standard rates).
Response: more stringent underwriting to identify such risks at application, or change policy conditions to exclude anti-selective claims.

exam_qa.q: What was the likely reason for high early cancer claim experience in early UK CI insurance?
exam_qa.a: Anti-selection combined with inadequate underwriting — persons who knew they were high cancer risks were being accepted at standard premium rates.

subsections.title: Legal Restrictions on Underwriting
bullets: Actuaries must consider legal and informal restrictions on underwriting in the specific country, e.g. restrictions on use of predictive genetic tests in underwriting.

key_terms.definition: Legal or informal restrictions in specific countries on requiring genetic tests to be taken or on using genetic test results in the underwriting process.

7.summary:
Income protection insurance

Claim definitions:
• Definitions can: be occupation based (eg own occupation, any occupation); be based on an abilities test (eg ADWs); change after a specified claim duration.
• Maybe maximum payment period for some causes (eg mental illness).
• Claim definition for housepersons can be: Category 1: dependent on ADLs; Category 2: inability to perform normal household duties.
• Exclusions (by cause of illness) may be applied.
• Expiry age normally linked to retirement.

Claims management:
• benefit limitation
• proportionate benefit
• rehabilitation / partial benefit
• telephone helplines.

Critical illness insurance

Claim definitions:
• Benefit paid on diagnosis of specific illness or undergoing specific treatment.
• Lump sum gives rise to possible windfall opportunities; some policies offer severity-based payments.
• Medical advancements accelerate claim payment and increase windfall claims.
• Medical advancements may reduce need for certain treatments, reducing claims.
• Policy wordings must follow medical advancements; may have reviewable terms.

Claims management:
• assessment period
• waiting period
• telephone helplines.

Long-term care insurance – immediate needs

Claim definitions:
• Protect individuals from their uncertain survival duration.
• Benefit escalation may be: pre-agreed with care home(s); fixed rate pa; linked to RPI.

Claims management:
• assessment period
• telephone helplines.

Long-term care insurance – pre-funded

Claim definitions:
• Claims definition – may be single or multiple events trigger. Order of events may matter.
• Determined by ADLs.
• Payments for lifetime, or maximum period / total amount.

Claims management:
• assessment period
• telephone helplines.

Private medical insurance

Claim definitions:
• PMI is usually an indemnity-based product.
• Cover may be restricted to acute (not chronic) conditions and/or secondary and tertiary (not primary) care.

Claims management:
• excesses / deductibles
• coinsurance
• internal limits
• no claims discounts (NCDs)
• claims pre-authorisation
• telephone helplines.

Cash plans

Claim definitions:
• Pay fixed cash amounts for specified medical events; not indemnity.

Claims management:
• excess
• coinsurance
• internal limits
• telephone helplines.

Marketability, competition and distribution
• Consumer demand analysis: target market size, corporate image fit, comparison with competitors.
• Distribution: survey intermediaries; specialist health brokers for complex products; consider substitution effects on existing policyholders.
• Loss leaders: valid only with reviewable premiums and genuine intent to return to profitability; requires cashflow modelling and lapse analysis.
• Cross-selling: warm customers; satisfy first; product and admin cost synergies.

Underwriting
• Underwriting and pricing are inextricably linked.
• Consistency: train underwriters; consider separate team to avoid contamination.
• Meeting pricing objectives: underwriting culture must align with pricing assumptions.
• Linking with claims: monitor experience and feed back into underwriting guidelines.
• Legal restrictions: e.g. genetic test restrictions.

8.title: Product Design and Pricing (2)

subsections.title: Overview
bullets: Chapter focuses on: cost of capital; risk management strategies (reinsurance, claims management); investment policy; renewal process (options, reviewability, income/capital conversion); regulation; ESG; administration/expenses/lapses.
Pricing methods for health and care business are outlined in SP1.

sections.title: Capital Requirements and Return on Capital

subsections.title: The Need for Capital
bullets: Capital is required at policy issue: initial expenses + supervisory reserves + solvency capital typically exceed day-1 premium; capital funds this shortfall.
Capital must be assessed at all stages of product design — actuary identifies extent of reserves and regulatory capital requirements.
Capital also required for product launch (development and marketing costs) and to absorb expense allowance overruns in early years.
Availability and cost of capital (internal or external) are important considerations in deciding whether to launch and at what price.

Unit-linked products offer capital efficiency: (i) reviewable charges reduce need for solvency capital against expense/claims excess; (ii) front-end charging closely matches initial costs, reducing early negative unit reserves.
Products with options and guarantees require more conservative assumptions → higher reserves → higher capital requirements.
Reinsurance can reduce capital requirements (reinsurers typically have lower capital requirements than direct writers); reinsurer default risk must be considered.
Initial capital modelled using new business model incorporating forecast new business volumes, model points, lapses, morbidity, development costs; scenarios assess likely capital range.

key_terms.definition: The shortfall arising at policy issue when the sum of initial expenses, required reserves and solvency capital exceeds the first premium received; funded from the insurer's capital.

Situation where actual expenses exceed the expense allowances in the premium basis or unit charges; particularly common in early years of a new product when development costs are not yet fully recouped.
Alternative term for expense overrun — actual expenses exceed those allowed for in the pricing basis.
Capital held in addition to policy reserves to meet supervisory solvency requirements, protecting policyholders against adverse experience scenarios.

The ability of unit-linked products to minimise capital requirements through reviewable charges (reducing solvency capital needs) and front-end charging structures (reducing early negative unit reserves).

exam_qa.q: Why is capital required to issue new products?
exam_qa.a: When a policy is sold, the company incurs significant expenses; it must also set up adequate reserves and solvency capital to meet supervisory requirements. The combined amount of initial expenses, reserves and solvency capital normally exceeds the day-1 premium, and capital is needed to fund this difference.

exam_qa.q: State two ways a unit-linked product achieves a relatively low capital requirement.
exam_qa.a: (1) Charges are often reviewable in light of ongoing experience, reducing need for solvency capital to cover risk that expenses/claims exceed charges. (2) A front-end charging structure can closely match initial charges to costs, reducing the size of unit funds (unit reserves) at early durations.

subsections.title: The Cost of Capital
bullets: Each new policy should be charged for the cost of the additional capital it requires — this is the investment return foregone by holding assets as 'locked-in' statutory capital rather than investing freely.
Traditional approach: profit-testing methods where annual cost of reserves and solvency capital is deducted from projected profits, discounted at the risk discount rate.
Some well-capitalised companies do not charge certain policies with the cost of solvency capital in order to remain competitive — the charge is then borne by other business.
Actuary applies WACC (weighted average cost of capital) encompassing capital from banks, reinsurers, venture capitalists, lenders and shareholders, each with different return demands.
Risk discount rate starts from risk-free rate (e.g. government bond yield), plus margin for riskiness of product cashflows.

key_terms.definition: The investment return foregone by holding assets as locked-in statutory capital (reserves and solvency capital) rather than investing them freely; charged to each new policy in the pricing basis.
The return foregone by holding capital as statutory reserves rather than deploying it in higher-returning uses; forms the basis of the cost of capital charge in pricing.

Weighted Average Cost of Capital — the weighted average return required by all capital providers (banks, reinsurers, venture capitalists, lenders, shareholders), used by the actuary to set the required overall return on capital.

The discount rate used to calculate the net present value of future profit flows in profit-testing; set to ensure the required shareholder return on capital is earned, starting from the risk-free rate plus a margin for product riskiness.

exam_qa.q: Explain how the insurer's cost of capital translates into a cost for the policyholder.
exam_qa.a: Capital is tied up as assets backing solvency requirements until no longer needed. The return on those assets is pegged at the rate available on those assets, which is less than shareholders expect. To compensate, premiums are increased to generate policy profits that in total provide the required shareholder return on capital. The greater the initial capital required, the higher the premiums.

subsections.title: Return on Capital
bullets: Return on capital is the reward to shareholders/sponsors for use of their financial resources to underwrite health and care risks.
The insurer incorporates required return on capital into business and pricing models — risk discount rate is chosen to ensure required shareholder return is earned if NPV ≥ 0.
Return on new product must be comparable to other products and ventures, making allowance for strategic role of new product in company operations.
Loss-leader rationale: if new product increases sales of other profitable products, lower return from the new product itself may be acceptable provided overall company return is satisfactory.
Return on capital should be viewed as potential distributable surplus over time — not a fixed target detached from business strategy.

key_terms.definition: The reward to shareholders or sponsors for use of their financial resources to underwrite health and care risks; the insurer's required return on capital is incorporated into business and pricing models.

The discount rate used to calculate the net present value of future profit flows in profit-testing; set to ensure the required shareholder return on capital is earned, starting from the risk-free rate plus a margin for product riskiness.
The potential profit available for distribution to shareholders over a period of time; the return on capital should be viewed as the potential distributable surplus rather than a fixed annual target.

subsections.title: Market-Consistent Pricing
bullets: Increasingly companies adopt market-consistent approaches — effectively using risk-free discount rate with risk allowance made elsewhere (e.g. within experience assumptions, or as overall adjustment like Solvency II risk margin).
Market-consistent pricing can lead to greater volatility in the theoretical price (as risk-free rate changes with bond yields).

Companies may use market-consistent approach to understand the difference between traditional and market-consistent valuations and assess continued appropriateness of premium rates as economic conditions vary.
Market-consistent pricing gives information consistent with added value of new business under market-consistent embedded value calculations.
Contracts appearing profitable under traditional methods may show embedded value loss under market-consistent methods.

key_terms.definition: A pricing approach using a risk-free discount rate with risk allowances made elsewhere (e.g. in experience assumptions or as a Solvency II risk margin), consistent with market-consistent embedded value calculations.
An explicit allowance in a market-consistent valuation for the risk that future experience deviates from best estimate; required under Solvency II.
EU regulatory framework for insurance companies specifying capital requirements, reserving standards and risk management requirements; uses a market-consistent valuation approach.
A measure of the value of an insurance company's in-force business, equal to the present value of future profits plus net assets; market-consistent embedded value uses risk-free discount rates.

subsections.title: Underlying Risk
bullets: Many underlying risks arise in creating a new product; must be considered in deciding on product design in the context of the company as a whole.

Key risks: (a) Product substitution — new product outsells and largely replaces an existing more profitable contract; (b) Sales success — product is too successful, using up capital required elsewhere and destabilising sales across the product range; (c) Sales failure — poor sales or poor morbidity experience, tying up capital that could be used elsewhere; (d) Expense overrun — expenses significantly higher than expected (e.g. development costs not fully recouped); (e) Product design failure — poorly worded policy conditions leading to unanticipated claims; (f) Product costing failure — under-priced due to underestimation of future claims experience (parameter error).

Nature and impact of risks depends on: type of product; client profile; distribution methods; culture and risk appetite; extent of free assets; synergies with existing portfolio; quality of operational and risk management processes (including reinsurance); reputational impact.
To manage risks: invest in policy wording research; analyse all available data carefully; use reinsurance to protect against parameter risk.

key_terms.definition: The risk that a new product outsells and largely replaces an existing more profitable contract, reducing overall company profitability.
Pricing error arising from inaccurate estimation of future claims experience, possibly due to lack of relevant data or inadequate underwriting of the insured risk.

Situation where actual expenses exceed the expense allowances in the premium basis or unit charges; particularly common in early years of a new product when development costs are not yet fully recouped.
The risk that a product is too successful in sales, using up capital required elsewhere and destabilising sales across the product range, potentially leading to supervisory insolvency.
Risk that poorly worded policy conditions lead to claims being paid that were not envisaged in the product design.

exam_qa.q: How does a product with poor morbidity experience 'tie up capital'?
exam_qa.a: Poor morbidity experience produces losses. As soon as the likely level of future experience becomes clear, the company must set up adequate supervisory reserves to cover resulting future claims costs. Extra capital is tied up in these larger-than-expected reserves.

subsections.title: Cost of Risk
bullets: Cost of risk = expected claims costs for the products sold.
If insurer specialises in health insurance risk with full claims management, risk results are likely to be good.
