If health insurance is a by-product with minimal claims management, risk results are likely to be poor — the financial penalty (increased claims) can be far in excess of the cost of operating a full claims management system.

subsections.title: Claims Management
bullets: Appropriate claims management systems must be in place at launch — inability to handle certain claim types constrains product design.
Claims management system must incorporate: data handling; record keeping; decision-making; payment processing. Requires appropriate software and staff.
For low-volume products (CI, IP, LTCI) a stand-alone system (off-the-shelf or third-party administrator) is feasible.
For PMI (high claims volume), system must be integrated with other office functions from outset, or a TPA used — duplication of policy/claims records increases error scope.
A 'turnkey' system (brought in from outside but integrating with existing systems) is required for large portfolios.
Additional claims management by product: PMI — pre-authorisation highly desirable; IP — rehabilitation and counselling for claimants; LTCI — counselling for claimants and families.

key_terms.definition: An external organisation that handles claims administration on behalf of the insurer; commonly used when the insurer lacks the internal systems or expertise to process claims efficiently.
A claims management system brought in from outside the company that integrates with existing systems with minimal customisation; required when portfolio size is large.
A separate software system for claims management that operates independently of the company's main policy record files; feasible for low-volume products such as CI, IP and LTCI.
See claims pre-authorisation — the insurer's approval of the course of treatment and/or provider before costs are incurred; particularly important for PMI to control claims from the outset.

exam_qa.q: Describe what is meant by 'pre-authorisation' of claims.
exam_qa.a: The course of treatment and/or treatment provider must be approved by the insurer at the time of claim, prior to the treatment being carried out. Failure to obtain authorisation (where required) can lead to the treatment not being covered and the policyholder bearing the cost.

subsections.title: Customer Risk Awareness
bullets: Customers are generally not aware of the impact of the health risks being insured — continued education of all 'customers' (insured, employees, brokers, media, regulators) is required.
Full, understandable literature on the product and daily training for all concerned (staff, brokers, customers, employers) is needed.

When health benefits are provided alongside employment, everyone must be made aware of the financial impact of health insurance relative to other employee benefits — it may reduce availability of other benefits or lead to lower salary offers.

subsections.title: Availability of Data
bullets: Availability of relevant and credible experience data is a key element of product pricing, particularly for new product launches.
Balance needed between most relevant data (own experience — may be limited) and most credible data (national/international population sources).

Potential external data sources: competitors (public supervisory reports); the market as a whole; population statistics (domestic and foreign); insurance statistics from other countries; data via reinsurers or consultants.

Approaches when data is scarce: use multiple sources to triangulate credible figures; build in margins for uncertainty; keep premium/benefit relationship reviewable; arrange adequate reinsurance to limit retained risk; collect own experience data as quickly as possible; monitor experience carefully and amend pricing basis on new business.

Where credible data is unavailable, actuary should question whether to launch until statistics are collected — management must be made clearly aware of risks involved if launching without adequate data.

subsections.title: Overview
bullets: Reinsurance is part of the company's risk control mechanism and can significantly impact product price — the reinsurance programme must be decided alongside product design and pricing.
Actuary decides scope and extent of reinsurance programme based on company's risk appetite; particularly important for large individual risks or accumulations of risks subject to a single cause.

subsections.title: Risk Appetite
bullets: Risk appetite varies by insurer and is influenced by availability of capital; reinsurance programme reflects desired risk profile.

High appetite for risk (low reinsurance coverage): insurer retains more profit but requires more stringent risk control in underwriting, policy wording, claims management; pricing assumptions for future claims may need to be more conservative.

key_terms.definition: The extent to which an insurer is willing to retain insurance risk rather than passing it to a reinsurer; influenced by availability of capital and reflected in the reinsurance programme.

subsections.title: Cost versus Benefit
bullets: High reinsurance removes substantial risk from the direct insurer but premiums charged to policyholders must cover reinsurance costs plus contribute to direct writer's expenses.

100% quota share example: direct writer passes all premium to reinsurer, receiving commission in return; commission must cover all direct writer's expenses. Reinsurer sets commission terms to meet its own profit requirements.
As retained proportion increases: insurer's net income provides greater margin over net expenses, but at cost of greater exposure to adverse claims experience.
Actuary must take into account insurer's views when considering the cost-benefit equation.

key_terms.definition: A reinsurance arrangement under which the reinsurer accepts a fixed percentage of every risk written by the direct insurer in exchange for the same percentage of premiums, paying the same percentage of all claims.
Payment made by the reinsurer to the direct writer under a quota share or similar arrangement, to compensate the direct writer for acquisition and administration expenses.

subsections.title: Arbitrage
bullets: Tax arbitrage: differences in taxation basis between insurer and reinsurer can create mutual benefit. Example: if insurer is taxed on investment returns but can claim tax relief on expenses, reinsuring passes excess investment return to reinsurer (taxed on profits), reducing insurer's overall tax bill.

Capital arbitrage: reinsurer may have lower capital requirements (e.g. due to more diversified book of business) — transferring risk to reinsurer reduces overall system capital requirements, benefiting both parties.

key_terms.definition: A situation where differences in the tax treatment of the insurer and reinsurer allow reinsurance to reduce the overall tax burden on both parties, making both financially better off.

A situation where the reinsurer has lower capital requirements than the direct insurer (e.g. due to greater diversification), so transferring risk to the reinsurer reduces overall system capital requirements.

subsections.title: Capital Assistance
bullets: Reinsurer can assist with capital strain at product launch — particularly where large indemnity commission is payable at inception.
Financial reinsurance: reinsurer capitalises future surplus flows at later policy durations to provide up-front lump sums to offset launch strain.

key_terms.definition: Reinsurance primarily used to manage capital and cashflow rather than to transfer insurance risk; the reinsurer capitalises future surplus flows to provide upfront lump sums to offset new business capital strain.
The shortfall arising at policy issue when the sum of initial expenses, required reserves and solvency capital exceeds the first premium received; funded from the insurer's capital.

subsections.title: Technical Assistance
bullets: Reinsurer can help with new launch or product redesign via: market surveys and customer opinions; product design and wording; pricing data; medical underwriting forms and wording; direct expert input into insurer processes (e.g. secondment of staff); risk sharing and design of treaties; background on regulatory issues.
Reinsurers are in contact with many insurers and share collected information where it does not betray confidentiality or infringe competition legislation.

subsections.title: Overview
bullets: Investment policy affects product design and pricing — deciding on suitable investment strategy should be part of the design process itself.

Main aim: (a) select investments appropriate to nature, term and currency of liabilities; (b) maximise overall return (income + capital gains); (c) extent to which appropriate investments may be departed from to maximise overall return depends on extent of free assets and risk appetite.
Investment policy is directly linked to the investment return assumption in the pricing basis.

subsections.title: Maximise Return
bullets: Rate chosen depends on return from assets in which reserves are invested — determined by asset-liability modelling exercise.

Health insurance products typically invest in fixed-interest assets/bonds; unit-linked or pre-funded LTCI (long investment period, need to mirror care cost increases) may use equity-based approach or index-linked assets.

Opportunities to mismatch to improve investment returns depend on: risk appetite of managers/owners; availability of free capital; extent to which portfolio is growing (income exceeding outgo creates short-term investment opportunities).

subsections.title: Asset-Liability Matching
bullets: Assets chosen with regard to nature of contract — whether benefits are fixed in monetary terms, terms of the policy, and currency.
Perfect matching is a theoretical ideal; allowance for mismatching should be made either as a margin in the assumed investment return or as an increased allowance for risk in the risk discount rate.

key_terms.definition: Investment strategy of selecting assets whose cashflow characteristics (amount, term, currency) mirror those of the liabilities, minimising investment risk.
A margin in the investment return assumption (or increase in the risk discount rate) to reflect the additional risk taken on when assets do not perfectly match liabilities.

subsections.title: Type of Investment
bullets: Amount, term, type and currency of investment must reflect product design — currency mismatching is undesirable (e.g. sterling products should not be backed by foreign-currency assets).

subsections.title: Liquidity
bullets: High liquidity required for PMI — encourages use of cash or quickly convertible investments.

Growing PMI portfolio requires net new policies each year (excess new policies over lapses) — reserves increasing, requiring capital injections as renewal income is at lower level than needed to subsidise first-year costs.
Assets backing PMI portfolio may need to be disinvested when outgo exceeds income — most likely when claims are heavier than expected or when fund is in decline (lapses exceed new business).
Return may be sacrificed to some extent to satisfy liquidity needs — short-term investments or cash used in such circumstances.

key_terms.definition: The ease with which assets can be converted to cash to meet short-term liabilities; high liquidity is particularly important for PMI due to its high claim frequency.

subsections.title: Consistency with Inflation
bullets: Investment return assumption must be consistent with inflation assumptions — relationship between assumed investment return and inflation should be realistic.
Four inflation variables, each potentially at different rates: claims cost inflation; premium inflation; administration expense inflation; claims expense inflation.

PMI claims inflation is complex: (i) increased policy utilisation (higher claim frequency); (ii) technology upgrades (more expensive procedures/equipment/drugs); (iii) age-related cost increases (older policyholders cost more to treat); (iv) like-for-like procedural cost increases.
PMI claims inflation drivers: supply costs, resource availability, demand for services, deals between insurers and hospitals — resultant rate may differ widely from retail prices or average earnings.
Expense inflation contributors: ordinary expenses driven by wage inflation and IT costs; claims administration expenses driven by wage inflation and specialist medical reports/tests costs.

key_terms.definition: The rate at which healthcare costs increase over time; driven by increased policy utilisation, technology upgrades, age-related cost increases and like-for-like procedural cost increases — may differ significantly from retail price or average earnings inflation.

The rate at which operating expenses increase over time; ordinary expense inflation driven by wage inflation and IT costs; claims expense inflation driven by wage inflation and cost of specialist medical reports and tests.

subsections.title: Short Term or Long Term
bullets: PMI is short-term — investment return has minimal impact; inflation of claims and expenses can be incorporated into premium at renewal; repricing can be carried out yearly in response to experience.
CI, IP and LTCI are long-term — investment return is more significant (especially for pre-funded LTCI where reserves are substantial).
Longer guarantee periods require larger margins for uncertainty in investment and inflation assumptions.

Inflation assumptions are crucial for long-term products, though reviewable premium basis provides some opportunity to reset assumptions at review (limited by TCF obligations and competitive constraints).

subsections.title: Renewability
bullets: In some territories, health insurers give policyholders guaranteed right to renew (may only apply to certain products).

Long-term policies (UK: CI, IP, LTCI — minimum potential 5-year term): insurer cannot cancel cover; must offer cover for entire policy term; rates may be reviewed; policy conditions cannot be changed. Referred to as non-cancellable.
Some countries allow conditionally renewable business — circumstances in which insurer can refuse renewal are specified.
Short-term policies (PMI): terms on renewal may be identical to those for a new entrant with same risk characteristics. Policyholder must notify insurer of changes in circumstances.
Short-term renewal premium may alternatively reflect experience in previous period via full experience rating (groups) or NCD (individual).
Insurer can change product conditions at short-term renewal (effectively withdrawing old plan and offering new one) or even cancel cover at renewal date.
Renewal rates significantly affect pricing — initial expenses must be spread appropriately over expected policy 'lifetime'.

Rate schedules may attract a mix of risks different from that expected — actuary must be aware of all policyholder classes to whom renewal premiums might apply and iteratively adjust rate levels/applicability.

key_terms.definition: Describes a long-term health insurance policy that the insurer cannot cancel during the policy term; cover must be provided for the entire term regardless of the policyholder's health status or claims experience.
Describes business where the insurer can refuse renewal only under specified circumstances as set out in the policy conditions.
Premium adjustment at renewal based on the actual claims experience of the group in previous period(s); commonly used in group PMI.

No Claims Discount — a premium reduction given to policyholders who have not claimed in a specified period; acts as a proxy for risk segmentation and operates similarly to an excess by creating a financial deterrent against small claims.

exam_qa.q: State a second possible advantage of NCD and experience rating systems (beyond reducing incidence of small claims).
exam_qa.a: Policyholders should be less likely to lapse their policies, as they may believe they would not get as good a deal on switching to a rival insurance company.

subsections.title: Reviewability
bullets: Reviewability: degree to which premiums, guaranteed renewable under long-term contracts, can be changed in amount at insurer's discretion.

All health and care insurance policies are subject to future experience uncertainty; insurer retains flexibility in benefits/premiums to keep cover appropriate and premiums consistent with original pricing targets.
Reviewable premiums allow insurer to alter premiums if portfolio claims experience differs from originally expected — typically reviewed every 5 years, with ongoing experience monitoring.
Reviewability relates to changes in claims experience of the whole portfolio — not to individual policies with bad claims history.
Guaranteed premium rates also offered, but at a premium to reviewable rates.

Reviews of premiums can result in 'selective lapsing' — healthy lives lapse (or accept reduced benefits) while those claiming/in poor health agree to increase. Some insurers restrict increases to a maximum percentage per year to reduce selective lapsing.

Where experience is better than expected, some insurers reduce premiums — this is communicated from the outset to set expectations, reducing selective lapsing and potentially allowing lower margins in pricing basis.
Two design extremes: full guarantee of future premiums (high guarantee loading) vs no guarantee whatsoever. Middle ground: guaranteed for first 5 years, fully reviewable thereafter.
A fully reviewable product is difficult to review in practice because of market pressures — bad experience alone is not usually good enough reason to justify a rate increase.

key_terms.definition: The degree to which premiums, guaranteed renewable under long-term contracts, can be changed in amount at the insurer's discretion, typically in response to changes in portfolio-wide claims experience.

The tendency for healthier, lower-risk policyholders to lapse their policies (e.g. in response to premium increases), leaving a worse-than-average risk portfolio and worsening future claims experience.

Additional premium charged on guaranteed-rate policies (versus reviewable-rate policies) to compensate the insurer for removing the insurer's right to increase premiums in response to adverse experience.

subsections.title: Example of a Statement of Good Practice
bullets: In some jurisdictions, statements of good practice outline how an insurer can review certain classes of business.
UK example (2005): co-operation between FSA, UK Ombudsman and ABI produced a Statement of Good Practice for reviewable long-term protection policies.

Consumer benefits: clearer explanations of reviewability; greater confidence that review increases/decreases are calculated fairly; continued availability of reviewable products; ability to buy protection at lower cost than guaranteed rates.

Practical implications for insurers: prominently show reviewability in product literature; base reviewable premiums on assumptions believed valid for full policy term; review assumptions regularly; use consistent assumptions for in-force and new business; increase premiums at review only for 'valid reasons' as defined; not aim to recoup earlier losses at reviews; notify customers of review results; consider option to reduce cover instead of paying higher premium; keep records of assumptions and analyses supporting reviews.

key_terms.definition: A document produced in some jurisdictions (e.g. UK 2005, produced by FSA, UK Ombudsman and ABI) outlining how insurers should conduct premium reviews of reviewable long-term protection policies.
Association of British Insurers — the UK insurance industry trade association; issues Statements of Best Practice on policy wording and industry standards.
Financial Services Authority — the former UK financial services regulator (now replaced by FCA and PRA); co-produced the 2005 Statement of Good Practice on reviewable premiums.

subsections.title: Increase Options
bullets: Increase options allow cover to be increased without further evidence of health (guaranteed renewability with increments).

Type 1: increases incorporated in original contract, operating automatically — at fixed rate (e.g. 3% pa) or linked to external index (e.g. RPI); premiums may be level, increase in line with benefit, or increase by other specified pattern; alternatively premiums are costed as they arise (costed increases start from a lower initial level but rise more steeply than pre-ordained increases).

Type 2: periodic option for policyholder to increase benefit by fixed percentage via new policy costed at then-current age and outstanding term; no re-underwriting (policyholder not re-examined) creating adverse selection — loading must be included in original premium basis to cover this option cost.

Risk of Type 2: if revised product design leads to higher sickness benefit value than anticipated, original loading was inadequate. Also, policyholders may be disappointed if unable to increase benefit in the way they expected, harming reputation and increasing lapses.

key_terms.definition: A policy feature allowing the policyholder to increase the level of benefit at specified intervals without providing further evidence of health (i.e. without re-underwriting).
Increase option Type 1 where premium increases are charged as they arise (costed at then-current age and term); starts from a lower initial premium but rises more steeply than pre-ordained increases.
Increase option Type 1 where benefit increases and corresponding premium increases are fixed in advance in the original contract; initial premium is higher but increases are predictable.
Increase option incorporated in the original contract and operating automatically, either at a pre-ordained rate (fixed % or index-linked) with pre-set premiums, or costed as they arise.

Increase option giving the policyholder a periodic right to increase benefit by a fixed percentage via a new policy costed at then-current age and term, without re-underwriting; adverse selection loading required in original premium basis.

subsections.title: Pricing Guarantees and Options
bullets: Pricing a guaranteed product must reflect likely changes in experience over policy term — difficult when claims experience is changing.
Main driver for switching to reviewable premiums: significant increases in reinsurer rates for guaranteed premiums (some reinsurers stopped offering guaranteed rates on certain products).
Guarantee considerations also apply to products with level premiums throughout or where benefit increases are incorporated in the original contract.
Additional margin required for guaranteed products to allow for unpredictability of future benefit increases.

subsections.title: Converting the Form of Benefits
bullets: Long-term health/care products focus on income (IP, LTCI) or lump sum (CI); any can be designed to encompass either; at claim, circumstances may make claimant wish to take payment in the other form (partially or entirely).

Converting capital to income (CI): CI insurer may use part/all of lump sum to purchase purchased life annuity (PLA) — guaranteed income for remainder of lifetime or fixed period. Customer disadvantage: income level unknown until claim paid (depends on age, term, annuity rates, health at claim). If annuity follows CI claim, impaired life annuity may be sought — market is specialised.
Converting income to capital (IP/LTCI): more difficult — benefit payment is contingent on both human survival and continuance of incapacity.

Conversion terms calculation: insurer's reserve is a starting point for capital value of income stream; reserve may contain statutory prudence margins and is on cohort basis — not an individual medically-assessed estimate. Anti-selection risk: claimants expecting short recovery take lump sum (worth more); those with long-term illness prefer income stream — increasing overall benefit payments.

key_terms.definition: An immediate annuity purchased on the open market to convert a lump sum (e.g. CI claim proceeds) into a regular income; not associated with a pension contract.
A PLA offered at enhanced (higher income) rates to reflect reduced life expectancy of the annuitant due to a serious medical condition following a CI claim.

Converting a stream of income benefit payments (e.g. under IP or LTCI) into an equivalent lump sum; more difficult than capital-to-income conversion as the benefit is contingent on both survival and continuance of incapacity.

Converting a lump sum benefit (e.g. CI proceeds) into a regular income stream, typically by purchasing a PLA; income level is unknown until claim time as it depends on age, term, annuity rates and health at claim.

exam_qa.q: Outline the main disadvantages of converting CI capital to income (from customer's perspective).
exam_qa.a: Customer will not know the level of income they will receive until the claim is paid, as it depends on: age of policyholder; term that income is to be paid; annuity rates available (dependent on interest rates, expected future mortality, competition, insurer's profit margin); state of health of the policyholder.

exam_qa.q: Explain the consequences of not allowing for each individual's state of health when calculating lump sum payments in lieu of income.
exam_qa.a: The insurer will suffer anti-selection: claimants who expect to recover shortly will take the lump sum (worth more than the income stream for a short claim); those with serious long-term illnesses will take the income stream. This will increase benefit payments over the whole portfolio.

subsections.title: Regulatory Requirements
bullets: Actuary must ensure product fits local regulatory environment: insurer authorised to write the class of business; appropriate sales routines and disclosure statements established.

In some territories, insurers cannot differentiate by gender in setting premium rates (EU from December 2012) — significant pricing implications; increased emphasis on target market, distribution channel and literature design to control mix of insured lives.
There may be direct restrictions on level of premium rates or charges for health insurance products.
Capital requirements depend on regulation (Solvency II framework); way supervisory solvency capital is calculated is specified in regulations and depends on type of business.
Regulatory regimes continue to develop — changes in equality legislation, capital requirement levels and sales process regulation all impact products and must be monitored carefully.

key_terms.definition: Insurance pricing that does not differentiate between male and female policyholders; mandatory in the EU from December 2012 following a European Court of Justice ruling.
EU regulatory framework for insurance companies specifying capital requirements, reserving standards and risk management requirements; uses a market-consistent valuation approach.

Capital required by the regulator (above policy reserves) to ensure insurers can meet obligations under adverse scenarios; method of calculation specified in regulations and varies by type of business.

subsections.title: ESG Considerations
bullets: As consumers become more aware of personal responsibility towards climate change/sustainability, they search for products designed with ESG factors in mind.

ESG factors: environmental (climate change, resource depletion, waste); social (human rights, modern slavery, working conditions); governance (bribery and corruption, executive pay, board diversity and structure).

Product design and pricing ESG issues: (i) offering appropriate ESG fund choices for unit-linked products; (ii) marketability risks if company is perceived as not meeting ESG standards (investment choices for non-linked assets, working practices, diversity, supplier ESG credentials).

Sustainable investment refers to investment approaches consistent with long-term sustainability of society and natural environment, relevant when investment is motivated by financial objectives with long-term sustainability regard.
Examples of sustainable investments: green energy companies (solar, wind, water); avoiding fossil fuels; green bonds; zero-emissions companies; fair-trade companies.
Increasing integration of ESG in investment practices across all insurance company types — due to risk/return and public interest as well as traditional ethical reasons.
Investment departments must screen assets and issuers, engage with investee companies (voting rights to encourage positive ESG practices); this increases costs passed on in pricing or fund charges.

Products should be designed in line with UN Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) and Principles for Responsible Investment (PRI) — an international organisation with six principles for incorporating ESG into investment analysis, ownership policies, disclosure, industry promotion, collaboration, and reporting.

key_terms.definition: Environmental, Social and Governance — a framework for evaluating the sustainability and ethical impact of an investment or business practice; increasingly integrated into insurance investment and product design.

Investment approaches that take account of ESG issues in a way consistent with the long-term sustainability of society and the natural environment, motivated by financial objectives with regard to long-term sustainability.
United Nations Sustainable Development Goals — 17 goals developed to promote global prosperity while protecting the environment; products should be designed in line with these objectives.

Principles for Responsible Investment — an international organisation dedicated to promoting ESG responsibility amongst investors; members commit to six principles covering ESG integration, active ownership, disclosure, industry promotion, collaboration and reporting.
Bonds issued to finance environmentally beneficial projects (e.g. renewable energy, energy efficiency); an example of a sustainable investment.

subsections.title: Removal of Barriers for Consumers
bullets: Care needed in product design to remove or minimise barriers for consumers — particularly for mental health conditions.

Measures: signposting customers declined cover to specialist brokers/support services; training claims staff to identify and compassionately deal with vulnerable customers; identifying underwriting process elements causing anxiety and changing them; bundling products with support services for mild mental health symptoms; providing rehabilitation services where appropriate.

subsections.title: Administration
bullets: Product launch requires expertise and guidance across: systems development; sales; underwriting; claims; customer service.

Administrative and claims systems must be fully operational from launch date — ideally tested and in place well before launch. Failures have caused missed opportunities, product failures, expense overruns and systems overloads.
Key staff must be recruited and trained before launch — not all staff suit the unique requirements of health insurance.
Even redesigning an existing product requires amending systems and retraining staff.

subsections.title: Expenses
bullets: Actuary must be aware of all expenses associated with a product and the contribution that expense allowances in pricing make towards covering them.
More detail on allowing for expenses in pricing models covered in SP1.

subsections.title: Lapses
bullets: Lapse rate assumptions in pricing basis depend on measures taken to control lapse rates, how historical experience is measured, and data used as starting point.
A company's own lapse rates are highly dependent on its distribution methods and after-sales service — statistics from another provider are at best a short-term measure.

Other key lapse determinants: level of economic confidence; ongoing competitiveness of the product. Own-product lapse experience should be calculated as early as possible, divided by distribution type if necessary.

Lapse and re-entry: in competitive markets, premium cutting can lead policyholders to lapse and take out a new policy at lower premium — most likely early in policy duration; only healthy lives can do this, worsening morbidity experience for remaining portfolio.

Early lapse of long-term policies almost invariably causes a loss (negative asset share); replacement policy incurs a further round of commission and expenses; worst case is repeated lapse and re-entry over several years.

Measures to reduce lapse and re-entry losses: introduce/increase premium reviewability; reduce initial and increase renewal commission; increase clawback of initial commission on early lapse; reduce initial commission for re-entrants who lapsed within 12 months either side; reduce (but not eliminate) underwriting requirements for new policy.

key_terms.definition: The practice where policyholders (especially on long-term contracts) lapse their existing policy and take out a new one with a lower premium due to competitive market price reductions; causes losses as early lapse has negative asset share and a new round of expenses is incurred.
Recovery by the insurer of initial commission paid to a distributor if the policyholder lapses the policy within a specified period; a tool to reduce lapse-and-re-entry losses.

The tendency for healthier, lower-risk policyholders to lapse their policies (e.g. in response to premium increases), leaving a worse-than-average risk portfolio and worsening future claims experience.
The accumulated value of premiums paid by a policyholder, less claims, expenses and charges; negative asset share at early durations means early lapse causes a loss to the insurer.

8.summary:
Cost of capital
• Regulation imposes significant reserving and statutory capital requirements that lead to capital strain at policy issue. Capital requirements can be assessed using model offices with sensitivity tests.
• Product design and reinsurance can be used to minimise capital requirements.
• Cost of capital is the loss of return on shareholders' investments resulting from holding reserves. It should be allowed for in the pricing models, taking into account: competing demands on the use of capital; the returns required by the different capital providers; the returns being obtained from other products or ventures.
• If the cost of capital is ignored in pricing, a subsidy must be provided from other business.
• Increasingly companies are adopting a market-consistent approach to product pricing, more consistent with embedded values, but can lead to greater volatility in the pricing.

Risks and risk management
• Need to consider risks linked to the product design and pricing process: product substitution; too many sales (capital problems); sales failure; expense overruns; product design failure; product costing failure (parameter error).
• Estimated claim rates and volatility are influenced by the claims management system. It should deal with all claims administration and payments, and be fully integrated where there are large claim numbers (eg PMI). It can be off-the-shelf or belong to a third party.
• Other claims control factors include pre-authorisation of PMI claims, rehabilitation of IP insurance claimants and counselling of IP insurance claimants and families of LTCI claimants.
• Customers need to be made 'risk aware' to achieve marketing success.
• A balance is needed between the most relevant data for pricing and the most credible data.

Reinsurance
• Reinsurance is part of the company's risk control mechanism and can reduce capital requirements. Cost of reinsurance must be met from premiums.
• Risk appetite drives extent of reinsurance; arbitrage opportunities (tax and capital) can make reinsurance mutually beneficial.
• Reinsurers can assist with capital strain at launch (financial reinsurance) and provide technical assistance.

Investment policy
• Main aim: appropriate nature/term/currency match to liabilities, maximise return, subject to risk appetite.
• PMI: short-term, high liquidity needed, investment return has minimal impact; repricing annually.
• CI/IP/LTCI: long-term, investment return more significant (especially pre-funded LTCI).
• PMI claims inflation is complex (utilisation, technology, age, like-for-like); differs from retail price or earnings inflation.
• Investment return and inflation assumptions must be consistent.

Renewal process and options
• Long-term policies: non-cancellable, premium rates may be reviewed, policy conditions cannot be changed.
• Short-term policies: terms on renewal may be identical to those for new entrant with same risk characteristics; full experience rating or NCD may apply.
• Reviewability: allows insurer to alter premiums if portfolio claims experience differs from expected; reviewed typically every 5 years.
• Selective lapsing risk associated with reviews — some insurers cap annual increases to reduce this.
• Increase options: Type 1 (automatic, pre-ordained or costed); Type 2 (periodic option, no re-underwriting, adverse selection loading required).
• Converting capital to income (CI → PLA) and income to capital (IP/LTCI) — anti-selection must be allowed for.

Other considerations
• Regulatory: authorisation, gender-neutral pricing, capital requirements per Solvency II framework.
• ESG: sustainable investment options for unit-linked; marketability if ESG standards not met; align with UN SDGs and PRI principles.
• Administration: all systems and staff must be in place and tested before launch.
• Lapses: own experience preferred; lapse and re-entry from premium cutting is a particular risk for long-term products.

9.title: Taxation Environment

subsections.title: Taxation in General
bullets: Taxation is a complex area for health and care insurers and policyholders; the extent and form can vary between countries and for different types of business within a country.

Common approaches to taxing health and care business: (i) tax on annual profits (excess of change in asset value over change in liability value); (ii) tax on investment income/gains less operating expenses; (iii) tax on premium income.
Insurers may also be subject to other taxes such as stamp duty, employment-related taxes, and other levies.
Policyholders (or their dependents) may be taxed on benefits received from health and care insurance business.
The taxation treatment of benefits may depend on whether the individual or their employer is paying the premium.

subsections.title: Impact of the Taxation Regime
bullets: The taxation environment can significantly impact the business written by an insurer in a given jurisdiction.

Impacts include: amount and timing of tax paid by insurer and policyholder; government may offer tax advantages to make certain products more attractive; taxation environment influences product design (e.g. certain restrictions may be placed on a product to receive favourable tax treatment); taxation treatment may make health and care insurance more or less attractive than equivalent products from other institutions subject to different tax rules; policyholders buying habits may be influenced by the tax treatment of policy proceeds.

The taxation environment can also change over time, with implications for existing and future business. A change in taxation for a particular product could make it more or less attractive to policyholders, influencing policy behaviour.

exam_qa.q: Give four ways in which the taxation environment can impact health and care insurance business.
exam_qa.a: (1) Amount and timing of tax paid by insurer and policyholder. (2) Government may offer tax advantages to make certain products more attractive. (3) Taxation influences product design — restrictions may be imposed to qualify for favourable tax treatment. (4) Policyholders buying habits may be influenced by tax treatment of proceeds.

subsections.title: Remainder of the Chapter
bullets: The chapter uses the UK as an illustration of the principles of health and care taxation.
The chapter provides an overview as at May 2023; candidates should keep up to date by reading the financial and insurance press.
In practice, UK health and care taxation is complex — much of the detail is beyond the scope of this course.

subsections.title: Introduction
bullets: In the UK, health and care insurance taxation depends on: (i) features of the contract (short or long-term, protection, specific wording for IP insurance); (ii) type of insurer (mutual or proprietary company).
New health and care business (PHI and long-term protection business written on or after 1 January 2013) is taxed on trading profits largely assessed from the company's accounts.
The taxation of policyholder benefits varies — covered in Section 4.

key_terms.definition: The term still used under the UK tax system to refer to income protection (IP) insurance.

subsections.title: Product Features
bullets: For UK tax purposes, health and care business is categorised into short-term and long-term business.

Long-term protection business is broadly defined as any long-term insurance contract under which benefits payable cannot exceed premiums paid, except on death or incapacity due to injury, sickness or other infirmity.

PHI business is defined in legislation as contracts providing specified benefits against risks of incapacity due to accident or sickness, where: (a) the contract is in effect for at least five years, until normal retirement age, or without time limit; and (b) the contract is either not terminable by the insurer, or terminable only in special circumstances specified in the contract.
Key conditions for PHI classification: (i) benefits payable on accident or sickness; (ii) term at least five years (or to retirement if less); (iii) contract broadly not cancellable by the insurer.
If a policy does not meet these conditions, it is deemed short-term business.

An accelerated critical illness contract can be considered long-term protection if the sum insured exceeds premiums paid but there is no surrender or survival benefit. An endowment assurance would not be long-term protection if the maturity benefit exceeded premiums.

key_terms.definition: Any long-term insurance contract under which benefits payable cannot exceed the amount of premiums paid, except on death or in respect of incapacity due to injury, sickness or other infirmity.
Health and care business that does not meet the conditions for long-term protection or PHI classification; taxed as general insurance business.

exam_qa.q: State the three key conditions for a contract to be classified as PHI business for UK tax purposes.
exam_qa.a: (1) Benefits are payable on accident or sickness as defined in the contract. (2) The term must be at least five years (or to retirement if less). (3) The contract is broadly not cancellable by the insurer.

subsections.title: Types of Insurer
bullets: The taxation treatment of health and care business differs for mutual insurance companies.
Mutual companies do not pay tax on their trading profits — the taxation treatment of mutual companies is beyond the scope of this course.
Mutuals still pay tax, but they would not generally declare trading profits in the same way as a proprietary firm, as all profits are retained to pay policyholder bonuses.
Taxation of short-term business for mutuals is considered briefly in Section 3.

key_terms.definition: An insurer without shareholders where policyholders receive the majority of profits via bonuses; mutual companies do not pay tax on their trading profits.
An insurer with shareholders that pays corporation tax on trading profits.

subsections.title: Products Taxed as Long-Term Business
bullets: Critical illness (CI) business is classified as PHI business (and hence long-term) even if there is a modest element of life cover, provided it meets the long-term conditions in Section 1.
If CI business does not meet the long-term conditions, it is classified as short-term business and taxed as general insurance.
Income protection (IP) business can be classed as either short-term or long-term depending on whether it meets the conditions in Section 1.
Long-term care insurance (LTCI) is normally classified as long-term business for tax purposes.

subsections.title: Post-2013 Taxation
bullets: PHI and long-term protection business written on or after 1 January 2013 is liable to corporation tax at the full rate on pre-tax trading profits per the entity's Companies Act accounts.
Pre-tax accounting profit broadly comprises: + premiums; + investment return (including change in value of assets); - claims; - expenses; +/- change in liabilities.
Other items such as deferred acquisition costs (DAC), intangibles, and other income may also be included.
Deferred acquisition costs (DAC) are initial expenses of acquiring business spread over the first few years of the policy term to avoid a large Day 1 loss.
Accounts may be prepared under IFRS or UK GAAP; many companies still prepare accounts on each basis.
Solvency II is not normally directly relevant to the tax position, except where the company uses Solvency II liabilities in its accounts.

Because taxable profit follows the accounts, changes in accounting policy (e.g. introduction of IFRS 17, or adoption of Solvency II in the accounts) have direct effects on taxable profit, absent specific provisions changing tax law.

This business is taxed in the same way as profits of any UK trading company; UK plc tax rules apply subject to certain adjustments, including some specific adjustments for PHI business (detail beyond scope).

key_terms.definition: Initial expenses of acquiring new business that are spread (deferred) over the first few years of the policy term in the accounts, avoiding a large Day 1 loss and subsequent taxable profits in later years.

International Financial Reporting Standard for insurance contracts, which came into effect for UK health and care insurers on 1 January 2023; changes in accounting basis directly affect taxable profits.

exam_qa.q: List the main components of pre-tax accounting profit for long-term business post-2013.
exam_qa.a: Premiums + investment return (including change in asset value) - claims - expenses +/- change in liabilities, plus potentially DAC, intangibles and other income.

exam_qa.q: Why does an accounting policy change (e.g. adoption of IFRS 17) have a direct effect on the tax payable by a long-term insurer?
exam_qa.a: Because taxable profit for post-2013 long-term business follows the profits declared in the entity's accounts. Any change in the accounting basis therefore directly changes the declared profit and hence the taxable profit, unless specific tax legislation is introduced to adjust for this.

subsections.title: Pre-2013 Taxation (I-E Basis)
bullets: Some long-term protection business written prior to 2013 may be taxed on an I-E (I minus E) basis.

Only some LTCI business and the death benefit element of accelerated CI insurance would have fallen into this category pre-2013; other long-term health and care products were taxed on a profits basis under the PHI definition.
The I-E result was designed to be a quick calculation of the sum of shareholder and policyholder profit.
Revenue account identity: Shareholder Profit = P(premiums) + I(income and gains) - E(expenses) - C(claims). Policyholder Profit = C - P, therefore: Shareholder Profit + Policyholder Profit = I - E.
Both the insurer and policyholders should be taxed on the profits they make; the total taxable amount is approximately I-E.
Mutuals are taxed solely on I-E since there is no shareholder profit. Additional tax may apply for proprietary companies (detail beyond scope).
Where an insurer has business on both I-E and trading profits bases, it must apportion trading profits and I-E income and expenses between the two categories.
Long-term insurers that had only ever written protection business had the option to elect to have all business taxed on profits post-2013, removing much of this complication.

key_terms.definition: A method of taxing long-term insurance business where the taxable amount is investment income and gains (I) minus management expenses (E); designed to approximate the sum of shareholder and policyholder profit.

exam_qa.q: Derive why the I-E basis approximates the total profit of an insurer and its policyholders.
exam_qa.a: Shareholder Profit = P + I - E - C. Policyholder Profit = C - P (claims exceed premiums, funded by investment returns). Adding these: Total Profit = I - E. So I-E captures the combined taxable profit of both parties.

sections.title: Taxation of a UK Proprietary Short-Term Business

subsections.title: Introduction
bullets: Health and care business classified as short-term (per Section 1) is taxed as general insurance business.
For the SA1 syllabus, PMI is classed as short-term business and taxed as per a general insurance contract.
IP insurance may also be taxed as short-term business if it does not meet the long-term conditions.
A UK proprietary short-term insurance company pays corporation tax on its taxable profits.

Taxable profits are based on profits disclosed in financial statements subject to adjustments for: (i) investment return (notably on equities); (ii) the underwriting result and technical provisions; (iii) other items such as depreciation and disallowable entertaining; (iv) overseas/foreign profits.
Pre-tax accounting profit broadly comprises: investment return (including change in value of assets) + the underwriting result.

subsections.title: Investment Return
bullets: Investment return of a short-term insurer comprises income and gains from assets held (e.g. gilts, bonds, deposits, equities).
No distinction is normally made for tax purposes between investment income and investment gains for a short-term insurer — both are taxed as income.
Dividends from UK equities are not subject to tax because dividend income is franked — tax has already been paid before it reaches the insurer.
Dividends from overseas equities are also generally not subject to UK tax.
Gains on equities (UK or overseas) are taxed as income on a fair value basis — unrealised gains and losses are brought into accounts as they accrue.

key_terms.definition: Dividend income that has already had tax paid upon it at the corporate level before it reaches the insurer, so no further tax is payable on receipt.
An accounting/tax basis under which unrealised gains and losses on assets (e.g. equities) are recognised in the accounts as they accrue, rather than only when the asset is sold.

exam_qa.q: Why are dividends from UK equities not subject to tax for a short-term insurer?
exam_qa.a: Dividend income is franked — it has already had tax paid upon it before it reaches the insurer, so no further tax is payable.

subsections.title: The Underwriting Result and Technical Provisions
bullets: A short-term insurer calculates its underwriting result by reference to accounting principles used in its annual Report and Accounts (e.g. UK GAAP); this typically comprises written premiums less claims and expenses less the increase in technical provisions.
UK health and care insurers must comply with IFRS 17, which came into effect on 1 January 2023.
Short-term insurers may want to defer tax by setting up reserves as large as possible; HMRC will scrutinise whether reserves are appropriate and not excessive.
A short-term insurer's technical provisions are in principle deductible for tax provided they are calculated under UK or International GAAP.

HMRC may require technical provisions to be supported by an actuarial opinion confirming that, on the basis of information available at the time the financial statements are adopted, the amount is not excessive.

Technical provisions that can normally be taken into account in calculating taxable profits: (i) reported claims provisions supported by case estimates or statistical provisions (limited to appropriate, i.e. not excessive amounts); (ii) provision for unearned premiums calculated in the most accurate manner available; (iii) provision for unexpired risk reserve established on a statistical basis; (iv) provision for internal and external claims handling expenses, to the extent expenses relate directly to claims for which provisions have been accepted by HMRC.

key_terms.definition: Written premiums less claims and expenses less the increase in technical provisions; the core trading result of a short-term insurer before investment return.
A proportion of premium received held for periods of cover falling beyond the end of the current accounting period.
The total amount required to cover expected future claims and expenses in the unexpired period of cover in excess of the UPR.
A reserve held for claims where the claim event has happened but the claim has yet to be reported to the insurer.

Reserves set up by an insurer to cover expected future claims and expenses; deductible for tax if calculated under UK or International GAAP and supported by an actuarial opinion confirming they are not excessive.

exam_qa.q: What actuarial opinion is required for technical provisions to be deductible for a short-term insurer?
exam_qa.a: A written actuarial opinion in accordance with actuarial standards confirming that, on the basis of information available at the time the financial statements are adopted, the amount of technical provisions is not excessive.

exam_qa.q: Give two examples of circumstances that could lead to disagreements between an insurer and HMRC over the size of the UPR.
exam_qa.a: (1) If the insurer does not have good quality data, e.g. if business is sold through a third party such as a bank. (2) If the risk is not spread uniformly over the year.

subsections.title: Other Items
bullets: Tax law may require other adjustments to profits or losses shown in the financial statements.
For example, depreciation and some amortisation charges are not allowable for tax purposes.

subsections.title: Overseas Profits
bullets: In some circumstances, foreign taxes may be credited against UK corporation tax.
The details are complex and beyond the scope of this course.

subsections.title: Tax Losses
bullets: If a UK proprietary short-term insurance company sustains a tax loss, no UK corporation tax will be due.

Three main ways to relieve trading losses: (i) carry back and set against the previous year's profits; (ii) carry forward and set against future trading profits; (iii) surrender to other companies in the same UK tax group and offset against their taxable profits.

key_terms.definition: The ability to set a current-year tax loss against profits of the previous year, generating a tax refund.
The ability to set a current-year tax loss against future trading profits to reduce future tax liabilities.
The surrender of tax losses to other companies within the same UK tax group to offset against their taxable profits.

exam_qa.q: State the three main ways in which a short-term insurer can relieve a trading tax loss.
exam_qa.a: (1) Carry back against previous year's profits. (2) Carry forward against future trading profits. (3) Surrender to group companies to offset against their taxable profits.

subsections.title: Differences in Taxation Treatment of Mutual, Health Provident and Proprietary Insurers
bullets: Main differences between mutual and proprietary health and care insurers: (i) underwriting losses and profits from mutual trading are exempt from tax — no tax relief may be given for expenses that are part of the mutual trade; (ii) investment return is taxed independently.
If a mutual makes an underwriting loss, this loss cannot be set against gross investment return.
This could lead to a potentially harsh tax basis for short-term mutual insurers; however, investment return is rarely large for short-term health insurers.
There are relatively few mutual short-term insurance companies in the UK.
Provident health insurers are health insurers where any profit is reinvested in better health and care services or used to reduce premiums or improve benefits (i.e. not for profit).
Provident health insurers are taxed either as mutuals or proprietary companies depending on the corporate structure within any holding group.

key_terms.definition: A health insurer where any profit is reinvested in better health and care services or used to reduce premiums or improve benefits (not for profit); taxed as mutual or proprietary depending on corporate structure.

exam_qa.q: How does the taxation of a mutual short-term insurer differ from a proprietary short-term insurer?
exam_qa.a: For a mutual, underwriting profits/losses from mutual trading are exempt from tax (and no tax relief may be given for expenses forming part of the mutual trade). Investment return is taxed independently and cannot be offset by an underwriting loss. For a proprietary insurer, corporation tax is paid on total taxable profits (underwriting result + investment return), and losses can be set against other income.

sections.title: Taxation of Health and Care Business for Policyholders

subsections.title: Overview
bullets: Taxation of benefits payable on health and care insurance policies may vary depending on: the type of business; who receives the benefit; who has paid premiums into the policy.

subsections.title: UK Policyholder Taxation
bullets: This section provides a high-level overview of policyholder taxation in the UK for CI, IP, LTCI and PMI.
In practice there are additional complexities beyond the scope of this course.
Both the taxation of premiums and the taxation of benefits is considered.

subsections.title: Critical Illness Insurance
bullets: Tax relief on premiums: in some countries premiums paid for insurance cover are eligible for tax relief (i.e. tax payable by the insured will be lower); tax relief is most likely offered when the State wishes to encourage take-up of a particular product.
In the UK, premiums under individual CI policies do not normally qualify for tax relief.
Benefits payable under stand-alone CI policies are tax-free.
For accelerated CI insurance, if the benefit is paid on death it may incur inheritance tax; this liability can be removed by structuring the product appropriately (details beyond scope).

Group CI — if the employer pays the premium: (i) it is usually deductible as a business expense for the employer in its corporation tax assessment; (ii) from the employee's perspective, payment by the employer would be taxable as a benefit in kind.

Under UK PAYE rules, employees pay tax on benefits in kind received from their employer — the value of such benefits is treated as additional salary; for insurance benefits the value is typically assessed using the premium paid on the employee's behalf.
The benefit (claim payment) under group CI is usually paid directly to the employee and is tax-free.

key_terms.definition: A reduction in the amount of tax payable by the insured, granted when premiums qualify for relief — typically offered by governments to encourage take-up of particular insurance products.
A non-cash benefit provided by an employer to an employee (e.g. employer-paid insurance premiums) that is treated as additional salary for income tax purposes under PAYE rules.
A tax that may arise on CI benefits paid on death under accelerated CI policies; can be mitigated by structuring the product appropriately (e.g. writing it in trust).

exam_qa.q: Explain the tax position for an employee whose employer pays CI insurance premiums on their behalf (group CI).
exam_qa.a: The premium is deductible as a business expense in the employer's corporation tax assessment. For the employee, the employer's premium payment is a benefit in kind and is taxable as additional salary under PAYE. However, any claim benefit paid directly to the employee is tax-free.

exam_qa.q: Why may governments offer tax relief on health and care insurance premiums?
exam_qa.a: Tax relief is most likely offered when the State wishes to encourage take-up of a particular insurance product — for example, in countries with compulsory PMI or limited State healthcare provision, tax relief is often offered on PMI premiums.

subsections.title: Income Protection Insurance
bullets: Individual IP — premiums: do not normally qualify for tax relief.
Individual IP — benefits: if the life insured pays the premium, benefits are tax-free. If the employer pays the premium, benefits are taxed as per group arrangements.
Group IP — premiums paid by employer: treated as a business expense; employer qualifies for tax relief at the corporation tax rate. Premiums are not assessed on the employee as a benefit in kind.
Group IP — premiums paid by employee: no tax relief is allowed.
Group IP — benefits payable to the employer: taxed as a trading receipt; the employer then passes the benefit to the employee as salary, which is taxable as income in the employee's hands.
Group IP — benefits in respect of employee-paid premiums: tax-free in the same way as individual policies.

LTCI — taxation may depend on: whether it is a pre-funded or immediate needs product; who receives the benefits; whether the benefits represent initial capital or interest earned. Detail is beyond the scope of this course.

key_terms.definition: An amount treated as income in a company's trading accounts for tax purposes; group IP benefits paid to an employer are taxed as a trading receipt before being passed to the employee as salary.

exam_qa.q: For group IP insurance, explain the tax treatment of benefits where premiums are paid by the employer.
exam_qa.a: Benefits are paid to the employer and taxed as a trading receipt. The employer then passes the benefit to the employee in the form of salary. The income will be taxable in the hands of the employee as employment income.

exam_qa.q: Compare the tax treatment of premiums for individual IP insurance paid by (a) the life insured and (b) the employer.
exam_qa.a: (a) Premiums paid by the life insured: no tax relief. Benefits are tax-free. (b) Premiums paid by the employer: deductible as a business expense (corporation tax relief for the employer); premiums are not a benefit in kind for the employee. Benefits are taxed as a trading receipt (and then salary) in the employer's/employee's hands.

subsections.title: Private Medical Insurance (PMI)
bullets: PMI claim payments usually indemnify policyholders — they do not create profit and are therefore not subject to tax.
Insurance premiums for PMI are exempt from VAT in the UK.

Most PMI premiums are subject to Insurance Premium Tax (IPT), which is expressed as a percentage of premium; at the time of writing (May 2023) IPT is 12% and is generally added to the premium by the insurer.
Long-term insurance premiums are exempt from IPT; IPT applies to general (short-term) insurance premiums.
Premiums paid by a corporate policyholder are usually paid out of pre-tax income (available for tax relief), whilst premiums paid by individual policyholders are paid from post-tax income.

key_terms.definition: A tax payable on general (short-term) insurance premiums in the UK, expressed as a percentage of premium (12% as at May 2023); generally added to the premium by the insurer. Long-term insurance premiums are exempt.
A tax paid on most products and services in the UK; not payable on any insurance premiums — PMI premiums are exempt from VAT.

A benefit that reimburses the policyholder for actual costs incurred (e.g. medical expenses under PMI), rather than paying a fixed sum; such payments do not create profit and are not subject to income tax.

exam_qa.q: Why are PMI claim payments not subject to income tax for the policyholder?
exam_qa.a: PMI claims usually indemnify the policyholder for costs incurred — they do not in themselves create a profit for the policyholder and are therefore not subject to tax.

exam_qa.q: What is Insurance Premium Tax (IPT) and how does it apply to PMI?
exam_qa.a: IPT is a tax on general (short-term) insurance premiums, expressed as a percentage of premium (12% as at May 2023). PMI is classified as short-term business and so is subject to IPT, which is added to the premium by the insurer. PMI premiums are exempt from VAT but not from IPT.

exam_qa.q: Compare the tax treatment of PMI premiums paid by a corporate employer versus an individual policyholder.
exam_qa.a: Corporate policyholder: premiums paid out of pre-tax income, so effectively receive tax relief at the corporation tax rate. Individual policyholder: premiums paid from post-tax income, so no tax relief.

9.summary:
Overview
• Tax can be based on: profits; investment income less expenses; premium income.
• Other taxes (such as stamp duty or employment related taxes) may also be payable.
• Policyholders (or their dependents) may be taxed on benefits.
• The taxation environment can change over time and may impact: the amount and timing of tax payable by an insurer; the attractiveness of insurance products (and compared to equivalent products offered by other institutions); the design of insurance products; policyholders buying habits.

The UK tax environment

Long-term business
• Long-term protection business is taxed as PHI business provided: benefits are payable on accident or sickness as defined in the contract; the term is at least five years (or to retirement if less); the contract is (broadly) not cancellable by the insurer.
• The majority of CI insurance, IP insurance and LTCI are classed as long-term business.
• Post January 2013, insurers pay corporation tax on pre-tax trading profits of long-term business. The accounts should be prepared in accordance with relevant accounting rules.
• Before January 2013, some long-term protection business was taxed on an I-E basis.

Short-term business
• Insurers pay corporation tax on tradable profits for short-term business (such as PMI). This is taxable profits adjusted for: investment return (excluding dividend income) – taxed on a fair value basis; the underwriting result and technical provision; other items (depreciation / disallowable entertaining); overseas / foreign profit.
• The underwriting result is premiums less claims and expenses less increase in technical provisions.
• The technical provisions are calculated on an accounting basis and an actuarial opinion is required to confirm the amount is ‘not excessive’. Technical provisions can include: reported claims provisions with case estimates or statistical provisions; provision for unearned premiums on the most appropriate basis; provision for unexpired risk reserve on a statistical basis; provisions for claims handing expenses.
• Tax losses may be: carried back and set against the previous year’s profits; carried forward and set against future trading profits; surrendered to other companies in the same UK tax group and used to offset the taxable profits of those companies.
• For a mutual insurer writing short-term insurance, underwriting losses / profits from mutual trading are exempt from tax. Investment income is taxed independently. If they make an underwriting loss, it cannot be offset against investment profits.

Taxation of policyholders
• Tax of policyholders may depend on the type of business, who receives the benefit and who has paid the premiums.

UK policyholder taxation
• CI insurance: individual policies – no tax relief on premiums, benefits of stand-alone policies are tax free; group policies – if the employer pays the premiums it is usually deductible as a business expense for the employer, the employee would be taxed on the premium as a ‘benefit in kind’.
• IP insurance: individual policies – no tax relief on premiums, benefits are tax-free if premiums are paid by the life insured; group policies – if premiums are paid by the employer for the employee classified as business expense for the employer and qualify for tax relief, if premiums are paid by the employee no tax relief. If benefits are paid to the employer, taxed as a trading receipt, if they are then passed to the employee as a salary income tax will be payable.
• LTCI – varies depending on whether pre-funded or immediate needs product.
• PMI: no tax on claims; premiums – insurance premium tax is payable, if paid by employer paid out of pre-tax income (ie available for tax relief) but if paid by policyholders paid from post-tax income.

10.title: Approaches to Regulation

subsections.title: Why Insurance is Regulated
bullets: Insurance business attracts more legislation than most industries because there is greater scope for the purchaser to lose out financially. With insurance, the policyholder must trust the insurer to pay valid claims as and when they arise in the future.

The uncertainty underlying insurance means that even a well-meaning insurer may have collapsed by the time a claim is made. There is a danger of policyholders suffering at the hands of unscrupulous or incompetent operators.

Insurance legislation aims to protect policyholders by implementing controls over: the classes an insurer can sell; the people who run the company; the level of financial strength; and information published about the company.
Because it is difficult to prevent every individual insurance company from collapsing, there are schemes to ensure that policyholders do not lose out if this were to happen.

The chapter covers: regulatory objectives (Section 1); regulatory responsibilities (Section 2); other legislation and professional guidance (Section 3); and regulatory approach by jurisdiction (Section 4).

subsections.title: Overview of Regulatory Objectives
bullets: The premise of health and care insurance is that a policyholder pays premiums in advance in return for a promise of future benefit. Due to the uncertainty of an insurer's liabilities, it is very difficult for policyholders to monitor the financial soundness of their insurer or make reasonably informed judgements about their exposure to risk.

Regulations and laws have been introduced in most countries primarily to protect the public as insurance consumers and policyholders. Most jurisdictions have an insurance regulator to develop new regulations, monitor compliance and take action to protect policyholders.

At a high level, regulatory objectives will often be focused on policyholder protection and financial stability. Examples of possible objectives include: promoting the safety and soundness of supervised companies; securing appropriate consumer protection; ensuring appropriate consumer outcomes; promoting effective competition in the interests of consumers; protecting and enhancing the integrity of the financial system; and promoting the development of the insurance industry.

Even where regulatory objectives are apparently similar across jurisdictions, the actual approach taken can differ significantly due to differences in legal frameworks, environment, or historical context.

Changes in regulatory objectives can be driven by: changes in the economic environment; a desire to increase harmonisation across jurisdictions; and advances in risk management. The 2008 global financial crisis caused many regulators to re-examine their approach. Harmonisation across jurisdictions was the driver behind the Solvency II directive in Europe.

There is a balance to be struck between the interests of the government, regulator(s), insurer, and policyholder. High levels of regulation could help reduce risk and protect policyholders, but excessive regulation leads to high compliance costs, higher premiums, less competition, and possibly lower insurance coverage.
Direct costs of regulation can be passed on through increased premiums. Indirect costs (e.g. changing consumer behaviour, reduced product innovation) are harder to quantify.

Health and care insurance is a global business. In addition to local regulation, regulators in individual jurisdictions may work with supra-national regulators to help set standards globally. An insurer operating across national boundaries must comply with regulations in each territory.
In addition to specific insurance regulation there may be additional legislation impacting health and care insurers in relation to consumer protection, equality, and climate change.

key_terms.definition: A regulatory body that operates across multiple jurisdictions or national boundaries, setting standards that individual country regulators must follow (e.g. EIOPA in the European Union).

exam_qa.q: Why is insurance business subject to more regulation than most other industries?
exam_qa.a: Because there is more scope for the purchaser to lose out financially. With insurance, payment is made in advance and the benefit is received in the future — the policyholder must trust the insurer to pay valid claims. Even a well-meaning insurer may have collapsed by the time a claim is made, and policyholders lack the expertise to assess the financial soundness of an insurer. This information asymmetry and the risk of insurer insolvency justify a higher level of regulatory oversight.

exam_qa.q: What factors can drive changes in regulatory objectives for an insurance regulator?
exam_qa.a: Changes in the economic environment (e.g. the 2008 global financial crisis prompted many regulators to re-examine their approach); a desire to increase harmonisation across jurisdictions (e.g. Solvency II in Europe); and advances in risk management techniques.

subsections.title: Overview
bullets: In order for a health and care insurer to meet its obligations to policyholders, many complex inter-related processes within the business need to work successfully together. In many jurisdictions, regulation and regulatory supervision will often cover almost every aspect of a health and care insurer's business.

A health and care insurance regulator will normally produce rulebooks setting out the detailed rules and principles that a health and care insurer operating in that jurisdiction must comply with. Regulators may also issue supervisory statements or guidance notes — not necessarily regulations, but regulators would expect firms to behave consistently with their content.

Types of regulatory responsibilities include: Authorisation; Business Standards; Supervisory Reporting; Monitoring; Valuation and Capital Requirements; Fit and Proper Person; Actions taken by Regulators; Transfer of Surplus; and Transfer of Liabilities between insurance companies.

key_terms.definition: A document produced by a regulator setting out the detailed rules and principles that a health and care insurer must comply with in that jurisdiction.

subsections.title: Authorisation
bullets: Health and care insurers will normally be required to obtain permission from the relevant regulator in order to carry out a regulated activity in a particular jurisdiction. Requirements and processes for obtaining permission will be set out in the relevant rulebook.

Regulations may set out the authorisation required to write certain classes of business (e.g. health and care, annuity business) and separate applications may be required for each class. A company will normally not be allowed to undertake insurance of a particular class unless specifically authorised to do so.

In some jurisdictions the regulator may require a new product to be pre-approved in advance prior to being sold. In others, pre-approval may not be required, but the regulator may expect the insurer to discuss particularly risky or novel products in advance.

Within a wider economic bloc, authorisation in one state may allow the insurer to sell in other countries within that bloc. For example, in the EU a company authorised in one member state may transact business in any other EU state, subject to insurance supervision in its home state.

key_terms.definition: The regulatory requirement and process by which an insurer obtains permission to write specific classes of business in a particular jurisdiction.

subsections.title: Business Standards
bullets: Health and care insurers will often be required to comply with detailed regulations regarding the operation of their business, covering both generic regulations that apply to a range of companies and insurance-specific regulations.

Business standards regulations set out requirements affecting companies in their day-to-day business. They are likely to cover senior management systems and controls, and market conduct (including treating customers fairly).

Senior management systems and controls may include: compliance; risk management; systems and controls; outsourcing; appointment of fit and proper persons and their responsibilities (including statutory actuarial roles); regular meetings with senior management at the firm; and regulatory processes describing the operation of the regulator's supervisory and disciplinary functions.

Market conduct rules often require a firm to act honestly, fairly, and professionally in accordance with the best interests of its clients. Key concepts covered include: (1) Communication with clients — all communications must be clear, fair, and not misleading, with specific rules on past performance in financial promotions and distance marketing/internet communications; (2) Information about the company, its service, and remuneration — an 'initial disclosure document' may be required at first contact, detailing the nature of the firm, its services, and how it is remunerated. Commission may be prohibited on some or all products in some jurisdictions; (3) Product information — at or before the point of sale, a document setting out key features of the contract (customer-specific expenses, charges, premiums, projections at different investment returns) may be required. The impact of charges could be shown as a reduction in yield; (4) Compensation — rules for dealing with complaints and paying compensation to customers.

Other market conduct areas include: assessment of suitability of products for clients; record-keeping; execution of deals; the right to cancel; claims handling; provision of regular and transaction-related information to the customer; and the underlying assets on which unit-linked business can base its benefits.

key_terms.definition: A regulatory principle requiring insurers to act honestly, fairly, and professionally in accordance with the best interests of clients. Interpretation and specific requirements vary by jurisdiction.
A document that a prospective policyholder may be required to receive when first contact is made, setting out information on the nature of the firm, its services, and how it is remunerated.
A way of expressing the impact of product charges — showing the effect of charges as a reduction in the investment return, so customers can see the cost of the product.

exam_qa.q: What types of information might a pre-sale product information document be required to include for a unit-linked product?
exam_qa.a: Key features including customer-specific expenses and charges; total premiums paid; total charges and impact of any deductions; projections of fund value at different points (e.g. every 5 years) using different investment return scenarios (e.g. 3%, 6%, 9%). The impact of charges shown as a reduction in yield based on a central investment return. Some jurisdictions fix the investment return rates to be used.

subsections.title: Supervisory Reporting
bullets: It is common for regulators to require health and care insurers to provide detailed regular reporting. The regulator will set out reporting requirements including the form (often standardised templates, possibly supplemented by management reports) and frequency of reporting.
