Information required will vary between regulators but is likely to include some or all of: details of the balance sheet (value of assets, liabilities, and capital requirements); details of the revenue account and basis of preparation (e.g. local GAAP or IFRS); components of capital requirements and assumptions/models used; details of the method of valuation of assets and liabilities (including models and assumptions); details of new business written over the year; a forward-looking analysis showing business plan projections for at least three years (including key assumptions); and possible stress and scenario testing on the business plan.

The timing of submissions is likely to be spread over the year. Year-end items (balance sheet, revenue accounts, new business) are submitted once results are signed off by internal governance. Business plans and stress/scenario testing are submitted at other times and are likely to form part of internal management reporting anyway.
Examples of supervisory reporting requirements under Solvency II are given in Chapter 11. Financial reporting is covered in more detail in Chapter 14.

subsections.title: Monitoring
bullets: The regulator will look to regularly monitor the ongoing operation of the health and care insurer to ensure it is being managed in an appropriate way.

Monitoring may include: regular assessment visits where regulators attend offices of the firm to look at specific aspects of operations; review of any reports the company has discussed at relevant governance meetings (e.g. Board, Audit Committee, Risk Committee); and supervisory reviews of insurance companies involving reviewing regulatory reports submitted by the company, where the degree of scrutiny may depend on the inherent risk of the company.
Examples of monitoring requirements under Solvency II are given in Chapter 12.

subsections.title: Valuation and Capital Requirements
bullets: Regulators will normally set out rules and/or principles for determining the valuation of assets and liabilities and in the determination of capital requirements. These are likely to include:
Valuation of assets — including what value to use (e.g. market value, book value), how to treat unlisted assets, any models used for valuation, restrictions on assets and admissibility limits.
Liabilities — including types of business included and how to treat discretionary increases and options/guarantees.

Assumptions — including how to derive discount rates (e.g. best estimate, risk-free, or prudent), derivation of demographic assumptions, whether persistency assumptions are included, and how to value options/guarantees.
Reinsurance — how to allow for reinsurance and in particular the risk of default of the reinsurer.

Capital requirements — the derivation of required capital that must be held, including the calculation method or principles, minimum levels required, and levels at which supervisory intervention should be expected. There may also be details of maintaining capital requirements within specific funds (e.g. long-term business fund or shareholder fund).
The approach taken in various jurisdictions for valuation and capital requirements is outlined in more detail in Chapter 13.

key_terms.definition: Regulatory restrictions on the types and/or amounts of assets that can be counted towards meeting a company's capital requirements.

A separate fund within a health and care insurer where profits on long-term insurance products (e.g. IP, CI, long-term care) are accounted for. Regulations govern how much can be transferred out of this fund.

subsections.title: Fit and Proper Persons and Statutory Actuarial Roles
bullets: A regulator will normally want to ensure that individuals who hold, or are proposed to hold, specified senior positions in regulated firms are vetted to ensure they satisfy appropriate 'fit and proper' criteria.

Regulatory rulebooks are likely to set out the rules by which individuals in senior roles will be assessed — this could include industry experience at a senior level, background checks, and possibly an interview with the regulator's staff.
In deciding whether a person is fit and proper, a firm should consider: personal characteristics (e.g. integrity); level of competence and experience; and qualifications and training.

Rulebooks also set out corresponding requirements for firms' ongoing assessment of the fitness and propriety of such individuals and the circumstances under which regulatory approval may be withdrawn.

In many jurisdictions, there is a statutory requirement for a health and care insurance company transacting long-term business to appoint actuaries into certain positions. Titles vary by jurisdiction — in the EU under Solvency II there is a requirement for a Chief Actuary; other jurisdictions require an Actuarial Function Holder, Appropriate Actuary, or Appointed Actuary.

Examples of types of responsibility allocated to statutory actuarial roles include: ensuring the adequacy of technical provisions; ensuring the appropriateness of methodology and assumptions used to calculate technical provisions; assessing the sufficiency and adequacy of the data used; and expressing an opinion on the adequacy of reinsurance arrangements.

Holders of statutory actuarial roles are usually not allowed to fulfil other roles within a firm that would cause a conflict of interest, or if they do, the firm is required to declare these roles to the regulator.

In respect of statutory actuarial roles, a health and care insurer is often required to: keep the actuary informed of business plans and seek advice on implications for policyholders; pay due regard to the advice of the actuary; and provide the actuary with adequate resources, data, and systems.
The details of statutory roles under Solvency II are examined in more detail in Chapter 11.

key_terms.definition: A regulatory standard requiring individuals in senior or statutory positions at regulated firms to demonstrate integrity, competence and experience, and appropriate qualifications and training.

A role that a health and care insurer transacting long-term business is legally required to appoint a qualified actuary to fill. Examples include Chief Actuary (Solvency II), Actuarial Function Holder, and Appointed Actuary.

The statutory actuarial role required under Solvency II in the European Union. Responsible for ensuring adequacy of technical provisions, appropriateness of methodology and assumptions, and adequacy of reinsurance arrangements.

exam_qa.q: What types of responsibility might be allocated to a statutory actuarial role?
exam_qa.a: Ensuring the adequacy of technical provisions; ensuring the appropriateness of the methodology and assumptions used to calculate technical provisions; assessing the sufficiency and adequacy of the data used to calculate the methodology and assumptions; and expressing an opinion on the adequacy of reinsurance arrangements.

subsections.title: Actions Taken by Regulators
bullets: Regulators will often have a range of powers available to meet their supervisory objectives and in particular to protect policyholders. Where a health and care insurer is acting in a way the regulator deems inappropriate, possible powers could include: requiring the insurer to remedy the situation; public censure; fines; requirement to purchase reinsurance; requirement to hold additional capital; revoking the insurer's permission to carry out regulated activities; revoking the ability of certain managers to hold senior positions; requiring the insurer to pursue a merger or acquisition; refusal to approve a transfer of liabilities between insurers; and criminal prosecutions.

In some jurisdictions, the regulator may only legally be able to exercise its powers in specific circumstances (e.g. where solvency falls below a certain level). In others, the regulator may have more discretion about the point at which it can take action and the extent of that action.

Where a regulator has discretion, it must carefully consider the circumstances of each case before deciding on an appropriate course of action. Actions taken by regulators should be proportionate to the nature, scale, and complexity of the risks inherent in an insurer's business.

Prior to taking action, possible factors the regulator will need to consider include: the proximity of failure of the insurer; the level of confidence that the insurer will act on the regulator's instructions; and the potential impact on policyholders and the stability of the financial system.

If the insurer's financial position is serious, the regulator may require it to close to new business. In less serious cases, the insurer may be required to establish a recovery plan, to be monitored closely by the regulator.

subsections.title: Treatment of Groups
bullets: Some insurance companies are of such size, market importance, and global interconnectedness that their distress or failure would cause severe adverse consequences across the global financial system. In this case they may be subject to additional regulatory scrutiny.

In 2013, the International Association of Insurance Supervisors (IAIS) announced its intended approach to the identification of 'global systemically important insurers' (G-SIIs). Shortly afterwards, the Financial Stability Board (FSB) published an initial list of nine such G-SIIs, updated annually.
G-SIIs were subject to enhanced supervision, including systemic risk management plans, enhanced liquidity plans, and effective separation of non-traditional or non-insurance business.

The FSB decided to discontinue the annual identification of G-SIIs in 2023. Going forward, the FSB will base its considerations of systemic risk in the insurance sector on the IAIS Holistic Framework for the assessment and mitigation of systemic risk in the insurance sector.

In 2020, the FSB adopted a holistic framework — an activities-based approach for assessing systemic risk — including an annual assessment of potential systemic risk from specific activities and exposures across insurance sectors. From 2023, the FSB publishes an annual list of insurers subject to resolution planning and resolvability assessment.

key_terms.definition: An insurance company of such size, market importance, and global interconnectedness that its distress or failure would cause severe adverse consequences across the global financial system. Subject to enhanced regulatory supervision.

An international body that monitors and makes recommendations about the global financial system. Published the initial list of G-SIIs and now oversees the Holistic Framework for systemic risk assessment in insurance.
The international standard-setting body for insurance regulation, responsible for developing the Holistic Framework for assessing and mitigating systemic risk in the insurance sector.

subsections.title: Transfers of Surplus
bullets: Regulations will usually be included to determine how much a company can legally transfer out of its business, for example dividend payments to shareholders or distributions from a subsidiary to a parent company.

The long-term insurance fund is where the insurer accounts for profits on long-term insurance products (IP insurance, CI insurance, and long-term care insurance). There will be a similar short-term insurance fund for any short-term surplus or profit.

These regulations may allow any amount disclosed as surplus in the supervisory valuation to be transferred out of the long-term insurance fund, or may specify that a certain level of capital requirement must be covered by surplus within the long-term insurance fund, hence restricting any transfer.

In some jurisdictions, the regulator may also consider the quality of capital remaining within the long-term fund following a distribution. Capital that is particularly illiquid may not act as a suitable buffer. The regulator will generally expect the insurer to have sufficient liquidity to pay claims after any distribution.
A regulator may require a minimum proportion of the insurer's capital to be held in certain liquid, secure asset classes (e.g. government bonds) to reduce this risk.

subsections.title: Transfer of Liabilities from One Health and Care Insurance Company to Another
bullets: Companies may, from time to time, transfer liabilities between one health and care insurance company and another — as a result of takeovers and mergers, or internal reorganisation within larger groups.

Regulators will require certain processes to be followed to ensure that the interests and benefits of policyholders — those being transferred, those remaining with the company (if applicable), and those existing policyholders in the receiving company — are protected.

Regulations may prohibit any transfer of business out of a particular jurisdiction, and equally there may be requirements to obtain either approval or non-objection from other interested regulators in other jurisdictions (e.g. where branch regulators or a supranational organisation such as the EU requires all interested regulators to be involved).

In the UK, transfers will often require approval from a Court (or similar body) within the jurisdiction concerned, and a report on the scheme from an independent expert (IE) appointed by the firm but approved by the regulator. The IE's duty is to assist the Court and give independent expert evidence on the proposed transfer.

The IE report would primarily assess the likely impact of the transfer on: the transferring policyholders; the remaining policyholders (if the transferred business is only part of the transferring company's portfolio); and the existing policyholders of the company to which the business is being transferred.
Key areas of consideration for the IE include: policyholder benefits and benefit expectations; security of policyholder benefits; and wider treating customers fairly (TCF) issues.

Prior to Court approval, the scheme must be adequately publicised in the press; all policyholders involved must be communicated to (sending a statement setting out the terms of the scheme and a summary of the IE's report); the receiving company must be authorised for that type of business and able to cover its regulatory capital requirements after the transfer; and any concerns from stakeholders (policyholders, employees, regulators) should be heard.

In the USA, insurance regulation is done at state level and regulations can vary between states. Insurance business can be transferred from one entity to another within a jurisdiction in two main ways: indemnity reinsurance (most common — one insurer reinsures its liabilities within another, original issuer remains liable to policyholders, no notice to policyholders generally required, policyholders have no right to object); or assumption reinsurance (actual policies are transferred from one insurer to another, generally requires approval of policyholders, policyholders have the right to object, can be a long and difficult process).

key_terms.definition: An expert appointed by the transferring firm (but approved by the regulator) to report on a proposed transfer of insurance liabilities between companies. The IE's duty is to assist the Court and give independent expert evidence on the impact of the transfer on all relevant groups of policyholders.

The most common approach to transferring insurance business in the USA. One insurer reinsures its liabilities within another insurer. The original issuer remains liable to the policyholder. No notice to policyholders is generally required and policyholders have no right to object.

A method of transferring insurance business where actual policies are transferred from one insurer to another. Generally requires approval of policyholders, who have the right to object. Can be a long and difficult process, with some states requiring approval of each individual policyholder.

exam_qa.q: What are the key areas of consideration for an independent expert (IE) when assessing a proposed transfer of insurance liabilities?
exam_qa.a: The IE would primarily assess the likely impact of the transfer on three groups: (1) the transferring policyholders; (2) the remaining policyholders of the transferring company (if only part of the portfolio is being transferred); and (3) the existing policyholders of the receiving company. Key areas of consideration include: policyholder benefits and benefit expectations; security of policyholder benefits; and wider treating customers fairly (TCF) issues.

subsections.title: Professional Guidance
bullets: In addition to local regulation, specific professional guidance may apply in respect of health and care insurance in certain jurisdictions.
The Institute and Faculty of Actuaries (IFoA) has issued specific professional guidance that applies to members. This guidance is issued through Actuarial Professional Standards (APSs).

The following APSs apply to certain members: APS L1 (Duties and Responsibilities of Life Assurance Actuaries) — applies to those who are appointed, or who provide support, to Chief Actuaries, Small Insurer Chief Actuaries, With-Profits Actuaries, Appropriate Actuaries, and Reviewing Actuaries, appointed by or in respect of UK authorised insurance companies and friendly societies writing long-term insurance business; APS L2 (The Financial Services and Markets Act 2000 (Communications by Actuaries) Regulations 2003) — applies to Actuarial Function Holders and With-Profits Actuaries appointed by or in respect of UK authorised insurance companies and Appropriate Actuaries of Friendly Societies. Also applies to Chief Actuaries.

Matters that may need to be communicated to regulators under APS L2 include: contravention of legislation by an insurer; significant risk that an insurer's assets may become insufficient to meet liabilities; significant risk that the insurer did not or may not take into account policyholder interests; and inadequacies in the insurer's relationship with the actuary (e.g. provision of information and resources).

Also of relevance to health and care insurance actuaries: APS X1 (Applying Standards to Actuarial Work, effective 1 July 2017) — sets out principles for members to determine which standards they must or should apply to a piece of work, including IFoA members based outside the UK; APS X2 (Review of Actuarial Work) — applies to all members, relates to the need to consider the extent to which review (including independent peer review) is required for any 'actuarial work'; APS X3 (The Actuary as an Expert in Legal Proceedings) — applies to actuaries appointed to act as expert witnesses in legal proceedings.

There may be guidance issued by other professional actuarial bodies in specific jurisdictions, to be followed alongside any IFoA guidance in accordance with APS X1. All IFoA members are also covered by The Actuaries' Code, irrespective of location.
Professional guidance is covered in more detail in Chapter 15.

key_terms.definition: Professional guidance issued by the IFoA that applies to members in specified roles or circumstances. Examples include APS L1, APS L2, APS X1, APS X2, and APS X3.

Duties and Responsibilities of Life Assurance Actuaries — applies to those who support or are appointed as Chief Actuaries, With-Profits Actuaries, Appropriate Actuaries, and Reviewing Actuaries at UK authorised insurance companies writing long-term business.

The Financial Services and Markets Act 2000 (Communications by Actuaries) Regulations 2003 — applies to Actuarial Function Holders, With-Profits Actuaries, Appropriate Actuaries, and Chief Actuaries. Sets out matters that may need to be communicated to regulators.
Applying Standards to Actuarial Work — sets out principles for all IFoA members (including those outside the UK) to determine which standards must or should apply to a piece of actuarial work.
Review of Actuarial Work — applies to all IFoA members. Relates to the need to consider the extent to which review (including independent peer review) is required for any actuarial work.
The Actuary as an Expert in Legal Proceedings — applies to actuaries appointed to act as expert witnesses in legal proceedings before courts, tribunals, or similar bodies.

subsections.title: Consumer Legislation
bullets: In many countries, specific legislation is in place to protect the consumer with regards to what is seen as fair or unfair in contacts for goods and services. A health and care insurance company will need to carefully consider any consumer legislation within the jurisdiction it is selling business to ensure contracts sold are compliant.

Features expected to be in a fair contract: must be drafted so customers can easily understand the terms; cannot contain unfair penalties (e.g. for cancellations — businesses can only recover losses reasonably incurred); must have clearly defined cancellation rights; must have clearly defined and explained charges; must have a clear definition of how the contract may be changed or varied; must provide a clear explanation of how the premium for a policy could be varied.

A term could be deemed 'unfair' if it is contrary to the requirements of good faith and causes a significant imbalance in the parties' rights and obligations to the detriment of the consumer. 'Good faith' exemplifies a general principle of fair and open dealing.

Examples of possible unfair terms include: the company can change the characteristics of the service after the customer has been bound by the contract; the customer is required to pay for services which have not been used when the customer ends the contract; the customer is required to pay disproportionate charges when they end the contract; the company has discretion over the price after the customer is bound; the customer is not allowed to cancel the service under any circumstances.
The body that oversees consumer protection may be independent of any other insurance industry regulator.

key_terms.definition: A term that is contrary to the requirements of good faith and causes a significant imbalance in the parties' rights and obligations to the detriment of the consumer. Such terms are prohibited under consumer protection legislation in many jurisdictions.

subsections.title: Equality Legislation
bullets: In many jurisdictions, anti-discrimination laws have been put in place to cover characteristics which cannot be used as a reason to discriminate against individuals in the provision of goods, facilities, and services.

A health and care insurer may not, by law, be allowed to discriminate directly or indirectly against someone on the basis of a protected characteristic. Examples of possible protected characteristics include: age; disability; gender; gender reassignment; marriage and civil partnership; pregnancy and maternity; race; religion and belief; and sexual orientation.

Direct discrimination is treating someone less favourably because of a protected characteristic. Differential treatment on the grounds of age may not be discrimination if it is shown to be 'a proportionate means of achieving a legitimate aim'. Indirect discrimination occurs when a policy which applies in the same way for everybody particularly disadvantages people with a protected characteristic — this is not discriminatory if it can be justified as a proportionate means of achieving a legitimate aim.

Age: changes brought in by anti-discrimination laws in the UK in 2010 made it unlawful to discriminate against customers on the grounds of age and on the grounds of pregnancy. Insurance prices can still be varied by age where this genuinely reflects risks or costs — age bands are permitted so long as they are based on appropriate data about risks. Age limits are permitted where relevant to risks or costs. Differences in treatment resulting from costs related to pregnancy or maternity are unlawful.

Disability: in the UK, the insurance industry has not been allowed to differentiate on the basis of disability for a number of years. An individual is regarded as disabled if they have a physical or mental impairment and the impairment has a substantial and long-term adverse effect on their ability to carry out normal day-to-day activities. People with HIV, cancer, multiple sclerosis, and some visual impairments are also protected. If an insurer wishes to decline or impose special terms on a disabled person, the adverse decision must be based upon information or data that is relevant to the risk being assessed, from a source upon which it is reasonable to rely, and the decision must be reasonable having regard to that information or data.

Gender: the EU Gender Directive aimed at implementing equal treatment between men and women in access to and supply of goods and services. Initially there was an opt-out for financial and insurance products meeting certain conditions. Following a Court of Justice of the EU decision, this was removed with effect from 21 December 2012 — from that point onwards, insurance companies in the EU have no longer been able to use gender as a rating factor for new business. Reviewable premiums are not treated as new business for this purpose. Care must be taken to avoid the use of proxy rating factors highly correlated to gender, which might be deemed indirect discrimination. The inability to differentiate by gender has significant implications for pricing, particularly for annuities and protection products where there are material observed differences in mortality and morbidity by gender — additional contingency loadings are needed for the risk of the business gender mix not being as expected within the unisex pricing.

key_terms.definition: A characteristic that cannot be used as a reason to discriminate against individuals in the provision of goods, facilities, and services under anti-discrimination legislation. Examples include age, disability, gender, race, religion, and sexual orientation.
Treating someone less favourably because of a protected characteristic. Age-based differential treatment is not discrimination if it is a proportionate means of achieving a legitimate aim.

A policy that applies equally to everyone but particularly disadvantages people with a protected characteristic. Not discriminatory if it can be justified as a proportionate means of achieving a legitimate aim. Applies to all protected characteristics except pregnancy and maternity.

EU regulation aimed at implementing equal treatment between men and women in access to goods and services. Following a Court of Justice of the EU decision, gender can no longer be used as a rating factor for new insurance business in the EU from 21 December 2012.

Insurance pricing that does not differentiate between males and females. Required for new business in the EU following the EU Gender Directive. Additional contingency loadings are needed for the risk that the actual gender mix does not match the assumed mix.

exam_qa.q: What are the pricing implications of the EU Gender Directive for health and care insurance products?
exam_qa.a: Insurers can no longer use gender as a rating factor for new business in the EU. Rather than simply averaging premium rates between males and females, additional contingency loadings are needed for the risk that the business gender mix by gender does not turn out as expected within the unisex pricing. This is particularly significant for annuities and protection products where there are material observed differences in mortality and morbidity by gender. Proxy rating factors that are highly correlated to gender must also be avoided as they could constitute indirect discrimination.

exam_qa.q: When may an insurer lawfully impose special terms on a disabled person?
exam_qa.a: It is lawful to decline or impose special terms on a disabled person for a health and care insurance policy provided that: (1) the adverse decision is based on information or data that is relevant to the assessment of the risk to be insured; (2) it is from a source upon which it is reasonable to rely; and (3) the decision is reasonable having regard to that information or data and any other relevant factors. Special terms cannot be imposed on the basis of disability alone without relevant supporting evidence.

subsections.title: Climate Change
bullets: There is widespread concern among policymakers and financial regulators about the damage that climate change could cause to the financial system and, conversely, the role the financial system can play in achieving an orderly transition to a low carbon economy.

Many regulators are working on regulations whose aims include ensuring that financial institutions: consider climate risks in business decision making and strategic planning; effectively disclose and report on climate-related risks and opportunities; adopt a consistent and reliable means of assessing, pricing, and managing climate-related risks; incorporate ESG (environmental, social and governance) factors into investment management decisions; incorporate financial risks from climate change into existing risk management processes; use scenario analysis to inform risk identification and estimate the impact of financial risks arising from climate change; and consider the impact of climate risks on the ability to meet obligations towards policyholders and other key stakeholders.

Some regulators have issued guidance on understanding and managing the financial risks from climate change. For example, in the UK, climate change was included as a scenario in the PRA's Insurance Stress Test 2019, involving both physical and transition channels, including shocks to asset values varying by sector.
Chapter 6 gives further details about how climate change risk can be categorised into physical, transition, and liability risks.

key_terms.definition: A framework for considering the environmental impact, social responsibility, and governance practices of companies. Regulators increasingly require ESG factors to be incorporated into investment management decisions.

Climate change risk is categorised into three types: physical risk (direct damage from climate events); transition risk (risks arising from the shift to a low-carbon economy, including shocks to asset values); and liability risk (risks from parties seeking compensation for climate-related losses). Covered in more detail in Chapter 6.

subsections.title: Introduction
bullets: This section provides a high level overview of some of the features of the regulatory approach in particular jurisdictions. Specific details are not required knowledge for the SA1 exam — the key is to take away ideas about how regulations could be applied, rather than specific details.
Five different jurisdictions are examined: United Kingdom, United States, China, Australia, and South Africa.

subsections.title: United Kingdom
bullets: In the UK, health and care insurers are regulated by two regulatory bodies: the Prudential Regulation Authority (PRA) and the Financial Conduct Authority (FCA).

The PRA is part of the Bank of England and is responsible for the prudential regulation of all deposit-taking institutions, insurance providers, and large investment firms. Its role is to provide an appropriate degree of policyholder protection and promote resilience against failure — but the PRA's role is not to ensure that no insurer fails.

The FCA is responsible for regulation of conduct in financial markets (and the infrastructure that supports those markets) and the prudential regulation of financial services companies that do not fall under the scope of the PRA (e.g. insurance brokers and smaller investment firms). The FCA acts as a conduct regulator for firms prudentially regulated by the PRA.

Both the PRA and FCA contribute to securing an appropriate degree of policyholder protection through their separate objectives. They have a statutory duty to co-ordinate their activities including policy making and supervision. The FCA adopts a forward-looking approach, seeking to prevent problems before they occur — it is concerned with the product lifecycle right from the design stage and can even ban products where necessary.

The PRA and FCA apply a principles-based approach to regulation in the UK. The 14 principles that a health and care insurance company in the UK would need to comply with include: conducting business with integrity; conducting business with due skill, care and diligence; acting in a prudent manner; observing proper standards of market conduct; maintaining adequate financial resources; having effective risk strategies and risk management systems; organising and controlling affairs responsibly and effectively; paying due regard to customers' interests and treating them fairly; communicating information clearly, fairly, and not misleadingly; managing conflicts of interest fairly; ensuring suitability of advice and discretionary decisions; arranging adequate protection for clients' assets; preparing for resolution in an orderly manner with minimum disruption to critical services; and dealing with regulators in an open and cooperative way.

The PRA and FCA assess the risk that a particular firm, activity, or issue poses to objectives and concentrate supervisory effort on high-risk areas. The PRA works closely with EIOPA and participates in international groups such as the IAIS.

The United Kingdom left the European Union on 1 January 2021. At the time of writing, the UK government is considering reforming capital rules for UK insurers. It is assumed within the Core Reading that Solvency II continues to apply to UK insurers.

key_terms.definition: UK regulator responsible for the prudential regulation of banks, insurance providers, and large investment firms. Part of the Bank of England. Its role is to provide an appropriate degree of policyholder protection and promote resilience to failure — but not to prevent all insurer failures.

UK regulator responsible for conduct regulation in financial markets and the prudential regulation of firms not covered by the PRA (e.g. brokers). Acts as conduct regulator for PRA-regulated firms. Adopts a forward-looking, product lifecycle approach.
European Insurance and Occupational Pensions Authority — the European insurance regulator with which the PRA works closely.

exam_qa.q: What is the difference between the roles of the PRA and FCA in the regulation of UK health and care insurers?
exam_qa.a: The PRA is responsible for prudential regulation — ensuring that insurers maintain adequate capital, manage risk appropriately, and are resilient to failure (though its role is not to prevent all failures). The FCA is responsible for conduct regulation — ensuring that firms act honestly, fairly, and in the interests of consumers, and is concerned with the product lifecycle from design through to sale. Both bodies have a statutory duty to co-ordinate their activities. The FCA also acts as conduct regulator for PRA-regulated firms.

subsections.title: United States
bullets: The United States takes a different approach to insurance regulation compared to the United Kingdom. Each individual state has its own insurance regulator, which supervises insurance companies domiciled in that state.

The work of the individual state regulators is co-ordinated through the National Association of Insurance Commissioners (NAIC). The NAIC provides guidance on the standards which apply to each state and sets out model laws for regulation which has supported significant uniformity of reporting, valuation, and capital requirements across each state. However, the NAIC does not have any power to enforce changes on each state.

There are also two federal entities involved in insurance regulation: the Federal Insurance Office (exists within the US Department of Treasury) which monitors all aspects of the insurance sector and advises on important international and national insurance matters — it does not have a supervisory role; and the Federal Reserve Board, which has consolidated oversight of insurance companies deemed as systemically important.

The US regulatory, legal, and tax framework has generally led to a preference for prescriptive rules and regulations combined with overall asset adequacy analysis, with relatively recent inclusion of certain principles-based requirements. This is covered in more detail in Chapter 13.

key_terms.definition: A US body that co-ordinates the work of individual state insurance regulators. Provides guidance and sets out model laws to support uniformity of reporting, valuation, and capital requirements across states. Does not have the power to enforce changes on individual states.

A US federal entity within the Department of Treasury that monitors all aspects of the insurance sector and advises on important international and national insurance matters. It does not have a supervisory role.

subsections.title: China
bullets: China has a large insurance market but with considerable differences to the UK and USA. The insurance industry in China only reopened in the 1980s and has experienced significantly higher growth than most western countries since then.

The China Banking and Insurance Regulatory Commission (CBIRC) was established in April 2018 by a merger of the China Banking Regulatory Commission (CBRC) and the China Insurance Regulatory Commission (CIRC). The CBIRC is responsible for supervising the establishment and ongoing business activities of banking and insurance institutions and for taking enforcement actions against regulatory violations.

The CBIRC has implemented significant changes to capital requirements, risk management, and transparency disclosures through the introduction of its C-ROSS framework. The C-ROSS framework has similarities to Solvency II, although it is also intended to reflect the specific circumstances of the Chinese insurance market rather than be a direct copy of Solvency II. This is discussed in more detail in Chapter 13.

key_terms.term: CBIRC (China Banking and Insurance Regulatory Commission)
key_terms.definition: The Chinese regulator responsible for supervising the establishment and ongoing business activities of banking and insurance institutions, and for taking enforcement actions against regulatory violations. Established in April 2018 by merging the CBRC and CIRC.

China's Risk-Oriented Solvency System — a capital requirements, risk management, and transparency disclosure framework that has similarities to Solvency II but is tailored to the specific circumstances of the Chinese insurance market.

subsections.title: Australia
bullets: Australia has a large developed financial services market. The Australian Prudential Regulation Authority (APRA) is the prudential regulator of the Australian financial services industry. It oversees banks, building societies, general insurance, reinsurance companies, life insurance, private health insurance, and most of the superannuation (company provided pensions) industry.

APRA was established on 1 July 1998 and is funded largely by the industries it supervises. Its mission is to establish and enforce prudential standards and practices designed to ensure that financial promises made by institutions it supervises are met within a stable, efficient, and competitive financial system.

APRA takes a risk-based approach to supervision designed to identify and assess those areas of greatest risk to a regulated entity (or to the financial system as a whole) and then apply its supervisory resources and attention to these risks in a targeted and cost-effective manner.

Limitations were identified with Australia's regulatory regime regarding risk-based capital, risk management, and disclosure requirements. Therefore APRA developed the Life and General Insurance Capital (LAGIC) standards. LAGIC has strong parallels to Solvency II and follows a similar three-pillar structure. LAGIC was implemented on 1 January 2013.

key_terms.definition: The prudential regulator of the Australian financial services industry, covering banks, insurers, and superannuation funds. Takes a risk-based approach to supervision. Established 1 July 1998.
Australian capital standards developed by APRA with strong parallels to Solvency II, following a similar three-pillar structure. Implemented on 1 January 2013.
The term used in Australia for company-provided pensions.

subsections.title: South Africa
bullets: South Africa has a large developed financial services market. Following the global financial crisis, the Financial Services Board (FSB) launched a project to establish a risk-based solvency framework for prudential regulation of life and non-life insurers in South Africa — the Solvency Assessment and Management (SAM) framework. The SAM framework was implemented as at 1 July 2018.

The SAM reforms were part of a comprehensive overhaul of existing financial sector legislation in South Africa, reflecting a shift to a 'Twin Peaks' model of financial regulation — separating prudential regulation from market conduct regulation.

From 1 April 2018 the FSB was split into: the Prudential Authority (PA) — responsible for regulating a wide range of financial institutions including banks, insurance companies, financial conglomerates, and financial market infrastructure. PA functions include licensing, ongoing supervision, and enforcement; and the Financial Sector Conduct Authority (FSCA) — responsible for market conduct regulation and supervision. It aims to enhance the efficiency and integrity of financial markets, promote fair customer treatment, provide financial education, promote financial literacy, and assist in maintaining financial stability.
So, one regulator takes responsibility for prudential regulation (the PA in South Africa, the PRA in the UK) and another for market conduct (the FSCA in South Africa, the FCA in the UK).

key_terms.definition: South Africa's risk-based solvency framework for prudential regulation of life and non-life insurers, implemented on 1 July 2018. Part of a broader Twin Peaks model reform.

A model of financial regulation that separates prudential regulation (ensuring financial soundness) from market conduct regulation (ensuring fair treatment of customers). Used in both South Africa and the UK.
The South African prudential regulator of banks, insurance companies, financial conglomerates, and financial market infrastructure. Responsible for licensing, ongoing supervision, and enforcement.
The South African market conduct regulator responsible for enhancing the efficiency and integrity of financial markets and promoting fair customer treatment.

10.summary:
Regulatory objectives
• Policyholder protection and financial stability.
• Changes driven by the economic environment, harmonization across jurisdictions and advances in risk management.
• Insurers need to comply with local regulations and any supra-national regulators.

Regulatory responsibilities

Regulators normally produce rulebooks setting out rules / principles. Additional guidance may also be provided. Regulation may cover:
• Authorisation – may be required for individual jurisdictions or granted as part of a wider economic area.
• Business standards – affect day-to-day business, may cover senior management systems and controls (eg compliance, risk management, outsourcing, regulatory processes) and market conduct (requirements to act honestly, fairly and professionally eg around communication with clients, information disclosure to customers, product information, complaints)
• Supervisory reporting – specify information and frequency of reporting, may include balance sheet, revenue account, capital requirements (and assumptions / model), details of new business, forward looking analysis, stress / scenario testing
• Monitoring – regular assessment visits, review of reports discussed at governance meetings, review of regulatory reports
• Valuation and capital requirements – rules / principles around the valuation of assets and liabilities, assumptions, reinsurance and capital requirements
• Fit and proper person – specify rules by which those in senior / statutory actuarial roles need to operate, eg Chief Actuary may be responsible for ensuring technical provisions, appropriate methodology / assumptions etc, requirements on the insurer to keep the actuary informed, pay due regard to their advice and provide them with adequate resources
• Actions taken by regulators – may be able to require insurer to remedy situation, impose fines, require reinsurance to be purchased, increase capital requirements etc, some groups may be viewed as too large to fail (global systematically important insurers, G-SIIs) are subject to enhanced supervision
• Transfer of surplus – regulations around how much an insurer can transfer out of insurance fund (eg to pay dividends), may require surplus to be held in liquid assets
• Transfer of liabilities between insurers – may require Court and regulatory approval, independent expert may review impact on policyholders (in terms of benefits and TCF), may require scheme to be publicised to policyholder / shareholders.

Other legislation and guidance

Professional guidance

The IFoA has issued the following Actuarial Profession Standards:
• APS L1 (‘Duties and responsibilities of life assurance actuaries’)
• APS L2 (‘The Financial Services and Markets Act 2000 (Communications by Actuaries) Regulations 2003’)
• APS X1 – Applying Standards to Actuarial Work
• APS X2 – Review of Actuarial Work
• APS X3 – The Actuary as an Expert in Legal Proceedings
• APS QA1 – Quality Assurance Scheme for Organisations.

Consumer legislation – May be specific legislation around what is a fair contract to avoid a significant imbalance.

Equality legislation – Discrimination based on factors such as age, disability, gender and pregnancy may be unlawful. Can cause issues when estimating the mix of business.

Climate change – Regulators are working on regulations that aim to limit the impact of climate change on financial systems.

Regulatory approach by jurisdiction

United Kingdom
• Prudential Regulation Authority (PRA) – responsible for the prudential regulation of insurers to provide policyholder protection and promote resilience to failure (but not prevent failure).
• Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) – responsible for market conduct within the insurance industry.

United States
• State regulation co-ordinated by the National Association of Insurance Commissioners (NAIC), sets out model laws to encourage uniformity of reporting, valuation and capital requirements across states.
• At a federal level: Federal Insurance Office – monitors all aspects of the insurance sectors, advises on important international and national insurance matters; Federal Reserve Board – consolidated oversight of systematically important insurers.

China
• China Banking and Insurance Regulatory Commission – covers establishment and ongoing business activities of banking and insurers, including enforcement against regulatory violations. Implemented changes around capital requirements, risk management and disclosure transparency through C-ROSS framework.

Australia
• Australian Prudential Regulation Authority – takes a risk-based approach to help identify areas of greatest risk to target in a cost effective manner. Developed Life and General Insurance Capital standards aligned to Solvency II in the EU.

South Africa
• Regulation split between the Prudential Authority and the Financial Sector Conduct Authority.
• The Solvency Assessment Management framework is a risk-based approach to prudential regulation.

11.title: Solvency II (1)

subsections.title: Overview
bullets: Chapter 11 covers the overview of Solvency II: Pillars 2 and 3, plus group reporting, statutory actuarial roles and impact on business culture and strategy. Pillar 1 is covered in Chapter 12.
All information in Chapters 11 and 12 is current as at May 2023; the Solvency II regulations continue to evolve.
The PRA has a dedicated Solvency II area (bankofengland.co.uk/prudential-regulation/key-initiatives/solvency-ii) which is an excellent and reliable source of information.
In 2021, the PRA began consulting on potential changes to how Solvency II is applied in the UK (the 'Solvency UK' project), in particular the matching adjustment.

subsections.title: Background and Scope
bullets: Solvency II is a system for insurance regulation applying to insurance firms in the EU. Its main objective is the adequate protection of policyholders and beneficiaries; it sets out a risk-based and harmonised EU-wide approach to the assessment of capital adequacy and to risk management and reporting for insurers.

Prior to Solvency II, insurance regulation was done at a national level across EU countries, with often very different approaches in different member states. Greater harmonisation was intended to lead to greater competition in insurance markets.
The Solvency II Directive applies to all EU insurance and reinsurance companies with gross premium income exceeding €5 million or gross technical provisions in excess of €25 million.
Solvency II became operative from 1 January 2016.
EIOPA (the European Insurance and Occupational Pensions Authority) provides technical advice and support to the European Commission for the development of Solvency II.

Transitional arrangements from the previous regulatory regime to Solvency II are available for some aspects (e.g. technical provisions, risk-free interest rates) for a defined period of up to 16 years, to avoid unnecessary disruption and maintain availability of insurance products.

Whilst Solvency II applies to countries in the EU, it has influenced insurance regulation in many other jurisdictions around the world, with a number of non-EU countries introducing aspects of 'Solvency II' type regulatory requirements.

key_terms.definition: A risk-based and harmonised EU-wide regulatory framework for insurance firms, focused on the adequate protection of policyholders and beneficiaries, covering capital adequacy, risk management and reporting. Operative from 1 January 2016.

European Insurance and Occupational Pensions Authority — the body of European supervisors that provides technical advice and support to the European Commission for the development of Solvency II. The PRA is the UK representative in EIOPA.

subsections.title: Structure — The Three-Pillar Framework
bullets: The Solvency II framework comprises three 'pillars': Pillar 1 (Quantitative requirements), Pillar 2 (Qualitative requirements and supervisory review), and Pillar 3 (Reporting, disclosure and market discipline).

Pillar 1 sets out the minimum capital requirements that firms must meet and specifies valuation methodologies for assets and liabilities ('technical provisions') based on market-consistent principles. Under Pillar 1 there are two distinct capital requirements: the Solvency Capital Requirement (SCR) and the Minimum Capital Requirement (MCR). The SCR can be calculated using a prescribed standard formula or a company-specific internal model approved by the national regulator. Supervisors may also impose a capital add-on.
Pillar 2 includes the supervisory review process, systems of governance and risk management. Each insurance company is also required to carry out an Own Risk and Solvency Assessment (ORSA).
Pillar 3 is the disclosure and supervisory reporting regime, under which defined reports to regulators and the public are required to be made.
Pillars 2 and 3 are often together referred to as 'Pillar 5' due to the synergies between them (e.g. the ORSA from Pillar 2 is one of the Pillar 3 defined reports to the regulator).

The combination of minimum capital standards, qualitative risk management requirements, a rigorous review process of companies' solvency by supervisors, and prescribed disclosures has been designed to deliver a more modern and secure prudential regulatory system.

key_terms.definition: An amount of capital that an insurance company must hold in addition to its technical provisions. Can be calculated using a prescribed standard formula or a company-specific internal model approved by the regulator.

An amount of capital that an insurance company must hold in addition to its technical provisions. The MCR is lower than the SCR and is the ultimate point of supervisory intervention — the point at which an insurance company would lose its authorisation.

Defined by EIOPA as 'the entirety of the processes and procedures employed to identify, assess, monitor, manage and report the short- and long-term risks an insurance undertaking faces or may face and to determine the own funds necessary to ensure that the undertaking's overall solvency needs are met at all times.' It is part of the risk management framework and should be used by senior management in strategic decisions.

exam_qa.q: Why does Pillar 3 require an insurance company to make public disclosures to the market?
exam_qa.a: The market discipline of public disclosures is intended to add an additional degree of confidence in the regime. A company's public disclosures enable other stakeholders (e.g. capital markets and rating agencies) to form an informed view on a company's capital and risk management. The discipline this imposes on a company (e.g. in wishing to maintain its share price or achieve a certain credit rating) should support the supervisory process and reporting.

subsections.title: Governance Requirements
bullets: Pillar 2 sets out requirements for the roles and responsibilities of key functions within the business, with the Board having overall responsibility for ongoing compliance with Solvency II.
All insurance companies are required to have: a risk management function; an actuarial function; a compliance function; and an internal audit function.

The organisational structure must have clear segregation of responsibilities — for example, persons carrying out the internal audit function must not assume responsibility for any of the other functions.

The actuarial function is primarily responsible for the coordination of the calculation of the technical provisions. It must ensure methodologies and assumptions used are appropriate, state whether there are enough premiums to cover future claims and expenses, and provide an opinion on the company's underwriting policy and the adequacy of its reinsurance arrangements.
If the company uses an internal model to determine its SCR, the responsibility for the detailed implementation of the model lies with the risk management function rather than the actuarial function.

key_terms.definition: The Solvency II function primarily responsible for coordination of the calculation of technical provisions, ensuring appropriateness of methodologies and assumptions, opining on underwriting policy and adequacy of reinsurance arrangements.
The Solvency II function responsible for the risk management framework; also responsible for the detailed implementation of an internal model (if used), rather than the actuarial function.

subsections.title: Own Risk and Solvency Assessment (ORSA)
bullets: In addition to calculating the MCR and SCR under Pillar 1, each insurance company is required to carry out an ORSA.

Risk assessment: The ORSA requires each insurance company to identify all the risks to which it is subject and related risk management processes and controls. This includes qualitative risks not necessarily assessed under Pillar 1, such as reputational risk.

Solvency assessment: The company must quantify its ability to continue to meet the MCR and SCR over the business planning horizon (usually three to five years), allowing for new business. This does not have to be at a prescribed confidence level but at a level the company feels is appropriate (e.g. relating to its own stated risk appetite or to achieving a target credit rating).

The ORSA is one of the elements considered by the supervisor when determining whether a further capital 'add-on' is required. Capital add-ons are added to the SCR and should be used only in exceptional circumstances.

Insurance companies must produce evidence to the supervisor showing that the ORSA is used by senior management, including in making strategic decisions. This 'embeddedness' of the ORSA has been stressed by the national regulator.

There is relatively little prescriptive guidance about the ORSA, which allows scope for companies to build a process that genuinely fits their business and is integral to their strategic planning, rather than being a regulatory 'tickbox' requirement.

key_terms.definition: An additional capital requirement added to the SCR by the supervisor. Used only in exceptional circumstances, e.g. where the risk profile of a company results in the standard formula for the SCR being inadequate and an internal model has not been developed to reflect this.

The requirement that the ORSA is genuinely used by senior management as a key part of the decision-making process, and that companies can demonstrate this use — not merely produced as a regulatory compliance exercise.

exam_qa.q: Onto which quantity does any capital add-on get added?
exam_qa.a: The capital add-on is added to the Solvency Capital Requirement (SCR).

subsections.title: Disclosure and Reporting Requirements
bullets: The disclosure requirements are intended to increase transparency and so are more extensive than under the previous Solvency I reporting regime.

Private supervisory reporting: The results of the solvency calculation, details of the ORSA and risk management processes must be disclosed privately to the supervisor in the Regular Supervisory Report (RSR), which includes both qualitative information and Quantitative Reporting Templates (QRT). The RSR must contain certain pre-defined sections (e.g. the ORSA will form part of the RSR). The RSR including QRT must be submitted annually, although under certain conditions a summary (material changes) RSR is acceptable. A subset of the QRT (to support the MCR calculation) is required quarterly.

The annual QRT will cover: the balance sheet; premiums, claims and expenses by line of business; own funds; capital requirements (SCR and MCR); assets; collective investment undertakings; derivatives; technical provisions (including ring-fenced assets and any matching adjustment applied); group reporting; and any reinsurance undertakings.
The quarterly QRT covers the MCR and SCR and some information on assets, technical provisions and own funds.

Public disclosure: Except for certain items that can be demonstrated to be of a confidential nature, extracts from the QRT and some qualitative information from the RSR are also disclosed in a public Solvency and Financial Condition Report (SFCR), produced annually. The SFCR must be published and a copy provided to the local regulator within 3 to 4 months of a company's year-end.
Local regulators are permitted to impose additional reporting requirements on insurance companies in the form of 'national specific templates'.

key_terms.definition: A private report from the insurance company to the supervisor, containing both qualitative information and the Quantitative Reporting Templates (QRT). Must be submitted annually; must contain certain pre-defined sections including the ORSA.
The quantitative parts of the private reporting from the insurance company to the supervisor. Must be submitted annually (with a subset submitted quarterly to support the MCR calculation).
The annual public report providing a company's Pillar 3 disclosures. Must be published and a copy provided to the local regulator within 3 to 4 months of the company's year-end.

subsections.title: Group Reporting Requirements
bullets: Solvency II aims to enable insurance groups to be supervised more efficiently through a 'group supervisor' in the home country, co-operating with other relevant national supervisors. This ensures group-wide risks are not overlooked and enables groups to operate more effectively whilst continuing to provide policyholder protection.

Solvency II also aims to address the double use of capital within an insurance group (e.g. where regulated entities make subordinated loans to each other) and double leverage (where a parent raises debt which is then used to fund an investment in a regulated subsidiary).

Each insurance group must cover its overall group SCR (which allows for diversification benefits across the group) as well as its group solvency floor (calculated as the sum of the MCRs, or local equivalent, for each insurance or reinsurance entity within the group). Each insurance subsidiary also needs to cover its own SCR and MCR.

Group supervision is normally carried out at the top level company, which may be within the EEA or in a 'third country' (non-EEA). Solvency II requires that where there is a third country parent, EEA supervisors must assess whether the third country parent is subject to 'equivalent' group supervision.

Equivalence: If an aspect of a third country regulatory regime is considered to be broadly compliant with Solvency II, that aspect can be said to be 'equivalent'. Equivalence can be 'full' (indefinite period), 'temporary' (until 31/12/2020 with possible 1-year extension) or 'provisional' (ten-year period with possible extension). For example, Bermuda and Switzerland have been granted full equivalence; Japan, Australia, Brazil, Canada, Mexico and the USA have been granted provisional equivalence.

key_terms.definition: Calculated as the sum of the MCRs (or local equivalent) for each insurance or reinsurance entity within the group. The insurance group must cover both its overall group SCR and this group solvency floor.

A determination that an aspect of a third country regulatory regime is broadly compliant with Solvency II. Can be 'full' (indefinite), 'temporary' (limited period) or 'provisional' (ten-year period with possible extension). Examples: Bermuda and Switzerland have full equivalence; USA, Australia and others have provisional equivalence.
A group-wide risk where regulated entities within a group make subordinated loans to each other, potentially inflating available capital. Solvency II group supervision aims to address this.

Where a parent company raises debt which is then used to fund an investment in a regulated subsidiary, potentially inflating the subsidiary's capital position. Addressed by Solvency II group supervision.

subsections.title: Impact on Business Culture and Strategy
bullets: Engagement with Solvency II is important throughout the business, including right up to senior management and Board level — for all insurance companies, not just those opting to use an internal model.
Solvency II is not just a reporting framework, but a risk management framework with implications for capital allocation, risk mitigation activities and performance management.
The regime may also have an impact on the optimal product mix for the company, and on product design.
It is likely to impact the optimal asset mix, since some asset classes have become relatively more attractive as a result of their lower capital requirements.
The availability, or otherwise, of risk diversification benefits may affect corporate structures and generate merger and acquisition activity.
Management information has changed to align Solvency II metrics with the business and strategic decision-making process.
The impact on the market of the external disclosures also needs to be considered.

subsections.title: Statutory Actuarial Roles under Solvency II
bullets: Under Solvency II there are particular statutory roles and responsibilities for the Chief Actuary.

The main responsibilities of the Chief Actuary under Solvency II are to: co-ordinate the calculation of the technical provisions; ensure the appropriateness of the methodologies and underlying models used, as well as the assumptions made in the calculation of technical provisions; assess the sufficiency and quality of data used in the calculation of technical provisions; compare the best estimate against experience; inform the governing body of the reliability and adequacy of the calculation of technical provisions; express an opinion on the overall underwriting policy; express an opinion on the adequacy of reinsurance arrangements; contribute to the effective implementation of the risk management system, with particular regard to risk modelling by the firm.

key_terms.definition: The statutory actuarial role under Solvency II responsible for coordinating technical provisions calculations, opining on underwriting policy and reinsurance adequacy, comparing best estimates against experience, and contributing to the risk management system.

11.summary:
Overview
• The Solvency II framework comprises three pillars: Pillar 1: Quantitative requirements; Pillar 2: Qualitative requirements and supervisory review; Pillar 3: Reporting, disclosure and market discipline.

Pillar 2
• The Board has overall responsibility for ongoing compliance with Solvency II.
• All insurance companies required to have a: risk management function; actuarial function; compliance function; internal audit function.
• Insurers must carry out an Own Risk and Solvency Assessment (ORSA), requires: identification of all risks (including qualitative risks) and related risk management processes and controls; quantification of ability to continue to meet MCR and SCR, allowing for new business; considered for strategic decisions.

Pillar 3
• Results of the solvency calculation, ORSA and risk management processes disclosed to the supervisor in the: Regular Supervisory Report (RSR), submitted annually; Quantitative Reporting Templates (QRT), submitted annually (with a subset quarterly).
• Public Solvency and Financial Condition Report (SFCR) produced annually.

Group reporting
• Each insurance group must cover its overall group SCR and group solvency floor. Each insurance subsidiary needs to cover its own SCR and MCR.
• Group supervision normally carried out at the top level company within the EEA. Additional rules apply to subsidiaries and parents located in a ‘third country’, ie non-EEA, depending on whether the country is equivalent or not.

Impact on business culture and strategy
• Engagement with Solvency II important throughout the business, up to senior management and Board level.
• Solvency II affects almost all activities, including: risk management and capital allocation; product mix and product design; asset mix; corporate structure; management information; external disclosures.

12.title: Solvency II (2)

subsections.title: Overview
bullets: Chapter 12 completes the coverage of Solvency II by considering Pillar 1 in greater detail. Sections 0 and 2 cover the valuation of assets and liabilities (technical provisions) under Pillar 1. Later sections look at how the required capital measures (SCR and MCR) are calculated and how the available capital to cover these requirements is assessed.

The Solvency II balance sheet for a health and care insurer comprises: on the liabilities side — best estimate liability, risk margin (together forming technical provisions), MCR, SCR, surplus, and other liabilities; on the assets side — assets covering technical provisions, other liabilities, MCR and SCR; own funds consist of basic own funds and ancillary own funds (with ineligible capital excluded).

subsections.title: Prudent Person Principle
bullets: Assets in the Solvency II balance sheet have three component parts: investments (e.g. equities, bonds, property); reinsurance recoveries; and participations (companies in which the insurance company owns at least 20% of the voting rights).

For non-linked assets, Solvency II uses a principles-based test known as the 'prudent person principle', developed from a legal concept requiring advisers only to make investment decisions that a 'prudent person' would make.

The prudent person principle requires: insurance companies to develop their own set of key risk indicators for investment decisions, rather than depending solely on risk assessments by third parties (including asset managers and credit rating agencies); companies to assess their ability to manage non-standard asset classes before investing; strong monitoring and control procedures for investments not traded on a regulated market.

The 'prudent person principle' requires, amongst other things: the assets to be appropriate for the nature and term of the liabilities; assets to be chosen in the best interests of policyholders; derivatives to be used only for reducing risk or efficient portfolio management; assets to be 'properly diversified' (e.g. by asset class, issuer, geographical area).

key_terms.definition: A principles-based test used by Solvency II for non-linked assets. Requires insurance companies to develop their own key risk indicators for investment decisions, rather than relying solely on third-party risk assessments. Assets must be appropriate for the nature and term of liabilities, chosen in the best interests of policyholders, and properly diversified.
Companies in which the insurance company owns at least 20% of the voting rights. Shown as an asset on the Solvency II balance sheet, valued using a market-consistent approach.

subsections.title: Method of Valuation
bullets: Investments: Assets are required to be valued at market value, based on readily available market prices in orderly transactions sourced independently (i.e. quoted market prices in active markets). If such prices are not available, mark-to-model techniques can be used — provided these are consistent with the overall market-consistent (or 'fair value' or 'economic value') approach. Unquoted equities, unquoted derivatives and property are examples of assets for which mark-to-model techniques may be required.
This was a significant change for much of Europe, where book values (i.e. original cost, possibly with depreciation) were often still used under previous regulatory regimes.

Reinsurance recoveries: Recoveries expected from reinsurance are shown as an asset on the balance sheet, rather than as a reduction in gross liabilities. The fair value principle applies; recoveries must be adjusted for the best estimate of expected losses due to reinsurer default (probability of reinsurer default is normally considered low, depending on the credit rating and security of the reinsurer).
Participations: The overall market-consistent approach continues to apply. Participations mainly arise when considering groups or where companies have outsourcing subsidiaries.

key_terms.definition: A valuation technique used for assets where quoted market prices in active markets are not available. Must be consistent with the overall market-consistent (fair value / economic value) approach — the amount at which assets could be exchanged between knowledgeable willing parties in an arm's length transaction.

Expected recoveries from reinsurance shown as an asset on the Solvency II balance sheet (rather than a reduction in gross liabilities). Adjusted for the best estimate of expected losses due to reinsurer default.

subsections.title: Overview of Technical Provisions
bullets: Based on a market-consistent approach, technical provisions should represent the amount that the insurance company would have to pay in order to transfer its obligations immediately to another insurance company.

Technical provisions consist of a best estimate liability (BEL) and a risk margin. For hedgeable cashflows (those which can be replicated by a financial instrument with a reliable market value), the value of the technical provision equals the market value of the hedging instrument — no separate BEL and risk margin calculation is required.
The technical provision calculation should be segmented by homogeneous product type.

Solvency II defines an 'SLT health' contract as one deemed to be written on a similar technical basis to life insurance (long-term business); 'Non-SLT health' is technically similar to non-life insurance (short-term business). SLT health covers income protection, stand-alone critical illness and long-term care. Non-SLT health covers products such as private medical insurance (PMI).

For a SLT health contract, it may be necessary to unbundle the technical provisions calculations into a health part and a non-health part where technically feasible and where both parts are material. Accelerated critical illness cover is likely to be designated as a life insurance obligation.

key_terms.definition: Similar to Life Techniques — a Solvency II health contract deemed to be written on a similar technical basis to life insurance (long-term business). Covers income protection (IP), stand-alone critical illness (SACI) and long-term care (LTC).
A Solvency II health contract technically similar to non-life insurance (short-term business). Covers products such as private medical insurance (PMI).

The market-consistent value of the insurer's insurance obligations — the amount the insurer would pay to transfer its obligations immediately to another insurer. Comprises the best estimate liability (BEL) plus the risk margin.

subsections.title: Best Estimate Liability (BEL)
bullets: The BEL is the present value of expected future cashflows, discounted using a 'risk-free' yield curve (i.e. term-dependent rates). No prudential margins — all assumptions should be best estimate.

Cashflow projections should ideally be performed on a policy-by-policy basis; approximations and grouped model points can be used provided certain conditions are met (including validation of accuracy).
For unit-linked business, the unit and non-unit components must be unbundled for the purposes of determining technical provisions.
It is possible for the BEL to be negative (there is no 'floor' of zero) and negative non-unit reserves can be held.

Assumptions: all should be best estimate, with no prudential margins. Projections should allow for all expected decrements and policyholder actions including lapses. Policyholder behaviour assumptions should reflect that policyholders are likely to behave differently in different circumstances (e.g. extent to which an option is in-the-money, policyholder awareness, financial position of the company).

Insurance companies must take into account all relevant available data, both internal and external, when arriving at assumptions that best reflect the characteristics of the underlying insurance portfolio.

Future premiums can be taken into account up to the 'contract boundary', broadly defined as the point at which the company can unilaterally terminate the contract, refuse to accept a premium or change premiums/benefits so that they fully reflect the risks. For long-term health business, this normally means the maturity/expiry date; for short-term annually renewable health business, it means to the end of the policy year.
Allowance for future expenses must include both overheads and directly attributable expenses, and future expense inflation. No closure reserve is required.

For some liabilities including financial guarantees and options, a market-consistent simulation or stochastic analysis is likely to be the most appropriate calculation approach, although a deterministic 'closed form' solution could be acceptable depending on the risks involved and the materiality.

key_terms.definition: The present value of expected future cashflows (benefits and expenses less premiums), discounted using a risk-free yield curve. Calculated using best estimate assumptions with no prudential margins.

The point at which a company can unilaterally terminate a contract, refuse to accept a premium, or change premiums/benefits so they fully reflect the risks. Future premiums can only be taken into account within the contract boundary when calculating the BEL.

exam_qa.q: What circumstances may cause the rate of policyholder exercise of an option to change?
exam_qa.a: Possible circumstances include: the extent to which the option is in-the-money or out-of-the-money; policyholder awareness of the option (e.g. from company communications or media coverage); changes in the financial position of the company offering the option.

subsections.title: Risk-Free Discount Rates
bullets: The BEL is calculated by discounting expected future cashflows using a risk-free yield curve. The risk-free discount rates are published by EIOPA on a monthly basis — insurance companies are not able to set their own discount rate assumptions.

Risk-free yield curves are published for each of the key currencies within the EU insurance market, ensuring consistency between insurance companies across the EU and allowing for different interest rate environments across different economies. EIOPA publishes yield curves for 53 different countries (all EEA countries plus material non-EEA countries such as Australia, China, India and the USA).

The rates are based on swap rates where there is a sufficiently deep and liquid swap market, or government bond rates otherwise. These rates are then adjusted by EIOPA to reflect the risk of default of the counterparty (i.e. credit risk adjustment), applied as a downward shift to observed market yields.

For longer maturities where data are not available, the yield curve is extrapolated to a defined long-term equilibrium rate using the Smith-Wilson method. Swap rates are available for up to 50 years for some markets (e.g. the UK).

Matching adjustment: Where insurers have long-term predictable liabilities and can hold matching assets to maturity, they are not exposed to the liquidity component of spread risk. In such cases, insurers are allowed to adjust the risk-free discount rate in line with the spread movements of their assets. The matching adjustment is derived by taking the spread on the portfolio of matching assets and deducting the 'fundamental spread' (an allowance for the credit risks retained by the insurer). This must be approved by the regulator; strict requirements apply to eligibility of assets and liabilities (assets must be ring-fenced, bond-like and held until redemption; liability risks must relate to longevity and expenses with no surrender risk). Failure to meet these requirements removes the right to use the matching adjustment for 24 months unless compliance is restored within 2 months.

Volatility adjustment: Where insurers have liabilities not eligible for the matching adjustment, they can alternatively add a 'volatility adjustment' to the risk-free discount rate. The purpose is to reduce the risk of forced sales of assets in the event of extreme bond spread movements. It is based on the spreads on a representative portfolio of assets for each relevant currency (or national insurance market). Risk-free discount curves including the volatility adjustment are also published by EIOPA. Use of the volatility adjustment is subject to certain risk management requirements (e.g. a liquidity plan and sensitivity analysis) and in some EU member states its use is subject to prior supervisory approval.

key_terms.definition: The rate used to discount BEL cashflows, published monthly by EIOPA. Based on swap rates (or government bond rates) adjusted for counterparty default risk. Extrapolated to a long-term equilibrium rate using the Smith-Wilson method for longer maturities.

An addition to the risk-free discount rate permitted for insurers with long-term predictable liabilities who hold matching assets to maturity. Derived as the spread on matching assets less the 'fundamental spread' (allowance for retained credit risk). Must be approved by the regulator; strict asset and liability eligibility requirements apply.
An allowance for the credit risks retained by the insurer when calculating the matching adjustment. Published by EIOPA. The matching adjustment equals the asset spread less the fundamental spread.
