An addition to the risk-free discount rate permitted for insurers whose liabilities are not eligible for the matching adjustment. Purpose is to reduce the risk of forced asset sales in times of extreme bond spread movements. Based on spreads on a representative portfolio of assets for each relevant currency or national insurance market.
The extrapolation method used by EIOPA to extend the risk-free yield curve to a defined long-term equilibrium rate for maturities where market data are not available.

subsections.title: Risk Margin
bullets: The risk margin is intended to increase the technical provisions to the amount that would have to be paid to another insurance company in order for them to take on the best estimate liability. It therefore represents the theoretical compensation for the risk of future experience being worse than the best estimate assumptions, and for the cost of holding regulatory capital against this.

The risk margin is determined using the 'cost of capital' method — based on the cost of holding capital to support those risks that cannot be hedged. These include all insurance risk, reinsurance credit risk, operational risk and 'residual market risk' (e.g. where the duration of liability cashflows is longer than the duration of available risk-free assets).

The cost of capital method involves three steps: (1) project forward the future capital the company is required to hold at the end of each projection period (e.g. year) during the run-off of the existing business — for Solvency II, the projected capital requirement is a subset of the SCR consisting of those risks that cannot be hedged in financial markets; (2) multiply these projected capital amounts by a cost of capital rate — for Solvency II this is a fixed rate of 6% per annum; (3) discount the product of the cost of capital rate and the capital requirement at each future projection point, using risk-free discount rates, to give the overall risk margin.

Since the projection of the SCR is potentially complex, various simplified approaches can be used (e.g. selecting a driver such as reserves or sum at risk which has an approximately linear relationship with the required capital, and projecting capital as the same percentage of that driver).
Although the risk margin must be disclosed separately for each line of business, it can be reduced to take into account diversification between lines up to legal entity level.

The UK changes to Solvency II also propose reducing the risk margin required to be held, which could be achieved by reducing the fixed cost of capital rate from 6% or modifying the way the cost of capital rate is determined.

key_terms.definition: The addition to the BEL that brings technical provisions up to the transfer value — the amount another insurer would require to take on the BEL. Calculated using the cost of capital method: projected future SCR (for non-hedgeable risks) multiplied by a 6% per annum cost of capital rate, discounted at risk-free rates.

The method used to calculate the risk margin under Solvency II. Projects future non-hedgeable capital requirements, multiplies by a cost of capital rate (6% p.a. under Solvency II), and discounts at risk-free rates to give the overall risk margin.
A component of risk included in the SCR subset used for risk margin calculations. Arises when the duration of liability cashflows exceeds the duration of available risk-free assets.

exam_qa.q: A health insurer sells only critical illness. At t=0, SCR = £10m and the sum at risk is £100m. Using the simplified driver approach, estimate the SCR at each year-end given projected sums at risk of £90m, £85m, £82m, £80m and £0m at t=1 through t=5.
exam_qa.a: At t=0, the SCR is 10% of the sum at risk. Assuming this proportion continues throughout run-off: SCR at t=1 = £9.0m, t=2 = £8.5m, t=3 = £8.2m, t=4 = £8.0m, t=5 = £0m.

subsections.title: Overview of the SCR
bullets: The SCR is a Value at Risk measure based on a 99.5% confidence interval of the variation over one year of the amount of 'basic own funds' (broadly assets minus technical provisions).

There is a prescribed list of risk groups that the SCR must cover: non-life underwriting risk; life underwriting risk; health underwriting risk; market risk; counterparty default risk; operational risk.

The SCR can be calculated using standard prescribed stress tests or factors, aggregated using prescribed correlation matrices — this approach is known as the standard formula. Alternatively it can be calculated using an internal model approved by the insurance company's supervisory authority.

Firms choosing to use the standard formula are expected to be able to justify that it is appropriate for their risk profile. If the firm is unable to convince the regulator that the standard formula is appropriate, the regulator could force it to use an internal model.
A combination of internal model and standard formula can also be adopted — this is referred to as a partial internal model.
Simplifications can be applied, provided they are proportionate to the nature, scale and complexity of the risks.

The national regulator has the power to require a capital add-on to be held in excess of the SCR calculated by the insurance company — for example where some aspect of the company's risk profile differs from the assumptions underlying the standard formula and the risk is not being internally modelled.

key_terms.definition: The prescribed approach for calculating the SCR using standard stress tests or factors for each risk module, aggregated using prescribed correlation matrices. Firms must be able to justify that the standard formula is appropriate for their risk profile.
A combination of internal model and standard formula approaches for calculating the SCR. Can be used for particular risk modules, sub-modules, or certain business units.

Company-specific parameters that can replace the prescribed parameters in certain parts of the standard formula (specifically the underwriting risk modules), with regulatory approval. May be used if they reflect the company's risk profile better than the standard parameterisation.

Funds with limitations on capital fungibility (restrictions on the extent to which capital can readily be transferred between funds). Additional constraints apply to the SCR calculation where ring-fenced funds exist.

subsections.title: Standard Formula
bullets: The Basic SCR (BSCR) is calculated by considering different modules of risks: market (equity, property, interest rate, credit spread, currency and concentration); counterparty default; insurance (separately for life, health and non-life business); intangible assets.

For health insurance business (Subject SA1), the insurance risk sub-module comprises three components: SLT health (disability/morbidity, mortality, longevity, lapse, expenses and revision risks); non-SLT health (premium and reserve risks, and lapse risk); CAT (health catastrophe risk — extreme events including epidemics, pandemics, climate change, anti-microbial resistance).

The intangible asset risk covers the risk that the value of intangible assets (e.g. goodwill and brand names) decreases (perhaps to zero), for example following deterioration in the public image of a company.

Revision risk is the risk of adverse variation in the amount of a reviewable annuity (e.g. under an income protection claim where the benefit can vary), due to changes in the legal environment or the state of health of the insured.

Standard formula calculation: The SCR is first calculated for each module. For each individual risk (stress), it is determined as the difference between the net asset value (assets less best estimate liabilities) in the unstressed balance sheet and the net asset value in the stressed balance sheet. SCRi is subject to a minimum of 0. The individual risk capital amounts are then combined using a specified correlation matrix.

The SLT health module SCR uses individual stresses (e.g. 35% increase in disability rates for the following year together with a permanent 25% increase thereafter; an immediate and permanent 20% decrease in disability recovery rates) which are then combined using a correlation matrix.

The non-SLT health module uses an approach that multiplies a volume measure by a specified standard deviation relating to that risk, then further multiplied by a factor that adjusts to the required confidence level.
The CAT module requires the company to consider the balance sheet impact under standardised scenarios.

Once SCRs for each module are calculated, a further specified correlation matrix is used to combine them to give the Basic SCR (BSCR). Aggregation is performed at different levels — within modules, then across modules.

The correlation matrix for combining the BSCR modules (market, default, life, health, non-life) specifies: market–default 25%; market–life 25%; market–health 25%; market–non-life 25%; default–health 25%; default–non-life 50%; life–health 25%; life–non-life 0%; health–non-life 0%.

The counterparty default risk module distinguishes between type 1 exposures (may not be diversified, counterparty likely to be rated — e.g. derivatives, reinsurance, cash deposits) and type 2 exposures (usually diversified, counterparty unlikely to be rated — e.g. receivables from intermediaries and policyholders).

Risk mitigation techniques can be recognised in the SCR provided any basis risk (mismatch between the risk and its mitigation technique) is immaterial or can be reflected in the SCR. Dynamic hedging is not permitted under the standard formula but may be allowed in the internal model.

To obtain the overall SCR, two adjustments are made to the BSCR: (1) an allowance for operational risk (SCR_op) — relatively simple, based on percentages of earned premiums and technical provisions, added to the BSCR with no recognition of partial correlation or diversification effects; (2) an allowance for the loss absorbing capacity of technical provisions and deferred taxes (Adj) — recognises the mitigating effect of reducing discretionary benefits under stressed conditions, and possible reduction in deferred tax liability.

key_terms.definition: The Basic Solvency Capital Requirement — calculated by combining the SCRs for each risk module (market, counterparty default, insurance for life/health/non-life, intangibles) using a specified correlation matrix. Does not include operational risk or loss absorbing capacity adjustments.
The risk of adverse variation in the amount of a reviewable annuity (e.g. under an IP claim where the benefit can vary), due to changes in the legal environment or the state of health of the insured.

Health catastrophe risk — the risk from extreme events including epidemics, pandemics, climate change and anti-microbial resistance. One of the three components of the health insurance risk sub-module.

The allowance for operational risk added to the BSCR to give the overall SCR. Calculated as a relatively simple formula based on percentages of earned premiums and technical provisions, with no diversification credit against other risk modules.

An adjustment to the BSCR recognising the mitigating effect of reducing discretionary benefits under stressed conditions, and the possible reduction in deferred tax liability. Calculated as the BSCR with loss absorbing effects less the BSCR without — applied as a reduction to the overall SCR.
Counterparty default exposures that may not be diversified and where the counterparty is likely to be rated — e.g. derivatives, securitisations, cash deposits and reinsurance arrangements.
Counterparty default exposures that are usually diversified and where the counterparty is unlikely to be rated — e.g. receivables from intermediaries and policyholders.

exam_qa.q: Why is the net asset value taken as 'assets – best estimate liabilities' rather than 'assets – technical provisions' in the standard formula SCR calculation?
exam_qa.a: Technical provisions = best estimate liabilities + risk margin. The risk margin calculation itself requires calculation of the SCR, which would introduce circularity into the SCR calculation. Therefore, to make the calculation practical, the SCR calculation uses just the best estimate liabilities component of technical provisions.

exam_qa.q: A company has SCR_SLT = £614.3m, SCR_Non-SLT = £250m and SCR_CAT = £100m. Calculate the health insurance risk module SCR using correlations of 50% between SLT and non-SLT, 25% between SLT and CAT, and 25% between non-SLT and CAT.

subsections.title: Internal Model
bullets: Provided it has been approved by the insurance company's regulatory body, an internal model can be used as a full or partial alternative to the detailed standard formula approach.

An internal model might be appropriate if the risk profile of the business differs materially from that covered by the standard formula, and/or if the company already uses such a model for risk management or other decision-making purposes. The supervisor can compel a company to develop an internal model if the standard formula is not appropriate.
Once authorised to use an internal model, the company cannot revert to using the standard formula for the SCR (except if it can justify the circumstances and obtains approval from the regulator).

The overall capital requirements from an internal model will generally differ from the standard formula and may be either higher or lower, depending on how the firm's tailored risk profile compares against the assumptions underlying the standard formula.

The internal model must still generate an SCR based on the stated requirements, including coverage of the risk types noted above and providing at least the equivalent protection to a 99.5% confidence level over one year.

The tests that the model must pass before it can gain approval are: (1) The 'use test' — companies must demonstrate that their internal model is widely used throughout all relevant areas of the business and plays a significant role in internal governance, risk management, decision-making, economic and solvency capital assessments and capital allocation processes; (2) Statistical quality standards — minimum quality standards must be met relating to assumptions and data, probability distribution forecasting, expert judgement, materiality and methods of aggregation; (3) Calibration standards — the SCR derived must have a calibration equivalent to VaR at 99.5% confidence over one year; companies can use different confidence levels/time periods as long as they demonstrate equivalent policyholder protection; (4) Profit and loss attribution — demonstrate how the categorisation of risk chosen in the internal model will be used to explain the causes and sources of actual profits and losses; (5) Validation standards — the internal model must have been fully validated by the insurance company and must be subject to regular control cycle review, including testing results against emerging experience; (6) Documentation standards — the design and operational aspects must be clearly and thoroughly documented, including theory/assumptions underlying the model and circumstances under which it does not work effectively.

The 'use test' is seen as one of the most challenging aspects of gaining internal model approval — companies need to embed the model throughout the company and develop an effective risk culture, evidenced by senior management sign-off, risk management processes and role in the ORSA.

Key challenges for internal models: historic data available to calibrate extreme events is limited, particularly for morbidity events; setting dependency structures and correlation factors that apply under extreme conditions; non-linearity and non-separability of risks (e.g. options that bite when interest rates fall to a certain level, or risks that are correlated under stress but not normally).

An internal model can be structured in any way the company chooses (provided the above tests are met), and can be based on stochastic simulations rather than stress tests plus correlation matrices, perhaps using copulas to model dependency structures. Copulas model the joint distribution function from marginal distribution functions of random variables.

key_terms.definition: One of the approval tests for an internal model — companies must demonstrate that the model is widely used throughout all relevant areas of the business and plays a significant role in internal governance, risk management, decision-making processes, and capital allocation. Seen as one of the most challenging aspects of internal model approval.

Internal model approval test requiring minimum quality standards to be met relating to assumptions and data (accurate, complete, appropriate, updated at least annually), probability distribution forecasting, use of expert judgement, materiality and methods of aggregation.

Internal model approval test requiring the SCR derived from the internal model to have a calibration equivalent to Value at Risk at 99.5% confidence over one year. Companies may use different confidence levels or time periods provided they demonstrate equivalent policyholder protection.
Internal model approval requirement to demonstrate how the risk categorisation chosen in the internal model will be used to explain the causes and sources of actual profits and losses.

Internal model approval test requiring the model to have been fully validated by the insurance company and subject to regular control cycle review, including sensitivity testing, independent reviews and checking model outputs against external data sources.

Internal model approval test requiring the design and operational aspects of the internal model to be clearly and thoroughly documented, including the theory and assumptions underlying the model and circumstances under which the model does not work effectively.

A function used to create a joint distribution function from the marginal distribution functions of random variables. Used in internal models to model dependency structures between risks (e.g. joint behaviour of interest rates and inflation).

The property where the capital required is not a linear function of the risk drivers. Arises e.g. in options and guarantees where liabilities increase sharply once a trigger level is reached — requires allowance beyond that provided by simple correlation matrices.

The property that different risks cannot be considered independently from each other. Risk drivers may be correlated under stress but not normally (e.g. government bond yields and corporate credit spreads during Covid-19).

subsections.title: MCR Overview and Calculation
bullets: The MCR is defined as a simple factor-based linear formula which is targeted at a Value at Risk measure over one year with 85% confidence. Insurance companies do not perform VaR calculations to determine their MCR — they apply the simple linear formula defined in the Solvency II rules.

For SLT health (and life) insurance business, the formula is based on technical provisions and capital at risk on death or disability, multiplied by specified factors. These factors vary according to whether the business is with-profits, unit-linked or conventional without-profits.
For Non-SLT health (and non-life) insurance business, the formula is based on technical provisions and written premiums, with factors varying by product type.
The MCR must be calculated and reported to the regulator quarterly.
The MCR has a floor of 25% and a cap of 45% of the SCR — this may bite for a significant number of health and care insurance companies.
There is an absolute minimum capital requirement of €3.7m for long-term insurance companies and €2.5m for short-term insurance companies (only likely to affect very small companies).

key_terms.definition: A simple factor-based linear formula targeted at VaR with 85% confidence over one year. Floor of 25% and cap of 45% of the SCR. Must be calculated and reported quarterly. Absolute minimum of €3.7m for long-term and €2.5m for short-term insurance companies.

subsections.title: Ladder of Supervisory Intervention
bullets: The SCR and MCR define the two rungs of a 'ladder of supervisory intervention', under which increasingly severe (prescribed) supervisory authority actions are taken as a company's eligible capital falls below the SCR (first point of intervention) and approaches the MCR. The MCR is the ultimate point of supervisory intervention, below which the company would lose its authorisation.

Breaching the SCR — first point of intervention: An insurance company must inform the regulator immediately if the SCR is breached or if a breach is likely within the next three months. Within two months of any breach, the company must submit a recovery plan to the regulator for approval. The company must rectify the breach within six months (extendable by a further three months; further extension possible in exceptional falls in financial markets). This could involve increasing eligible own funds or reducing the company's risk profile and so its SCR.

Breaching the MCR — ultimate point of intervention: An insurance company must inform the regulator immediately if the MCR is breached or if a breach is likely within the next three months. Within one month of any breach, the company must submit a short-term finance scheme to the regulator for approval. The company must rectify the breach within three months. If the MCR is breached, the regulator may restrict or prohibit the disposal of the company's assets. If the short-term finance scheme does not work, the regulator would withdraw the company's authorisation to sell any more business.

key_terms.definition: The two-rung framework under Solvency II defining increasingly severe regulatory actions as capital falls: (1) SCR breach — first point of intervention, recovery plan required within 2 months, breach must be rectified within 6 months; (2) MCR breach — ultimate point of intervention, short-term finance scheme required within 1 month, breach must be rectified within 3 months or authorisation is withdrawn.

subsections.title: Own Funds and Tiering
bullets: The phrase 'own funds' refers to assets in excess of technical provisions, plus it also includes any subordinated liabilities. Own funds are split into basic and ancillary own funds, which are then tiered based on specific criteria.

Basic own funds is broadly capital that already exists within the insurer. Ancillary own funds is capital that may be called upon in certain adverse circumstances, but which does not currently exist within the insurer (e.g. unpaid share capital).

The capital is tiered based on its loss absorbency and permanence. Tier 1 capital is of the highest quality and is the most loss absorbent and permanent form of capital (e.g. paid-up ordinary share capital). Tier 3 is of the lowest quality (e.g. subordinated debt).

The criteria used to tier capital include: whether it is paid up or not; whether it is available immediately to absorb losses; its ranking in a wind-up (subordination of the capital); the extent of any obligation to pay dividends/interest; its outstanding term; the existence of any incentives to redeem the capital. The first four criteria relate to loss absorbency; the final two relate to permanence.

Restrictions are placed on the quality of capital that can be used to cover the MCR and SCR: the SCR must be backed by at least 50% Tier 1 capital and less than 15% Tier 3; the MCR must be backed by at least 80% Tier 1 capital and no Tier 3 capital.

key_terms.definition: Assets in excess of technical provisions plus any subordinated liabilities. Split into basic own funds (capital already existing within the insurer) and ancillary own funds (capital callable in adverse circumstances but not currently existing within the insurer). Tiered into Tier 1 (highest quality), Tier 2 and Tier 3 (lowest quality).
Capital that already exists within the insurer — broadly assets minus technical provisions plus subordinated liabilities. Tiered into Tier 1, 2 and 3 based on loss absorbency and permanence.

Capital that may be called upon in certain adverse circumstances but which does not currently exist within the insurer (e.g. unpaid share capital). Subject to prior supervisory approval before it can be recognised.

The highest quality own funds under Solvency II — most loss absorbent and permanent (e.g. paid-up ordinary share capital). At least 50% of SCR and at least 80% of MCR must be covered by Tier 1 capital.
The lowest quality own funds under Solvency II (e.g. subordinated debt). Less than 15% of the SCR can be covered by Tier 3 capital; no Tier 3 capital can be used to cover the MCR.

subsections.title: Data Quality Standards
bullets: EIOPA has issued advice on 'Standards for Data Quality' under the implementing measures, highlighting the importance of having good quality data for the valuation of technical provisions.

Good data quality is crucial because: the more complete and correct the data, the more consistent and accurate the final estimates will be; a wider range of methodologies for calculating the best estimate becomes possible; validation of methods is more reliable and leads to more credible conclusions; effective comparisons over time and in relation to market data are possible, leading to better knowledge of the business performance.

The issue of data quality is also relevant to other areas of the solvency assessment, such as the SCR using either the standard formula or internal models. A consistent approach to data quality needs to be taken across Pillar 1, without disregarding the different objectives.

key_terms.definition: EIOPA guidance requiring that data used for technical provisions valuation (and more broadly across Pillar 1) must be accurate, complete and appropriate, with data sets updated at least annually. Good data quality enables wider methodology application, more reliable validation and better market comparisons.

12.summary:
Valuation of assets
• Valued using a market-consistent approach, ie at market value where available and mark-to-model where not.
• Recoveries expected from reinsurance shown as an asset on the balance sheet.

Valuation of technical provisions
• Based on a market-consistent approach, represent the amount the insurer would pay to transfer its obligations immediately to another insurer.
• Best estimate liability (BEL) and a risk margin: BEL is the present value of expected future cashflows, discounted using a ‘risk-free’ yield curve (ie term-dependent rates); Discount rate may be increased by a matching adjustment or a volatility adjustment if prescribed conditions met; Risk margin is intended to increase the technical provision to the amount that would have to be paid to another insurer for them to take on the BEL. Determined using the ‘cost of capital’ method.
• For unit-linked, the unit and non-unit components must be unbundled.

SCR
• Value at Risk measure based on a 99.5% confidence level of the variation over one year of the amount of ‘basic own funds’.
• Can be calculated using either standard formula or internal model (or a mixture).

Standard formula
• The Basic SCR (BSCR) is calculated by considering different modules of risks: market (interest rate, equity, property, credit spread, currency and concentration); counterparty default; insurance (separately for life, health and non-life business); intangible assets.
• The health insurance risk module comprised of SLT health, non-SLT health and catastrophe.
• SCR calculated separately for each risk module, results are aggregated using a specified correlation matrix to give the BSCR.
• The overall SCR obtained by adding allowances for the operational risk module and the loss absorbing capacity of technical provisions and deferred taxes.

Internal models
• Internal model must pass the following tests to gain approval: use test; statistical quality standards; calibration standards; profit and loss attribution; validation standards; documentation standards.

MCR
• Defined as a simple factor-based linear formula targeted at a Value-at-Risk measure over one year with 85% confidence.
• Floor of 25% and cap of 45% of the SCR.

Supervisory intervention levels
• Increasingly severe supervisory authority actions taken as a company’s eligible capital falls below the SCR (first point of intervention) and approaches the MCR.
• MCR is the ultimate point of supervisory intervention, below which the company would lose its authorisation.

Quality of capital resources
• ‘Own funds’ refers to assets in excess of technical provisions, plus it includes any subordinated liabilities, split into basic and ancillary own funds, which are then tiered based on specific criteria.
• Restrictions placed on the quality of capital used to cover the MCR and SCR.

Data quality
• EIOPA has issued advice on ‘Standards for Data Quality’, highlights the importance of having good quality data for the valuation of technical provisions.

13.title: Other Approaches to Solvency Regulation

subsections.title: Overview
bullets: This chapter provides high-level examples of approaches to statutory reporting and solvency regulation in other jurisdictions around the world. Students are not expected to know the detail in this chapter but the principles highlighted could be examinable.
The introduction of Solvency II in the EU has had an impact on insurance solvency regulation in many other jurisdictions around the world.

A high-level comparison of differences in approaches between the EU and certain other jurisdictions is outlined. The comparison is not intended to be exhaustive; rather it is intended to highlight some material differences in approach.

subsections.title: Overview
bullets: The United Kingdom left the European Union on 1 January 2021 without an EU-wide arrangement for the operation and regulation of financial services. Further discussions are ongoing between the UK and EU with regards to financial services; at the time of writing (May 2023) there remains considerable uncertainty with regards to the outcome.
Prior to exit, the UK was part of the EU and Solvency II applied to UK insurers. At the point of exit the UK was aligned with Solvency II regulations.
It is unclear the extent to which this alignment will continue. The UK government is considering reforming UK insurers' capital rules to potentially reduce the capital requirements on UK insurers.
Immediately following the UK's exit, UK-based insurance companies are no longer able to provide services in the EU and will become a third-country insurance undertaking.
These issues are not considered further in this version of the Core Reading but this section will be amended in the future.

key_terms.definition: An insurer based outside the EU that wishes to operate within the EU. Following Brexit, UK-based insurers became third-country undertakings with respect to EU markets. Third-country equivalence is the mechanism by which such undertakings may operate.

subsections.title: Overview
bullets: Solvency regulation in the United States has largely been a state-based system for many years. This system is comprised of state insurance departments (currently 50 states along with the District of Columbia and five territories), and can best be described as a national system of state-based regulation.

The National Association of Insurance Commissioners (NAIC) helps regulators to protect consumers and helps maintain the financial stability of the industry. Ultimate regulatory responsibility for insurer solvency rests with each state insurance department and the State insurance commissioner.
The NAIC provides guidance on the standards / model laws which apply to each state, supporting uniformity of reporting, valuation and capital requirements across each state.

There are two federal entities involved in insurance regulation: (1) the Federal Insurance Office — monitors all aspects of the insurance sector and advises on important international and national insurance matters, but does not have a supervisory role; (2) the Federal Reserve Board — has consolidated oversight of insurance companies deemed as systemically important.

To ensure that legal obligations to policyholders are met when they come due, health and care insurers are required to maintain reserves, capital and surplus at all times allowing for an adequate margin of prudence. Accounting standards, risk-based capital requirements, minimum statutory reserves and state-specific minimum capital requirements form the backbone of the reserve and capital adequacy requirements. Conservatism is a pervasive concept in specification of these requirements.

key_terms.definition: The US body that coordinates the work of individual state regulators by providing guidance on standards and model laws that apply to each state, supporting uniformity of reporting, valuation and capital requirements. Does not hold ultimate regulatory responsibility — this rests with each state insurance department.

A federal entity that monitors all aspects of the US insurance sector and advises on international and national insurance matters. It does not have a supervisory or regulatory role over individual insurers.
A federal entity with consolidated oversight of insurance companies deemed as systemically important to the US financial system.

subsections.title: Risk-Based Capital and Reserves
bullets: The primary measure of required capital is defined by the Risk-Based Capital (RBC) system. The RBC calculation uses a standard formula to determine a capital requirement from which regulators produce a baseline specified level of regulatory actions and intervention for weakly capitalised insurers. A significant portion of the RBC formula is derived from the annual statement, which is based upon statutory accounting.

The annual statement is the financial information that an insurer must submit to the NAIC on an annual basis, including details such as the fair value of assets held and details of liabilities held by the insurer such as premium income, claims incurred and commission payments.

The RBC amount explicitly considers the size and risk profile of the insurer and provides for higher RBC charges for riskier assets or for riskier lines of business so that more capital is needed as a result.

Regulators have the authority to take preventative and corrective measures that vary depending on the capital deficiency indicated by the RBC result. These measures are designed to provide for early regulatory intervention to correct problems before insolvencies become inevitable, thereby minimising the number and adverse impact of insolvencies.

The formula applies factors to balances taken from the statutory financial statements. The factors are appropriate across the industry rather than on a company-by-company basis, providing for a level of consistency and objectivity across companies. The factors will not cover every risk that company faces. The RBC system includes factors for asset risk, insurance risk and business risk.

The RBC system operates in conjunction with other aspects of US solvency regulation that focuses on company-specific factors and risks such as annual solvency reviews by regulators, periodic risk-focused exams, and stress testing / asset adequacy analysis.

RBC is intended to be a minimum regulatory capital standard and is not necessarily the full amount of capital that a health and care insurer would want to hold to meet its safety and competitive objectives. The RBC is calibrated to identify 'weakly capitalised companies' — this could be considered similar to the Minimum Capital Requirement (MCR) under Solvency II.

In addition to the required capital, insurers have conservative reserve requirements which until recently were largely calculated using formulaic approaches (such as net premium methods). This system locks in certain assumptions at issue and therefore produces reserves that do not change in response to the economic environment.

key_terms.definition: The primary measure of required capital for US health and care insurers. Uses a standard formula applied to balances from the annual statutory statement to determine a capital requirement. Considers size and risk profile of the insurer (asset risk, insurance risk, business risk). Calibrated to identify weakly capitalised companies — analogous to the MCR under Solvency II.

The financial information a US insurer must submit to the NAIC annually, including fair value of assets, premium income, claims incurred and commission payments. Forms the basis of the RBC formula calculation.

subsections.title: Solvency Modernisation Initiative
bullets: In June 2008 insurance regulators began the Solvency Modernisation Initiative (SMI), which was a critical self-examination of the United States' insurance solvency regulation framework and included a review of international developments regarding insurance supervision.

SMI also considered what regulators could learn from the financial crisis and what could be done to respond to changes in risk management practices, the economic environment, and increased globalisation.
SMI focused on key issues such as capital requirements, governance and risk management, group supervision, statutory accounting and financial reporting, and reinsurance.

key_terms.definition: A critical self-examination of the US insurance solvency regulation framework initiated in June 2008. Reviewed international developments, lessons from the financial crisis, and changes in risk management and globalisation. Focused on capital requirements, governance, group supervision, statutory accounting, financial reporting and reinsurance.

subsections.title: Principle-Based Reserving
bullets: In the USA, reserve calculations were formula-driven for over 150 years. As part of the SMI it was noted that as insurance products became more complex and company risk management techniques diverged (e.g. underwriting), the accuracy of these reserves varied widely across the industry.

Standard Valuation Law adopted by the NAIC back in 2009 introduced a new method for calculating life insurance policy reserves. This new method, referred to as Principle-Based Reserving (PBR), replaces the current formulaic approach to determining policy reserves with an approach that more closely reflects the risks of the highly complex products.
The intention was that reserves would reflect the insurer's own experience of morbidity, lapses, expenses, etc., along with the cost of any options or guarantees offered.
The improved calculation is expected to right-size reserves, reducing reserves that are too high for some products and increasing reserves that are too low for other products.

Unless otherwise specified, the PBR methodology applies to new health and care insurance contracts issued on and after 1st January 2017. Consistent with many previous updates or changes in US insurance regulation, existing business already sold will carry on being valued under the previous valuation requirements.

The PBR valuation defines three reserve calculations: (a) a net premium reserve; (b) a deterministic reserve; (c) a stochastic reserve. The product features and consequent risk profile of the contract determines which of the reserve calculations are required, with the reserve being the higher of the required calculations.

Under PBR, insurers hold the highest of: the net premium reserve calculated using prescribed assumptions; a deterministic reserve calculated using a single prescribed economic scenario; a stochastic reserve. The deterministic and stochastic reserves are calculated on prudent bases.

key_terms.definition: A US reserve methodology adopted from 2009 (applying to new contracts from 1 January 2017) that replaces formulaic approaches. Insurers hold the highest of: (1) a net premium reserve using prescribed assumptions, (2) a deterministic reserve using a single prescribed economic scenario, and (3) a stochastic reserve. Aims to right-size reserves to reflect the actual risk profile of complex products.
One of three reserve calculations under PBR. Calculated using a single prescribed economic scenario on a prudent basis.
One of three reserve calculations under PBR. Calculated using a range of scenarios on a prudent basis.

subsections.title: ORSA
bullets: As part of the SMI a US equivalent of the Own Risk Solvency Assessment required under Solvency II was introduced. This requires an insurer or insurance group to make its own assessment of the adequacy of its risk management and current and prospective solvency positions under normal and severe stress scenarios.

The ORSA will require insurers to analyse all reasonably foreseeable and relevant material risks (i.e. underwriting, credit, market, operational, liquidity risks, etc.) that could have an impact on an insurer's ability to meet its policyholder obligations.

The ORSA is intended to be a tool to help supervisors understand the risks insurers are exposed to, and how adequate insurers' risk management practices are at managing those risks. It will also be used to assess groups' own assessment and management of their capital at group level.

An insurer subject to ORSA requirements is expected to: (1) regularly, no less than annually, conduct an ORSA to assess the adequacy of its risk management framework, and current and estimated projected future solvency position; (2) internally document the process and results of the assessment; (3) provide a confidential high-level ORSA Summary Report annually to the lead state commissioner if the insurer is a member of an insurance group and, upon request, to the domiciliary state regulator.

The ORSA requirements in the US satisfy the International Association of Insurance Supervisors (IAIS) Insurance Core Principles (ICPs) No. 16 — Enterprise Risk Management, and are broadly similar to other countries' ORSAs. In particular the US equivalent of the ORSA is broadly the same as the ORSA under Solvency II.

key_terms.definition: A US ORSA equivalent introduced as part of the SMI. Requires an insurer or group to assess the adequacy of its risk management and current and prospective solvency under normal and severe stress scenarios, covering all material risks (underwriting, credit, market, operational, liquidity). Must be conducted at least annually. Broadly aligned to the ORSA under Solvency II and IAIS ICP No. 16.

subsections.title: Key Differences in US and EU Solvency Valuations
bullets: The main differences between the solvency regimes in the EU and US are:

Approach — The insurance regulatory regimes in the US and EU have similar purposes, but the approach taken varies in some aspects, particularly in respect of the determination of reserves and capital.

Capital — In the US the RBC approach is intended to identify weakly capitalised companies and allow supervisory intervention. In the EU Solvency II sets a minimum level of capital (MCR) but requires a higher level of capital (SCR) representing a greater level of security, focusing on the specific risks borne by the company. The responsiveness of the capital requirements to risk and the level of calibration to the life insurer's own portfolio of risks and controls is also different between US RBC and EU Solvency II.

Statutory Reserving — The US approach to statutory reserving has historically been reflective of the long-term nature of the liabilities, for the most part using formulaic prescribed methods and assumptions including margins for prudence, although this will change somewhat for new business with the introduction of PBR. Solvency II requires a current market-consistent estimate of liabilities with an explicit risk margin.

exam_qa.q: What are the key differences between the US and EU (Solvency II) solvency regimes?
exam_qa.a: Three main differences: (1) Approach — similar purposes but different approaches to reserves and capital determination. (2) Capital — US RBC identifies weakly capitalised companies (analogous to MCR); Solvency II sets a higher SCR calibrated to company-specific risks. (3) Statutory Reserving — US has historically used formulaic methods with prudence margins (changing under PBR); Solvency II requires a market-consistent BEL plus an explicit risk margin.

subsections.title: Introduction
bullets: The China Banking and Insurance Regulatory Commission (CBIRC) is responsible for supervising the establishment and ongoing business activities of banking and insurance institutions.
CBIRC is responsible for taking enforcement actions against regulatory violations.
The CBIRC has implemented significant changes to its capital requirements, risk management and transparency disclosures through the introduction of its C-ROSS framework.

China has introduced a three-tiered regulatory framework (referred to as C-ROSS) that is intended to strengthen capital requirements, risk management and disclosures of Chinese insurance companies, bringing insurance regulation in line with global standards.

Whilst the CBIRC aimed to make C-ROSS comparable to international standards, it also wished to reflect the characteristics of the Chinese insurance market, with particular recognition that China's insurance market is still relatively young in comparison to the US or European insurance markets.

key_terms.term: CBIRC (China Banking and Insurance Regulatory Commission)
key_terms.definition: The Chinese regulatory body responsible for supervising the establishment and ongoing business activities of banking and insurance institutions, and for taking enforcement actions against regulatory violations.

China's three-tiered regulatory solvency framework introduced by the CBIRC. Intended to strengthen capital requirements, risk management and disclosures of Chinese insurance companies, bringing regulation in line with global standards while reflecting the characteristics of the Chinese insurance market. Has similarities to Solvency II but is adapted for the Chinese context.

subsections.title: C-ROSS Framework
bullets: The C-ROSS framework is similar to Solvency II with three tiers:

Pillar I — Quantitative Capital Requirements. Pillar I will include various risk factors applied to premium, reserve and catastrophe risk based on lines of business. The risks covered in Pillar I are the quantitative risks for life business — e.g. insurance risks, market risk and credit risk.

Pillar II — Qualitative Capital Requirements. It has three components: (1) a rating system to determine the level of intervention applied by the regulator; (2) an explicit assessment of the risks management process of the company (equivalent to ORSA); (3) a series of liquidity risk indicators.

Pillar III — Improved risk disclosure and transparency. Pillar III is intended to cover unsupervisable risks which include the systematic risk of the entire market. The CBIRC's aim is to build up a mechanism which improves transparency and communication across market participants.

key_terms.definition: Quantitative Capital Requirements under C-ROSS. Includes risk factors applied to premium, reserve and catastrophe risk by line of business. Covers quantitative risks for life business including insurance risk, market risk and credit risk.

Qualitative Capital Requirements under C-ROSS. Three components: (1) a rating system to determine the level of regulatory intervention; (2) an explicit assessment of the company's risk management process (equivalent to ORSA); (3) a series of liquidity risk indicators.

Improved risk disclosure and transparency under C-ROSS. Covers unsupervisable risks including the systematic risk of the entire market. Aims to build a mechanism that improves transparency and communication across market participants.

subsections.title: Differences to Solvency II
bullets: The main differences between the solvency regime in China (C-ROSS) and Solvency II are:

Asset valuation — The asset valuation approach in C-ROSS is largely similar to the accounting value under Chinese GAAP, which means it can be directly extracted from the insurer's GAAP balance sheet with minor amendments. Under Solvency II, assets are valued mark-to-market.

Discount rate — The method of determining the discounting yield under C-ROSS is similar to the method used for the valuation of Chinese GAAP liabilities. Under Solvency II the current risk-free curve is used for liability discounting.

Own solvency risk assessment — In the current Chinese market, it was felt to be difficult for insurers to do their own solvency risk assessments. So all insurers follow the same rules to derive the Chinese equivalent of the Own Risk Solvency Assessment (referred to as Solvency Aligned Risk Management Required Assessment). Under Solvency II the ORSA gives the insurer more room to use internal models, which are not allowed under C-ROSS.

The introduction of C-ROSS creates a number of challenges for health and care insurers in China, including: the production of the results is a complex process; regular and timely C-ROSS reporting means significant additional workload for companies; for Pillar II substantial effort will be required to establish risk and capital management frameworks that meet the CBIRC requirements; health and care insurers will need to consider ways to streamline reporting processes and automate much of the work in order to shorten reporting time and reduce operational risks.

key_terms.term: Solvency Aligned Risk Management Required Assessment
key_terms.definition: The Chinese equivalent of the ORSA under C-ROSS. Unlike Solvency II's ORSA, all Chinese insurers follow the same prescribed rules to derive this assessment — internal models are not permitted.

exam_qa.q: What are the key differences between C-ROSS and Solvency II?
exam_qa.a: (1) Asset valuation: C-ROSS uses Chinese GAAP accounting values; Solvency II uses mark-to-market values. (2) Discount rate: C-ROSS uses a rate similar to Chinese GAAP liability valuation; Solvency II uses the current risk-free curve. (3) Own solvency risk assessment: C-ROSS requires all insurers to follow the same prescribed rules (no internal models); Solvency II ORSA gives insurers more room to use internal models.

subsections.title: Introduction
bullets: The Australian Prudential Regulation Authority (APRA) oversees prudential regulation of insurers.

APRA takes a risk-based approach to identify and assess areas with the greatest risk to the firm (or financial system). It then applies its regulatory resources and attention to these areas in a targeted and cost-effective manner.
Limitations around risk-based capital, risk management and disclosure requirements led APRA to develop the Life and General Insurance Capital (LAGIC) standards to address these.

key_terms.definition: The Australian regulatory body that oversees prudential regulation of insurers. Takes a risk-based approach, targeting regulatory resources at areas of greatest risk to the firm or financial system. Developed the LAGIC capital standards.

subsections.title: Life and General Insurance Capital Standards
bullets: In 2009, APRA began its Life and General Insurance Capital project (LAGIC) to change the capital standards for insurers in Australia. The proposed changes were designed to increase risk sensitivity and improve consistency in standards across financial services industries, in response to significant changes in legislation following the global financial crisis.

The revised LAGIC regime became effective on 1 January 2013 and includes amended capital requirements, more risk-sensitive capital formulae and some changes to quality of capital requirements. LAGIC has strong parallels to Solvency II with a three-pillar approach. LAGIC also includes an individual capital adequacy assessment report — similar to ORSA.
Pillar I of the LAGIC standard sets out the approaches to establish the capital requirements for life insurers. The capital amount is described as the Prescribed Capital Amount (PCA).

The LAGIC capital requirement is that insurers must hold sufficient capital at a level of 99.5% Value-at-Risk over a one-year period. The PCA can be calculated using either APRA's Standard Method or an internal model approved by APRA. The Standard Method calculates the capital amount based on insurance risk, asset risk, asset concentration risk and operational risk.
This is very similar to the Solvency Capital Requirement (SCR) under the Solvency II regime.

The APRA can apply a 'supervisory adjustment' if it considers that the methodology does not produce an appropriate outcome in the particular circumstance of the insurer. This adjustment could lead to an increase or decrease in required capital. This is similar to the capital 'add-on' that may be required under Solvency II.
The additional LAGIC pillars cover the requirements for good risk management and governance, and the requirement for regular disclosures by the insurer.

At the time of writing, the LAGIC approach has provisional equivalence with Solvency II. Provisional equivalence means equivalence granted for a ten-year period with possible extension for further ten-year periods.

key_terms.definition: The Australian capital standards framework developed by APRA, effective from 1 January 2013. Has strong parallels to Solvency II with a three-pillar structure. Requires insurers to hold capital at 99.5% VaR over one year (the Prescribed Capital Amount). Has provisional equivalence with Solvency II.

The capital amount required under LAGIC (Australia). Insurers must hold capital at a level of 99.5% Value-at-Risk over a one-year period. Calculated using APRA's Standard Method or an approved internal model. Analogous to the SCR under Solvency II.

APRA's standard formula for calculating the Prescribed Capital Amount (PCA) under LAGIC. Calculates the capital amount based on insurance risk, asset risk, asset concentration risk and operational risk.

A form of Solvency II third-country equivalence granted for a ten-year period, with possible extension for further ten-year periods. Australia's LAGIC framework has provisional equivalence with Solvency II.

subsections.title: Introduction
bullets: The Prudential Authority (PA) is responsible for regulating a wide range of financial institutions including banks and insurance companies. The PA's functions include licensing, ongoing supervision and enforcement.

The Financial Sector Conduct Authority (FSCA) is responsible for market conduct regulation and supervision. It aims to enhance the efficiency and integrity of financial markets, promote fair customer treatment by financial institutions, provide financial education and promote financial literacy, and assist in maintaining financial stability.
A recent introduction has been a risk-based solvency framework for prudential regulation of insurers referred to as the Solvency Assessment and Management (SAM) framework.

key_terms.definition: The South African body responsible for regulating banks and insurance companies. Functions include licensing, ongoing supervision and enforcement.

key_terms.term: FSCA (Financial Sector Conduct Authority) — South Africa
key_terms.definition: The South African body responsible for market conduct regulation and supervision. Aims to enhance market efficiency and integrity, promote fair customer treatment and financial literacy, and assist in maintaining financial stability.

South Africa's risk-based solvency framework for prudential regulation of life and non-life insurers, implemented in July 2018. Largely based on Solvency II with a three-pillar structure, adapted for the South African market and economic environment. Designed to comply with criteria for Solvency II third-country equivalence.

subsections.title: Solvency Assessment and Management Framework
bullets: The Prudential Authority in South Africa implemented a risk-based solvency framework for the prudential regulation of life and non-life insurers in South Africa in July 2018. This framework is referred to as the Solvency Assessment and Management (SAM) framework.
The SAM framework is largely based on Solvency II and has a three-pillar structure:

Pillar 1 sets out the quantitative regulatory requirements that insurers need to comply with. Pillar 1 requires insurers to determine their balance sheet and capital requirements on a SAM basis, which are used to determine their financial soundness from a regulatory perspective. The main regulatory capital requirement under SAM reflects the amount of Own Funds that a company requires to survive a 1-in-200 year loss event. This is similar to the SCR under Solvency II.
Pillar 2 deals with the qualitative requirements and rules on supervision of insurers. It aims to establish a system of sound governance and risk management.

Pillar 3 sets out the regulatory reporting and public disclosures required by insurers. Insurers will have to disclose private information to the Prudential Authority and public information to the market.
Insurers may use the Standardised Formula to calculate the capital requirements, or may apply to the local regulator for approval to calculate the capital requirements using an Internal Model.
A guiding principle from the start of the SAM development process was that SAM should comply with the criteria for Solvency II third-country equivalence.

Whilst the starting point for SAM was Solvency II, SAM has been adapted for features unique to the South African market and economic environment. Examples of where adaptations have been made include using risk-free rates to reflect the South African context, and calibrating certain risks such as market risk to the South African market environment. As a result of these adaptations, there are some differences between SAM and Solvency II.

A further example of adapting capital requirements to the local environment is the treatment of microinsurance in South Africa. In order to encourage wider access to insurance for people on low incomes, South Africa has reduced the minimum capital requirement for new microinsurers relative to the SAM SCR requirement.

key_terms.definition: The quantitative regulatory requirements pillar of the SAM framework. Requires insurers to determine their SAM balance sheet and capital requirements. The main regulatory capital requirement reflects the Own Funds needed to survive a 1-in-200 year loss event — analogous to the SCR under Solvency II.
The qualitative requirements and supervision pillar of the SAM framework. Aims to establish a system of sound governance and risk management.
The regulatory reporting and public disclosure pillar of the SAM framework. Insurers must disclose private information to the Prudential Authority and public information to the market.

An adaptation of the SAM framework for the South African context. The minimum capital requirement for new microinsurers is reduced relative to the standard SAM SCR requirement, to encourage wider access to insurance for people on low incomes.

subsections.title: Overview
bullets: Directionally, most country regulators in Asia are building more robust regulatory and solvency frameworks, although the sophistication of the approach varies depending on the jurisdiction.
The approach ranges from Solvency II equivalency status to the much simpler minimum capital requirements.

The regulation sophistication scale for countries in the Asia Pacific region (from least to most sophisticated) is as follows: Minimum Capital — e.g. Myanmar; Formula-based — e.g. India, Vietnam, Brunei; Risk-based capital — e.g. Indonesia; Economic capital and ORSA — e.g. China, Japan, Malaysia, Australia.

subsections.title: Overview
bullets: A number of countries in Latin America are introducing Solvency II-type regulatory frameworks, or are introducing aspects of Solvency II such as risk-based capital calculations. This has represented a significant change to their previous regulatory models.
Mexico was the first country to adopt a Solvency II-like regulatory framework.
Brazil has been granted provisional Solvency II equivalence from 1 January 2016 for ten years. Chile is also developing a risk-based capital approach modelled after Solvency II.
Brazil and Chile have both introduced their equivalent of Own Risk Solvency Assessments which companies will need to comply with.
It is likely that the introduction of risk-based capital requirements in Latin America will lead to a greater focus on risk management in these countries.

subsections.title: Non-Examinable Background References
bullets: The following are non-examinable background references for material in Chapters 11, 12 and 13:
Deloitte SAM Pillar 1 Requirements for Solo Insurers and Insurance Groups
Milliman Report: Analysis of China's new C-ROSS solvency capital regime (2016)
NAIC White Paper: The U.S. National State — Based System of Insurance Financial Regulation and the Solvency Modernization Initiative (2013)
fsb.org
naic.org

13.summary:
United Kingdom
• Currently follows the Solvency II regulations of the EU, unclear how this will change following the UK’s exit from the EU.

USA
• National system of state-based regulation. National Association of Insurance Commissioners (NAIC) assists regulators to protect consumers and maintain financial stability.
• Insurers are required to maintain prudent reserves, capital and surplus at all times.
• Risk-based capital (RBC) system – uses a standard formula to determine capital requirements, derived from annual statement, considers size and risk profile of the insurer. Regulators can take preventative and corrective measures where the RBS result is considered too low, it is not the full amount of capital an insurer would need to hold. Conservative reserves are also required.
• Solvency Modernisation Initiative (SMI) – review started in 2008 following financial crash.
• Principle-Based Reserving (PBR) – replaced formula-driven approaches, insurers hold the highest of prescribed net premiums reserve, single economic scenario deterministic reserve and a stochastic reserve.
• ORSA – insurers are required to analyse all reasonably foreseeable and relevant material risks that might impact its ability to meet policyholder obligations.
• Key differences to Solvency II are: approach to regulation; capital – RBC is a ‘weaker’ level of capital requirement than SCR under Solvency II; statutory reserving – US approach historically formula-based with margins in assumptions for prudence, changing going forward following PBR, Solvency II requires market-consistent estimate of liabilities plus an explicit risk margin.

China
• C-ROSS framework has three tiers: Pillar I – quantitative capital requirements including risk factors applied to premiums and reserves for risks such as insurance, market and credit; Pillar II – qualitative capital requirements composed of a rating to determine the level of any regulatory intervention, explicit assessment of insurer’s risk management processes (ORSA equivalent) and a series of liquidity risk indicators; Pillar III – risk disclosure and transparency.
• Key differences to Solvency II are: Asset valuation – accounting value for C-ROSS, mark to market for Solvency II; Discount rate – C-ROSS similar to Chinese GAAP liability valuation, Solvency II uses risk-free curve; Own solvency risk assessment – C-ROSS doesn’t allow internal models.

Australia
• Life and General Insurance Capital – intended to increase risk sensitivity and provide consistency, strong parallels to Solvency II (has provisional equivalence): required to hold capital to 99.5% VaR over 1 year, calculated using standard method or approved internal model; regulator (APRA) can apply supervisory adjustment if capital considered inappropriate; required to produce an individual capital adequacy assessment report (similar to ORSA).

South Africa
• Solvency Assessment and Management framework – risk-based solvency framework with 3 pillars, based on Solvency II adapted for South African market: Pillar 1 – quantitative regulatory requirements, capital requirements reflect 1-in-200 year loss event, can use standardised formula for approved internal model; Pillar 2 – qualitative requirements and rules on supervision; Pillar 3 – regulatory reporting and public disclosure.

Asia / Pacific
• Range of approaches taken from minimum levels of capital (eg Myanmar) to formula based approaches (eg India), risk-based capital (eg Indonesia) and economic capital and ORSA approaches (eg Australia and China).

Latin America
• Mexico adopted Solvency II-like framework.
• Brazil granted provisional equivalence.
• Chile developing risk-based capital approach modelled on Solvency II.
• Brazil and Chile both have ORSA equivalents.

14.title: Profit Reporting

subsections.title: Overview of Financial Reporting
bullets: Health and care insurance companies report regularly on their financial position. Financial statements give information about financial position and performance to a range of users for financial decision-making.

Reports typically cover: the value of assets and liabilities at a point in time (balance sheet); profit or loss earned over a period (revenue statement); and a description of the approach taken in producing the information.
Reasons for disclosing financial position include: demonstrating that the company has sufficient assets to cover its liabilities; and showing the size and source of profits earned over a period.
Users of financial information include regulators, shareholders, creditors, employees, tax inspectors, and the general public.

In the UK, insurers prepare two sets of financial information: supervisory reports (to assess solvency and available capital) and profit reporting / statutory accounts (to assess profit, value creation, and shareholder value).

Some health and care insurers provide supplementary financial reporting to inform shareholders of the true value of their interest in the business and of the change in that value over time as a result of management of the company's resources.
Some proprietary companies supplement published profit information with embedded value (EV) methods, though this is becoming less common following Solvency II.

key_terms.definition: A financial statement showing the value of a company's assets and liabilities at a particular point in time.
A financial statement showing the profit or loss earned by a company over a reporting period.
Profit reporting produced under legislation, tailored to the needs of shareholders and other external parties; aimed at assessing profit and value creation.

A supplementary reporting method representing the present value of expected future transfers to shareholders from existing in-force business, together with any net assets attributable to shareholders.

exam_qa.q: Why might a health and care insurer produce two separate sets of financial information?
exam_qa.a: One set (supervisory reports) is aimed at the regulator and focuses on assessing solvency and available capital. The other (statutory accounts/profit reporting) is aimed at shareholders and other external parties and focuses on profit and value creation. These have different objectives and may use different bases.

subsections.title: Overview
bullets: It is common for health and care insurance companies to be required by law to provide detailed regular reporting to their local regulator in the form of statutory accounts.

The regulator normally sets out the reporting requirements, subject to overriding regulations such as Solvency II. Separate legislation typically requires insurers to produce regular statutory accounts and submit these to the appropriate government department.
In most countries, a particular set of accounting standards is in place governing how a company should determine and present its statutory accounts.
The nature of standards depends on: the purpose of the reporting; the intentions of those who set the standards; and the intended users of the financial reports.
Financial statements must show a 'true and fair view' of the financial position — free from material misstatements and faithfully representing financial performance and position.
Standards are often maintained by bodies such as the International Accounting Standards Board (IASB) and the Financial Accounting Standards Board (FASB).
There is a trend towards codifying the generally accepted accounting principles (GAAP) employed in different jurisdictions.
The two most adopted sets of standards are International Financial Reporting Standards (IFRS) and US GAAP.

key_terms.definition: Generally Accepted Accounting Principles — the set of accounting standards and practices used in a jurisdiction to prepare and present financial statements.
International Accounting Standards Board — the body that produces IFRS standards.
Financial Accounting Standards Board — the primary body producing US GAAP guidance, including for health and care insurance actuarial and accounting work.
A requirement in many countries that financial statements be free from material misstatements and faithfully represent the financial performance and position of the company.

subsections.title: Different Approaches to Company Reporting Standards
bullets: Rules-based vs principles-based: principles-based standards allow management to use discretion to reflect the nature of the company, its liabilities, and its risk profile. Both US GAAP and IFRS are principles-based.

Discretion can make it harder to compare companies and easier for management to manipulate accounts. Areas where discretion matters include: discount rates/margins used in valuation basis; sensitivity analysis disclosed; and level of consolidation.
Going-concern vs wind-up basis: profit reporting typically assumes the company will continue selling new business (going concern). Solvency measures may be more concerned with a wind-up basis.

Profit recognition: profit is recognised in the period in which it accrues. Accrual means allowing for income and outgo when earned or incurred, not when cash is received or paid. For long-term health and care business, the date of accrual can be ambiguous.

Profit could accrue at point of sale, end of contract, or evenly over the contract term. The accruals principle precludes deliberate smoothing from one period to the next, but where liabilities are smoothed by management discretion it may be more misleading not to allow smoothing to be reflected in profits.

Consolidation: the size of the balance sheet depends on consolidation (offsetting) rules. Consolidation occurs in: presenting liabilities net of reinsurance; presenting policyholder obligations net of future premiums; netting derivative contracts; and adding the net value of a subsidiary rather than presenting assets and liabilities separately.
Consolidation can make it harder to spot underlying trends. Disclosures should be sufficiently detailed to serve the customer without compromising a company's competitive advantage.

Calculation complexity: valuing health and care insurer liabilities and capital requirements can involve stochastic calculations. There is a trade-off between timeliness and computational complexity; newer standards demand complex calculations be performed quickly.

Reporting basis: uncertain liabilities require assumptions about insurable events, customer behaviour, and market returns. Assumptions can be best estimate or prudent. Financial reporting standards concerned with demonstrating solvency are more likely to require prudent margins; newer standards tend to require best estimate assumptions with an explicit risk amount.

Consistency with other financial reports: financial reports are most useful when comparable — across different companies, different industries, or for a single company at different dates. Solvency standards may allow comparison only within the insurance sector; statutory reporting standards are more likely to require consistency across many industries.

key_terms.definition: Accounting standards that prescribe specific rules to be followed, leaving less room for management judgement.
Accounting standards that set out broad principles allowing management to exercise discretion to ensure reports reflect the nature of the company. Both IFRS and US GAAP are principles-based.
The assumption that the company will continue in operation for the foreseeable future and continue to sell new business.
A reporting basis used when liquidation is imminent; asset values are typically lower than on a going-concern basis.
Recording revenue when earned and expenses when incurred, irrespective of when actual cashflows occur. This gives the most accurate representation of financial position.
The level of granularity at which figures are presented, in particular whether individual components are presented before or after offsetting against each other.
Reporting assumptions that are unbiased best estimates of expected future experience, with no explicit prudential margin.
Reporting assumptions that include a margin for adverse deviation, used particularly in solvency-focused reporting.

exam_qa.q: Why do statutory reporting standards typically require consistency across companies of many industries?
exam_qa.a: Investors compare companies across industries when making investment decisions. Consistent standards make it easier for investors to compare statutory accounts of companies in different sectors (e.g., insurance, mining, banking) and make more informed decisions. For example, an investor in a broad market index needs to analyse accounts across all sectors and benefits from consistency in presentation.

exam_qa.q: Give examples of areas where management discretion in principles-based reporting could make it difficult for account users to compare across companies.
exam_qa.a: Examples include: different approaches to risk allowance (e.g., discount rates or valuation margins); different levels of sensitivity analysis disclosed under different assumptions; and the level of consolidation (e.g., netting reinsurance and/or tax).

subsections.title: Overview
bullets: IFRS standards are the most widely adopted accounting standards in the world, produced by the IASB.

EU regulations require all listed insurance companies to use EU-adopted IFRS when preparing their consolidated financial statements. In the UK, all listed companies must use IFRS for their consolidated accounts.
Even in countries that continue to use local accounting standards (e.g. China), those local standards are increasingly influenced by IFRS principles.
The term IFRS embraces both IFRSs and International Accounting Standards (IASs).
IAS 1 Presentation of Financial Statements sets out the overall requirements for general purpose financial statements prepared under IFRSs.
Key IFRS standards for health and care insurers: IFRS 17 Insurance Contracts; IFRS 7 Financial Instruments: Disclosures; IFRS 9 Financial Instruments.

key_terms.definition: International Financial Reporting Standards — the most widely adopted accounting standards globally, produced by the IASB. Embraces both IFRSs and IASs.
Presentation of Financial Statements — sets out the overall requirements for general purpose financial statements prepared and presented in accordance with IFRS.
Insurance Contracts — the IFRS standard specifically governing accounting for insurance contracts, replacing IFRS 4 from 1 January 2023.
Financial Instruments — specifies how an entity should classify and measure financial assets, financial liabilities, and some contracts to buy or sell non-financial items.
Financial Instruments: Disclosures — sets out disclosure requirements relating to financial instruments.

subsections.title: IFRS Principles
bullets: IFRS is principles-based. The main principles are: fair representation; going concern; accruals based; comparability; compliance; reporting period; understandability; offsetting.
Fair representation: financial statements must present fairly the financial position, financial performance, and cashflows of an entity.
Going concern: statements are normally prepared assuming the entity will continue in operation for the foreseeable future with no intention to liquidate or materially curtail activities.
Accruals based: financial statements (except cashflow information) must use the accrual basis — revenue recorded when earned and expenses when incurred, regardless of when actual cashflows occur.
Comparability: presentation and classification of items must be retained from one period to the next unless a change is justified by a change in circumstances or a new IFRS requirement.
Compliance: an entity must make an explicit and unreserved statement of compliance with IFRS if its statements comply with all IFRS requirements.
Reporting period: financial statements are presumed to be prepared at least annually. Changes in the annual reporting period must be disclosed.
Understandability: financial information should be readily understandable by users who have knowledge of the business, its economic activity and accounting concepts.
Offsetting: assets and liabilities, and income and expenses, may generally not be offset.

key_terms.definition: The requirement that financial statements present fairly the financial position, performance, and cashflows of an entity — analogous to the 'true and fair view' requirement.
Presentation and classification of items must be retained from one period to the next unless justified by changed circumstances or a new IFRS.
Assets and liabilities, and income and expenses, may generally not be offset against each other in the financial statements.

subsections.title: Accounting for Insurance Contracts – IFRS 17
bullets: IFRS 17 took effect from 1 January 2023, replacing IFRS 4. It is much more prescriptive than IFRS 4 and its adoption has led to substantial changes to financial disclosures and the systems and processes that produce them.

IFRS 17 is the first comprehensive international accounting standard applicable to all types of health and care insurance contracts. It aims to make financial statements more relevant, comparable, and transparent.

Main criticisms of IFRS 4 that IFRS 17 addresses: sensitivity of profits to reserving assumptions; lack of comparability across insurers; early recognition of premiums and profits for long-term contracts.

IFRS 17 describes three valuation (measurement) methods: the General Measurement Model (GMM) / Building Block Approach (BBA); the Variable Fee Approach (VFA); and the Premium Allocation Approach (PAA).
The GMM is the default valuation methodology for any insurance contract under IFRS 17, appropriate for traditional long-term life business such as term assurance and without-profit annuities.
GMM has four building blocks: fulfilment cashflows; time value of money; risk adjustment (RA); contractual service margin (CSM). The first two are often combined as the best estimate liability (BEL).

Best estimate liability (BEL): similar to Solvency II — derive best estimate assumptions, derive a discount rate from observable market data, discount projected cashflows, and allow for the cost of any guarantees.

Risk adjustment (RA): similar to the Solvency II risk margin — represents the value of guarantees not captured in the BEL (i.e. non-market risks). May be calculated using VaR, CTE, or cost of capital (CoC). IFRS 17 does not specify the confidence level — this is at the insurer's choice.

Contractual service margin (CSM): a new concept. At inception, CSM = (Initial premium – Initial expenses) – (BEL + RA). It is set so that zero profit arises at the point of sale. If BEL + RA exceeds the initial net cashflow, a loss is recognised immediately (CSM cannot be negative).
