The CSM represents 'future profit' or 'unearned profit'. It is released (written down) over the term of the policy in line with a chosen 'coverage unit' measure (e.g. number of policies in-force). This smooths the emergence of profit over the policy term.

The CSM also offsets changes in the BEL and RA due to non-investment assumption changes, so reported profit is driven more by actual experience than assumption changes. If the CSM is insufficient to absorb an increase in BEL + RA, the shortfall is recognised immediately as a loss.

The VFA may be used for unit-linked, with-profits, or other contracts where fulfilment cashflows vary in line with the value of a defined pool of assets. Under VFA, the discount rate is calculated with reference to the asset pool, and the CSM is 'unlocked' to absorb changes in BEL and RA due to discount rate movements.
Under GMM, the discount rate is based on market observable data and the CSM is not unlocked when the discount rate changes — discount rate changes flow through to profit/loss.

Profitable and unprofitable contracts should not be offset. Each policy is categorised as: loss-making at inception; profitable with no significant risk of loss; or any other profitable contract. Losses are recognised immediately; profits emerge over the contract term.
Disclosure requirements include: insurance revenue earned; insurance service expenses incurred; and insurance finance income and expenses.

Transitional requirements: companies were required to calculate a transitional CSM for in-force business. Three approaches: Full Retrospective Approach (FRA) — must be used if practicable; Modified Retrospective Approach (MRA) — simplified version using reasonably available information; Fair Value Approach (FVA) — transitional CSM = fair value of policies less fulfilment cashflows at transition date.
The implementation of IFRS 17 took much longer than planned and has been complex and expensive for many health and care insurers.

key_terms.term: GMM (General Measurement Model) / BBA (Building Block Approach)
key_terms.definition: The default IFRS 17 valuation methodology for insurance contracts. Defines how initial measurement and re-measurement occurs over the life of a contract using four building blocks: fulfilment cashflows, time value of money, risk adjustment, and CSM.

An IFRS 17 measurement model used where a significant proportion of cashflows vary with the value of a defined pool of assets (e.g. unit-linked, with-profits). Mandatory for contracts with direct participation features.
A simplified IFRS 17 model similar to the unearned premium method used by general insurers. May be used for short-term health and care business.

The probability-weighted estimate of the present value of future net cashflows arising from fulfilment of policyholder obligations. Calculated using best estimate assumptions and a market-observable discount rate.

The IFRS 17 component representing the value of non-market risks not captured in the BEL (analogous to the Solvency II risk margin). Calculated using VaR, CTE, or cost of capital; the confidence level is chosen by the insurer.

An IFRS 17 building block representing the unearned (future) profit on a contract. Set at inception so no profit is recognised at sale; released over the contract term as insurance services are provided. Cannot be negative.

The measure used to determine how the CSM is released over the contract term (e.g. number of policies in-force). Contracts must remain in annual cohorts — policies written more than one year apart cannot be grouped.
The preferred IFRS 17 transitional approach — determines the position at transition as if IFRS 17 had always applied throughout the full policy lifetime.
A simplified IFRS 17 transitional approach used when FRA is not practicable; uses reasonably available information to produce a result as close as possible to FRA.
An IFRS 17 transitional approach used when MRA is also not practicable; transitional CSM = fair value of policies less fulfilment cashflows at the transition date.

exam_qa.q: Explain why the CSM would be expected to be positive for a profitable regular premium contract despite initial expenses likely exceeding the initial premium.
exam_qa.a: For a regular premium contract, the BEL would be negative because the present value of future premiums (net of future benefits and expenses) exceeds current obligations — the profit loadings in future premiums are expected to recover initial expenses and provide profit margin. As long as the RA is smaller than the discounted value of all profit loadings, the CSM = (Initial premium – Initial expenses) – (BEL + RA) will be positive.

exam_qa.q: What are the main criticisms of IFRS 4 that IFRS 17 addresses?
exam_qa.a: IFRS 4 was criticised for: (1) the sensitivity of reported profits to reserving assumptions; (2) the lack of comparability between different health and care insurance companies (companies could use local standards or different methodologies); and (3) the early recognition of premiums and profits for long-term contracts.

exam_qa.q: How does the CSM smooth the emergence of profit under IFRS 17?
exam_qa.a: The CSM is set at inception to equal the present value of future profit loadings. It is then released over the contract term in line with a coverage unit measure, causing profit to emerge steadily rather than at policy inception. Additionally, assumption changes affecting the BEL and RA are absorbed by the CSM (rather than flowing directly to the P&L), so reported profit is driven more by actual experience than by changes in assumptions.

exam_qa.q: What is the key difference between the GMM and VFA with regard to the discount rate and CSM?
exam_qa.a: Under the GMM, the discount rate is based on market observable data and is not linked to an asset pool; changes in the discount rate flow through directly to the profit and loss statement, and the CSM is not unlocked. Under the VFA, the discount rate is calculated with reference to the pool of assets backing the contract, and the CSM is unlocked at each future period to absorb changes in BEL and RA arising from discount rate changes — so these changes are deferred into future profit releases rather than being recognised immediately.

subsections.title: Accounting for Financial Instruments
bullets: Accounting for financial instruments is currently contained in IFRS 9. IFRS 9 specifies how an entity should classify and measure financial assets, financial liabilities, and some contracts to buy or sell non-financial items.

Under IFRS 9, the default measurement is fair value through profit and loss (FVPL) — changes in fair value are recognised in profit and loss as they arise — unless restrictive criteria are met for classifying the asset at Amortised Cost or Fair Value through Other Comprehensive Income (FVOCI).
FVOCI items include some types of unrealised gains and losses that are not recognised as profit or loss in the IFRS accounts.

This approach is consistent with a move towards more 'market-consistent' recognition. Assets are mainly measured at fair value (e.g. market value where there is a deep and liquid market), with changes in value coming through as profit or loss.

key_terms.term: FVPL (Fair Value through Profit and Loss)
key_terms.definition: The default IFRS 9 measurement basis: financial assets are measured at fair value with changes recognised directly in profit and loss as they arise.

key_terms.term: FVOCI (Fair Value through Other Comprehensive Income)
key_terms.definition: An IFRS 9 classification where changes in fair value are recognised in other comprehensive income rather than directly in profit and loss — applicable where restrictive criteria are met.

An IFRS 9 measurement basis for financial assets where the asset is held at the amount initially recognised, adjusted for repayments and amortisation. Applicable only where restrictive criteria are met.

subsections.title: Overview
bullets: Companies listed in the United States are required to prepare accounts under US GAAP — the generally accepted accounting principles adopted by the US Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC).

US GAAP guidance for health and care insurance actuarial and accounting work is primarily produced by the Financial Accounting Standards Board (FASB). Standards are numbered in order of official release.

While IFRS is not expected to be used by US businesses in the foreseeable future, IFRS remains relevant to US companies due to: mergers and acquisitions (looking outside the US); non-US stakeholders requiring IFRS financial information; and non-US subsidiaries of multinational companies.

key_terms.definition: Generally Accepted Accounting Principles adopted by the US Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC). The primary accounting framework for companies listed in the United States.
Securities and Exchange Commission — the US regulatory body that adopts US GAAP for listed companies.

subsections.title: Principles
bullets: US GAAP is also principles-based but has some industry-specific rules. Main principles: fair presentation; going concern; compliance; historical cost; revenue recognition; matching; full disclosure; objectivity; consistency; offsetting.

Fair presentation: objective is fair presentation in accordance with US GAAP — analogous to the IFRS requirement. Reports must be free from material misstatements and faithfully represent financial performance and position.
Going concern: financial statements normally prepared on a going-concern basis, unless liquidation is imminent (wind-up/break-up basis).
Compliance: an entity claiming compliance with US GAAP must comply with all requirements of US GAAP (unlike IFRS, no explicit and unreserved statement of compliance is required).

Historical cost: companies should value assets and liabilities at 'acquisition cost' rather than fair market value. This is reliable and non-subjective but not always meaningful economically — hence the movement towards fair values. FASB has become more open to fair value information in recent years.

Revenue recognition: revenue should be recorded when earned rather than when received. Losses must be recognised when they become probable, whether or not they have occurred. US GAAP augments this principle with detailed industry-specific rules.

Matching: expenses should be matched with revenues wherever reasonable — expenses are not recognised when incurred but when the work undertaken makes its contribution to revenue. The Deferred Acquisition Cost (DAC) asset and Deferred Profit Liability (DPL) both follow the matching principle.

DAC: a notional asset equal to deferrable acquisition costs, expected to be recovered from future premiums or margins. Written down (amortised) as margins emerge. Amortising DAC more slowly releases profit more rapidly.

DPL: a reserve set up when the premium-paying period is shorter than the insurance period, to offset excess profits being earned during the premium-paying period. Released over the life of the contract. Neither DAC nor DPL impacts total profit, only the pattern of recognition.

Full disclosure: information disclosed should be sufficient to make a judgement, while keeping preparation costs reasonable. Material items (those likely to have a material impact on financial position or results) should be disclosed.
Objectivity: information should be factual and verifiable as far as possible.
Consistency: information should be presented consistently from period to period.

Offsetting: assets and liabilities, and income and expenses, may generally not be offset. Under US GAAP, offsetting of derivatives is optional (under IFRS it is required when stated conditions are met).

key_terms.definition: US GAAP principle requiring assets and liabilities to be valued at acquisition cost rather than fair market value. Reliable and objective, but not necessarily economically meaningful; movement towards fair values has occurred.
Expenses should be matched with revenues wherever reasonable — expenses are recognised when the work they relate to contributes to revenue, not simply when incurred.

A notional asset under US GAAP representing deferrable acquisition costs expected to be recovered from future premiums or margins. Amortised over the life of the policy as margins emerge. Does not affect total profit, only the timing of recognition.

A reserve under US GAAP set up when the premium-paying period is shorter than the insurance period. Offsets excess profits recognised during the premium-paying period and is released over the contract life. Does not affect total profit, only the timing of recognition.
Revenue should be recorded when earned. Losses must be recognised when probable, whether or not they have actually occurred. Augmented by detailed industry-specific rules under US GAAP.

Information disclosed should be sufficient to make a judgement, while keeping preparation and use costs reasonable. Customarily limited to events with material impact on financial position or results.

exam_qa.q: Describe how a Deferred Profit Liability (DPL) operates and how it relates to the matching principle.
exam_qa.a: If the premium-paying period is shorter than the insurance period, premiums are recognised more quickly than the insurance coverage is provided. This means income and outgo are not matched. The insurer sets up a DPL reserve to offset the excess profits being earned during the premium-paying period. This reserve is then released (amortised) over the life of the contract, effectively spreading the recognition of future premiums over the period of insurance coverage and matching income more closely with the provision of cover.

exam_qa.q: Why does the choice of accounting basis (e.g. speed of DAC amortisation) not affect total profit?
exam_qa.a: The choice of accounting basis only affects the pace at which profit is recognised (allowed to emerge) in the financial statements. It does not change the actual profit earned. For example, amortising a DAC asset more slowly reduces the write-down charge in each period and so accelerates profit recognition, but the total profit over the life of the contract remains unchanged.

subsections.title: Differences Between US GAAP and IFRS
bullets: While there are similarities between US GAAP and IFRS, there are also important differences.

Definition of insurance contracts: under US GAAP, health and care insurance products are split into three categories each subject to a different standard — traditional non-linked (with separate rules for 'short duration business'); unit-linked, unitised with-profits, 'investment' and 'limited pay' contracts; and non-unitised with-profits.

Asset recognition: under US GAAP, financial assets are classified as: trading (market value); available for sale (cost value for P&L, market value for balance sheet); held to maturity (amortised cost).
Compliance: under US GAAP an entity does not need to make an explicit and unreserved statement of compliance, which is required under IFRS.
Consolidation principles differ between US GAAP and IFRS.
Revenue recognition: differences exist in how revenue is recognised. US GAAP also has a number of detailed industry-specific rules not present in IFRS.
Expense recognition: differences exist in the measurement and recognition of expenses.
Offsetting of derivatives: under US GAAP offsetting is optional; under IFRS it is required when stated conditions are met.

sections.title: Supplementary Value Based Reporting (Long-Term Business Only)

subsections.title: Background
bullets: The primary accounts aim to show a 'true and fair' profit, though the extent to which they do so is still a matter of debate within both the accountancy and actuarial professions.

A 'true and fair' view is a dynamic concept requiring compliance with accounting standards except where these would present a misleading picture given the particular circumstances of the reporting entity.
The methods used in primary accounts may include a level of prudence beyond that acceptable for general companies, which can distort the picture for shareholders of long-term insurers.

subsections.title: Development of Supplementary Financial Reporting
bullets: Supplementary financial reporting in long-term insurers arose primarily from the desire to inform shareholders of the true value of their interest in the business and the change in that value over time — reflecting management of the company's resources. This has both defensive and capital raising benefits.

Defensive: if profit and value are understated, the company may be vulnerable to a takeover at a price that does not give fair value to shareholders. Capital raising: understatement of value compromises the company's ability to raise debt or equity capital.
Embedded value (EV) techniques are intended to measure a realistic, risk-adjusted valuation of shareholder cashflows arising from in-force business and net assets.
A company's embedded value = present value of expected future transfers to shareholders from existing in-force business + value of any net assets separately attributable to shareholders.
Profit can be defined as the change in embedded value over the reporting period plus the profit transfer.

The usefulness of supplementary EV reporting was impaired by a lack of consistency and transparency in the methods and assumptions adopted by different health and care insurance companies. This led to the development of European Embedded Value (EEV) principles and Market Consistent Embedded Value (MCEV) principles. EEV and MCEV principles are outside the scope of the current SA1 syllabus.

With increasingly realistic and risk-sensitive solvency reporting (Solvency II) and accounting standards (IFRS 17), many question whether embedded values will continue to be needed. Some health and care companies in Solvency II jurisdictions are no longer reporting on embedded value.

key_terms.definition: A realistic, risk-adjusted valuation of shareholder cashflows from in-force business and net assets. Comprises the present value of in-force (PVIF) component and free surplus plus required capital. Profit = change in EV over the period + profit transfer.
A set of principles developed to address shortcomings of traditional embedded values and to improve consistency and transparency of embedded value reporting.
A further development of embedded value principles based on market-consistent valuation of shareholder cashflows.
The present value of future shareholder cashflows arising from existing in-force business — a component of embedded value.

subsections.title: Implications of Solvency Regime
bullets: The applicable solvency regime can have significant implications for the embedded value calculation and consequently for the analysis of change in embedded value over a period.

In jurisdictions where statutory reporting is on a prudential basis, the emergence of prudential margins forms the PVIF component (present value of future shareholder cashflows from in-force covered business) in the analysis of change in embedded value.
Where statutory reporting requires liabilities on a best estimate basis (e.g. Solvency II), there is no explicit future release of prudential margins in the PVIF.

Even under Solvency II, there may still be a PVIF component in relation to: profits expected beyond Solvency II contract boundaries; any difference between best estimate investment returns in the EV projection basis and discount rates in the BEL (including matching or volatility adjustment); and the release of the risk margin, after allowing for the cost of holding it.

Under Solvency II, the 'required capital' in the EV calculation includes the SCR (or MCR), and may include the risk margin unless it is allowed for in the PVIF. The 'free surplus' = own funds – SCR = MVA – TP – SCR.

Embedded value = Free surplus + Required capital – Cost of holding required capital + PVIF. If PVIF = 0 and the company uses the Solvency II risk margin as the measure of cost of residual non-hedgeable risks and lock-in cost of holding regulatory capital, then embedded value simplifies to shareholder 'own funds'.
The similarity between EV results and supervisory balance sheet results under Solvency II has led many in the EU to question whether embedded values will still be needed in future.

Some health and care companies in Solvency II jurisdictions are no longer reporting on embedded value. Companies that continue to report EV have likely aligned their EV reporting with the Solvency II process.

Benefits of aligning EV reporting with Solvency II include: creation of efficiencies and resource synergies; avoiding manual intervention and spreadsheet-based processes; greater comparability and reconciliation between the two metrics; opportunity to align pricing, risk management, and performance reporting; and improvement in quality of EV reporting.

key_terms.definition: Under Solvency II: FS = own funds – SCR = MVA – TP – SCR. Represents the consolidated shareholder 'own funds' in excess of those required to cover the SCR (or MCR).
The component of embedded value representing the capital the insurer is required to hold (e.g. SCR or MCR under Solvency II), plus potentially the risk margin.
Under Solvency II: the excess of market value of assets over technical provisions. 'Own funds' = Free surplus + SCR.

exam_qa.q: Outline the possible benefits of bringing EV reporting and Solvency II processes in line.
exam_qa.a: Benefits include: creation of efficiencies in reporting processes and resource synergies; Solvency II implementation provided an opportunity to improve processes and reduce manual/spreadsheet-based approaches; producing EV from the Solvency II balance sheet allows greater comparability and reconciliation between the two metrics (improved transparency); provides an opportunity to align pricing and risk management decisions with performance reporting (supporting the use test for internal model firms); and improvement in the quality of EV reporting as Solvency II has raised the bar on data requirements, review, and documentation.

14.summary:
IFRS
• The IFRS standards (produced by the IASB) are the most widely adopted accounting standards in the world. EU regulations require all listed companies to use them when preparing their consolidated financial statements.
• IFRS standards of particular interest to health and care insurers are: IFRS 17 Insurance Contracts; IFRS 9 Financial Instruments; IFRS 7 Financial Instruments: Disclosures.
• Principles-based, main principles are fair representation, going concern, accruals based, comparability, compliance, reporting period, understandability and offsetting.

IFRS 17 Insurance Contracts
• IFRS 17 took effect from 1 January 2023. Addresses criticisms of previous regime (IFRS 4).
• General Measurement Model (GMM) – has four building blocks: the fulfilment cashflows; the time value of money; the risk adjustment (RA); the contractual service margin (CSM)
• Variable Fee Approach (VFA) – may be used for contracts in which the cashflows vary in line with the value of a pool of assets (eg unit-linked, with-profits).

Accounting for financial instruments

Currently contained in IFRS 9.

US GAAP
• Set of generally accepted accounting principles adopted by the US Securities and Exchange Commission (‘SEC’). Developed by many different organisations with the primary body of guidance for the health and care insurance actuarial and accounting work being produced by the Financial Accounting Standards Board (‘FASB’).
• Principles-based, with some differences from and some similarities to IFRS.
• The main underlying principles are: fair presentation; going concern; compliance; historical cost; revenue recognition; matching; full disclosure; objectivity; consistency; offsetting.

Supplementary reporting
• Embedded value (EV) techniques are intended to measure a realistic, risk-adjusted, valuation of shareholder cashflows arising from in-force business and net assets. Profit then defined as the change in embedded value plus the profit transfer.
• The usefulness of supplementary EV reporting impaired by a lack of consistency and transparency in the methods and assumptions adopted by different insurance companies. Led to the development of the European Embedded Value (EEV) principles and Market Consistent Embedded Value (MCEV) principles.
• Under Solvency II, the basic liability (BEL) is determined on a best estimate basis and so there are no prudential margins to be released in the EV PVIF.
• May still be a PVIF component in relation to: profits expected beyond the Solvency II contract boundaries; any difference between the best estimate investment returns assumed in the EV projection basis and the discount rates used in the BEL (including any matching or volatility adjustment); the release of the risk margin, after allowing for the cost of holding it.
• Some health and care insurers in jurisdictions that have implemented Solvency II are no longer reporting on EV.

15.title: Professional Standards and Guidance

subsections.title: Overview
bullets: Actuaries practising in or advising health and care companies often work in positions of considerable responsibility. Their work can directly impact not only their direct client but also a wide range of other stakeholders (eg policyholders, employees and shareholders).

To maintain trust, many actuarial organisations (eg the Society of Actuaries in the US or the Institute and Faculty of Actuaries in the UK) set and maintain frameworks of professional standards of practice.
Standards of practice often include information about the responsibilities of individual actuaries when it comes to protecting the 'public interest'.
This chapter covers the principles of professional guidance (Section 1), examples of professional guidance for the UK (Section 2), and international standards (Section 3).

Descriptions of standards, materials and resources in this chapter reflect the up-to-date versions as at May 2023. Candidates are not required to have knowledge of changes made after this date for the purpose of Subject SA1.

subsections.title: Core Principles
bullets: When carrying out work for a health and care company, an actuary is expected to comply with all relevant legal, regulatory and professional requirements, including any professional standards or guidance set by the professional body to which they belong.

Professional actuarial standards are not static; they evolve over time to reflect: changes in the market for actuarial work; changes to local regulation; and changes in international actuarial standards.
Actuarial organisations may require their members to keep up-to-date with changes to professional standards.

Actuarial organisations will also normally have disciplinary procedures for situations where a member's conduct falls below the standards of behaviour, integrity, competence or professional judgement expected by the association, other members or the wider public.

Given the variety of roles actuaries carry out, it is very difficult for actuarial organisations to put in place one definitive set of rules covering every aspect of work. Consequently, many adopt a more principles-based approach to regulation in place of a prescribed set of rules.
This principles-based approach often extends to codes of conduct, which set out the standards of professional conduct expected of members.

Examples of high-level principles in an actuarial organisation's code of conduct include: (a) a requirement to act honestly and with the highest standards of integrity; (b) a requirement to perform professional duties with competence and care; (c) a requirement to ensure communications are clear, appropriate for the intended audience, and meet all applicable reporting standards; (d) a requirement to act with impartiality; (e) a requirement to comply with all relevant legal, regulatory and professional requirements; (f) a requirement to perform duties with courtesy and professional respect.

Codes of conduct normally also provide guidance on the types of behaviours expected from members with regards to these principles. There can be collaboration between actuarial organisations globally around these professional standards.

key_terms.definition: A regulatory approach that relies on high-level principles of expected conduct rather than a prescriptive set of detailed rules, giving practitioners flexibility in how they comply.
A document issued by an actuarial organisation setting out the standards of professional conduct and behaviours expected of its members.

exam_qa.q: Why do many actuarial organisations adopt a principles-based approach to professional standards rather than a detailed rules-based approach?
exam_qa.a: Because actuaries carry out such a wide variety of roles, it is very difficult to produce one definitive set of rules that covers every aspect of work. A principles-based approach provides flexibility and can be applied across diverse contexts.

subsections.title: Scope
bullets: The codes of professional conduct of actuarial associations will specify the scope of application with regards to their members.
For example, whether the code applies to all categories of membership (eg students and qualified actuaries), regardless of where the actual member is located.

subsections.title: Overview
bullets: The Institute and Faculty of Actuaries (IFoA) is responsible for setting and maintaining ethical standards for its members in the UK.

The IFoA has a responsibility under its Royal Charter to regulate the actuarial profession in the public interest. As part of that function, it sets and maintains a framework of mandatory standards for its members.

The IFoA's current standards framework comprises: (i) The Actuaries' Code – an overarching, mandatory ethical code of practice; (ii) Actuarial Profession Standards (APSs) – build upon the Code and impose specific requirements, often for particular practice areas or types of work; (iii) Non-mandatory guidance – to assist with the application of the IFoA's standards framework.
There are also Guidance Notes for specific topics, although these are no longer produced by the IFoA.
The IFoA requires members carrying out work within UK Geographic Scope to comply with the Financial Reporting Council's (FRC's) Technical Actuarial Standards (TASs).

The Code, all current APSs and non-mandatory guidance can be found under the 'Upholding Standards' section of the IFoA's website (actuaries.org.uk/standards). The FRC's TASs can be found at frc.org.uk/actuaries/actuarial-policy/technical-actuarial-standards.

key_terms.definition: The UK professional body for actuaries, responsible for setting and maintaining ethical standards and regulating the actuarial profession in the public interest.
Mandatory standards issued by the IFoA that build upon the Actuaries' Code and impose specific requirements, often for particular practice areas or types of work.
Technical standards issued by the FRC that are mandatory for IFoA members undertaking technical actuarial work within the FRC's UK Geographic Scope.

subsections.title: The Actuaries' Code
bullets: The Actuaries' Code is the overarching ethical code of the IFoA. All members of the IFoA are expected to uphold the Code, including student members.

The Code applies at all times to members' conduct in their work as actuaries and is also taken into account where members' conduct in other contexts could reasonably be considered to reflect on the actuarial profession.

The Code sets out six core principles that all IFoA members are expected to observe to support the IFoA in acting in the public interest: (a) integrity; (b) competence and care; (c) impartiality; (d) compliance; (e) speaking up; (f) communication.
The Code applies at all times to members' conduct in relation to an actuarial role and also to other conduct if that conduct could reasonably be considered to reflect upon the actuarial profession.
The content of the Code is outside the scope of Subject SA1, however all members (students and actuaries) of the IFoA are expected to be familiar with the Code and comply with its principles.

key_terms.definition: The overarching mandatory ethical code of the IFoA, focused on six principles: integrity, competence and care, impartiality, compliance, speaking up, and communication. Applies to all IFoA members including students.

exam_qa.q: List the six core principles of the IFoA Actuaries' Code.
exam_qa.a: Integrity; competence and care; impartiality; compliance; speaking up; communication.

subsections.title: Actuarial Profession Standards (APSs)
bullets: Members are required to comply with all applicable provisions of APSs.

APS L1 – Duties and Responsibilities of Life Assurance Actuaries: relevant to all members once appointed to one of the statutory roles. The main statutory actuarial role relevant to SA1 is the Chief Actuary (covered in Chapter 11).

APS L2 – The Financial Services and Markets Act 2000 (Communications by Actuaries) Regulations 2003: provides guidance relating to the statutory obligation to 'whistleblow'. Matters that may need to be communicated to regulators include: contravention of legislation by an insurer; significant risk that an insurer's assets may become insufficient to meet liabilities; significant risk that the insurer did not or may not take into account policyholder interests.

APS X1 – Applying Standards to Actuarial Work (effective 1 July 2017): sets out principles to be applied by members to determine which standards they must or should be applying to a piece of work. Mainly relevant to members undertaking work outside the UK geographic scope of the TASs.

APS X2 – Review of Actuarial Work: applies to all members and relates to the need to consider the extent to which review (including independent peer review) is required for any 'actuarial work'. Outlines factors to consider when deciding whether review is required (eg complexity, significance, extent of judgement required). Reviewers should have appropriate experience and expertise and be able to perform the review objectively.

APS X3 – The Actuary as an Expert in Legal Proceedings: applies to actuaries appointed to act as expert witnesses in legal proceedings held before courts, tribunals or similar. Members must not act as expert witnesses if they do not have the necessary knowledge and skill or if their advice is not independent and objective. The fee paid to the expert witness should not be linked to the outcome of the proceedings.

APS G1 – The Chief Actuary in Non-Life Insurance: may also be relevant to health and care insurers. Sets out specific obligations including a requirement to consider whether it is necessary to discuss the role with their predecessor before taking it on and to clearly communicate the standpoint from which they are acting.
APS QA1 – Quality Assurance Scheme for Organisations: promotes the application by organisations of effective quality controls, in order to assure high quality in relation to actuarial work.

key_terms.definition: APS covering Duties and Responsibilities of Life Assurance Actuaries; relevant once appointed to a statutory actuarial role such as Chief Actuary.
APS providing guidance on the statutory obligation to whistleblow under the Financial Services and Markets Act 2000 (Communications by Actuaries) Regulations 2003.
APS setting out principles to determine which actuarial standards apply to a piece of work; mainly relevant for work outside UK TAS geographic scope.
APS covering the review of actuarial work, including the need to consider whether independent peer review is required.
APS governing actuaries acting as expert witnesses in legal proceedings; requires independence, objectivity, and appropriate knowledge and skill.
APS covering the Chief Actuary in Non-Life Insurance; also relevant to health and care insurers. Sets specific obligations including predecessor consultation.
Quality Assurance Scheme for Organisations APS; promotes effective quality controls to assure high quality actuarial work at the organisational level.

exam_qa.q: Describe the main responsibilities of the Chief Actuary.
exam_qa.a: To co-ordinate the calculation of the technical provisions; to ensure the appropriateness of methodologies, underlying models and assumptions used; to assess the sufficiency and quality of data; to compare the best estimate against experience; to inform the governing body of the reliability and adequacy of technical provisions; to express an opinion on the overall underwriting policy; to express an opinion on the adequacy of reinsurance arrangements; to contribute to the effective implementation of the risk management system, with particular regard to risk modelling.

exam_qa.q: Under APS L2, what matters may need to be communicated to the regulator?
exam_qa.a: Contravention of legislation by an insurer; significant risk that an insurer's assets may become insufficient to meet its liabilities; significant risk that the insurer did not or may not take into account policyholder interests.

exam_qa.q: What conditions must be met for an IFoA member to act as expert witness under APS X3?
exam_qa.a: The member must have the necessary level of knowledge and skill; their advice must be independent and objective; and the fee paid must not be linked to the outcome of the proceedings.

subsections.title: Guidance Notes
bullets: Guidance Notes include specific mandatory requirements which members are expected to comply with, as well as non-mandatory material.
Guidance Notes in particular are beyond the scope of Subject SA1.

subsections.title: Other Non-Mandatory Resource Material
bullets: The IFoA also provides non-mandatory guidance and resource material for members, their employers and other stakeholders.

'Speaking Up: A Guide for Members' – sets out the IFoA's view of good practice in relation to speaking up. The IFoA has also produced 'Whistleblowing: A Guide for Employers of Actuaries'. A confidential advice line gives guidance on when and how best to raise concerns.

'Conflicts of Interest: A Guide for Members' – builds on the provisions of the Actuaries' Code in relation to conflicts of interest and sets out views on good practice regarding identifying conflicts and how they might be managed and reconciled.

'A Guide for Ethical Data Science' – intended to complement existing ethical and professional guidance and is aimed at addressing the ethical and professional challenges of working in a data science setting.
Details of the advice line and all non-mandatory guides can be found at: actuaries.org.uk/standards/standards-and-guidance/non-mandatory-guidance.

subsections.title: New TAS Framework
bullets: In the UK, responsibility for setting and maintaining 'technical' standards rests with the Financial Reporting Council (FRC), which also acts as an oversight body for the regulation of actuaries in the UK by the IFoA. The FRC is independent of the IFoA.

In December 2016, the FRC published a new framework of Technical Actuarial Standards (TASs) which applies to 'technical actuarial work' completed on or after 1 July 2017. Compliance with the TASs is mandatory for all IFoA members undertaking technical actuarial work within the FRC's UK Geographic Scope.

The TAS framework covers technical actuarial work done in relation to UK operations of entities, as well as overseas operations which report into the UK, within the context of UK law or regulation. It is not limited to work undertaken by an actuary.
The UK FRC is planned to relaunch as the Audit, Reporting and Governance Authority (ARGA) in 2024, which is expected to provide oversight of the UK actuarial profession.
Subject SA1 candidates are expected to be familiar with the underlying principles of the relevant TASs, but will not be examined on the detail.

The new framework of TASs consists of: (i) a framework document; (ii) a generic Technical Actuarial Standard (TAS 100); (iii) three specific TASs (TAS 200, 300 and 400); (iv) a glossary of defined terms used in the TASs.

Framework document: sets out the authority, scope and application of the TASs and also sets out the FRC's Reliability Objective – that users for whom actuarial information is created should be able to place a high degree of reliance on that information's relevance, transparency of assumptions, completeness and comprehensibility, including the communication of any uncertainty inherent in the information.

TAS 100 (Generic TAS) – Principles for Actuarial Work: comprises six high-level outcome-focused principles applicable to all technical actuarial work within the FRC's UK Geographic Scope. These are: judgement; data; assumptions; models; communications; documentation. Supported by more detailed 'provisions'. The proportionality principle applies – TAS 100 does not require work disproportionate to the nature, scale and complexity of the decision or the benefit to users.

Specific TASs: TAS 200 (Insurance), TAS 300 (Pensions), TAS 400 (Funeral Plan Trusts). TAS 200 is of most relevance to SA1. Its purpose is to promote high-quality technical actuarial work on particular insurance matters where the FRC has identified a high degree of risk to the public interest. TAS 200 applies to health and care, life, and general insurance.

Scope of TAS 200 includes: technical actuarial work relating to prudential regulatory balance sheets; financial statements; general insurance business written by Lloyd's Syndicates; general insurance tax; prudential regulatory capital requirements and the Own Risk and Solvency Assessment; insurance transformations; audit and assurance; pricing frameworks; the exercise of discretion concerning with-profits life insurance policies.

TAS 200 supports the six TAS 100 principles and also includes specific provisions for the listed technical actuarial work. Under the assumptions principle, best estimate, central estimate or similar measures shall be neither optimistic nor pessimistic and shall not contain adjustments to reflect a desired outcome. Under the models principle, communications shall describe the nature of any cashflows that are quantified including their timing.

key_terms.definition: The UK body responsible for setting and maintaining technical actuarial standards (TASs); also acts as an oversight body for the regulation of actuaries in the UK by the IFoA.

Generic Technical Actuarial Standard comprising six high-level, outcome-focused principles (judgement, data, assumptions, models, communications, documentation) applicable to all technical actuarial work within the FRC's UK Geographic Scope.

Specific Technical Actuarial Standard covering insurance (health and care, life, and general insurance). Promotes high-quality technical actuarial work on insurance matters where the FRC has identified high public-interest risk.

The FRC's overarching objective that users of actuarial information should be able to place a high degree of reliance on its relevance, transparency of assumptions, completeness and comprehensibility, including communication of any inherent uncertainty.

The principle in TAS 100 that nothing in the standard should be interpreted as requiring work that is disproportionate to the nature, scale and complexity of the decision or assignment to which the work relates and the benefit users would expect to obtain from it.
The planned successor body to the FRC, expected to provide oversight of the UK actuarial profession.

exam_qa.q: What are the six high-level principles in TAS 100?
exam_qa.a: Judgement; data; assumptions; models; communications; documentation.

exam_qa.q: What is the FRC's Reliability Objective?
exam_qa.a: That users for whom actuarial information is created should be able to place a high degree of reliance on that information's relevance, transparency of assumptions, completeness and comprehensibility, including the communication of any uncertainty inherent in the information.

exam_qa.q: What does the assumptions principle in TAS 200 require regarding best estimate calculations?
exam_qa.a: Measures, assumptions and judgements used to derive any estimates described as 'best estimate', 'central estimate' or similar shall be neither optimistic nor pessimistic and shall not contain adjustments to reflect a desired outcome.

subsections.title: Introduction
bullets: Most major actuarial organisations around the world have their own frameworks of professional standards and codes of professional conduct.
Whilst there are some differences, there is a considerable amount of consistency in the approach taken to standard-setting by many of the major actuarial organisations around the world.
One reason for this consistency is the influence of the International Actuarial Association (IAA) and the Actuarial Association of Europe (AAE).

subsections.title: International Actuarial Association (IAA)
bullets: The IAA is a worldwide association of professional actuarial bodies. Its aim is to represent the actuarial profession and promote its role, reputation and recognition in the international domain.
One of the ways it does this is by developing 'model' International Standards of Actuarial Practice (ISAPs).

There is no requirement placed upon member associations to implement those model standards, but the IAA encourages its member associations to take one of a number of steps including adopting those ISAPs directly or having in place standards of practice that are substantially consistent with them.

There are currently (May 2023) 74 full members of the IAA including the IFoA from the UK, the Society of Actuaries from the USA, and actuarial organisations from South Africa, Australia, Ireland, China and India.

As model standards, ISAPs are not binding on any individual actuary unless their actuarial membership association requires them to apply those standards. The overall aim of ISAPs is to achieve consistency of standards applying to actuaries internationally.

key_terms.definition: A worldwide association of professional actuarial bodies whose aim is to represent the actuarial profession internationally and promote consistency in standards through model International Standards of Actuarial Practice (ISAPs).

Model standards of actuarial practice developed by the IAA. Not binding on individual actuaries unless adopted by their membership association, but designed to promote international consistency of standards.

exam_qa.q: Are ISAPs binding on individual actuaries?
exam_qa.a: No. As model standards, ISAPs are not binding on any individual actuary unless their actuarial membership association requires them to apply those standards.

subsections.title: Actuarial Association of Europe (AAE)
bullets: The AAE aims to promote the actuarial profession in Europe. Its role is to provide advice and opinions to the various organisations of the European Union – the Commission, the Council of Ministers, the European Parliament, EIOPA and their various committees – on actuarial issues in European legislation.

The AAE develops European Standards of Actuarial Practice (ESAPs), which are also model standards of practice. Much like the IAA's ISAPs, these are not binding on individual actuaries; however the AAE encourages its member associations and other actuarial standard-setters to have in place standards of practice that are substantially consistent with its ESAPs.
The AAE has issued ESAPs on areas such as General Actuarial Practice and Actuarial Practice in relation to the ORSA under Solvency II.

The AAE has also developed a 'Code of Professional Conduct' which has a slightly different status to model ESAPs in that member associations of the AAE are required to have a code that reflects the requirements of the AAE Code.

The values required by the AAE Code of Professional Conduct are: concern for the public interest; integrity; independence; collaboration and respect; transparency and accountability. These are similar to the principles underlying the IFoA's Actuaries' Code.

key_terms.definition: A European actuarial body that promotes the actuarial profession in Europe, develops model European Standards of Actuarial Practice (ESAPs), and provides actuarial advice to EU institutions.
Model standards of actuarial practice developed by the AAE. Not binding on individual actuaries but member associations are encouraged to have standards substantially consistent with them.

An AAE conduct code that member associations are required to reflect in their own codes. Its values are: concern for the public interest, integrity, independence, collaboration and respect, and transparency and accountability.

exam_qa.q: What are the key values in the AAE Code of Professional Conduct?
exam_qa.a: Concern for the public interest; integrity; independence; collaboration and respect; transparency and accountability.

exam_qa.q: How does the status of the AAE Code of Professional Conduct differ from the AAE's ESAPs?
exam_qa.a: ESAPs are model standards that member associations are encouraged (but not required) to adopt. The AAE Code of Professional Conduct has a stronger status – member associations of the AAE are required to have a code that reflects its requirements.

15.summary:
Principles of professional conduct
• Actuaries are expected to comply with any relevant professional standards or guidance set by the professional body to which they belong. Standards evolve over time to reflect the market, regulations and international actuarial standards.
• Typically principles-based and include codes of conduct and disciplinary procedures.

Professional Standards Framework – UK
• The Actuaries' Code – sets out 6 core principles: integrity, competence and care, impartiality, compliance, speaking up and communication.
• Actuarial Profession Standards (APSs) – the IFoA has issued the following APSs: APS L1: Duties and Responsibilities of Life Assurance Actuaries; APS L2: The Financial Services and Markets Act 2000 (Communications by Actuaries) Regulations 2003; APS X1: Applying Standards to Actuarial Work; APS X2: Review of Actuarial Work; APS X3: The Actuary as an Expert in Legal Proceedings; APS G1: The Chief Actuary in Non-Life Insurance; APS QA1: Quality Assurance Scheme for Organisations.
• Guidance Notes – specific mandatory requirements members are expected to comply with.
• Other non-mandatory resource material – issued by the IFoA, include: Speaking up – a guide for actuaries; Speaking up – a guide for employers of actuaries; Conflicts of interest – a guide for actuaries; Ethical data science.
• Technical Actuarial Standards – principles-based and issued by the FRC, of relevance to Subject SA1 are: TAS 100 (Generic TAS): Principles for Actuarial Work; TAS 200: Insurance. The FRC has also developed a 'Framework for FRC Technical Actuarial Standards' describing the authority, scope and application of all TASs, and a glossary of terms.

International Actuarial Professional Standards
• Consistency in the approach to setting professional standards is influenced by the International Actuarial Association (IAA) and the Actuarial Association of Europe (AAE): IAA – worldwide association of professional actuarial bodies, develops model International Standards of Actuarial Practice (ISAPs), encourages member associations to adopt or have standards that are consistent with these; AAE – fulfils a similar role within Europe, also provides advice and opinions to organisations within the EU, develops European Standards of Actuarial Practice (ESAPs) similarly to ISAPs, has a 'Code of Professional Conduct' and requires member associations to have a code that reflects these requirements.

16.title: Capital Management

subsections.title: Chapter Overview
bullets: Capital requirements are considered from three viewpoints: the company's own (economic capital), the regulator's (regulatory capital), and the rating agencies' (rating agency capital). These are covered in Section 1.
It is not sufficient to look only at current capital availability — companies project solvency positions into the future for various reasons covered in Section 2.

Section 3 covers the sources of capital for health and care insurance companies and how available capital changes over time, including proprietary vs mutual companies and market-consistent valuations.
Section 4 discusses ongoing solvency assessment; Section 5 explores the inextricable link between capital, risk and economic value.
Section 6 covers methods of raising capital or reducing required capital (equity, subordinated debt, securitisation, reinsurance, derivatives, ALM).
This chapter includes some references to with-profits policies; detailed knowledge of these is not required for this course.

subsections.title: Perspectives on Capital and Types of Required Capital Assessment
bullets: Capital strength is assessed from the policyholder, regulator or shareholder perspective. Policyholders and regulators focus on meeting obligations in extreme circumstances; shareholders focus on maintaining a rating and writing new business.

The most onerous perspective varies: for some companies the shareholders require more capital than the regulator; for others the regulatory requirement is more onerous. This depends on the nature of in-force business and the company's risk appetite.

Two key concepts: required capital — the capital needed to support the business; and available capital — the capital available to meet that requirement. In health and care insurance the word 'capital' can mean either, so context is important.
Available capital = value of assets − value of liabilities. The definitions of both need to be clarified (e.g. BEL vs technical provisions under Solvency II).

For a proprietary company, available capital measures shareholders' capital currently invested. For a mutual, available capital supports the corporate entity and is not earmarked for distribution to current policyholders.
Required capital: the level of assets required in excess of liabilities so that policyholder claims can be met with a high degree of certainty when they fall due.

Three types of required capital assessment: (1) Economic capital — an internal determination based on the company's risk profile, risk appetite and ongoing business strategy; (2) Regulatory capital — solvency requirements prescribed by regulators, defining asset/liability values and capital requirements; (3) Rating agency capital — the view of rating agencies, whose adequacy standards matter for companies wishing to achieve or maintain a particular credit rating.
All three approaches consider policyholder protection to some extent, as there is a link between meeting policyholder claims and the company's broader risk appetite.

Reasons capital is required — split into two categories: (1) Risk capital: to cover day-to-day risks including market risk, credit risk, insurance risk (e.g. mortality, persistency, expenses), operational risk (e.g. outsourcing, terrorism, systems failures), liquidity risk, and group risk; (2) Working capital: to support the ongoing business strategy — fund new business (including attracting intermediaries), fund overheads and organic expansion costs (hardware, software, product development, premises, overseas initiatives), and acquire other companies or blocks of business.

Capital needs can be exacerbated by: mis-selling compensation costs (e.g. PPI); enforced flat charging structures (cap on insurer charges); lower investment returns in low-inflation environments; adverse demographic changes (e.g. increasing longevity raising LTCI costs); implications of treating customers fairly (TCF); and changing distribution patterns (e.g. introducing internet sales).

Companies subject to Solvency II or similar regimes are required to look at all risks as part of the ORSA, making them increasingly aware of the capital employed and the need to manage it and the return on it more effectively.

key_terms.definition: The level of assets required in excess of liabilities so that policyholder claims (or shareholder requirements) can be met with a high degree of certainty when they fall due.
The capital available to meet the required capital — equal to the value of assets minus the value of liabilities, with both values needing careful definition.
An internal determination of required capital based on the company's own risk profile, risk appetite and ongoing business strategy.
Solvency requirements prescribed by regulators, defining the regulatory value of assets, liabilities and associated capital requirements.
Capital adequacy as assessed by rating agencies; important for companies wishing to achieve or maintain a particular credit rating.
Capital required to cover the day-to-day risks a company faces: market, credit, insurance, operational, liquidity and group risks.
Capital required to support the company's ongoing business strategy, including funding new business, organic expansion and acquisitions.

exam_qa.q: From what three perspectives is capital strength typically assessed, and what is each perspective primarily concerned with?
exam_qa.a: Policyholder and regulator perspectives: primarily concerned with the company's ability to meet its obligations in extreme circumstances. Shareholder perspective: concerned with whether the company has sufficient capital to target a particular credit rating and/or to continue writing new business.

exam_qa.q: Distinguish between risk capital and working capital.
exam_qa.a: Risk capital is capital required to cover day-to-day risks (market, credit, insurance, operational, liquidity, group). Working capital supports the ongoing business strategy: funding new business, overheads and development costs of organic expansion (hardware, software, product development, premises, overseas), and acquisitions of companies or blocks of business.

subsections.title: Why Solvency Projections Are Fundamental
bullets: There are several reasons why projecting solvency is fundamental to the successful management of a health and care insurer.
To understand and monitor the evolution of the company's risk profile over time.

To allow the impact of key business strategy decisions to be assessed — in particular, to show the amount of new business strain and hence the volume of new business that can be supported by available capital.
To estimate the pattern of capital releases, assess whether the company is achieving a suitable return on capital, and assess the cost of holding required capital when calculating the embedded value.

To meet statutory requirements — for example, Solvency II requires projections under Pillar 2 ORSA; projections of solvency capital are also needed to determine the risk margin under Pillar 1 and may form part of the SCR calculation if an internal model is used.

To support successful risk measurement and risk management more broadly — projections can be used with stress and reverse stress testing (starting from a known adverse outcome, such as breaching a regulatory capital requirement, and working out what events could cause it) to help companies understand their risks.

key_terms.definition: A Solvency II Pillar 2 requirement for insurers to regularly assess their own risk and solvency position, including forward-looking projections of solvency capital.
Starting from a known adverse outcome (e.g. breaching a regulatory capital requirement) and working backwards to identify what events could cause that outcome.
The initial capital cost of writing new business, reflecting the difference between premiums received and the upfront costs and reserves required.

exam_qa.q: List the main reasons why projecting solvency is fundamental to the successful management of a health and care insurance company.
exam_qa.a: (1) To understand and monitor the evolution of the company's risk profile. (2) To assess the impact of key business strategy decisions, including the volume of new business supportable. (3) To estimate the pattern of capital releases and assess return on capital / cost of holding capital for embedded value purposes. (4) To meet statutory requirements (e.g. Solvency II ORSA, risk margin determination, internal model SCR). (5) To support broader risk measurement and management, including stress and reverse stress testing.

subsections.title: Sources of Available Capital
bullets: In an insurance context, available capital = value of assets − value of liabilities. The definition of both terms may differ between economic, regulatory and rating agency assessments (Section 4).

Proprietary companies: the nucleus of available capital is the initial capital injected by proprietors. Over time, available capital is augmented by: additional amounts subscribed by shareholders or invested via subordinated loan stock; profits retained within the company (including 100% of with-profits surplus to which shareholders are entitled). Available capital is depleted by: distributions to shareholders as dividends; losses incurred on without-profits business (shareholders bear 100% of any losses).

Mutual companies: available capital typically stems from: any initial (or occasionally subsequent) capital injections; consistent under-distribution of surplus in respect of past generations of with-profits policyholders (often not deliberate, but a result of imprecise historic calculation techniques).
For both company types it is important to recognise that capital availability can be viewed from both a regulatory and a realistic perspective.

Most mutual health and care insurers operate on an 'entity' basis: available capital belongs to the corporate entity, not to any particular generation of policyholders, and is therefore not earmarked for distribution to current policyholders.

In a mutual, the amount of available capital may vary over time due to smoothing: if asset values rise significantly but liabilities are smoothed upward only slowly, the resultant increase in available capital needs to be retained to support the reverse situation (when assets fall and liabilities are smoothed downward slowly).

key_terms.definition: The process by which a mutual organisation transfers to being a proprietary (shareholder-owned) company — by sale to another company or by floating on a stock exchange.

The approach by which available capital in a mutual is regarded as belonging to the corporate entity rather than to any specific generation of policyholders, and is therefore not earmarked for distribution.
Debt whose repayment is subordinated to policyholder claims; counts as available capital from a policyholder perspective (increases free assets) but is a liability from a shareholder perspective.

subsections.title: Assessing Available Capital — Market-Consistent Valuations
bullets: Techniques for assessing available capital have evolved; one commonly used measure of assets and liabilities is market value.

The traditional approach of valuing liabilities using an expected earned rate on assets can give advance credit for any risk premium included in the return, which may not be suitable. There must be consistency between asset and liability valuation methods.

The market value of assets can usually be directly observed, but liabilities must be estimated using market-consistent techniques. The difference between market value of assets and market-consistent value of liabilities is referred to as 'realistic capital'.

In a market-consistent valuation, liability cashflows (not usually traded) are valued in line with the traded assets they most closely resemble: e.g. a fixed liability due in ten years is valued in line with a ten-year zero-coupon bond; an embedded financial option is valued in line with the market price of a similar option.

Where no exact traded price exists, market-consistent valuations use option pricing techniques and interpolation/extrapolation from existing prices to arrive at a value in a way that ensures no arbitrage is introduced (switching between asset classes should not appear to create value).

Simplified and alternative approaches are used in practice to make the valuation process easier: certainty equivalent valuation, risk-neutral valuation, and state-price deflators (stochastic discount factors that allow values to be presented using 'real-world' expectations rather than the less familiar 'risk-neutral' expectations).

Market-consistent techniques appropriately allow for non-diversifiable (systematic) risks — market risk and credit risk — which vary with interest rates, equity levels, volatilities, credit spreads and defaults.

Insurance cashflows are also subject to non-market risks (e.g. mortality, lapse rates). The appropriate allowance when estimating market value of liabilities is a complex area; however, economic theory says the appropriate starting point is best estimate assumptions. A risk margin is then typically added (e.g. via Solvency II technical provisions) because anyone accepting such a liability will require compensation for the risk that mortality or lapses may be worse than expected.

Realistic capital is the best measure of available capital from both policyholder and shareholder perspectives: from the policyholder viewpoint it captures all net assets available to meet claims (using best estimate liabilities) and its distribution best measures downside risk exposure; from the shareholder viewpoint it defines the capital actually locked into the company.

key_terms.definition: The difference between market value of assets and market-consistent value of liabilities; regarded as the best measure of available capital from both policyholder and shareholder perspectives.
A valuation approach in which liability cashflows are valued in line with traded assets of similar characteristics, ensuring no arbitrage is introduced.
Valuation of a derivative or risky asset by discounting expected payoffs using a risk-neutral probability measure; introduced in earlier actuarial subjects.
Stochastic discount factors that allow values to be presented using real-world expectations rather than risk-neutral expectations; used as an alternative market-consistent valuation approach.

Risks that cannot be diversified away — e.g. market and credit risk (interest rate movements, equity levels, volatilities, credit spreads, defaults) — which are appropriately captured within market-consistent liability values.

exam_qa.q: Explain what is meant by a market-consistent valuation of liabilities.
exam_qa.a: Liability cashflows (which are not usually traded) are valued in line with the traded assets they most closely resemble. Where no exact traded price exists, option pricing techniques and interpolation/extrapolation from existing prices are used in a way that ensures no arbitrage is introduced. The result ensures that the choice of backing assets does not affect the value of the liability.

exam_qa.q: Why is realistic capital considered the best measure of available capital from a policyholder perspective?
exam_qa.a: (1) It captures the value of all net assets available to meet policyholder claims and includes a realistic value of all existing liabilities (best estimate of assets needed to cover claims). (2) It captures the interaction between asset and liability values and market movements, so its distribution best measures policyholder exposure to downside risk. (3) Requiring that policyholder claims be met with a given probability is equivalent to requiring that realistic capital is positive with the same probability — it is only when realistic capital is negative that shareholders would possibly allow the company to default.

subsections.title: Economic Capital
bullets: Capital requirements cannot be assessed adequately by only considering the current value of liabilities and available assets — health and care insurers operate in a volatile environment, particularly with respect to market risk, and must be able to meet liabilities in those circumstances.

The company also needs to fund its ongoing business strategy; this requires projections that allow for both the size and probability of downside risks and the capital needed to support the future new business strategy.

Economic capital: an internal assessment of the value of assets required in excess of liabilities, allowing for the company's ongoing business strategy, so that claims can be met with a high degree of certainty when they fall due.

Economic capital is calculated by determining realistic capital across a number of scenarios using two approaches: (1) Stochastic modelling — commonly used to assess market and credit risks by running realistic capital across a large number of real-world economic scenarios; can also be used for other risks where a distribution of outcomes is available (e.g. stochastic morbidity models for CI, multi-state models for IP). (2) Stress tests — where the distribution of a particular risk is less clearly understood, the impact is assessed by applying stress tests to realistic capital.
Interdependencies between the various risks must be assessed in order to determine the overall economic capital requirement.

Time horizon for economic capital: some companies consider a one-year period; others the full run-off of the in-force book. There is no single correct answer. If risks can be managed through capital markets (e.g. hedged), a shorter horizon is sensible; if risks cannot be hedged, a longer horizon may be preferred. Calibration of stresses/scenarios must use a consistent time horizon and time step frequency.
Under Solvency II, all companies must assess their economic capital through the ORSA. Insurance companies in many other jurisdictions outside Europe also produce an ORSA.

key_terms.definition: An internal assessment of assets required in excess of liabilities, allowing for the company's ongoing business strategy and all risks, so that claims can be met with a high degree of certainty.
Running the realistic capital calculation across a large number of real-world economic scenarios to assess the distribution of capital outcomes, particularly for market and credit risks.
Assessment of the impact of a particular risk on realistic capital when the distribution of outcomes is less well understood; applied where stochastic modelling is not feasible.

exam_qa.q: Describe the two approaches used to calculate economic capital.
exam_qa.a: (1) Stochastic modelling: realistic capital is calculated across a large number of real-world economic scenarios, commonly used for market and credit risks. Can also be used for other risks when a distribution of outcomes is available (e.g. stochastic morbidity models for CI or multi-state models for IP). (2) Stress tests: where the distribution of a particular risk is less clearly understood, the impact is assessed by applying stress tests to realistic capital. Interdependencies between risks must also be assessed.

subsections.title: Regulatory Capital
bullets: Regardless of the outcome of any economic capital calculation, a health and care insurer must be able to meet its regulatory capital requirements.

Despite the greater risk sensitivity of many modern regulatory regimes, regulatory capital calculations may target a different measure (and level) of security for policyholders than internal economic capital calculations. For example, internal calculations could allow for premiums beyond applicable contract boundaries.
Any regulatory requirement to undertake an ORSA may include a solvency capital projection which may be equivalent or similar to an internal economic capital assessment.

subsections.title: Rating Agency Capital
bullets: The financial strength rating obtained by a company can drive the cost of raising additional capital and can also affect the health and care market's willingness to do business with the company.
It is therefore important for health and care insurers to understand the capital adequacy standards used by the rating agencies.
In recent years, rating agency capital adequacy standards have moved to embrace economic capital measures and other internal assessments of capital requirements (e.g. the ORSA).

subsections.title: Modelling — Practical Considerations and Proxy Models
bullets: Some forms of solvency projection, particularly for regulatory purposes, can raise practical difficulties: in theory, the probability of insolvency requires stochastic calculations to assess regulatory balance sheets at future time steps within a stochastic real-world model of future investment and insurance conditions — so-called 'stochastic within stochastic' or 'nested calculations'.

The outer model projects what is expected to happen to assets and liabilities in the future (a real-world stochastic model projecting investment returns, interest rates, inflation, withdrawals, claims, etc.).
The inner model calculates the liability at any particular time (e.g. pricing an option or guarantee) and would usually be calibrated to be market-consistent.

Nested calculations can require, say, 10,000 inner simulations for each time step of each of 10,000 outer simulations — an enormous number of calculations. It is therefore often desirable to simplify.

Simplification techniques include: closed-form solutions to estimate the regulatory balance sheet (e.g. the Black-Scholes formula for options/guarantees rather than a stochastic inner model); and replicating portfolios (a type of proxy model where assets are chosen to closely replicate liability behaviour).

The number of model runs required for economic capital or Solvency II internal capital models presents additional problems; it is often not computationally feasible to run the full model as many times as needed for all required scenarios. Short timescales for Solvency II reporting compound this.

Therefore, in recent years, several different types of 'proxy model' (also called 'light models') have been developed to allow much quicker and more frequent model runs for high-level decision-making or analysis.

Proxy model techniques include: polynomial or curve-fitting approaches; kriging (a form of multi-dimensional interpolation modelled by Gaussian processes); and replicating portfolio approaches. All are designed to be a simplification of the full ('heavy') model, reproducing its results to within an acceptable degree of approximation.

The replicating portfolio approach selects assets that closely replicate the behaviour of the liabilities, making those assets easier to model. However, this approach is becoming less popular as it can be difficult to find assets that replicate sufficiently well, e.g. due to non-market risks such as morbidity/longevity.

Polynomial proxy model example: using two risk factors x and y, liabilities can be modelled as f(x) = a₁x + a₂x² + a₃y + a₄y² + a₅xy + a₆x²y + a₇xy² + a₈x²y². In practice models include 10–20 risk factors and up to fourth-order terms. Risk factors might include interest rates, equity index level, index volatility, inflation, morbidity rates, etc.

Calibration process for proxy models: fitting scenarios are generated (using expert judgement), the heavy model is run under these scenarios, and the proxy model is calibrated to provide an acceptable fit (e.g. using least squares regression or another optimisation algorithm).

Validation: performed using a set of out-of-sample scenarios, comparing proxy and heavy model results. It is particularly important to obtain a good fit in the tails of the distribution (relevant for SCR or other high-confidence VaR measures).

Overfitting risk: if the heavy model is subject to simulation error, care must be taken not to overfit the proxy model (using too many factors and parameters), as this makes out-of-sample testing likely to be poor. Re-calibration is required periodically as the goodness of fit gradually deteriorates over time.

key_terms.definition: A modelling approach requiring stochastic inner simulations (market-consistent, to value liabilities at a future point) nested within each step of a stochastic outer simulation (real-world, to project future conditions).
The real-world stochastic model used to project future investment and insurance conditions (returns, rates, inflation, claims, withdrawals, etc.).
The market-consistent stochastic model used to calculate the value of liabilities (e.g. cost of options or guarantees) at a future point in time within the outer model.

A simplified approximation of the full ('heavy') actuarial model, designed to reproduce the heavy model results to an acceptable degree of accuracy but much faster, enabling high-frequency model runs.
A type of proxy model where assets are chosen to closely replicate the behaviour of the liabilities, making future modelling of those liabilities easier.
A form of multi-dimensional interpolation modelled by Gaussian processes, used as a proxy model technique for approximating the results of a heavy actuarial model.

exam_qa.q: Explain why 'nested calculations' present practical difficulties in solvency projection modelling.
exam_qa.a: To calculate the probability of insolvency under a regulatory basis, stochastic calculations are required to assess the regulatory balance sheet at future time steps within a stochastic real-world (outer) model. However, calculating the liability at each future time step may itself require a stochastic (market-consistent inner) simulation. This leads to a requirement for, say, 10,000 inner simulations for each time step of each of 10,000 outer simulations — an enormous number of calculations that is often not computationally feasible.

exam_qa.q: Describe how a polynomial proxy model is calibrated and validated.
exam_qa.a: Calibration: fitting scenarios are generated (using expert judgement), the heavy model is run under these scenarios, and the proxy model is fitted to the results using a least squares regression or other optimisation algorithm. Validation: a set of out-of-sample scenarios (not used in fitting) are run through both models and the results compared. A good fit in the tails of the distribution is particularly important if the SCR (or other high-confidence VaR) is being modelled. The model must be re-calibrated periodically as its goodness of fit deteriorates over time.

sections.title: The Relationship Between Capital, Risk and Economic Value

subsections.title: The Inextricable Link: Risk, Capital and Economic Value
bullets: Capital management is much more than ensuring adequate available capital — risk, capital and economic value are inextricably linked (Figure 16.1 in Core Reading).

Key interactions: (1) Risk exposure determines capital needs; required capital is a function of the tail of the risk distribution. (2) The market price of risk is a key driver of value creation; price depends on the overall characteristics of the risk distribution. (3) Capital utilisation has a frictional cost and hence reduces value creation.

Risk drives the amount of required capital and policyholders' propensity to do business with the company — higher risk requires higher capital if policyholders are to continue being attracted to the company.
Risk also drives shareholder value directly: the market price of financial risk is a core driver of shareholders' required return.

The amount of capital held modifies shareholder value through the frictional cost of holding capital (e.g. from a shareholder's perspective, a health and care company may not be the most tax-efficient place to have capital locked in).
Economic capital analysis can help companies optimise their portfolio of risks — identifying opportunities to release economic capital without compromising a given policyholder risk tolerance.

Where a company has high concentration of risk in particular areas, it can reduce required capital by reducing that concentration.
