bullets: Treated similarly to corresponding domestic assets; additional assumptions regarding future exchange rates are needed, consistent with those used in liability outgo projections. There may be a greater risk of default in some cases.

sections.title: Volatility of Capital Values and Investment Income for Different Asset Types

subsections.title: Fixed-Interest Securities
bullets: Income: fixed in monetary terms — no volatility unless a borrower defaults.

Capital value: depends on outstanding term. Very short-dated bonds have very little volatility (limiting case: money-market investments with near-zero volatility). Long-dated bonds can be quite volatile — e.g. if gross redemption yields rise from 6% to 9%, long-dated bond values fall by around 30%; a fall from 10% to 7% adds about 30% to value.

subsections.title: Index-Linked Gilts
bullets: Income: not generally very volatile, though uncertain. E.g. with inflation sequence 3%, 8%, 14%, 3%, 9%, the income stream starting at $100 would be approximately: 100, 103, 111, 127, 131, 142 — different each year but not highly volatile.
Capital value: usually more volatile than fixed-interest gilts of equivalent term, in both real and monetary terms.

subsections.title: Equities
bullets: Income: dividends paid by companies. From a well-diversified portfolio, the income stream is fairly stable — directors generally aim to maintain or increase dividend payments; problem companies are diversified away.

Capital value: potentially very volatile — market prices are fixed by supply and demand and can be heavily influenced by investors' emotions (confidence, fear, greed), sometimes moving to irrationally high or low levels.

subsections.title: Property
bullets: Income: from an individual property, the short-term rent is fairly certain (fixed for lease terms of, say, five years); uncertainty arises at rent review; possibility of zero rent (tenant default, vacant property). From a diversified portfolio, the rental stream is not likely to be very volatile.

Capital value: lack of price data (far fewer transactions) and heterogeneity make values very difficult to assess — estimated periodically by surveyors with significant subjectivity. Not generally as volatile on a day-to-day basis as equities, but can be fairly volatile over an extended period.

subsections.title: Cash and Other Money-Market Investments
bullets: Income: very close to prevailing short-term interest rates; can change significantly from year to year.
Capital value: virtually no volatility.

subsections.title: Overseas Investments
bullets: Any of the above categories can be held overseas, with broadly similar income and capital volatility to equivalent domestic assets; currency fluctuations cause additional volatility in domestic currency (sterling) terms.

sections.title: Suitability of Particular Types of Assets

subsections.title: Asset Features Affecting Suitability
bullets: The suitability of asset types for a health and care insurer depends on asset features including: term; currency; nature (real or fixed); expected return; security; volatility; marketability; expertise required; size of the portfolio; size of the free assets; tax position of the company; admissibility.

Main asset types the examiners expect candidates to discuss in relation to specific classes of business and free assets: ordinary shares and preference shares; government bonds and other fixed-interest securities; index-linked securities; direct property investments; cash and other money-market investments; overseas investments.

key_terms.definition: The extent to which a particular asset can be counted towards regulatory capital or solvency requirements; inadmissible assets may be held but do not count for solvency purposes.

18.summary:
Investment principles
• To maximise investment return, subject to meeting all contractual and TCF obligations and recognising the uncertainties involved and the overall risk that shareholders (of proprietary companies), regulators and policyholders will tolerate.

Asset-liability management
• Main aim is to determine a suitable investment strategy.
• Can investigate asset-liability matching requirements using deterministic or stochastic model office projections.

The effect of liabilities
• Assets chosen that are appropriate for the liabilities, ie assets should have similar term to the liabilities and respond to background influences (eg inflation and currency movements) in the same way that the liabilities do.
• The uncertainty underlying the timing of liability cashflows means that insurers need a high level of marketability for many of their assets.

ALM process
• Ease of matching depends on the type of business involved.
• Involves a liability projection system and an asset model.
• An economic scenario generator (ESG) is a model that produces numerous simulations of possible future economic outcomes, used as an input to the stochastic projection model.

Derivatives
• Used as an integral part of a company's asset-liability strategies, typically to protect against a severe adverse scenario that only arises in limited circumstances.

The effect of free assets
• Large free assets allow a more risky investment policy, eg more volatile assets, fewer 'secure assets', mismatching by term, nature and currency.

Future investment income and capital values
• Asset proceeds projections performed separately for each asset class: fixed-interest and index-linked securities, equities and property. Cash assumed as an infinite income stream, an initial lump sum inflow or a combination.
• Consider the volatility in the capital values and income from assets and how volatility differs between asset types.
• Projections of assets and liabilities useful when the relationship between the two is looked at. Either a deterministic or a stochastic model could be used.
• Asset-liability models can incorporate an assessment of the likely level of solvency at each period in the future.

20.title: Analysis of Surplus, Embedded Value and Profit

subsections.title: Overview
bullets: This chapter follows from Chapter 19 (analysis of experience) and introduces three tools for analysing company performance: analysis of surplus (for long-term business such as CI), analysis of embedded value (for long-term business), and analysis of profit (for short-term business such as PMI).
The chapter also considers customer value as a measure of profitability.

subsections.title: Purpose and Definition
bullets: An analysis of surplus provides a reconciliation between the start-year and end-year supervisory surplus, breaking the change down into contributory factors.
Surplus is defined as assets minus liabilities. 'Surplus arising' over a period is the change in the amount of surplus during that time — this can be positive or negative.
The analysis is an attempt to quantify the various elements that have caused the total change in surplus. It is better described as an 'analysis of surplus arising'.

A complication is 'interaction terms' — for example, where expenses are lower than assumed and investment returns are higher. An element of surplus arises from earning excess return on assets that were not 'supposed' to be there; this could be attributed to either expense surplus or investment return surplus. There is no uniquely correct approach, so a systematic method (Section 3) is used to ensure interaction terms are neither missed nor double-counted.

Reasons to perform an analysis of surplus: show the financial effect of divergences between valuation assumptions and actual experience; determine which assumptions are most financially significant; show the financial effect of writing new business; validate calculations and assumptions; provide a check on valuation data and process; identify non-recurring components; reconcile values for successive years; provide management information; provide data for executive remuneration schemes; provide information for publication; demonstrate that the variance in individual sources is a complete description of the variance in the total; give information on trends for the actuarial control cycle; comply with regulatory requirements (e.g. under Solvency II, profit and loss attribution is an internal model approval requirement).

key_terms.definition: Assets minus liabilities under the applicable supervisory (regulatory) valuation basis.
An element of surplus that arises as a combined effect of two or more sources of variance, making it ambiguous which single source it should be attributed to.
A form of analysis of surplus required under Solvency II as part of the internal model approval process, attributing changes in financial position to their sources.

exam_qa.q: Why might an insurance company want to perform an analysis of surplus?
exam_qa.a: To show the financial effect of divergences between valuation assumptions and actual experience; determine the most financially significant assumptions; show the effect of new business; validate calculations and assumptions; provide a check on data and process; identify non-recurring components to inform distribution decisions; reconcile successive years' values; provide management information and executive remuneration data; provide publication information; demonstrate completeness of the variance description; give trend information for the actuarial control cycle; and comply with regulatory requirements such as Solvency II P&L attribution.

subsections.title: The Sources of Surplus
bullets: Surplus arises as a result of differences between what was expected to happen (per the valuation assumptions, including implicit assumptions such as no new business) and what actually happened. Any change in the valuation assumptions themselves will also give rise to positive or negative surplus.
Possible components of the analysis of a simplified supervisory surplus (assets minus liabilities) are as follows.

Opening adjustments usually reflect changes to the model or data used to calculate the surplus, including corrections, changes to model point groupings, and methodology changes (e.g. changes to economic scenario generators or proxy modelling approaches).

Return on opening surplus results from investment returns on the opening surplus. This may be expressed as the actual return, or as the expected (risk-free) return only, with any actual difference recorded in the next item. For example, a surplus of 200 at the start of the year with 0.5% risk-free return and 1.0% actual return gives an 'expected' return of 1 and a 'difference between actual and expected' of 11 (applied to total assets), or alternatively 2 of 'actual return on surplus' and 10 of 'excess return on BEL-backing assets'.

Economic variances result from differences between actual and expected investment returns, inflation, and currency exchange rates. These will usually be the major source of variation. They include mismatch profits or losses where assets backing liabilities do not move in line with the liabilities following changes in economic conditions. Changes in asset mix may be separately identified. Under Solvency II, where a matching or volatility adjustment is used, the surplus will reflect the difference between actual spread movements (and defaults) relative to those allowed for in the adjustments.

The actual-to-expected investment return item may be split between the actual return relative to an internal view of the expected return, and this expected return relative to the expected investment return (the valuation discount rate).

Changes in economic assumptions may be shown separately or combined with economic variances, due to the close relationship between actual economic outcomes and the expected economic assumptions, particularly regarding yields and bond value movements.

Non-economic (or insurance) variances result from differences between actual and expected demographic and operational factors such as morbidity, mortality, persistency, and expenses. Variances from one-off or exceptional expenses may be identified separately.
Changes to non-economic (or insurance) assumptions may be shown separately or combined with the insurance variances.
New business impacts on surplus if liabilities differ from the assets accrued in respect of new policies (premiums less initial outgo, plus investment return to valuation date).
Other variances might include capital injected into the fund or tax changes.

Unexplained movements arise because it is unrealistic to expect the entire movement in surplus to be fully explained. Management should specify an acceptable level of unexplained movement (e.g. as a percentage of total liabilities), but should be mindful that a small residual could mask material errors that broadly offset each other.

When deciding how to classify variances, consistency from one year to the next is important so that year-by-year comparisons remain meaningful. For example, changes in policyholder lapse rates driven by economic conditions could be classed as either non-economic or economic variance.

key_terms.definition: Surplus arising from differences between actual and expected investment returns, inflation, and currency exchange rates — typically the largest source of variance.
Surplus arising from differences between actual and expected demographic and operational factors such as morbidity, mortality, persistency, and expenses.
Adjustments made to the opening surplus to reflect corrections, methodology changes, or changes to model point groupings before the analysis begins.
The residual difference between the final rolled-forward balance sheet and the actual year-end balance sheet that cannot be attributed to any identified source.

Under Solvency II, an addition to the risk-free discount rate allowed for insurers holding qualifying illiquid assets, derived using EIOPA-published fundamental spreads. Differences between actual and assumed credit experience give rise to investment variances.

exam_qa.q: List the possible components of an analysis of supervisory valuation surplus.
exam_qa.a: Opening adjustments; return on opening surplus; economic variances (investment returns, inflation, currency); changes in economic assumptions; non-economic (insurance) variances (morbidity, mortality, persistency, expenses); changes in non-economic assumptions; new business impact; other variances (e.g. capital injections, tax changes); unexplained movements.

exam_qa.q: Why is consistency in the classification of variances important in an analysis of surplus?
exam_qa.a: Because the purpose of the analysis is partly to identify trends in experience. If the same item is classified differently from one year to the next — for example, lapse rate changes due to economic conditions being classified as non-economic in one year and economic in another — year-by-year comparisons become meaningless and misleading.

sections.title: Quantifying the Surplus Arising from Each Source

subsections.title: The Balance Sheet Projection Approach
bullets: For the analysis of surplus to act as a check that no errors have been made in the valuation, it is essential that a logical and robust calculation process is in place.

A typical process is to project forward the insurer's previous supervisory balance sheet assuming experience was as per the insurer's valuation basis. This involves rolling forward the balance sheet from the previous valuation date (both assets and liabilities) allowing in turn for corrections, assumption changes, changes to economic conditions, new business and experience variances, to produce an expected balance sheet. The actual and expected balance sheets can then be compared, the difference being the 'unexplained'.

The process is equivalent to re-running the valuation model one element at a time. One possible approach is: (1) Re-run the start-year valuation model; (2) Make any opening adjustments to the balance sheet; (3) Run the adjusted model with the new non-economic assumptions to obtain the surplus from assumption changes — each type of change (morbidity, persistency, expense, mortality) should ideally be implemented in turn so that the impact of each can be separately identified; (4) Roll the model forward to the new valuation date using actual investment returns and economic scenarios calibrated at the new valuation date — this gives the total economic variance, which may be further broken down; (5) Add new business written in the inter-valuation period to show the impact of new business on surplus; (6) Adjust for known differences between actual and modelled non-economic experience over the inter-valuation period (e.g. if expenses were higher than expected) — each experience item should be adjusted in turn to identify each separately; (7) Adjust for other items such as capital injections or tax changes; (8) Compare the final rolled-forward balance sheet to the actual year-end balance sheet — any difference is the 'unexplained'.

The amount of surplus from each source depends on the order in which sources are dealt with; there is no uniquely correct method. The company would not usually want to change the method from that used in previous analyses.
It is essential to test the resulting contributions from each item for reasonableness — a contribution so determined may be the difference between two miscalculated items.
The analysis is also unlikely to identify a fundamental error in the balance sheet if it is simply repeated at each valuation.

For morbidity, mortality, or lapse items it may not be possible to get an accurate figure from the modelled approach — the model applies an average rate to all policies while actual claims may be concentrated in large policies. In such cases, the variance may be derived from analysing a file of data movements.
For items such as capital injections or tax changes, the rolled-forward balance sheet must also be adjusted.

key_terms.definition: The standard methodology for analysis of surplus: the opening balance sheet is rolled forward step by step — adjusting for each source of change in turn — to produce an expected year-end balance sheet. The difference from the actual year-end balance sheet is the 'unexplained'.

exam_qa.q: Describe the balance sheet projection approach to quantifying surplus from each source.
exam_qa.a: The previous supervisory balance sheet is rolled forward from the start of the year, changing one element at a time: opening adjustments; non-economic assumption changes (each type separately); rolling forward with actual economic conditions (giving economic variances); adding new business; adjusting for actual vs modelled non-economic experience (each item separately); adjusting for other items such as capital or tax. The final rolled-forward balance sheet is compared to the actual year-end balance sheet — any remaining difference is the unexplained. The change in surplus after each step is attributed to that source.

sections.title: Analysing Solvency II Balance Sheet Surplus

subsections.title: Impact of the Definition of Surplus
bullets: So far the chapter has considered a simplified surplus defined as assets minus best estimate liabilities (BEL). In practice, liabilities may be defined differently — for example, under some regimes they include prudential margins. The definition of liabilities impacts the analysis of surplus.

Under Solvency II, surplus could be defined as assets minus total technical provisions (BEL plus risk margin). In this case the analysis of surplus must also account for changes in the risk margin over the valuation period — for example, changes to the allowances for diversification within the risk margin calculations. Business decisions such as changes in reinsurance strategy may have a significant impact on the risk margin element and may need to be identified separately. Given the complexity of the risk margin calculation, a simplified approach would typically be used.

Similarly, surplus could be defined as assets minus liabilities minus capital requirements. In this case the analysis must also account for changes in the solvency capital requirements, including: changes in the size of components of the SCR; changes from a prescribed regulatory capital model to an internal model approach; inclusion of new risk factors if an internal model approach is used. A proxy model is likely to be used given the modelling complexity.
A full analysis of the Solvency II balance sheet therefore needs to allow for changes in the risk margin and the SCR, in addition to changes in the BEL.

key_terms.definition: Under Solvency II, the component of technical provisions (in addition to the BEL) that represents the cost of holding capital against non-hedgeable risks if the liabilities were transferred to another insurer. Changes in the risk margin form part of the full Solvency II analysis of surplus.

The risk-based capital requirement under Solvency II targeting a 99.5% VaR over one year. Changes in the SCR must be included in any analysis of surplus defined as assets minus liabilities minus capital requirements.

exam_qa.q: What additional components must be considered when performing a full analysis of Solvency II surplus (defined as excess of assets over total technical provisions)?
exam_qa.a: Changes in the risk margin must be included, in addition to changes in the BEL. Sources of risk margin changes include changes to diversification allowances and changes in reinsurance strategy. Given the complexity of the risk margin calculation, a simplified approach would typically be used.

subsections.title: Business Applications
bullets: The results from an analysis of surplus can be used in a number of ways to drive business decisions.

The impact on capital resources of writing new business over the year can be used to provide a limit on the amount of new business that could be written going forward, or as motivation to redesign products to be more capital efficient.

A trend of consistently positive or negative experience surpluses for a particular item (e.g. persistency) could indicate that the best estimate valuation assumption for that item needs to be updated. It may be appropriate to hold greater prudential margins (if allowable under the solvency reporting regime) to allow for greater uncertainty. However, care should be taken as any discrepancy may be due to short-term fluctuation that may not be a longer-term feature — consideration of the trend over a reasonable period (by product type) is important.

Such a trend might also indicate that the design of a contract should be altered — for example, increasing mortality or morbidity surpluses under a unit-linked contract with explicit mortality or morbidity charges might indicate that the charges could be reduced.

A financially weak company may use the analysis of surplus to help drive decisions on how to de-risk the balance sheet and so protect the solvency of the company — for example, a large economic variance in a fund with low levels of surplus may imply that the investment strategy is too risky and should be revisited.
New items identified in the analysis may be used to inform the risk identification process, particularly for highlighting new risks.

exam_qa.q: How can a consistently negative non-economic experience variance for a particular assumption inform an insurer's strategy?
exam_qa.a: It indicates that the best estimate valuation assumption for that item may need to be updated (e.g. if persistency is consistently worse than assumed, the lapse rate assumption should be strengthened). It may also suggest holding greater prudential margins where the regime permits. If it reflects a structural issue (e.g. mortality under a unit-linked contract with explicit mortality charges), the product design itself may need to be revisited — in this case by reducing the charges if surpluses are persistently positive.

sections.title: Analysing the Change in Embedded Value

subsections.title: Components of Analysis of Change
bullets: The embedded value (EV) may consist of: free surplus allocated to the covered business; required capital, less the cost of holding required capital; and the present value of future shareholder cash flows from in-force covered business (PVIF). EV = net assets + PVIF.
When performing an analysis of change in EV over a year, the change in free surplus, change in required capital (where relevant), and change in PVIF are typically identified separately.

For a simplified EV (net assets + PVIF), the key elements of the analysis of change are: return on net assets; expected return on in-force business; experience variances; operating assumption changes; new business contribution; and other items.

Return on net assets: the actual investment return earned on the starting net assets. This is not included in the PVIF calculation and therefore needs to be allowed for separately. For a market-consistent embedded value, the expected return would be the risk-free rate. For the purpose of the analysis, the return may be split into expected return and excess of actual over expected return.

Expected return on in-force business: This is the expected change in the value of the in-force business over the year, calculated on the economic and demographic assumptions at the start of the year. It can be seen as the 'unwind' of the discount rate on the opening value of the in-force business. Using the identity PVIF(t+1) = PVIF(t)(1+r) - S(t), where r is the discount rate and S(t) is expected surplus arising, the change in PVIF is PVIF(t) × r - S(t). The first term (unwind) increases PVIF; the second term (expected surplus transferred to net assets) reduces PVIF but increases net assets by the same amount. So overall expected EV does not change due to in-force business — it just shifts between PVIF and net assets. In a risk-neutral MCEV, EV is expected simply to increase by the risk-free return each year.

Experience variances: the impact of differences between actual and expected experience over the year on the surplus emerging and the value of the in-force business at the end of the year. These may be split into economic and non-economic variances. Differences in actual vs expected surplus arising flow through to net assets; changes in expected future profits flow through to PVIF.

Operating assumption changes: the impact of changes to each element of the valuation basis and EV projection basis, where these changes are often identified separately. The impact of changes to economic assumptions may be identified separately from non-economic assumption changes. Economic assumption changes in the EV projection basis include changes in the risk discount rate. There are two bases required for EV calculations: the projection (experience) basis and the supervisory valuation basis. Both impact the PVIF; only the supervisory valuation basis impacts net assets (though if net assets are not taken at market value — e.g. due to required capital lock-in — the projection basis can also affect them).

New business contribution: assessed by projecting future profits from new business written in the analysis period, including appropriate allowance for the full expenses incurred in acquiring the business. EV does not include the value of future new business, so any new business written results in a change in EV. Where reserving basis contains prudential margins, new business may cause a reduction in net assets but an increase in PVIF; overall EV increases if business is profitable.
Other items: model changes, capital injections, tax changes, exceptional expenses — as for the analysis of surplus.

The above simplified analysis may be refined by splitting each movement component into its separate impact on free surplus, on required capital, and on PVIF. These are then summed to give the total impact on EV.

key_terms.definition: Present Value of In-Force business — the present value of future shareholder cash flows from the in-force covered business, forming part of the embedded value.
The expected increase in PVIF resulting from the passage of time, equal to the discount rate multiplied by the opening PVIF. Represents future profits being one period closer.
The expected change in PVIF over the year under the start-of-year economic and demographic assumptions; equal to PVIF(t) × r – S(t) where r is the discount rate and S(t) is expected surplus arising.

An EV calculated using market-consistent economic assumptions; for such an EV the expected return on net assets and the discount rate both equal the risk-free rate, so EV is expected simply to increase by the risk-free return each year.
The change in EV from new business written in the analysis period, assessed by projecting future profits including all acquisition expenses.

exam_qa.q: Explain why, for an MCEV, the expected surplus from in-force business does not change the total EV.
exam_qa.a: The expected surplus arising during the year is already included in the PVIF at the start of the year. During the year it emerges as actual surplus and transfers to the net assets (free surplus) component. So the net assets increase by S(t) and the PVIF decreases by S(t); the total EV is unchanged by this transfer. The EV only increases due to the 'unwind' (PVIF × risk-free rate), which equals the risk-free return on net assets for an MCEV — so overall EV increases by the risk-free return.

exam_qa.q: Explain why changes in the projection (experience) basis will normally not impact the net assets.
exam_qa.a: Net assets are the excess of assets over liabilities. The value of assets at the calculation date is not impacted by assumptions about the future; assets are taken at market (or fair) value. The value of liabilities depends on the supervisory valuation basis (the cashflows being valued depend only on that basis), not the projection basis. Therefore changes to the projection basis affect only the PVIF component of EV, not the net assets component.

subsections.title: Impact of Solvency Regime on Analysis of Change in Embedded Value
bullets: As outlined in Chapter 14, the applicable solvency regime for a health and care insurer can have implications for the EV calculation and consequently for the analysis of change in EV.
In jurisdictions where supervisory reporting is performed on a prudential basis, the emergence of those prudential margins forms the PVIF component in the analysis of change in EV.

Where the statutory reporting regime requires liabilities stated on a best estimate basis (for example under Solvency II), there would be no explicit future release of prudential margins to form the PVIF component. In this case the PVIF component would be zero, and the analysis of change in EV would effectively be an analysis of change in free surplus.
The analysis would also have to consider the change in required capital (net of the cost of holding it), if this is identified separately from free surplus.

Even where no prudential margins are expected to emerge on a supervisory valuation basis, it is possible that the insurer may expect some PVIF beyond that allowed for in its statutory liabilities — for example under Solvency II where profits were expected beyond the Solvency II contract boundaries.

Where the analysis of change in EV is effectively an analysis of change in free surplus, the process would be similar to that described above for the analysis of surplus, with one additional item: the expected return on the value of in-force (i.e. the risk-free rate applied to the opening PVIF).

Some health and care companies in jurisdictions where Solvency II has been implemented no longer report on an EV, and therefore no longer carry out a regular analysis of change in EV. If the PVIF is zero or immaterial under Solvency II, there is no added value achieved by performing an analysis of change in EV beyond what is already obtained through performing an analysis of surplus.

key_terms.definition: Because Solvency II requires best estimate liabilities, there are typically no prudential margins to release and hence the PVIF is zero or very small; the analysis of change in EV effectively becomes an analysis of change in free surplus.

exam_qa.q: Why do some health and care insurers under Solvency II no longer produce an analysis of change in embedded value?
exam_qa.a: Under Solvency II, liabilities are stated on a best estimate basis, so there are no prudential margins to release into the PVIF. The PVIF is therefore zero or immaterial. In this case an analysis of change in EV provides no additional information beyond what is already provided by an analysis of surplus. Performing an analysis of EV is therefore redundant for many EU insurers.

sections.title: The Analysis of Profit in a Short-Term Insurance Portfolio

subsections.title: Approach and Key Indices
bullets: For long-term insurances, the analysis of surplus looks at the surplus arising each year throughout the lifetime of the policy. For existing business there may be little chance to react to adverse experience because premium rates are usually fixed.

For short-term insurances such as PMI, there is the opportunity at renewal to change prices, product design, underwriting, or claim conditions — so it is useful to analyse profit more regularly throughout the year.

For short-term insurance business such as a PMI portfolio, the company would typically assess the profitability of business by yearly cohort, but perform quarterly analyses to establish up-to-date positions. The quarterly calculations will be fed through to the price being charged for new business, to take account of the most recent changes in claim trends.

Three key indices that the short-term insurer can monitor: (1) Rate of incidence of claim = number of separate claims divided by the exposed to risk; (2) Average cost per claim = total incurred cost of claims divided by the number of separate claims; (3) Loss ratio = total incurred cost of claims divided by the relevant earned premium.
The loss ratio is quick and simple to calculate, but any trends in either frequency or size of claim will be hidden. If data allows, it is more useful to monitor the first two indices separately.

Each of these indices will be calculated for each cohort of business under review and compared with the assumptions made in the original pricing. Careful consideration must be given to the definition of a claim and how claims are recorded in the data.

For PMI, duration-based cohorts are often reviewed since many portfolios exhibit a selection effect. Loss ratios in the early years tend to be lower than those experienced by cohorts of longer duration, depending on the method and stringency of medical underwriting at outset.

Incurred claims must include allowance for the total ultimate expected claim cost: all claims paid to date; all claims reported but for which payment has yet to be made; and all claims incurred but not yet reported (IBNR).

It is vital that the definition of a claim remains consistent so that trends in experience can be meaningfully analysed. Any comparison with other insurers must be done carefully if the insurers may have a different approach to what constitutes a single claim (e.g. market practice may not be uniform if a patient is treated in hospital for a complaint and then re-admitted three months later for a recurrence).

Given that incurred claims include estimates of outstanding claims and IBNR, any comparison between insurers must allow for different reserving bases. It is very unlikely that two insurers would put the same estimate on total ultimate claims when the final claims payments are not yet known.

With the use of a no claims discount (NCD) system of premium rating, it is crucial to be able to assess experience by cohort accurately and quickly, in order to adjust (if necessary) the methodology of promotion/demotion rules. Such a system can only work effectively where there is sophisticated IT support.

For group arrangements, the analysis of profitability is more straightforward in practice, with each scheme's claims compared to premiums and related expenses. This is because group schemes are often experience rated retrospectively, so that past premiums are adjusted for the scheme's own claims experience.

The analysis of smaller PMI schemes (typically arrangements covering two to 100 persons insured) is more complicated, being a mixture of the individual and group approaches. It is not considered examinable for this subject.

key_terms.definition: The number of separate claims divided by the exposed to risk — measures the frequency of claiming.
The total incurred cost of claims divided by the number of separate claims — measures the severity of claims.
The total incurred cost of claims divided by the relevant earned premium — a combined measure of claims experience that hides separate frequency and severity trends.
Total ultimate expected claim cost, including: claims paid to date; reported but not yet settled (RBNS); and incurred but not yet reported (IBNR).

Premium rating system where policyholders are rewarded for claim-free periods with discounts and penalised for claims with higher premiums — requires accurate and timely cohort experience analysis and sophisticated IT.

exam_qa.q: Why is the loss ratio a less useful measure of claims experience than the rate of incidence and average cost per claim?
exam_qa.a: The loss ratio combines claim frequency and claim severity into a single number. If it deteriorates, it is impossible to tell from the ratio alone whether this is due to more claims being made (frequency) or each claim costing more (severity). Knowing the source of deterioration is essential for deciding on corrective action — for example, tightening underwriting would address high incidence, while cost control measures would address high severity. The loss ratio is also quick and easy to calculate but is therefore less informative as a management tool.

exam_qa.q: Why must incurred claims include IBNR when analysing the profitability of a PMI portfolio?
exam_qa.a: The analysis must reflect the total ultimate cost of claims, not just claims that have been reported or settled to date. IBNR claims have occurred but not yet been reported to the insurer — excluding them would understate the true claim cost in a period and distort both the loss ratio and profitability assessment. For a meaningful comparison with premiums earned, the full liability must be estimated.

subsections.title: Customer Value Measurement and Management
bullets: Customer value measurement and management relates to the value that is placed on an existing or potential new customer throughout their lifetime.

'Customer value' is often monitored through persistency rates. However, it may also be based on embedded value measures, including profits arising from the expectation of multiple future policy purchases through cross-selling, fulfilling customers' changing needs over their lifetime.

Analysis of the impact on customer value of different business decisions (e.g. changes to marketing, distribution channel, underwriting approach, product design, pricing) can help the company to maximise its profits or embedded value.

Simulation approaches can be used to achieve this, although the modelling requirements are typically complex — including a need to allow for the potential impact that a change in strategy may have on customer behaviour. There is often limited data or experience on which to base such assumptions, and similarly in relation to potential future policy purchases.

As such, a more 'scenario'-based approach to modelling the impact on customer value could be used, where expert opinion is used to assess the impact of decisions on the business where it is not possible to use traditional financial models.
Embedded value analysis techniques can be used to identify drivers of profit or loss at customer level.

Similarly, health and care insurance companies with large 'legacy' portfolios or closed funds may perform customer value analysis in order to maximise the profit that can be extracted from that portfolio or fund — for example, outsourcing administration or claims management functions may allow costs to be reduced, increasing the value of each policy that remains in force. Any impact on increased lapses as a result of the change in service levels would need to be considered as part of the analysis.

key_terms.definition: The total value placed on an existing or potential new customer throughout their lifetime, taking into account current policy value, potential future policy purchases, and cross-selling opportunities.
The practice of selling additional products to existing customers — a key component of lifetime customer value in health and care insurance.
A closed or run-off portfolio of in-force business from which the insurer aims to extract maximum profit, often through cost reduction initiatives such as outsourcing.

exam_qa.q: How can a health and care insurer use customer value analysis to maximise profit from a closed legacy portfolio?
exam_qa.a: The insurer can use embedded value analysis techniques to identify drivers of profit or loss at customer level. Cost reduction initiatives such as outsourcing administration or claims management can increase the value of each remaining in-force policy. However, any impact on lapse rates resulting from a change in service levels must also be assessed — higher lapses could erode the benefit of cost savings. A scenario-based or simulation approach may be used to model the net impact on customer value.

20.summary:
Analysis of surplus
• Provides a reconciliation between the start-year and end-year supervisory surplus, breaking the change down into contributory factors.

Sources of surplus
• Possible components of an analysis of surplus (where surplus is defined as assets in excess of best estimate liabilities) are: opening adjustments (including corrections, changes to model point groupings, methodology changes); return on opening surplus; economic variances (including those on investment returns (which may be further subdivided), inflation and exchange rates); changes in economic assumptions; non-economic (or insurance) variances; changes in non-economic (or insurance) assumptions; new business; other variances; unexplained movements.
• To analyse Solvency II surplus in full, the analysis should also consider: changes in the risk margin, eg due to changes in the allowances for diversifications within the risk margin calculations; changes in the solvency capital requirements, eg due to changes in the size of components of the solvency capital requirement.

Quantifying the surplus from each source
• Based on rolling forward the balance sheet from the previous valuation date (both assets and liabilities) allowing for corrections, assumption changes, changes to economic conditions, new business and experience variances, to produce an expected balance sheet.
• The actual and expected balance sheets compared, the difference being the ‘unexplained’.

Analysis of change in embedded value
• Change in each component (eg free surplus, required capital, PVIF) typically identified separately.
• Key items in the analysis are: investment return on opening free surplus and required capital; ‘unwind’ of the discount rate (expected return on in-force business); economic and non-economic experience variances; changes in economic and non-economic assumptions in both the supervisory reserving and experience (or projection) bases, including the discount rate; new business.
• If required capital is identified separately from free surplus, the analysis needs to take into account factors such as changes in the methodology used to determine the period over which capital requirements are released, or changes to the parameters used to determine the related frictional costs.
• In jurisdictions where the PVIF is zero (or minimal), the analysis of change in EV would effectively be an analysis of surplus.

Analysis of profit
• For short-term insurances, profit is analysed on a more regular basis.
• Looks at trends in the frequency and size of claims, separately or combined.
• Care is needed when dealing with experience-rating systems.

Customer value
• Customer value measurement and management relates to the value placed on an existing or potential new customer throughout their lifetime.
• Monitored through persistency rates or EV measures.
• Analysis of the impact on customer value of different business decisions helps maximise profits or EV.

23.title: Implications of Strategies

subsections.title: Overview
bullets: This chapter continues the discussion of setting strategies, focusing on the implications of doing so.
Section 1 covers capital implications. Section 2 considers the analysis of alternative strategies. Section 3 covers modelling strategies.

subsections.title: Capital Required
bullets: On a per-contract basis, the capital requirement is the amount of finance a company needs in order to write that contract — i.e. the new business strain.
For the company as a whole, the capital requirement is the finance needed to carry out its business plans.
A profitable health and care operation will attract business and need more capital to support growth; an unprofitable portfolio will need capital to support losses.
Parties interested in the level of capital held by the insurer: regulator, policyholders, directors, credit rating agencies, and financial advisers.

The regulator requires certain capital levels to be established at launch; thereafter capital requirements reflect business in-force at each quarter (or other investigation point). Minimum levels must be comfortably exceeded. Regulatory capital requirements are covered in Chapters 11, 12 and 13.
Policyholders expect sufficient capital to meet future claims; their interests are overseen by the regulator.
Directors are responsible for ensuring capital is adequate and used efficiently; they seek outside capital input when levels run low.

Credit rating agencies are interested in free asset levels as an indicator of ability to meet responsibilities. A good credit rating benefits share price and the ability to raise capital, and lowers borrowing costs.
Financial advisers will recommend only adequately capitalised health insurers that can meet their promises; capital adequacy should be a key criterion in product recommendation.

key_terms.definition: The capital consumed when a new policy is written; arises because initial expenses and reserve requirements exceed the first premium received.
The total finance the company needs to carry out its business plans, covering both growth and loss scenarios.

subsections.title: Sources of Capital
bullets: Three main sources of capital: (1) paid-up share capital; (2) retained profit; (3) new capital injected.
New capital injected can come from: reinsurance; existing shareholders or parent company; investment or capital markets; sale of subsidiaries, portfolios, or assets.
Retained profit is an important source of capital for many companies.

Reinsurance can provide capital in two ways: (a) paying increased reinsurance commission to 'factor' future surplus streams (lending against predicted future reinsured flows of premiums less expenses and claims) — quota share is most common, though any original terms reinsurance may be used; (b) via a 'contingent loan' structured as reinsurance commission, repayable through future reinsurance premiums or emerging surplus, arranged so supervisory authorities do not require a reserve for repayment.
Under Solvency II, financial reinsurance effectiveness is much reduced (or eliminated) as best estimate liabilities already incorporate future profits.

key_terms.definition: Reinsurance used primarily as a capital management tool — factoring future surplus or providing contingent loans — rather than for risk transfer.
A proportional reinsurance arrangement ceding a fixed percentage of every risk to the reinsurer; most commonly used form for financial reinsurance.

subsections.title: Reviewing Risk Levels
bullets: The level of capital employed must be in line with the level of risk taken on.
If an insurer finds risk levels are unacceptably high, reinsurance should be considered to reduce them.
Equally, if capital is insufficient for the level of risk taken on, reinsurance should again be considered.

subsections.title: Use of Reinsurance
bullets: Reinsurance should always be considered as a tool to manage capital employed, commensurate with the required rate of business growth and the expected investment return.
Without reinsurance, the insurer would need more capital to finance growth, but the return on that capital may fall to unacceptable levels.
Reinsurance can reduce the need for capital; even if it reduces profit, an appropriate return on capital can be maintained.
The focus is on managing financing risks, with the aim of maximising profitability and increasing return on capital.

subsections.title: Projecting Solvency
bullets: An actuary must be able to demonstrate solvency on an ongoing basis.
Part of the periodic review of capital needs involves projecting the future solvency position under various scenarios.

Particular strategies may be tested using deterministic models; however, solvency should also be tested regularly on a stochastic basis — allowing current assumptions to deviate from expected — and the effect on available capital assessed.
This enables the actuary to make recommendations on future capital needs.
Modelling of future solvency is considered further in Section 3.

key_terms.definition: Solvency projections where assumptions are allowed to vary randomly around expected values, enabling assessment of the distribution of capital outcomes.

subsections.title: Economic Capital
bullets: When considering capital implications of alternative strategies, the insurer must decide whether to assess this on a regulatory or economic basis.
Economic capital is an internal assessment of the level of capital required to support the company's risk appetite and ongoing business strategy.
It is normally determined as the value of assets required in excess of liabilities so that, allowing for all risks, claims can be met with a defined degree of certainty when they fall due.
Companies within Solvency II (or similar regimes) must now effectively assess economic capital through the ORSA (Own Risk and Solvency Assessment, described in Chapter 11).
The distinction between economic and regulatory capital has therefore lessened under Solvency II.

key_terms.definition: An internal assessment of the capital required to support the company's risk appetite — assets in excess of liabilities sufficient to meet claims with a defined certainty.
Own Risk and Solvency Assessment — a Solvency II requirement under which insurers must assess their own economic capital position.

subsections.title: Profitability
bullets: The actuary's objective to improve or maintain profitability may raise a number of alternative courses of action.
Main objectives often include: maximising profit, increasing market share, maintaining required solvency levels, and improving customer satisfaction.
The client will usually want an estimate of the financial impact of each change to enable a decision; it is rarely easy to predict the impact of change.

Where no relevant data are available, assumptions may be based on 'gut feel'; the key is to monitor experience as soon as changes are made and revise assumptions accordingly (part of the monitoring stage of the control cycle).

It may not always be possible to achieve surplus from each and every policy; the actuary aims for product lines to be collectively profitable, avoiding lapse and re-entry problems and maintaining in-force volumes.
When a change is made, it may not be possible to alter premiums for policies already in-force — overall profitability must be viewed across the whole book, allowing for cross-subsidies.
The preferred strategy is reached iteratively, maximising the potential for return to profitability while minimising the negative impact on customer loyalty.
A stochastic approach may be used if there is sufficient confidence in parameters for incorporation into a suitable statistical distribution.

subsections.title: Competition
bullets: The insurer should take account of its competitive position when considering alternative strategies.
Courses of action adopted by competitors may be instructive as to what may or may not be appropriate.
In developed markets where competitors' products are very similar, other insurers may be facing near-identical decisions.
In markets with less competition, competitors' actions may be less relevant.

However, following competitors blindly is dangerous (as covered in Chapter 2); there are many other factors to consider. If little other information is available, following the rest of the market may be the safest course.

subsections.title: Tax
bullets: In making any strategy change, the insurer must be aware of any tax implications.
If taxation rules change, the insurer must assess the impact on its business; the actuary can advise on maximising benefits or mitigating adverse effects.

The actuary must understand how a new product is to be taxed as part of the insurance business, and how benefits and/or premiums are taxed in the hands of the policyholder (the insurer may be responsible for collecting tax).
The effects of large volumes of a new product on the company's overall tax position must also be considered, using actuarial projections of the company as a whole.

subsections.title: Legal
bullets: The insurer must be aware of the potential impact if regulations, laws, or legal precedents (e.g. court rulings) change.

Legal implications must be investigated — for example, any new policy wording should be examined by specialist lawyers to ensure the insurer's intentions are properly expressed and that current legal requirements for that country are met.

In the UK market, legal restrictions on medical underwriting include: (a) Use of genetic information — UK insurers currently have no right to use genetic test information in processing applications for health insurance. (b) Discrimination — legally forbidden to discriminate in underwriting by sexual orientation, disability, or religion unless actuarial or other evidence supports it; gender cannot be used as a rating factor at all. (c) Access to Medical Records Act — sets out individuals' rights regarding disclosure of information in medical records obtained for insurance purposes, restricting information doctors provide to insurers. (d) Data Protection Act — provides individuals with a right of access to any information relating to them, indirectly constraining underwriters' ability to store sensitive medical information.

key_terms.definition: Legislation setting out individuals' rights regarding disclosure of information contained in their medical records obtained for insurance (e.g. underwriting) purposes.
Legislation giving individuals a right of access to any information relating to them, constraining underwriters' ability to store sensitive medical information.

subsections.title: Model Risk
bullets: Modelling is a key component of the analysis of alternative strategies; performing sufficiently sophisticated modelling can be problematic. Practical considerations with modelling and proxy models were covered in Chapter 16.
Models are widely used in insurance but are only approximations of reality.

Model risk arises from the use of a model's results without knowing they are wrong or insufficiently accurate/appropriate for the intended purpose. This can lead to inappropriate disclosures and/or sub-optimal business decisions (e.g. when assessing and managing risks or capital).

Types of model risk include: (1) models incorrectly specified; (2) models correctly specified but incorrectly built and used unknowingly; (3) models correctly built and specified but used inappropriately for a specific purpose; (4) models with an inappropriate level of complexity (over-simplified or over-complex); (5) misinterpretation of results when an appropriate model has been used; (6) data or assumptions inconsistent with the model's methodology for the defined purpose; (7) the process of using the model is not accurately carried out; (8) the model is unknowingly used where its limitations indicate results will be inappropriate; (9) expert judgement is inadequately applied (when determining assumptions or the suitability of an approximation), leading to inappropriate outputs.

Managing model risk requires a strong culture of checking and documentation, supported by the compliance or internal audit function; senior management must satisfy itself that models are fit for purpose.

Model risk is managed by: (a) model owners approving methodology and ensuring adequate testing is performed; (b) reviewing assumptions, limitations, and expert judgement to confirm they are appropriate for the specific application; (c) ensuring processes have sufficient checks so the model is used appropriately and the process carried out accurately; (d) regularly validating models to ensure use remains appropriate; (e) repeating validation when material changes occur.

key_terms.definition: The risk that arises from using a model's results without knowing they are wrong or insufficiently accurate for the intended purpose, leading to poor business decisions or disclosures.
The process of regularly checking that a model remains appropriate for its intended use; must be repeated when material changes occur.

23.summary:
Managing capital
• The main sources of capital are: paid-up share capital; retained profit; new capital injected (reinsurance, existing shareholders / parent, investment / capital markets, sale of subsidiaries / portfolios / assets).
• The level of capital should be in line with the risk taken on. Reinsurance could be used to manage this.
• Ongoing solvency demonstrated on a regular basis using projections with scenario testing and stochastic modelling to enable future capital needs to be determined.
• Insurers need to consider whether this is done on a regulatory or economic basis. Solvency II requires economic capital to be assessed.

Analysis of alternative strategies
• Want to know the financial impact of alternatives, although rarely easy to predict. An iterative process may be used to achieve the strategy that maximises profits but minimises adverse effects on customer loyalty. A stochastic approach may be used.
• Consideration of competitors’ strategies will help in the decision-marking.
• The actuary will need to understand the tax implications of a chosen strategy.
• Legal implications should be investigated.

Modelling considerations
• Types of model risk include models: being incorrectly specified or built; being used inappropriately; having an inappropriate level of complexity; results being misinterpreted; data / assumption being inconsistent with the purpose; not being used accurately or used when limitations show it is inappropriate; having expert judgement inadequately applied giving inappropriate output.
• Model risk can be managed by ensuring: owners approve methodology and check adequate testing is performed; assumptions, limitations and expert judgement is reviewed; processes have sufficient checks to avoid misuse; models are regularly validated so their use remains appropriate; validation is repeated when material changes occur.

24.title: National Healthcare Systems

subsections.title: Healthcare Systems
bullets: Healthcare systems are defined as the provision and funding of medical services.

A typical healthcare system involves: (a) the public sector (provided and financed by governments); (b) the private sector (provided by private practices, financed by the individual, family, employer, etc); (c) informal care (e.g. provided by one's family). The relative importance of each element varies by country.
An ideal healthcare system should also include the functions of resource generation (ensuring enough staff, equipment, etc) and stewardship (the overall management of the system).

key_terms.definition: The provision and funding of medical services; an ideal system also encompasses resource generation and stewardship.
Such advice and treatment as provided by a general practitioner. Not typically covered by PMI in the UK, although elsewhere primary care may be an insured benefit.
The overall management of the healthcare system, involving the supervision, monitoring and enforcement of health policies.

subsections.title: Structure of Chapter
bullets: Section 1: The importance of healthcare provision within society, including aims of healthcare systems and government responsibility.
Section 2: Various forms of healthcare systems worldwide and how they have developed historically; also covers the role of private insurance.
Section 3: Factors influencing the demand for healthcare services.
Section 4: Summary measures of population health levels and how they can be used to prioritise healthcare provision.
Section 5: Quality Adjusted Life Years (QALYs) in detail.
Section 6: Considerations in financing (funding) healthcare systems.
This chapter does not address considerations regarding national provision of long-term care or sickness absence payments.

subsections.title: Aims of Healthcare Provision
bullets: The WHO defined health in 1946 as a state of complete physical, mental and social well-being — it is not only the absence of disease or infirmity.

The Alma-Ata Declaration of 1978 is the statement by the WHO and UNICEF on the importance of primary care as the basis for healthcare. It identified public health care as key to achieving the goal of Health for All.

The original goals of Health for All were set for 2000 and then extended to 2020. These include measures of health equity: (a) the extent of childhood stunting, survival (maternal mortality, child mortality and life expectancy); (b) the reversal of major pandemics (TB, HIV, malaria, violence, tobacco-related illness); (c) the eradication of certain diseases (depending on region). Further goals included improvement of access to water, sanitation, food, shelter and care.

Health also depends on: societal factors (such as levels of education); and infrastructure (quality of roads to get from clinics to hospitals, telephones to call an ambulance, electricity to keep drugs refrigerated at a clinic).

In September 2000, the leaders of 189 countries agreed to the Millennium Declaration, translated into eight measurable Millennium Development Goals (MDGs) to be reached by 2015. These were replaced by the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) in 2016.

The WHO World Health Report 2003 (Shaping the Future) discusses the two-way causal relationship between economic development and health: low income constrains healthcare access, while ill-health limits earning capability and contributes to poverty. Better health is a prerequisite and major contributor to economic growth and social cohesion.

Three of the eight MDGs are directly health-related: (a) reduce child mortality; (b) improve maternal health; (c) combat HIV/AIDS, malaria and other diseases. There is mixed evidence as to whether MDGs were achieved; reduction in poverty has been achieved due significantly to improvements in China and India.

The UN Sustainable Development Goals comprise 17 goals with 169 targets to be met by 2030. Goal 3 (ensure healthy lives and promote well-being for all at all ages) is of particular relevance, with targets including ending AIDS, tuberculosis, malaria, reducing maternal mortality and ending preventable deaths of newborns and infants under 5.

key_terms.definition: Statement by the WHO and UNICEF on the importance of primary care as the basis for healthcare; launched the Health for All initiative.
Eight measurable goals agreed by 189 countries in 2000 to be reached by 2015; three are directly health-related (child mortality, maternal health, infectious diseases).
17 goals with 169 targets agreed by the UN to be met by 2030, replacing the MDGs in 2016; the majority (14 of 17) are health-related.

exam_qa.q: What is the Glossary definition of primary care?
exam_qa.a: Primary care is defined as such advice and treatment as provided by a general practitioner. This is not typically covered by a PMI policy in the UK, although elsewhere primary care would be an insured benefit.

subsections.title: Government Responsibility
bullets: The WHO says the ultimate responsibility for the overall performance of a country's healthcare system lies with government, which should involve all sectors of society in its stewardship.

Stewardship involves the supervision, monitoring and enforcement of health policies. One problem with some systems is that they are run looking only at the needs of the public health sector, without considering other parties (e.g. private sector, transportation).
Health issues are normally debated at the highest government levels.

The four main objectives of a government's provision of healthcare: (1) protecting the nation's health; (2) subsidising the poor; (3) balancing the budget; (4) following social culture and/or political promises.

Three important aspects to the objective of establishing the best and fairest healthcare system: best (highest level of healthcare); fairest (equity); with available resources (subject to financial and other constraints).
Governments' health policies should cover the regulation of private medical providers and insurers, as well as State provision and funding.
Healthcare insurance products are not only financial services products, but also often form an integral part of the social security framework of a nation.

exam_qa.q: State the four main objectives of a government's provision of healthcare.
exam_qa.a: The four main objectives are: (1) protecting the nation's health; (2) subsidising the poor; (3) balancing the budget; (4) following social culture and/or political promises.

subsections.title: Objectives of Healthcare Systems
bullets: The WHO World Health Report 2000 (Health Systems: Improving Performance) identifies three fundamental objectives of healthcare systems: (a) improving the health of the population they serve; (b) responding to people's expectations; (c) providing financial protection against the costs of ill-health.

The objective of good health is twofold: the best attainable average level (goodness); and the smallest feasible differences among individuals and groups (fairness). Goodness means a health system responds well to what people expect; fairness means it responds equally well to everyone, without discrimination.

The World Health Report 2000 presented for the first time an index of national healthcare systems' performance against three overall goals: (1) level and distribution of good health; (2) level and distribution of responsiveness to the expectations of the population; (3) fairness of financial contribution.
The UK was ranked 18 out of 191 member countries; the USA was ranked 35. Rankings were based on estimated data from 1997.

Key health outcome indicators — WHO: Disability-Adjusted Life Expectancy (DALE) and infant mortality. OECD: avoidable mortality by selected conditions, infant mortality, perinatal mortality, low birth weight, incidence of infectious diseases, avoidable hospitalisations, cancer survival rates, in-hospital AMI mortality, vaccination rates, breast/cervical cancer screening.

Key responsiveness indicators — WHO: patient-rated dignity, autonomy and confidentiality, promptness of attention, quality of basic amenities, access to support networks during care, choice of care provider. OECD: waiting times.
Key equity indicator — OECD: equity of patient-reported health status. Key efficiency indicator — WHO: composite measure of performance.
In 2013, analysis by the European Observatory on Health Systems and Policies highlighted how undertaking comparisons of different health systems can drive improvements.

key_terms.definition: Achieving the best attainable average level of health in the population served.
Achieving the smallest feasible differences in health outcomes and access among individuals and groups; responding equally well to everyone without discrimination.
A WHO health outcome indicator combining life expectancy with adjustments for time spent in ill health or with disability.

subsections.title: History of Healthcare Systems
bullets: Organised health systems in the modern sense barely existed a little over 100 years ago. Since then, health systems have undergone overlapping generations of reforms, including the founding of national healthcare systems and the extension of social insurance schemes.

The industrial revolution (18th century) led societies to recognise the toll of death, illness and disability from infectious diseases and industrial accidents, prompting the development of organised healthcare.

Bismarck (Chancellor of Germany) enacted a law in 1883 requiring employer contributions to health coverage for low-wage workers — the first State-mandated social insurance model. Similar legislation spread to Belgium (1894), Norway (1909) and the UK (1911).
In 1911, the UK introduced a National Insurance scheme providing medical benefits and unemployment insurance, paid for through deductions from wages.

The Second World War (1939–45) damaged or destroyed health infrastructures in many countries. The Beveridge Report of 1942 identified healthcare as one of three basic prerequisites for a viable social security system — called the Beveridge model, seen by the WHO as the most widely influential model of a healthcare system. The UK's NHS was established in 1948.

New Zealand became the first country to introduce a national health service in 1938; Costa Rica laid the foundation for universal health insurance in 1941. In the post-war period many countries extended systems to cover most or all of the population.

Three overlapping generations of 20th century healthcare system reforms: (1) founding of national healthcare systems; (2) promotion of primary healthcare; (3) third-generation reforms — new universalism. These were prompted by perceived failures in health and a quest for greater efficiency, fairness and responsiveness.

First generation — founding of national healthcare systems (mostly 1940s and 1950s in richer countries, later in poorer ones): by the late 1960s, many systems were under great stress as costs rose; efforts to reach the poor were incomplete; colonial healthcare systems in Africa and Asia largely excluded indigenous populations; major urban hospitals received around two-thirds of government health budgets despite serving only 10% to 20% of the population.

Second generation — promotion of primary healthcare: the 1978 Alma-Ata Declaration defined primary healthcare as essential healthcare based on practical, scientifically sound and socially acceptable methods made universally accessible at a cost the community can afford. However, many such programmes were considered partial failures due to inadequate funding and equipment, insufficient training, and poorly functioning referral systems. In developed countries, primary care has been better integrated; managed care revolves around strengthening primary care and avoiding unnecessary hospitalisation.

Third generation — new universalism: since the late 1980s there has been a gradual shift towards delivery to all of high-quality essential care, defined by criteria of effectiveness, cost and social acceptability. It implies explicit choice of priorities; it is inadmissible to exclude whole groups from the population. By the late 1980s, transformation from communist to market-oriented economies was under way in China, central Europe and the former Soviet Union, leading to widespread privatisation, more competition and greater reliance on market mechanisms. Universal health insurance has been introduced in the Republic of Korea, Malaysia, Singapore and Taiwan. In developed countries already with universal coverage, changes focused on who determines how resources are used and how funds are pooled and paid to providers.

In the USA, there has been a great shift of power from providers to insurers, who now largely control access between doctors and patients, through mechanisms such as fund holding for GPs (UK), Health Maintenance Organisations (USA), and Independent Practice Associations (New Zealand).

key_terms.definition: The first State-mandated social insurance model, originating in Germany in 1883; requires employer contributions to health coverage for workers.

Healthcare system model originating from the UK's Beveridge Report (1942), identifying healthcare as a basic prerequisite for a viable social security system; seen by the WHO as the most widely influential model.

Third-generation reform concept: delivery to all of high-quality essential care defined by criteria of effectiveness, cost and social acceptability — rather than all care for everyone, or only basic care for the poor.

A process whereby an insurer intervenes in the provision of medical care with the dual objective of optimising the quality of treatment for the policyholder and controlling the cost (by such means as preferred provider utilisation and claims pre-authorisation).
Treatment paid for by the patients themselves, rather than being covered by insurance or being State-funded.

exam_qa.q: What is the Glossary definition of managed care?
exam_qa.a: The term given to a process whereby an insurer intervenes in the provision of medical care with the dual objective of optimising the quality of treatment for the policyholder and controlling the cost (by such means as preferred provider utilisation and claims pre-authorisation).

exam_qa.q: What does the Core Reading mean by out-of-pocket healthcare?
exam_qa.a: Out-of-pocket healthcare is treatment paid for by the patients themselves, rather than being covered by insurance or being State-funded.

subsections.title: Role of Private Health Insurance
bullets: Private health insurance here refers to insurance to cover the costs of medical treatment (e.g. PMI in the UK); it does not include CI or IP insurance.

An OECD study (2004) recognised that governments often look to private health insurance as a possible means of addressing health system challenges — enhancing the role of insurance as an alternative source of health financing, increasing system capacity, or promoting it to further additional health policy goals such as enhanced individual responsibility.

Private health insurance accounts for only around 6% of total health expenditure globally, but varies significantly by country: USA — 35% of total health expenditure (2013); Canada, Chile, France, Ireland and Slovenia — 13% to 20%; other OECD countries — generally 6% or lower; UK — around 4% of total health expenditure (2014), with private out-of-pocket expenditure higher at 15%.
The OECD has recognised that public policy is the primary determinant of the role and size of private health insurance arrangements in most OECD countries.
Private health insurance typically focuses on coverage of eligibility gaps based on categories of individuals, health services or providers not covered by public health systems.
A concern about private health insurance is that it creates differences in access to care based on insurance status and often income, which contradicts the WHO's stated objective of fairness.

Main advantages of private health insurance as part of a country's healthcare system: (1) reduces financial and other burdens on the public healthcare system; (2) provides greater choice for individuals; (3) allows some individuals access to a better standard of healthcare; (4) encourages individuals to take responsibility for their own health and healthcare provision; (5) may lead to a more efficient healthcare system (both public and private); (6) may increase competition between health providers, e.g. through preferred provider arrangements.

Main disadvantages of private health insurance as part of a country's healthcare system: (1) likely to lead to greater inequity in the provision of healthcare; (2) increases the complexity of the healthcare system; (3) increased administration involved, e.g. setting and enforcing regulation; (4) fewer people in the State system, which may reduce efficiencies in the State system; (5) may lead to greater expectations of healthcare which the State systems may not be able to meet.

Examples of eligibility gaps — (a) categories of individuals: means testing means only those with income/savings below a threshold qualify for public care; (b) health services: individuals may be required to pay for dental, eye care, medicines, non-emergency services; (c) providers: greater choice of hospital, specialist or GP may only be available privately via insurance.

key_terms.definition: The gap between what a public health system covers and what an individual needs; coverage of eligibility gaps is the typical focus of private health insurance.

exam_qa.q: Outline the main advantages and disadvantages of using private health insurance as part of a country's healthcare system.
exam_qa.a: Advantages: reduces burden on public system; greater individual choice; access to better standard of care; encourages individual responsibility; may improve efficiency; may increase competition between providers. Disadvantages: likely to increase inequity; increases system complexity; increases administration; reduces numbers in State system (potential efficiency loss); may raise expectations the State cannot meet.

exam_qa.q: Give examples to explain the ways in which a public health system might necessitate the need for insurance to cover eligibility gaps based on: (a) categories of individuals; (b) health services; (c) providers.
exam_qa.a: (a) Means testing — only those with income/savings below a threshold qualify for public care, so others need private cover. (b) Individuals may be required to pay for certain services (e.g. dental treatment, eye care, medicines, non-emergency services). (c) If individuals wish greater choice of provider (hospital, specialist, GP), this may only be available privately via insurance.

subsections.title: Characteristics of Healthcare Demand
bullets: When considering the healthcare sector compared to other sectors of the economy, specific aspects need to be taken into account: (1) utility from diagnosis — there is utility to be acquired from accessing healthcare expertise (people derive value from diagnosis as well as from treatment); (2) third-party payer — in the majority of cases, a third-party payer is responsible for paying for treatment, removing the direct link between willingness to pay and utility received; (3) information asymmetry — the patient does not have the medical expertise to assess the advice or services provided; (4) difficulty measuring health outcomes — health outcomes are very difficult to measure and there is limited opportunity for comparison; (5) unpredictable vital need — when the need arises, the requirement for healthcare is vital and the timing is mostly unpredictable.

Desired health is usually greater than actual health. People seek healthcare services in order to improve health, rather than for healthcare's own sake. This is known as derived demand. In some cases, access to healthcare services does not always lead to direct health improvements.
Derived demand: demand for healthcare services is caused by the demand for improved health — demand for one good (healthcare) occurs as a result of demand for another (health).
