Many economies are dealing with continuously increasing demand for health and therefore healthcare services, driven by: (1) demographic factors — ageing population and associated increase in comorbidities; (2) technological factors — demand for access to the latest innovations in treatment; (3) higher levels of average education, income and awareness — raising expectations and propensity to spend on healthcare; people are generally willing to spend a higher proportion of their income on healthcare as they become wealthier.

key_terms.definition: Where demand for one good or service occurs as a result of demand for another. Demand for healthcare services is derived from the demand for improved health.
An entity other than the patient responsible for paying for treatment, which removes the direct link between willingness to pay and utility received.
In healthcare, the situation where the patient lacks the medical expertise to assess the advice or services provided — one of the key characteristics distinguishing healthcare from other markets.

subsections.title: Measuring Health Life Expectancy
bullets: With increasing life expectancy, measuring population health levels on the basis of mortality rates alone has become less and less relevant in many populations.
Summary measures of population health are measures that combine information on mortality and non-fatal health outcomes to represent population health as a single number.
An example of such a summary measure is the Quality Adjusted Life Years (QALY), which is a single measure combining quantity and quality of future life expectancy.

Benefits of using summary measures: force exact definition of epidemiological inputs on mortality and disability; stimulate the application of uniform classifications and the collection of missing information; systematically check the internal consistency of epidemiological information; set agendas for international comparative data collection and epidemiological research.

Factors included in summary measures: infant mortality rates; life expectancies; cause of death profiles (percentage of deaths in the population attributable to specified diseases and causes); prevalence of disability (percentage of population with moderate to severe disability, e.g. preventing the person from carrying out daily activities independently or from participating in educational, economic or social activities). Related socio-economic conditions could also be considered, such as percentages of households with water, sanitation and direct energy supply.

Eight uses for summary measures (WHO, 2002): (1) comparing the health of one population to the health of another; (2) comparing the health of the same population at different points in time; (3) identifying and quantifying overall health inequalities within populations; (4) providing appropriate and balanced attention to the effects of non-fatal health outcomes on overall population health; (5) informing debates on priorities for health service delivery and planning; (6) informing debates on priorities for research and development in the health sector; (7) improving professional training curricula in public health; (8) analysing the benefits of health interventions for use in cost-effectiveness analyses.

key_terms.definition: A measure that combines information on mortality and non-fatal health outcomes to represent population health as a single number.

subsections.title: Prioritisation
bullets: Healthcare markets have unique characteristics (third-party payers, information asymmetry, etc.) that mean they usually function inefficiently and have an economically sub-optimal allocation of resources.

Governments usually want healthcare systems to do more than function efficiently: they may want to reduce inequities in healthcare access for certain social groups, and may be willing to move away from an economically efficient allocation of resources to achieve social policy goals.
Healthcare systems are usually subject to significant political interference and are rarely allowed to function even as limited free markets. Heavy government regulation is a usual characteristic.

As healthcare markets are not free markets there is a consequent imbalance between supply and demand; the prioritisation of treatments and services is key. Payers ration services by: using waiting lists; offering explicit benefit packages; excluding whole sectors of the population from treatment; or using a combination of methods.

Rationing by waiting lists: if people have to wait a long time for State-funded treatment, fewer will eventually require it (some will source treatment privately, some will die before receiving treatment, some may recover and no longer require treatment). However, the condition may worsen, requiring more expensive treatment eventually.

Ranking treatments using cost-effectiveness criteria: this approach measures the benefits of treatment using a measure of healthcare utility (such as QALY) against the cost of treatment, producing a cost per unit benefit. By setting a threshold, payers ensure treatments producing the highest overall social benefit are allowed, while treatments that benefit a few people at very high cost are not paid for from pooled funds. A variation is to manage waiting list lengths according to relative healthcare utility — those with the highest QALY have the shortest waiting lists.

All methods of rationing attempt to ensure a basic level of healthcare needs is met for the population. The exact level is usually defined by a society's willingness to spend on healthcare as opposed to other public services.
Under new universalism, the aim is to deliver to all high-quality essential care, where essential is defined mostly by criteria of effectiveness, cost and social acceptability.

key_terms.definition: Methods used to control access to healthcare services given constrained resources; includes waiting lists, explicit benefit packages, cost-per-QALY thresholds, and population exclusions.

exam_qa.q: Explain how the use of waiting lists effectively rations State healthcare services.
exam_qa.a: If people have to wait a long time to obtain State-funded treatment, there will be fewer people requiring treatment eventually because: some people will source treatment privately (more likely the longer the waiting list); some people will die before receiving treatment; some people may recover and so no longer require treatment. (On the other hand, the condition may worsen, requiring more expensive treatment eventually.)

exam_qa.q: What is the aim of a healthcare system under what the WHO calls new universalism?
exam_qa.a: Under new universalism the aim is to deliver to all high-quality essential care, where essential is defined mostly by criteria of effectiveness, cost and social acceptability.

subsections.title: Introduction
bullets: Modelling techniques using the concept of Quality Adjusted Life Years (QALYs) attempt to measure the relative effectiveness of a medical treatment or surgical procedure by summarising the expected additional life expectancy as a result of the treatment or procedure, taking into account the quality of life thereafter.

QALYs provide a single health state measure combining quantity and quality of life — a generic measure that sums years spent in different health states using weights on a scale of 0 (dead) to 1 (perfectly healthy) for each health state.
Example: 5 years of perfect health = 5 QALYs; 2 years in a state measured as 0.5 of perfect health followed by 3 years of perfect health = 4 QALYs.
In practice, a discount factor will often be applied to future QALYs.

key_terms.definition: A single health state measure combining quantity and quality of life; sums years spent in different health states weighted on a scale of 0 (dead) to 1 (perfectly healthy). Used to measure the relative effectiveness of medical treatments or procedures.

subsections.title: Uses of QALYs
bullets: Two important uses for models using QALYs: (1) to help allocate resources to the most cost-effective treatments; (2) to help decide what treatment option will be best for a particular patient.
The cost of treatment divided by the QALYs gained by the treatment gives a measure of the cost-effectiveness (or utility) of the treatment.

The value allocated to each health state reflects the quality of life within that state; the overall approach allows morbidity and mortality improvements to be combined into a single weighted measure: QALYs gained.

Cost per QALY gained has been suggested as a criterion for prioritising between different healthcare programmes or treatments. Economists' recommendations are to rank health issues from the lowest cost per QALY to the highest and to select from the top until available resources are exhausted.

Issue with strict application: all resources would be allocated only to cost-effective treatments (low cost per QALY); treatments with a high cost per QALY would not be performed. This could be argued as unfair or unethical, and discourages development of innovative new treatments.
However, a ranking can help identify which treatments are most and least cost-effective, so consideration can be given to using the most effective ones more.
This method is often used to rank different types of medical interventions, including drugs, to determine the best use of resources.

key_terms.definition: The cost of treatment divided by the QALYs gained; used as a criterion for prioritising healthcare programmes or treatments by cost-effectiveness.

exam_qa.q: Estimate the cost per QALY gained for a treatment where: cost is £1,500 plus follow-up drugs at £200 pa; expected life expectancy without treatment is 5 years at 30% of normal function; expected life expectancy with treatment is 10 years where the first 2 years are at 10% of normal function and the remaining years are healthy. Ignore discounting and inflation.
exam_qa.a: Quality adjusted life expectancy without treatment: 5 × 0.3 = 1.5 QALYs. Quality adjusted life expectancy with treatment: 2 × 0.1 + 8 = 8.2 QALYs. QALYs gained: 8.2 − 1.5 = 6.7. Cost per QALY gained: (1,500 + 200 × 10) / 6.7 = £522.

subsections.title: Opinions on QALYs
bullets: The three main perceived drawbacks with QALYs are: (1) quality of life being expressed in terms of numbers; (2) value assigned to life varies with the health state of the person; (3) all emphasis is placed on the size of health improvement, ignoring the starting point.

Expressing quality of life in terms of numbers: the health status index approach and the focus on life years as units of measurement caused initial resentment. The core of the QALY procedure, however, is not to value health improvements but rather to value health states.

Assigning a value to quality of life: QALYs assign a value to life itself according to the health state of the individual. A life in a wheelchair is considered not only less healthy but also of less value — this position is ethically highly controversial and disabled people find it repugnant. It is very difficult to quantify an individual's state of health (0 to 1 scale); results can be subjective and vary greatly. No single agreed approach exists.

Based on QALY gained — ignoring the starting point: the emphasis is on the size of health improvement; a small but significant improvement for a person in a bad state may be preferred by society to a more substantial improvement for a person in a less severe state. An increase from 10% to 20% of healthy function is arguably a better use of resources than 80% to 95%, even though it would have a smaller QALY gained. Also: is a QALY gained for someone aged 20 really of equal value to a QALY gained at age 80?
Rule of rescue: ethical rules will often apply in this context — in particular the obligation to save human life almost regardless of cost.

Possibly as a result of these drawbacks, QALY approaches are not being widely used in practice. Alternative measures of utility exist, such as saved young life equivalents. Data required (e.g. transition probabilities between health states and treatment costs) is relatively sparse.

Benefits of QALYs: a useful tool to aid decision making; models help develop better understanding; sensitivity testing should be carried out on uncertain parameters (e.g. quality of life adjustment factors); can help focus the mind on the long-term effectiveness of treatments (a more expensive operation may be cheaper in the long run due to less follow-up treatment or lower risk of reoccurrence); particularly useful in the private sector where patients wish to be more involved in their treatment decisions.

key_terms.definition: The ethical obligation to save human life almost regardless of cost; applies in healthcare prioritisation contexts and can conflict with strict cost-per-QALY decision-making.
An alternative measure of healthcare utility to QALYs that some consider more appropriate; it explicitly weights life-years by age.

subsections.title: Purpose of Healthcare Financing
bullets: The WHO Report of 2000 says the purpose of healthcare financing is to make funding available, as well as to set the right financial incentives for providers, to ensure that all individuals have access to effective healthcare.
This means reducing or eliminating the possibility that an individual will be unable to pay for such care, or will be impoverished as a result of trying to do so.

subsections.title: Functions of Health System Financing
bullets: To ensure individuals have access to health services, three inter-related functions of health system financing are crucial: (1) revenue collection; (2) pooling of resources; (3) purchasing of interventions. The three functions are often integrated in a single organisation; there is an increasing trend to introduce a separation between revenue and purchasing.

Revenue collection: the process by which the health system receives money from households and organisations or companies, as well as from donors. Methods: general taxation; mandated social health insurance contributions (usually salary-related and almost never risk-related); voluntary private health insurance contributions (usually risk-related); out-of-pocket payments and donations. Most high income countries rely heavily on general taxation or mandated social health insurance; low income countries depend far more on out-of-pocket financing. Donations are an important source for developing countries but may require compliance with donors' priorities. For general taxation-funded organisations, collecting is done by the ministry of finance and allocated to the ministry of health through the government budgetary process.

Pooling of resources: the accumulation and management of revenues such that the risk of having to pay for healthcare is borne by all members of the pool and not by each contributor individually. Pooling is traditionally known as the insurance function — whether explicit (people knowingly subscribing to a scheme) or implicit (as with tax revenues). Its main purpose is to share the financial risk associated with health interventions for which the need is uncertain. When people pay entirely out of pocket, no pooling occurs. Pooling reduces uncertainty for both citizens and providers; by increasing and stabilising demand and the flow of funds, it can increase the likelihood that patients can afford services and that a higher volume of services will justify new provider investments.

Community-rated contributions are fair in that everyone pays the same contribution regardless of age, gender, medical history, etc. Risk-related contributions are fair in that the contribution reflects the individual's expected cost to the system — those expected to use it more pay a higher contribution.

For activities where there is no uncertainty or the cost is low, funds can go directly from collection to purchasing without pooling. Consumer preferences for insurance packages often focus on interventions of high probability and low cost, although these are best paid out of current income or through direct public subsidies for the poor.

Purchasing of interventions: the process by which pooled funds are paid to providers to deliver a specified or unspecified set of health interventions. Can be passive or strategic. Passive purchasing: following a predetermined budget or simply paying bills when presented. Strategic purchasing: a continuous search for the best ways to maximise healthcare system performance by deciding which interventions should be purchased, how and from whom; actively choosing interventions through selective contracting and incentive schemes.

Recently, Chile, Hungary, New Zealand and the UK have tried to introduce an active purchasing role within their public health systems. In the UK, NHS has taken an active role in purchasing; the private sector is being used to treat NHS patients; independent treatment centres have been established for procedures such as hip and knee replacements, hernia repair, gallbladder and cataract removal.

key_terms.definition: The process by which the health system receives money from households, organisations and donors; methods include general taxation, mandated social health insurance contributions, voluntary private insurance contributions, and out-of-pocket payments.

The accumulation and management of revenues so that the risk of paying for healthcare is borne by all pool members rather than each contributor individually; traditionally known as the insurance function.

The process by which pooled funds are paid to providers to deliver a specified or unspecified set of health interventions; can be passive (paying bills) or strategic (actively selecting best-value interventions).
Following a predetermined budget or simply paying bills when presented, without actively selecting interventions for value.
A continuous search for the best ways to maximise healthcare system performance by deciding which interventions to purchase, how and from whom, using selective contracting and incentive schemes.
Insurance contributions where everyone pays the same amount regardless of individual risk factors such as age, gender or medical history.
Insurance contributions where the amount paid reflects the individual's expected cost to the system — those expected to use services more pay higher contributions.

exam_qa.q: Explain how each of community-rated and risk-related contributions can be seen to be fair.
exam_qa.a: Community-rated contributions can be seen as fair in that everyone pays the same contribution, regardless of age, gender, medical history, etc. Risk-related contributions can be seen as fair in that the contribution made reflects that individual's expected cost to the system — i.e. individuals expected to use the system more pay a higher contribution.

subsections.title: Other Considerations in Financing
bullets: For personal healthcare, it is not the public-private dichotomy that is most important in determining health system performance, but the difference between prepayment and out-of-pocket spending.

Private financing, particularly in developing countries, is largely synonymous with out-of-pocket spending or contributions to small, voluntary and often highly fragmented pools. In contrast, public or mandatory private financing (from general taxation or contributions to social security) is always associated with prepayment and large pools.

Fairness of financial risk protection requires the highest possible degree of separation between contributions and utilisation — particularly so for interventions that are high cost relative to the household's capacity to pay. Prepayment makes it possible to spread the financial risk among pool members.

Healthcare systems throughout the world attempt to spread risk and subsidise the poor through various combinations of organisational and technical arrangements. In practice, in most health systems, risk and income cross-subsidisation occurs via a combination of pooling and government subsidy.

subsections.title: Global Funding Systems
bullets: Examples of healthcare funding systems around the world:
1. Funding primarily through general taxation (UK).
2. Funding through local taxation combined with provider management by local councils (Denmark).
3. Social health insurance paid by the employer and the employee with multiple non-competing autonomous insurers (France).
4. Social health insurance paid by the employer and the employee with competing insurers (Germany).
5. Compulsory social insurance paid by individuals with competing insurers and a government-approved insurance plan (Switzerland).
6. Voluntary insurance, with tax subsidies, paid by employers (USA).
7. Voluntary insurance, paid by individuals, with tax subsidies (Australia).
8. Catastrophe insurance plus a tax-protected savings account (Singapore).

subsections.title: Funding Pressures
bullets: OECD Health Statistics 2015 shows that healthcare spending continues to put pressure on government budgets. The OECD produces updated statistics each year.

In almost all OECD countries, total spending on healthcare rose faster than economic growth in the period from 2000 to 2010, as measured by the average ratio of healthcare spending to GDP. The average ratio has broadly stabilised at around 9.0% in the last few years.

Three factors pushing up healthcare spending: (1) the ageing population and associated increase in comorbidities; (2) technological factors — demand for access to the latest innovations (e.g. use of expensive MRI units nearly doubled between 2002 and 2011; CT scanner use has also increased); (3) rising expectations — higher income, education and awareness levels raise expectations and propensity to spend on healthcare. These factors will continue to keep costs high.

The USA had the highest spending in 2020 at 18.8% of GDP (13.1% in 2000); Turkey had the lowest at 4.6% (4.9% in 2000); the UK spent 12% of GDP (6.9% in 2000). Some figures may have been affected by increased spend due to COVID-19.

The USA spent around ,700 per person on healthcare in 2013, considerably higher than the ,450 per person OECD average. Switzerland had the next highest per person spend, still around 80% higher than the OECD average.
Governments of most OECD countries meet the majority of the burden of healthcare costs (average around 70%); this proportion has increased in many countries in recent years.

Given the urgent need to reduce budget deficits, many OECD governments continue to have to make difficult choices: (1) curb the growth of public spending on health; (2) cut spending in other areas; (3) raise taxes.

exam_qa.q: State three factors that are pushing up healthcare spending.
exam_qa.a: The demand for healthcare is being driven by: (1) the ageing population; (2) technological factors; (3) rising expectations.

24.summary:
Healthcare systems
• Defined as the provision and funding of medical services. Attaining good health also depends on societal factors and infrastructure. WHO defines ‘health’ as ‘a state of complete physical, mental and social well-being’, not only the absence of disease or infirmity.
• There is a two-way causal relationship between economic development and health.
• Healthcare systems have three fundamental objectives (WHO 2000): improving the health of the population they serve; responding to people’s expectations; providing financial protection against the costs of ill-health.
• WHO says the ultimate responsibility for the overall performance of a country’s healthcare system lies with government, which should involve all sectors of society in its stewardship. For every country this means establishing the best and fairest healthcare system possible with available resources.

History of healthcare systems
• The generations of healthcare system reforms are: the founding of national healthcare systems; the promotion of primary healthcare; third-generation reforms – ‘new universalism’.
• Developments prompted by perceived failures in health and a quest for greater efficiency, fairness and responsiveness to the expectations of the population.
• Most governments have considered private health insurance to address some of the challenges of health systems. Use of insurance varies significantly by country, it typically focuses on coverage of eligibility gaps based on categories of individuals, health services or providers not covered by public systems. Insurance can result in concerns around equity.

Demand for healthcare
• Specific aspects to consider, eg utility from diagnosis, third-party payers, information asymmetry, difficulty in measuring outcomes, need for healthcare can be vital.
• Demand for healthcare is continuously increasing, and is a ‘derived demand’ from the demand for health.

Measurement of population health levels and prioritisation
• Summary measures of population health combine information on mortality and non-fatal health outcomes to represent population health as a single number.
• Uses for such measures include: comparisons between different populations, or of one population over time; identifying and quantifying health inequalities; focusing attention on non-fatal outcomes; helping to prioritise service delivery, planning, research and training; analysing the benefits of health interventions.

Prioritisation
• Healthcare systems are not ‘free’ as they are subject to political influence.
• Prioritisation of treatments or services is essential, eg ranking treatments using cost-effectiveness criteria (such as cost per unit benefit based on QALYs).
• Rationing aims to ensure a basic level of healthcare needs is met for the whole population.

Financing
• To ensure individuals have access to health services, three interrelated functions of health system financing are crucial: revenue collection (eg general taxation, insurance contributions, out-of-pocket and donor payments); pooling of resources (ie sharing the risk between all members of the pool); purchasing of interventions (eg passive, or – more usually – strategic).
• Growing healthcare spending is putting pressure on government budgets.

25.title: Best Practice

subsections.title: Overview
bullets: This chapter covers areas of best practice in the provision of health insurance that the health and care actuary should be aware of.
Section 1 covers general areas of best practice, relating to: sales; continual monitoring of TCF (treating customers fairly); product design; data sharing; and pricing.
Section 2 covers guidelines that relate to health insurance products. It is more important to understand the reason behind the guidelines than the specific details themselves.
It may be useful to keep abreast of current developments in these areas, e.g. via websites and periodicals listed in Chapter 1's Further Reading section.

subsections.title: Sales
bullets: Companies operating in a country need to comply with local sales regulation. Possible examples of sales best practice include:
Requirements on firms relating to the sales process — e.g. where a firm is giving advice then the policy that is recommended should meet the customer's needs.
Firm status disclosure — the firm must provide information to its customers on the service it is providing in an understandable format.
Proposals on fair treatment of consumers — e.g. whether commission should be disclosed, an unfair inducements rule, and cancellation periods.
Product information measures — to ensure customers get key product information at a time when it can influence their decision making.
Claims handling standards — to require firms to deal with claims fairly and promptly.
Training and competence regime — for individuals selling and managing insurance contracts.
Complaint proposals — to require firms to meet certain standards when handling complaints and to provide customers with access to the local ombudsman service.
Regulations may vary by product — e.g. there may be stronger regulations for selling LTCI due to the investment element of the product.

subsections.title: Continual Monitoring of Treating Customers Fairly (TCF)
bullets: There is generally a regulatory requirement for a health and care insurer in a particular jurisdiction to treat customers fairly, although there may be variations between jurisdictions in terms of what is actually deemed to be treating customers fairly.

Even if there is no direct regulatory requirement in a particular jurisdiction, there is likely to be an expectation by the local regulator that customers are treated fairly. This reinforces the need for continual monitoring by the insurer in the relevant areas, which include the sales process, the claims process, and the overall management of the customer relationship.

Policyholders form expectations about the benefits to which they are entitled and the level of service standards they will receive. These expectations (policyholders' reasonable expectations, or PRE) arise mainly from the sales process — i.e. from what the individual was told to encourage them to buy. Additionally, insurance company advertising and any regular communications from the insurer will further define what the policyholder expects.
The company has a duty of care to analyse policyholders' reasonable expectations and to correct matters where there is disparity between belief and reality.
This may all be part of an insurer's responsibilities under any 'treating customers fairly' initiative of the local regulator.

Ways to monitor policyholders' expectations include: random monitoring of the selling process (e.g. recording telephone calls); follow-up interviews with customers at a later stage (were their expectations met?); break-down analysis by seller; look at lapse and early cancellation rates by seller; monitor proportion of claims reported which were valid (by policy conditions), perhaps by seller; and monitor claims disputes and complaints.

key_terms.definition: A regulatory requirement (or expectation) that health and care insurers treat customers fairly across the sales process, claims process and overall customer relationship management.

Expectations formed by policyholders about the benefits and service levels they are entitled to under their policy; arise mainly from the sales process, advertising, and regular communications from the insurer.

exam_qa.q: Suggest ways that a health insurer can monitor the expectations of policyholders to ensure customers are being treated fairly.
exam_qa.a: Random monitoring of the selling process (e.g. recording telephone calls); follow-up interviews with customers at a later stage (were their expectations met?); break-down analysis by seller; look at lapse and early cancellation rates, e.g. by seller; monitor proportion of claims reported which were valid (i.e. according to policy conditions), perhaps by seller; monitor claims disputes and complaints.

subsections.title: Product Design / Standardisation of Definitions
bullets: Variation in product design is a feature of a competitive marketplace. In developed markets there may be at least as many variations in products as there are players. In some countries this has led to the standardisation of product terms (e.g. disease definitions).

Standardisation wording of policy terms/conditions should be as robust as possible in differentiating between what is, and what is not, covered in order to: create a clear expectation of the scope and limitations of the cover; allow valid claims to be paid promptly; and minimise the number of disputed claims to avoid disappointment.

Standardisation will potentially allow consumers to compare price and other product benefits instead of trying to compare different definitions. Consumers should also have greater assurance in claims results with a reduced incidence of one insurer paying a claim and another rejecting it due to differences in policy definitions.
Standard policy definitions could also make the task of ensuring consumer protection easier for an insurance regulator in a jurisdiction.

For example, in the UK, through the ABI (Association of British Insurers), there was success in obtaining widespread agreement to a single set of definitions among product providers, although the effect on subsequent volumes of sales is impossible to gauge.
Cover offered by different insurers in the UK has become more 'similar' in recent years. Comparisons are now easier, which has received a favourable response from consumer groups and brokers.

In coming up with the set of definitions, the parties involved kept in mind the importance of having clear, unambiguous policy wording — hoped to help reduce the number of disputed cases when claims arise.
Policy wording standardisation has been introduced in a number of jurisdictions such as Malaysia, Singapore, China, Canada, India, and Taiwan for critical illness insurance products.
The UK IP insurance industry and the PMI industry have also agreed a set of common definitions for many of the terms used in policies.

subsections.title: Data Sharing
bullets: In some jurisdictions, industry healthcare insurance data may be collected by a central body, with the objective of compiling generic statistics that insurers may use for pricing and reserving.

For example, in the UK, mortality data is collected by the Continuous Mortality Investigation (CMI), supported by the Institute and Faculty of Actuaries, for income protection and critical illness insurance products.

For critical illness insurance, to date there have been too few claims to issue a table of claim diagnosis rates that is fully credible at all ages of interest. However, the CMI has published separate tables for males and females, smokers and non-smokers, split by policy duration.

Data on IP insurance is more extensive and the CMI produce regular reports on both individual and group IP cover experience. Results are of the form of inception and termination rates, split by several factors such as deferred period and occupation class.
The ABI collects data from its members on policies, premiums and claims, which it compiles and publishes in quarterly and annual bulletins to highlight market trends.

For example in South Africa, the Actuarial Society of South Africa has various committees involved in data collection with respect to critical illness and disability experience. In addition, the AIDS committee maintains an AIDS model and associated data and statistics.

Data collection is also performed by some reinsurers on an annual basis to illustrate other features of the health and care insurance market — e.g. Swiss Re produce figures on new business sales and proportions of claims split by cause for CI insurance in the UK.

key_terms.definition: UK body supported by the Institute and Faculty of Actuaries that collects mortality and morbidity data for IP and CI products; publishes experience tables for pricing and reserving use.

subsections.title: Pricing
bullets: Due to the commercial sensitivity of the information, there will often be little to connect the pricing activity of competing insurers, except possibly the support of a limited number of reinsurers.
However, the level of competitors' rates will be an important consideration for an insurer in determining the final premiums to charge.
Care is required by insurers in sharing any information on pricing to avoid the possibility of the insurers being accused of collusion over setting prices.

subsections.title: Regular Review
bullets: An insurer should regularly review its internal and external product literature, and its internal processes, to ensure that they continue to reflect current legislative and regulatory requirements and market practice.

subsections.title: Overview
bullets: The following section illustrates some examples of best practice that have been agreed in various jurisdictions. The examples cover a number of different health and care products.
These examples are for illustration only and best practice could vary significantly from one jurisdiction to another.
Full details of the Statements used in the UK are available on the ABI's website (abi.org.uk).

subsections.title: Best Practice — Income Protection Insurance
bullets: The following is an example of best practice issued by the Association of British Insurers (ABI) for Health and Care insurers selling income protection business in the UK.

The Statement of Best Practice covers: the description of Income Protection Cover in specific customer-facing literature (referred to as a 'Key Features Document'); Guidance Notes for certain policy terms and conditions; and examples of certain generic terms and the meaning of those terms.

Key Features Document: the Statement gives a model Key Features Document. It requires that the cover is adequately and clearly described to customers by providing clear answers to pre-determined questions and headings, such as 'When will my plan pay out?'.

Guidance Notes cover 10 policy terms: (1) Definition of Incapacity – Own Occupation; (2) Definition of Incapacity – Any Occupation; (3) Limitation of Benefit; (4) Proportionate/Rehabilitation Benefits; (5) Change of Occupation; (6) Claims Notification Period; (7) Deferred Period; (8) Waiver of Premium; (9) Pregnancy Clause; (10) Linked Claims. For each term, the Guidance Note describes its purpose, typical policy wording, and obligations on the insurer. It also gives guidance on how to apply the term, but recognises that practice will vary between insurers.

Generic Terms — where any of the following terms are used, the meanings given in the Statement should be used and other terms should not be used in their place: (1) Deferred Period — 'the period of incapacity before any benefit is paid' (not 'waiting period' or 'elimination period'); (2) Incapacity — to be used rather than 'disability'; (3) Generic Product Name — 'Income Protection' or 'Income Protection Cover' (not 'PHI'); (4) Model Exclusions — Aviation, Criminal Acts, Drug Abuse, Hazardous Sports and Pastimes, HIV/AIDS, Self-inflicted Injury, War and Civil Commotion.

key_terms.definition: Customer-facing product literature required by the ABI Statement of Best Practice for IP; provides clear answers to pre-determined questions and headings to ensure the cover is adequately and clearly described.
The ABI-mandated generic term for 'the period of incapacity before any benefit is paid'. Terms such as 'waiting period' or 'elimination period' should not be used instead.
The ABI-mandated generic term to be used in IP policy literature rather than 'disability'.
The ABI-mandated generic product name for IP insurance; the product should not be referred to as 'PHI' (Permanent Health Insurance) in customer-facing literature.

subsections.title: Best Practice — Critical Illness Cover
bullets: The example in this section relates to a critical illness insurance product from Singapore.

In 2014, the Life Insurance Association of Singapore (LIA) introduced a new framework for new critical illness business. The guidelines set out revised standard definitions for 37 common critical illnesses, and required the policies should have flexibility for more medical conditions to be covered.

The framework aims to provide customers in Singapore with more varied critical illness products, and encourage life insurers to innovate products and services to better meet customers' evolving needs.

The statement provides for a common way of describing critical illness cover to potential buyers at the point of purchase — e.g. that CI insurance pays out a lump sum if the life insured is diagnosed as suffering from one of the critical illnesses or has undergone a surgical procedure covered by the policy. CI plans can be sold as stand-alone or as an optional rider attached to a main policy.

Each CI condition or surgery covered by the policy is precisely defined in the policy contract and benefits will be paid only if the policyholder suffers from a condition that meets the standard definition.

The guidelines provide standard definitions for the severe stage of some of the 37 most common critical illnesses; updated from preceding definitions to reflect advances in clinical practices, medical science and technology.

The five most common CI conditions (accounting for ~90% of severe-stage claims per Gen Re's 2012 Dread Disease Survey across Hong Kong, Malaysia, Australia, UK and Singapore) are: major cancers; heart attack of specified severity; coronary artery by-pass surgery; stroke; and kidney failure.

Example of a standard definition — heart attack: Death of heart muscle due to obstruction of blood flow, evident by at least three of: (i) history of typical chest pain; (ii) new characteristic electrocardiographic changes; (iii) elevation of cardiac biomarkers (CKMB or Cardiac Troponin T or I at 0.5ng/ml+); (iv) imaging evidence of new loss of viable myocardium or new regional wall motion abnormality (confirmed by a Cardiologist specified by the Company).

key_terms.definition: Framework introduced by the Life Insurance Association of Singapore providing standard definitions for 37 common critical illnesses, updated to reflect advances in medical science, and requiring flexibility for more medical conditions to be covered.

subsections.title: Best Practice — Sales of Individual and Group PMI
bullets: The following is an example of best practice issued by the ABI for Health and Care insurers selling PMI cover in the UK. The statement replaced the previous version (July 2006) and is mandatory for all ABI members offering PMI cover.

Features and needs — insurers must provide clear and easy to understand explanations of the product and the cover provided, including: benefits payable (including exclusions and restrictions); type of underwriting used (e.g. moratorium or full underwriting) and implications for cover; requirement to make full disclosure; potential for premiums and/or policy terms to vary at renewal; arrangements for making claims (including any pre-authorisation requirement); arrangements for complaining about and cancelling the policy; and implications for cover when switching from one policy or insurer to another (including from a group scheme to an individual scheme).

It is particularly difficult to make clear to customers exactly what is and is not covered if conditions excluded are not explicitly set out in the policy (as is the case for full medical underwriting). The moratorium period can also cause confusion — the insurer must make it clear that if pre-existing conditions become eligible for cover following a specified symptom-free period, this should not cause policyholders to forgo medical treatment. In addition, some conditions (e.g. those requiring continuous monitoring) are not likely to become eligible for cover.

Additionally, the insurer must provide the customer with the following further information (in prescribed formats): ABI consumer guide 'Are you buying PMI?'; 'Your underwriting options'; and 'Your PMI cover for treatment for long-term/chronic conditions'.
'Are you buying PMI?' is a standard guide explaining certain features of PMI, such as premium increases and the consequences of changing product or insurer.

Common definitions — ABI insurers must use prescribed definitions for 10 terms: acute condition; cancer; chronic condition; day patient; diagnostic tests; inpatient; nurse; outpatient; pre-existing condition; and treatment.
Significant effort has been made in agreeing these 10 definitions; historically there have been significant differences in the wordings used by different PMI insurers.

Definition of 'chronic condition' — a disease, illness or injury that has one or more of the following characteristics: needs ongoing or long-term monitoring through consultations, examinations, check-ups, and/or tests; needs ongoing or long-term control or relief of symptoms; requires rehabilitation or for you to be specially trained to cope with it; continues indefinitely; has no known cure; or it comes back or is likely to come back.

Chronic conditions are generally not covered by UK PMI policies. The aim of the explanation is to ensure the customer clearly understands what constitutes such a condition so that any claims arising from it will not be paid. This is very important to insurers.

Clarifying the definition of 'chronic' is particularly difficult as conditions that are 'acute' (and therefore covered by PMI) can turn into 'chronic' ones. No matter how well it has been explained, the customer is unlikely to appreciate the difference between the two types of condition at a time when they are ill and have just been told their illness cannot be cured — to then be advised that their private medical costs will no longer be covered can be extremely upsetting, and this must be handled with great sensitivity.

Medically, too, there can be considerable doubt over whether a condition is chronic or not. Medical opinion on individual cases can vary, and this at a time where the doctors involved need to be sensitive to the customer's feelings. A clear definition is essential.

Implications of disputes — if customers can demonstrate that a chronic condition would be covered (e.g. if this point was badly explained at point of sale), the claim may need to be paid. This will cause problems for the insurer as these claims will not have been allowed for in pricing (and reserving for) the product. Premium increases are likely to lead to more customers lapsing cover.

Considering insurers individually, if one insurer gets more of these claims compared to its competitors (e.g. because of less clear policy wording), it will have to increase its premiums (and so become uncompetitive) or risk making losses.
Disputed cases should be avoided, even if the insurer ends up winning, as they can be expensive (e.g. cost of legal fees) and can lead to bad publicity.

In addition to using agreed common definitions, ABI insurers must also: have a clear heading and separate section (or leaflet) to explain their cover for cancer; use the sample template as a means of explaining their cancer cover; and be clear about when cover starts and ends, what treatments are covered, limits on benefits, circumstances in which cover would not be provided, and when cover might be withdrawn.

key_terms.definition: A disease, illness or injury that has one or more of the following: needs ongoing/long-term monitoring or control; requires rehabilitation or special training; continues indefinitely; has no known cure; or comes back or is likely to come back. Generally not covered by UK PMI.
A condition that is generally covered by PMI, in contrast to a chronic condition. The boundary between the two can be medically uncertain and must be handled sensitively.

A form of PMI underwriting under which pre-existing conditions are not explicitly set out; instead they are excluded for an initial period and may become eligible for cover after a specified symptom-free period.
A form of PMI underwriting under which excluded conditions are explicitly set out in the policy at the time of issue based on the applicant's medical history.

exam_qa.q: Explain the difficulties around providing a full explanation of cover for pre-existing conditions where a moratorium basis applies.
exam_qa.a: It is particularly difficult to make clear to customers exactly what is and is not covered if the conditions excluded are not explicitly set out in the policy (as is the case for full medical underwriting). The moratorium period can also cause confusion — the insurer must make it clear that if conditions that were pre-existing become eligible for cover following a specified symptom-free period, this should not cause policyholders to forgo medical treatment. In addition, some conditions (such as those that require continuous monitoring) are not likely to become eligible for cover.

exam_qa.q: Why has the UK PMI industry gone to such great lengths with the explanation of chronic conditions?
exam_qa.a: Chronic conditions are generally not covered by UK PMI policies. The aim of the explanation is to ensure that the customer clearly understands what constitutes such a condition and that any claims arising from such a condition will not be paid — this is very important to the insurers. If the customer can demonstrate that they thought that a particular condition would be covered by the policy (e.g. if this point was badly explained at point of sale), the claim may need to be paid. These additional claims will cause problems for the insurer as they will not have been allowed for in pricing (and reserving for) the product. The required premium increases are likely to lead to more customers lapsing cover. Considering insurers individually, if one insurer gets more of these claims compared to its competitors (e.g. because of having less clear policy wording), it will have to increase its premiums (and so become uncompetitive) or risk making losses. Disputed cases should also be avoided, even if the insurer ends up winning, as they can be expensive (e.g. the cost of legal fees) and can lead to bad publicity for the insurer concerned.

subsections.title: Misrepresentation and Treating Customers Fairly
bullets: In some jurisdictions, there may be an industry best practice with regards to the continuing fair treatment of claims for life, critical illness, income protection and other long-term protection insurance contracts in the light of evolving industry practice, regulations and any treating customers fairly requirements and experience.

The code could cover the fair treatment of misrepresentation (or non-disclosure) occurring during the application process and discovered at the point of claim. The code could also consider issues with respect to vulnerable customers such as those with disabilities or mental health conditions.
Insurers classify each misrepresentation case as innocent, careless, or deliberate/reckless. Three high-level categories of misrepresentation and their associated outcomes are:

Innocent — (a) customer acted honestly and reasonably in all the circumstances, including the customer's individual circumstances but only where these were known to the insurer; (b) a reasonable person would have considered the information was not relevant to the insurer; (c) the misrepresentation would have resulted in a different underwriting outcome. Outcome: The insurer may pay the claim in full.

Careless — (a) the misrepresentation resulted from insufficient care or failure to exercise reasonable care (from an understandable oversight to serious negligence); (b) a reasonable person would have considered the information was relevant to the insurer; (c) the misrepresentation would have resulted in a different underwriting outcome. Outcome: The insurer may apply a proportionate remedy.

Deliberate or reckless — (a) the misrepresentation was deliberate or reckless; (b) on the balance of probabilities, the customer knew (or must have known) the information was both incorrect and relevant to the insurer, or acted without any care as to whether it was correct or relevant; (c) the misrepresentation would have resulted in a different underwriting outcome. Outcome: The insurer may void the policy (decline the claim and cancel the policy from inception).
The overall principle is that the severe remedy of voiding a policy from outset should be confined to the most serious cases of misrepresentation.

Assessing claims — customers cannot be expected to provide information they are not asked for; in these circumstances there is no misrepresentation. In assessing claims, insurers should consider all circumstances, including how clear and concise the relevant questions were. Not much weight should be given to 'catch all' or 'memory test questions'.

Impact of UK guidance — since the introduction of the original guidance in January 2008, the number of claims to the UK Financial Ombudsman Service (FOS) on long-term protection policies has significantly reduced. There has also been a reduction in the number of claims being declined due to misrepresentation, but there has been a significant number of proportionate payments (i.e. where there has been careless misrepresentation).

Non-contestability clauses — in some jurisdictions, insurers now use non-contestability clauses to simplify the management of misrepresentation and non-disclosure. These state that once an applicant has successfully completed underwriting and gone on risk, the insurer is not able to challenge a claim after a defined period of time has elapsed (e.g. two years) on the basis of deliberate, reckless or careless misrepresentation on the original application. In practice, insurers may still be able to deny fraudulent claims after the non-contestable period, but it can be a difficult legal test to meet.

key_terms.definition: Misrepresentation where the customer acted honestly and reasonably, and a reasonable person would not have considered the information relevant to the insurer. Outcome: insurer may pay the claim in full.

Misrepresentation resulting from insufficient care or failure to exercise reasonable care (from an understandable oversight to serious negligence), where a reasonable person would have considered the information relevant. Outcome: insurer may apply a proportionate remedy.

Misrepresentation where the customer knew (or must have known) the information given was both incorrect and relevant to the insurer, or acted without any care as to whether it was correct or relevant. Outcome: insurer may void the policy from inception.
The outcome applied for careless misrepresentation; the insurer adjusts the claim settlement proportionately based on what terms would have been offered had the correct information been provided.

A clause stating that once an applicant has successfully completed underwriting and gone on risk, the insurer cannot challenge a claim after a defined period (e.g. two years) on the basis of deliberate, reckless or careless misrepresentation on the original application.

25.summary:
Best Practice
• Need to comply with local financial sales regulation; may include requirements on sales procedure, disclosure (including product information), fair treatment, claims handling standards and complaints.
• Could have a regulatory obligation to treat customers fairly (TCF), including analysing policyholders’ reasonable expectations (PRE) and correcting matters where there is disparity between belief and reality. PRE arise mainly from the sales process, advertising and any regular communications from the insurer.
• Insurers will monitor processes to ensure they do not create a keenness to claim, while making sure that policyholders are aware of the features of their contracts.
• Many countries have Statements or frameworks of Best Practice that aim to ensure customers are clear on what is and is not covered, that all valid claims are paid promptly, the number of disputed claims is minimised and customers are treated fairly.
• Each Statement gives guidance on the description of the product that should be given to the customer (e.g. in a Key Features Document) and guidance on, or model (or common) definitions for, certain terms used in product literature.
• In the UK, industry data on IP and CI insurances are collected by the CMI. The ABI collects and publishes some high-level data, as do some reinsurers. The ABI has issued codes of practice for long-term healthcare insurance products regarding misrepresentation and treating customers fairly.
• Three categories of misrepresentation: innocent (pay in full), careless (proportionate remedy), deliberate/reckless (void from inception). Non-contestability clauses (e.g. two-year period) are used in some jurisdictions to simplify management of misrepresentation post-underwriting.

key_terms.definition: This benefit is provided when a policy pays the sum insured upon death or diagnosis of a critical illness, whichever occurs first. If the life insured suffers a critical illness, then the sum insured is paid and the policy is terminated, ie payment of benefit is ‘accelerated’ forward from payment on death. Some policies accelerate a portion of the sum insured in which case the contract stays in force and pays the balance of the sum insured upon subsequent death. Most policies accelerate 100% of the sum insured.

In some jurisdictions, legislation may set down the rights that an individual has in relation to the disclosure of the information contained in their medical records. It could apply to reports obtained for insurance purposes (ie underwriting and claims) or in connection with employment.
Short-term insurance covering accident, sickness and unemployment. See also Creditor insurance.
Activities of daily living are a number of functional tests against which disability can be measured. See Incapacity.

Activities of daily working are an alternative set of functional tests against which disability can be measured. The functional tests are specifically related to tasks that might be encountered in the workplace, against which incapacity (disability) can be measured (eg dexterity, ability to follow instructions). See Incapacity.
Illnesses or conditions of a non-degenerative nature where a cure is a reasonable prospect. This should be contrasted with Chronic illnesses. See Chronic illnesses.
In some jurisdictions, PMI may only cover surgery and other treatment for illnesses deemed to be acute.

A group of people with something definitive in common, such as holding a membership or being an employee of an organisation (eg a motoring organisation such as the RAC in the UK or the armed forces), but not common employment. See Voluntary group.

This phrase relates to the practice in some PMI markets of calculating premiums with allowance for the increasing probability of claim as the age of the policyholder increases over the prospective period of cover (often to age 65). Therefore such policyholders are not subject to the age-related increases that affect PMI products with standard pricing. Provided policyholders renew their policies each year, premiums will not increase because of policyholders’ increasing age. However, the insurer usually retains the right to increase premiums subsequently to allow for medical inflation or medical inflation in excess of levels assumed in the original calculation. The PMI contracts would still be written on an annual renewal basis.

People will be more likely to take out insurance contracts when they believe their risk to be higher than the insurance company has allowed for in the premium, ie the benefits are worth more than the premiums payable. This is known as anti-selection. An example in critical illness cover may be where an individual begins to suffer pains in the chest; they take out a policy without mentioning the chest pains and then go to the doctor to see if the pains are due to heart disease. If they are, then there is a greater likelihood of a claim for benefit. Anti-selection is also recognised in the tendency for sick or sub-standard lives to legitimately renew policies or take up options providing additional cover without evidence of health.
See Tied Agents.

The period during which the insurer will assess a condition before deciding upon their acceptance of a claim under critical illness or total permanent disability cover. The assessment period will not normally be longer than 12 months, provided all the evidence needed has been provided. Also, the assessment period should only apply to claims for the conditions that must be permanent for cover to apply.

The asset share is the retrospective accumulation of past premiums, less expenses and the cost of cover, at the actual rate of return on the assets. The accumulation could be done for a single contract or a group of contracts. It is also referred to as the earned asset share or the retrospective earned asset share.

This term is generally used to describe companies which offer financial services that encompass both banking and insurance operations. A major objective is to cross sell the products of one operation to the customers of the other.

Benefit limitation is required so that the claimant with an IP policy retains a financial incentive to return to work. Benefit payments under an IP policy will be subject to an upper limit, typically specified in a maximum benefit formula. A major part of the maximum benefit formula will be a maximum replacement ratio (eg 75%). Many companies impose more stringent limitations for higher salaries in excess of specified limits, and many companies have an overall maximum benefit. When calculating the amount to be paid to the policyholder, many companies deduct any other income that the claimant may receive if benefits are up to the maximum. The effect on income net of tax is also very relevant as there may be certain circumstances where the benefit permitted by the formula is more than that required to maintain a claimant’s net income at his or her pre- disability level.

These are cheaper policies (generally PMI policies) with restricted cover. Examples are PMI policies with excesses (the insured bears the first tranche, a pre-specified fixed sum, of each claim), policies contingent on public service waiting periods (cover is only provided where the official waiting list is longer than a pre-specified time, often six weeks) and policies that provide in-patient cover only. The term can also apply to CI policies (restricted list of diseases covered) and IP policies (limited benefit period). See Limited benefits, Excess.
This refers to a premium rate applied uniformly per head on large schemes across a membership type (independent of actual members’ ages or gender). It can also be referred to as a Unit rate.

Typically the burning cost is the estimated cost of claims in the forthcoming insurance period, calculated from previous years’ experience adjusted for changes in the numbers insured, the nature of cover and medical inflation. The term can also be used to describe the historic cost of claims only.

C-ROSS is the regulatory framework for insurance companies in China. The C-ROSS framework is the Chinese equivalent of Solvency II. The C-ROSS framework does have some differences to Solvency II to reflect the characteristics of the Chinese insurance market.

This term relates to the practice of charging for cover by forecasting the likely claims on an individual basis and charging this, adjusted for expenses and profit, as the premium. In effect, the insurance company separates out a set of medical benefits (such as dental claims or mental health claims) and passes this risk onto the provider - giving them a proportion of the insurance premium for each person managed up-front rather than an amount per claim. The risk that funds are insufficient to cover treatment lies with the provider of the healthcare service. In some jurisdictions, capitation may apply in the field of dental covers where the dentist anticipates the costs incurred by his/her patient.

A career break clause allows the policyholder to resume his or her coverage after a break in employment (eg study, retrain, travel, start a family), subject to time restrictions. Career break is an increasingly common feature of IP contracts.

key_terms.term: Cash plan or Health cash plan
key_terms.definition: This product pays a pre-specified cash sum on the occurrence of certain medical events (hospital stays, physiotherapy, need for spectacles, etc). Typically the benefits paid are low relative to the true cost and represent cash rather than indemnity benefits. There is also normally coinsurance and an annual limit.

This is a requirement that may be found on IP and possibly CI insurances, whereby the insured must notify the insurer of a change in occupation, possibly resulting in the insurer reclassifying the policyholder to a different occupation class and so revising premiums or disability definition.

Chronic illnesses are degenerative and/or generally incurable. The purpose of any treatment is palliative. This should be contrasted with Acute illnesses. In some jurisdictions, PMI may not generally cover treatment for chronic illnesses, although some insurers may offer treatment for cancer that includes palliative care.

This is the rate at which claims, in the course of payment, escalate under IP or LTCI policies. Unless otherwise stated, they compound annually. Escalation rates for claims often can fall into three categories such as increase in an index or earnings, or they could increase at a fixed rate. Benefit increases can apply both prior to and during claim payment. Increases may be subject to a maximum or minimum rate.

In order to improve claims handling procedures, many companies require that claimants notify them of potential claims at an early stage. The notification requirements of companies generally fall into two broad categories: The insured is required to notify the company a set time after the incapacity begins, irrespective of the deferred period. The insured is required to notify the company at a set time before the end of the deferred period. The purpose of this period is twofold: to ensure that valid claims are ready to be paid at the end of the deferred period and for the purposes of early intervention from a claims management perspective.

To protect itself from financial disadvantage, an insurer will set up rules for dealing with claims that are notified very late (ie notification occurs after the end of the deferred period). Practice on the late notification of a claim varies greatly, and may depend on whether the notification is received within a certain period from the end of the deferred period. The options might be: Backdate the claim to the end of the deferred period (possibly requiring supporting medical evidence to be provided). Do not backdate benefits, so that benefits will be lost: -         Treat the date of notification as the date the benefit becomes payable. -         Assume the commencement of incapacity to be the date of notification. -         Assume that incapacity started at a set period prior to notification. Reduce the benefit payable. Refuse / decline the claim. Subject all late notified claims to individual consideration.

When certain group insurance policies are to be renewed, prospective insurers are given information on claims history over previous scheme years. This comes in one of two forms: Claims paid, where the amounts actually paid by previous insurers in each scheme year are totalled, or Claims incurred, where the amounts relative to all claims that commenced in a particular year of insurance are totalled; this equates to amounts paid plus outstanding reserves. Claims paid or incurred will also be integral components in many forms of reporting, eg the assessment of profitability in management reports, and appropriate usage is important.

Some PMI insurers require (others recommend) that intended courses of treatment or surgery be approved by them for policy coverage before costs are incurred. This process can often provide opportunity for the insurer to manage care provision or reduce after-the- event claim denials.

Coinsurance is the general term given to a PMI policy condition whereby the policyholder is required to pay, at least in part, for medical expenses incurred, maybe on a percentage basis. See also Excess. The insured and the insurer share the cost of the claim in fixed proportions (eg insured pays 20% of the claim). In other contexts, coinsurance also refers to the situation where two insurers share the contract with the policyholder and also may refer to reinsurance on an original terms basis.
See Loss ratio.

Community rating most often refers to the practice of charging all policyholders (or a significant subset of the persons insured) the same premium rate irrespective of rating factors such as age, gender and medical history. Community rating sometimes refers to the process of applying tabular rates to applicants irrespective of claims history.

The standard or comprehensive PMI cover provides for full reimbursement of all medical costs incurred in hospitals within the appropriate bands and for such other treatments as the policy stipulates. Occasionally some high policy limits will apply (per year or per section of risk). See Policy limits.

A continuation option describes a benefit under an insurance policy whereby the insured can choose, without having to provide evidence of health, to continue the cover provided by the policy under circumstances where it has otherwise ceased. Such circumstances might apply where the individual has left their place of work and is no longer covered by an employer-sponsored scheme or where an individual policy has expired. The terms under which the option is effected are those applicable to a healthy life for the age at the date when the option arises.
This is ‘no worse terms’ acceptance whereby the new PMI insurer promises only to carry forward such cover for medical conditions as existed under the previous insurance policy. See No worse terms.

Many of the largest group PMI schemes will self-insure. Some of these will seek to limit the possible downside by insuring against an extreme experience. Insurance will be arranged to cover the excess of a pre-agreed claims fund. Such an arrangement is called cost plus cover or stop loss cover See Stop loss.

This relates to experience rating of group healthcare insurance products. The term credibility refers to the factor, between 0 and 1, which represents the proportion of the final risk premium that is derived from past experience, the balance coming from book rates. The value of the factor depends on the size of the scheme. See Experience rating.

This is a form of cover that is taken out to protect a loan or mortgage. It may consist solely of life insurance that will repay the outstanding loan if the borrower dies before it is repaid. Often cover is extended to pay off the loan following a total and permanent disability and, more recently, critical illness. Creditor insurance can also be taken out to cover repayments during temporary disability or unemployment.

This term can be used to refer to the type of contract (see Accelerated critical illness benefit and Stand-alone critical illness plans) that provides benefits on the diagnosis of a ‘critical illness’, or to the specific illnesses covered under such contracts. These illnesses are defined by the insurer, and may cover conditions such as cancer, heart attack, kidney failure, major organ transplant, multiple sclerosis, stroke. The illnesses or conditions are typically perceived by the public to be serious (ie life threatening or lifestyle threatening), and to occur frequently.

This term relates to the increasing practice of treating the more straightforward operations in hospital surgical units on the day of admission, occupying a bed but being discharged on the same day (ie no overnight stay).
See Excess.

This is a term most often encountered in IP insurance, meaning the period of incapacity before any benefit is paid. For example in the UK, the most common deferred periods in the market are 4, 13, 26 and 52 weeks. Other less common deferred periods are 0, 1, 8 and 104 weeks. 28 weeks is often found as a deferred period under group arrangements. See Waiting period.

The majority of companies effectively have a standard definition of incapacity such as ‘totally unable through sickness or accident to perform your own occupation and not following any other for profit or reward’ although the exact words used will vary. This definition is normally applied throughout the duration of the contract. Some companies amend their definition of incapacity after a certain claim period has elapsed (usually two years). Most companies reserve the right to apply an alternative definition of incapacity depending upon the occupation of the insured. Some companies have introduced a definition of incapacity based on an abilities test. Companies will also generally use different definitions of incapacity when cover is offered to housepersons and where cover is maintained on a restricted basis during a period of unemployment. See Housepersons, Unemployment benefit, ADWs.

This is the marketing of products directly to the potential customers with no intermediary or salesperson involved. It could be via mail, by advertising in newspapers or periodicals, by telephone, email or the internet. In all situations the customer is invited to apply for the product, usually directly to the insurance company.
An insurance company that employs the services of salespeople to sell only their products directly to the customer is said to operate a direct salesforce.

Earned premium is the proportion of premiums written and received that relates directly to the expired period of cover. For example, consider: A policy commencing on 1 October of year N with an annual premium of £300. This £300 would contribute £75 to earned premiums in the year N and £225 to earned premiums in the year N + 1. The second annual premium would contribute £75 to earned premiums in year N + 1 and £225 to those in year N + 2 etc. A policy commencing on 1 October of year N with a single premium of £800 for two years’ cover. Such a policy would contribute £100 to earned premiums in the year N, £400 to earned premiums in year N + 1 and £300 to earned premiums in the year N + 2.

EIOPA is the European Insurance and Occupational Pensions Authority, one of the EU’s main financial supervisory bodies. Its core responsibilities are to support the stability of the financial system, transparency of markets and financial products across the EU, as well as the protection of insurance policyholders, pension scheme members and beneficiaries. It has powers to develop and implement technical standards and has been heavily involved in the development of Solvency II.
This is surgery that is deemed to be non-emergency.

These terms (the latter more often in the United States) are used to describe the policy condition whereby the insured is responsible for the first £x or $x of any claim. The excess / deductible may operate not on individual claims but on the aggregate of claims over a policy year, or be applied per life or per policy.

These are causes of disability (or ‘perils’) that are explicitly excluded from the cover provided by a policy. The most common exclusions are: war, terrorism, acts of violence, civil unrest self-inflicted injury or attempted suicide drugs alcohol hazardous pastimes or sports aerial activity other than as a fare-paying passenger criminal acts failure to seek or follow medical advice. A further common exclusion for PMI business is treatment relating to standard pregnancy, and potentially also complications of pregnancy and childbirth.

Experience rating is the process by which partial or full credibility is given to the past claims history of the risk in assessing the premium payable at renewal. If the premium for a risk is assessed by only using the claims history for that risk, the experience is said to be 100% or fully credible. If the risk is assessed using both the clams history for the risk and the claims history for the rating group to which the risk belongs, the experience is less than 100% or partially credible.

Meaning optional, this is a process of reinsurance where there are no obligations on either the insurance company or the reinsurer to offer and accept risks. The insurance company offers facultative business when and where it chooses, and the reinsurer can decide whether or not to accept it.

The FCA is the UK regulator responsible for regulation of conduct in financial markets (and the infrastructure that supports those markets) and the prudential regulation of financial services companies that do not fall under the scope of the PRA (eg insurance brokers and smaller investment firms).
including setting technical actuarial standards, and issuing accounting standards in the UK.

This phrase relates to an agreement between a PMI insurer and a hospital (or hospital chain) whereby all surgical procedures of a type will be charged at a particular cost per case rate, regardless of the individual complexity. These rates are commonly referred to as ‘case rates’ or ‘procedure pricing’. The price includes all care, including any that may be necessary following complications.

This term refers to a benefit level in a group risk arrangement, below which the member is not subject to individual underwriting. Free cover is usually a function of the number of members or the aggregate of benefits provided. Free cover limits are also known as ‘non-selection limits’.
This is the set of Generally Accepted Accounting Principles (‘GAAP’) that may be used to produce accounts in a particular jurisdiction. For example, you can get US GAAP, Chinese GAAP, UK GAAP etc.

This phrase refers to the individual’s own doctor, who provides primary medical care and is normally the first port-of-call for all health concerns. The GP may or may not hold all individual health records pertinent to that person.

Normal medical protocols would involve the patient being referred for secondary care after an initial consultation with his or her general practitioner. In the UK, most PMI policies require a GP referral as a pre-condition to cover.

Group business is insurance covering a number of individuals under a single policy. The members of the group are linked in some way (eg employees in the same company or holders of the same credit card) and there is often a sponsor (eg employer) who facilitates in the payment of premiums and administrative matters. Membership may be compulsory or voluntary.
This describes the situation where the benefit / premium relationship is set from the outset for the duration of the policy. See Reviewable premiums.
See Cash plan.

This is a form of health organisation akin to insurance that combines a range of coverages on a group basis. A group of doctors and other medical professionals offer care through the HMO for a monthly subscription. However, only visits to professionals within the HMO network are covered by the policy. All visits, prescriptions and other care must be cleared by the HMO in order to be covered. A primary doctor within the HMO handles referrals. They are more common in the USA.

PMI insurers sometimes split the hospitals permitted under the policy into categories (by price of treatment), known as hospital bands, usually three in number. The policy premium is generally scaled in line with the policyholder’s choice of hospital group. In some jurisdictions, insurers are moving away from this classification and using more sophisticated methods of rating, such as geographical rating factors.

People who are not in paid employment but whose contribution to a family might be measured in financial terms. The majority of IP insurers also provide housepersons cover. Two categories have been identified by reference to the definition of incapacity, as follows: Category 1:         Ability to perform certain activities of daily living (ADLs). Category 2:         Confinement to home or hospital or inability to perform normal household duties. See ADLs.

International Financial Reporting Standards (‘IFRS’) are the accounting standards developed and maintained by the International Accounting Standards Board. They are intended to provide a consistent basis for accounts globally, and are currently used in over 100 countries including the EU.

This is an immediate annuity purchased by an impaired life requiring long-term care. It provides regular payments guaranteed throughout the annuitant’s lifetime, and thus provides protection against the uncertain survival duration.
