According to the ABI Statement of Best Practice for IP in the UK: The term ‘incapacity’ should normally be used instead of the term ‘disability’, in particular as regards the definition of disability clause in the policy contract, which should be called the definition of incapacity clause.

Income protection insurance (formerly known as permanent health insurance (PHI)) provides cover against incapacity. The benefit is paid as an income (usually monthly) for as long as the disability exists up to a pre-defined age, usually 60 or 65, or retirement if earlier. This is a protection policy that insures against the loss of income, as a result of sickness or injury, during the term of the policy.

There are two types of option under which increases in the benefit can be secured without formal underwriting. The first covers those options whereby the increases in benefit are incorporated in the original contract and operate automatically. They may be subject to predetermined premiums, which may: be level throughout the lifetime of the policy increase in line with the benefit increase by some other specified pattern. Alternatively, increments may be costed as they arise. The increase can be a fixed monetary sum, or be in line with a fixed percentage or some form of earnings or prices index. This second type of option is that which includes a periodic opportunity for the policyholder to increase the benefit by a fixed percentage, with the new policy being costed in the normal way, based on the then-current age and outstanding term. Increase options are found under critical illness and income protection insurance policies.

This term relates to claims where the claim event has happened but the insurer has yet to receive notification. The insurer is required to hold reserves against such events when assessing results or submitting accounts for a particular period.

Under the principle of indemnity, the insured is restored to the same financial position after a loss as before the loss. PMI is usually an indemnity contract. However some policies incorporate cash limits in particular circumstances, and so do not provide full indemnity.

This is insurance covering an individual under a single policy document. Where the insurance additionally covers immediate family members on a joint life basis, this is also considered to be individual business.

In some jurisdictions, industry bodies have been set up to support health and care insurance companies. An example of an industry body is the Association of British Insurers in the UK, which is a trade association which supports insurance companies in the UK. An example of the work done by the ABI is the development of industry standard wording for health and care insurance companies in the UK.
This is a person who is admitted to hospital and occupies a bed overnight.

Insurance intermediaries are independent of any particular financial services company (contrasted with a member of a direct salesforce). They select and recommend the products that they consider to be most appropriate for the customer using various criteria. They are known in some territories as brokers or financial advisers. See Direct salesforce and Tied agents.

In some jurisdictions general insurance premiums may be subject to tax. For example in the UK most general insurance premiums are subject to an insurance premium tax (IPT), currently (May 2018) at the rate of 12.0%. So, for example, this tax is levied on PMI premiums.
Insurtech is a term used to describe the use of innovations in technology to provide savings and or efficiencies from the current models used by insurance companies.
This is a classification of diseases and surgical operations, by both coding and wording, in order to maintain an international standard. Most countries classify diseases using ICD-9 or ICD-10 codes.

The IAA is a worldwide association of professional actuarial bodies. Its aim is to represent the actuarial profession and promote its role, reputation and recognition in the international domain. One of the ways it does this is by developing ‘model’ International Standards of Actuarial Practice.
This is surgery that is undergone to advance the diagnosis (nature and extent of the complaint). It is generally covered under PMI products but may not be so under MME products.

Irreversible conditions are those that cannot be cured by medical treatment and/or surgical procedures at the time of the claim. This term is generally used to define CI conditions - for example, blindness, deafness, loss of speech and paralysis of the limbs.

Insurance companies in some jurisdictions may be required by legislation to provide new policyholders with a short synopsis of the product which is easy to read and capable of being understood. This documentation could set out the required information such as aims and purposes of the policy, nature of the policyholders’ commitment, a description of the risk factors and illustrative projections.

Keyperson products are taken out by an employer to cover key employees. They fall into two categories: Those designed to provide compensation for loss of profits. Those designed to cover the key employee’s salary (to facilitate the temporary recruitment of a replacement). The insurance should cover sickness, incapacity or death of the key employee.

A number of IP providers offer policies under which, in the event of a claim, benefit payments are restricted to a certain number of years. These policies are sometimes referred to as budget plans because they are cheaper than contracts that provide benefit to the terminal age. See Budget policies.

These relate to IP claims whereby a claimant, having been in receipt of claim payments, recovers and returns to work, but within a specified period (the ‘link period’) suffers a recurrence of the same disability, and is eligible for immediate claim payments without the imposition of another deferred period. The standard definition refers to a linked period of six months. The link period is also sometimes called the ‘off-benefit’ period.

This type of insurance can be used to help provide financial security against the risk of needing either home or nursing-home care as an elderly person, ie post-retirement. The contract could pay for all the costs of care throughout the remainder of life (an indemnity contract), or could provide a cash lump sum or annuity to contribute towards the costs of care. in terms of ADLs.

The ratio of claims incurred, including allowance for ultimate settlement amounts, to the relevant premium. In some instances the ratio is calculated with expenses also in the numerator, but this statistic should be more properly termed the combined or operating ratio.

MME in the UK is a PMI product variant that pays a fixed amount (from a schedule relating to severity) to policyholders who undergo surgery of a non-investigative and non-cosmetic nature. MME in the USA is a comprehensive PMI product type offering reimbursement for the costs of primary, secondary and tertiary care as defined in the policy.

Managed care is the term given to a process whereby an insurer intervenes in the provision of medical care with the dual objective of optimising the quality of treatment for the policyholder and controlling the cost (by such means as preferred provider utilisation and claims pre-authorisation).

Under this approach, an individual’s proposal to effect insurance will be underwritten without regard to that individual’s past medical history, ie there are no exclusions for pre-existing medical conditions. It is most commonly used for group PMI business, or it may be offered to an individual on transfer from a group to individual PMI policy.

This term generally refers to the annual increase in the average cost of medical treatment per insured life. It could reflect increases due to an increase in treatment costs and/or an increase in average incidence.

Microinsurance is the term used for the growing market in insurance products (including health insurance) that are characterised by low premiums and low coverage limits. Based on a pooling or community approach, microinsurance is generally targeted at the low wealth segments of a population and provides a social benefit in providing access to insurance cover for such socio-economic groups. It is currently well developed in India and some parts of Africa.

Under Pillar 1 of Solvency II, there are two distinct capital requirements that have to be held in excess of technical provisions: the Solvency Capital Requirement (‘SCR’) and the Minimum Capital Requirement (‘MCR’). The MCR is the capital level below which the insurer would lose its authorisation. It is determined using a simple factor-based linear formula which is targeted at a Value at Risk over one year with 85% confidence.

Certain PMI products (and possibly also group CI insurance), in place of formal medical underwriting at inception, do not cover medical conditions that existed during a pre- specified period, often five years, but will then cover these conditions if no treatment, symptoms or advice takes place for a period of, typically, two or three consecutive years after first taking out the policy (or after receiving further treatment). The insurer is however on immediate risk for all other conditions. See Pre-existing conditions.

Under PMI, an NCD system refers to an increasing discount to some reference premium applied for each year that no claims are made, to a limit. For each claim made, the level of discount reduces and could result in a premium higher than the reference premium. This is used as an own-experience proxy for more accurate risk segmentation.

In certain circumstances, on PMI or group business policy renewal, a new insurer will agree to offer cover at least as comprehensive and with no additional underwriting conditions as the policyholder’s current policy; the renewal or ‘switch’ is accepted on no worse terms.

A non-contestability clause states that once an applicant has successfully completed underwriting and gone on risk, the insurer is not able to challenge a claim after a defined period of time has elapsed (eg two years) on the basis of deliberate, careless or negligent non-disclosure on the original application.

Non-medical limits denote the maximum (long-term) policy benefits for which one can propose without needing an automatic medical examination or PMAR. The fact that a benefit falls within the non-medical limit is no guarantee that the proposer will not be asked to attend a medical examination. Insurers always reserve the right to call for any additional medical evidence that they feel is necessary in the light of any information that they already have. See Private Medical Attendant’s Report.

In non-proportional reinsurance, the reinsurance protection covers amounts above certain pre-defined limits, rather than splitting individual claims in given percentages. Under PMI, it will often apply to a portfolio of risks, not individual risks; the reinsurer will pay when the losses on the whole portfolio exceed an agreed limit. Examples of non-proportional reinsurance include stop loss and catastrophe excess of loss cover.

Occupational classes are used to rate proposers in many lines of health and care insurance. Examples of occupations that fall into different sample classes are: Class 1 - actuary, accountant, archaeologist, astrologer, author. Class 2 - doctor, dog breeder, counsellor. Class 3 - teacher, plumber. Class 4 - roofer, Heavy Goods Vehicle driver.
Open enrolment refers to the process in some markets whereby an insurer is obliged to accept all proposers for insurance at standard rates.
See Loss ratio.

The original terms method of reinsurance is where the reinsurer has a virtually identical contract to the insurance company in respect of the reinsured portion of the risk. The reinsurer receives the same gross premiums for its share as the insurance company, except that the policy fee is normally retained in full. The reinsurer is responsible for its full proportionate share of the sum insured in the event of a claim, and, likewise, for any surrender values that are paid.
This is a person who attends hospital for treatment or consultation but who does not occupy a bed. In some markets this is known as ‘ambulatory treatment’.

Under Pillar 1 of Solvency II, the phrase ‘own funds’ refers to assets in excess of technical provisions and subordinated liabilities. These are split into basic and ancillary own funds, which are then tiered based on specific criteria.

Under Pillar 2 of Solvency II, each insurance company is required to carry out an Own Risk and Solvency Assessment (ORSA). The ORSA has been defined as: The entirety of the processes and procedures employed to identify, assess, monitor, manage and report the short and long term risks an insurance undertaking faces or may face and to determine the own funds necessary to ensure that the undertaking’s overall solvency needs are met at all times. A number of other jurisdictions where Solvency II does not apply now require insurers to carry out ORSA assessments that are similar to the assessments required under Solvency II under their own regulatory regime. For example ORSAs are required by insurers in the US and China.

This term is used to describe a health condition that is expected to last throughout the insured person’s life, irrespective of when the cover ends or the insured person retires. This definition would be used, for example, in relation to a claim for total permanent disablement (TPD) benefit under a CI contract.
This type of insurance provides specified fixed benefit amounts in the event that the insured suffers the loss of one or more limbs, or other specified injury.

An alternative way of assessing disability that is largely independent of the age and the occupation (if any) of the person being assessed. Personal capability assessment involves assessment of the ability to complete everyday tasks of living (eg climbing stairs, bending, lifting and carrying). See Activities of Daily Living (ADLs), Activities of Daily Working (ADWs).

This is the maximum amount that can be paid out under a policy, sometimes expressed over a defined period of time (eg annual limit). Some indemnity policies limit the benefits payable under particular sections of the policy (eg PMI policies may limit payments for physiotherapy to £500 in any policy year). Such policies only provide partial indemnity. See Indemnity policy.

Under the terms of such an exclusion, cover is not provided in respect of any of the conditions listed in the policy that the life insured has already suffered, ie where the condition pre-existed at the commencement of cover. It is also usual to exclude cover for any condition where the life insured has previously suffered from another medical condition that gives a materially greater risk of that condition occurring. PEC exclusions can be used in PMI, CI and IP contracts. See Moratorium.

A PMI policy may restrict cover to such treatment as is carried out in certain medical establishments (with whom it has special arrangements, often financial) or may limit the scale of reimbursement outside these establishments. The premium for such policies where treatment must take place in a PPO will usually be cheaper than if the claimant had the choice of any possible provider.

In some jurisdictions insurers may have a clause in their IP policies terms which covers how claims should be treated with respect to complications with pregnancy. Actual practice may vary, but one approach could be that ‘disability attributable to pregnancy or complications thereof is not covered unless the condition continues for more than 13 weeks after the termination of the pregnancy. The deferred period is then deemed to commence’.

Primary care is the term given to such advice and treatment as provided by a general practitioner. This is not typically covered by a PMI policy in the UK, although elsewhere primary care would be an insured benefit. See Secondary care, Tertiary care.
This report is sought by the insurer to provide further insight into an individual’s state of health at the proposal or claim stage.

This is a product under which in return for premiums, an insurer promises to pay certain sums of money on the occurrence of certain medical events. The product is generally classified as short-term insurance, the events are frequently surgical in nature (predominantly so in the UK) and the amounts paid usually indemnify costs incurred by the individual in receiving treatment.

Profit sharing relates to the practice under group scheme business whereby the insurer rewards the group for better than expected experience with some share in the profit arising. This share might be expressed as a cash refund or as a discount against the future premium.

Under IP policies, if a claimant takes up employment in an occupation that is different to the one from which they were originally incapacitated, it is usual for the continuing benefit to be reduced. The reduction will relate to the ratio that the gross earnings from the new job bear to those from the occupation against which disability was being claimed. It is a standard requirement that disability from the original occupation continues whilst a proportionate benefit is being paid. Some companies require a full claim to be established before a proportionate benefit can be claimed.
prudential regulation of all deposit-taking institutions, insurance providers and large investment firms.

This IP benefit is payable when a claimant is no longer totally unable to follow his or her original occupation and returns to it in a reduced capacity. The amount of benefit is usually calculated in the same way as that for proportionate benefit, as described above. Rehabilitation can also apply to the process of counselling whereby disability counsellors assist disabled persons with advice on practical matters to do with the benefit and their disability, in order to aid a return to work.

This refers, in the context of income protection insurance, to the ratio of net (in benefit) income to net pre-disability income. A value of less than one is desirable from the insurer’s viewpoint, to provide a financial incentive for the claimant’s return to work, especially given that expenses in disability may be less than those in normal (working) health.

This term relates to claims that have been notified to the insurer, but the sum insured due has yet to be agreed and paid. The insurer is required to hold reserves against these claims, when assessing results or submitting accounts for a particular period.
This clause limits the countries in which the policyholder may be resident to make a valid claim, to ensure effective claims management.

An index published by the Office for National Statistics (ONS) in the UK. It is an average measure of change in the prices of goods and services bought for the purpose of consumption by the vast majority of households in the UK. It is compiled monthly and has been published regularly since 1948. The RPI includes prices for food and drink, tobacco, housing, household goods and services, personal goods and services, transport fares, motoring costs, clothing and leisure goods and services. The data are collected through surveys. See National Average Earnings Index (NAEI).

This is a form of premium offered by income protection and critical illness insurance providers. It allows the insurer to alter the premiums if the prospective claims experience (or any aspect of the premium basis specified in the contract) for the portfolio is different from that which was originally expected. Most companies that employ reviewable rates undertake the reviews every five years, though experience monitoring is done on a regular basis. See Guaranteed premium rates.

These are extra benefits that can be added to a basic policy either at commencement of the cover or sometimes at defined policy anniversaries of the contract. These benefits would be underwritten at outset and would normally affect premium rates and possibly initial underwriting requirements. For marketing reasons, some riders are provided for the policyholder at no additional charge.

A number of additional benefits may be added to standard IP covers, such as: hospitalisation benefit lump sum TPD benefit critical illness benefit terminal illness benefit unemployment and health waiver of premium benefit death benefit State benefit option (this pays an additional IP benefit if you do not receive State benefits) fatal accident benefit retirement protection option.

Risk equalisation applies in some markets whereby the profits or losses on specified policies or risks are pooled and reapportioned among participating insurers so that each shares in the average market experience.

The risk premium method of reinsurance is used when a long-term insurer wishes to reinsure only the risk element (mortality or morbidity) of a policy. The insurer and reinsurer agree a set of risk premium factors to be applied to the benefit reinsured; these factors will generally (if permitted by legislation) vary by age, gender and smoker status. Under CI and other health contracts, these risk premium rates are often used as the basis for the insurer’s office premium rates.

Secondary care is the term given to such advice and treatment as provided by hospitals, consultants and other specialists, usually after referral by the patient’s general practitioner. See Primary care, Tertiary care.

Most companies offer different rates for smokers and non-smokers for long term contracts, reflecting the different morbidity or mortality of smokers and non-smokers. Smoker / non- smoker differentials would not apply to the pricing of larger group schemes that are fully experience-rated. Smoking status is defined by each insurer, eg not used smoking materials of any kind for the last three years.

Solvency II refers to the set of regulatory requirements implemented on 1 January 2016 for insurance firms in the EU, based on a three pillar approach. The aim of EU solvency rules is to ensure that insurance undertakings are financially sound and can withstand adverse events, in order to protect policyholders and the stability of the financial system as a whole.

Under Pillar 1 of Solvency II, there are two distinct capital requirements that have to be held in excess of technical provisions: the Solvency Capital Requirement (‘SCR’) and the Minimum Capital Requirement (‘MCR’). The SCR is a Value at Risk measure calibrated to a 99.5% confidence level. It can be calculated using a prescribed standard formula approach, or by using a company-specific internal model which must be approved by the regulator.

These are policies that only provide cover against critical illness and do not provide (or accelerate in the case of a stand-alone rider) any benefit in the event of death. Following payment of the critical illness benefit, the policy terminates. Occasionally such policies may offer a nominal sum in the event of death before a critical illness is suffered. See Accelerated critical illness benefit.

In many jurisdictions, there is a statutory requirement for a health and care insurance company transacting long-term business to appoint actuaries into certain positions. The titles applied to these roles vary depending on local regulations. For example in the European Union under Solvency II there is a statutory requirement to have a Chief Actuary, whilst other jurisdictions have a requirement for an Actuarial Function Holder, Appropriate Actuary or Appointed Actuary. The detail of any statutory actuarial roles and their responsibilities in a particular jurisdiction will be set out in the local regulations.

Similar to cost plus, stop loss is a reinsurance contract whereby an insurer’s claims exposure will be restricted at some multiple of premium. The stop loss arrangement will stipulate a loss ratio above which the reinsurer becomes responsible for all or the majority of further claims.

In some jurisdictions this could be used as an alternative to a GP referral whereby the insurer uses Data Protection legislation to request, on behalf of the applicant, a full copy of medical records held by the GP. Use is controversial but perceived advantages are a fuller medical history and faster turnaround speed.
Under a surplus reinsurance arrangement, the long-term insurer will cede to the reinsurer all sums that exceed its retention on each individual life.

In the context of PMI, switch applies to the process whereby an existing policyholder, individual or group, changes insurer on renewal, possibly without further underwriting or with simplified underwriting ie a declaration of good health.

Technical Actuarial Standards are professional standards issued by the Financial Reporting Council, which cover both generic and specific areas of actuarial work. Each TAS is that is within the scope of that TAS.

Under Solvency II, the technical provisions consist of the best estimate liability and the risk margin. The latter is intended to increase the technical provisions to the amount that would have to be paid to another insurance company in order for them to take on the best estimate liability. It therefore represents the theoretical compensation for the risk of future experience being worse than the best estimate assumptions, and for the cost of holding regulatory capital against this.

This refers to the marketing of products via the telephone. Usually enquiries are generated by direct marketing (see above) with the customer telephoning the advertiser (usually the insurance company). The aim is to complete all application procedures over the telephone. The policy will then be despatched together with a direct debit instruction for completion and a copy of the completed application for signature by the applicant confirming the answers given to the underwriting questions. See Direct marketing.
This is a medical condition that is expected to result in the person’s death within a short period, eg twelve months.

Tertiary care is medical care that is only provided in specialist centres, eg neurosurgery, burns care. It is usually on referral from secondary medical care personnel, by specialists working in a centre that has personnel and facilities for special investigation and treatment (eg treatment in a specialist neurological unit). See Primary care, Secondary care.

Also known as appointed representatives, tied agents are salespeople who act more independently than a member of a direct salesforce (see above), but sell only the products of one insurance company, ie they are ‘tied’ to one company. They enjoy more freedom in how they operate and may employ salespeople themselves to sell the products of the selected company. See Direct salesforce.

This disability cover is often included within a critical illness product. It is the permanency of the disability that distinguishes it from income protection cover, although ‘permanent’ needs to be defined carefully. ‘Total’ in practice means the failure of ability to perform a major or substantial part of the job or function.

In many jurisdictions, insurers are expected to act honestly, fairly, and professionally with the best interests of its clients. This is often referred to as treating customers fairly. The interpretation of what constitutes treating customers fairly can vary significantly by jurisdiction. In some jurisdictions, this concept is well established, and can be enshrined in local law. For example in the UK the regulatory concept of Treating Customers Fairly (‘TCF’) enshrined within UK regulations is that a firm must pay due regard to the interests of its customers and treat them fairly.

A treaty is a formal agreement between an insurer and a reinsurer setting out the terms of a reinsurance arrangement. A treaty imposes an obligation on the reinsurer to accept automatically business ceded within the scope of the treaty. It also usually imposes a similar obligation on the insurer to pass business to the reinsurer that falls within the scope and other terms of the treaty

Where cover for unemployment is provided under an IP policy, it is usually for an amount lower than that which would be payable on incapacity. The level is usually identical to that offered under the Housepersons benefit. Some companies provide cover during unemployment but change to a definition of incapacity that requires confinement to the house (this may be extended to include a medical institution) or being unable to go outdoors without assistance. The student should note that this is not unemployment insurance, which is payment if the insured becomes unemployed.
See bulk rate.

This is the set of Generally Accepted Accounting Principles used in the USA and other countries. It is based on historical cost, revenue recognition and matching principles, with standards being issued by the Financial Accounting Standards Board (‘FASB’).
This describes a collection of policyholders for whom membership of a PMI scheme is voluntary. It may include affinity groups or employment groups. See Affinity group.

This is a feature adopted by friendly societies under which sickness benefits will not be paid for a specified period after the member first joins the society. This waiting period may also be applied to any additional benefit from the date that the member buys the additional units of cover. It might also be called a no-claim period. See also Deferred period.

This refers to the practice whereby the premium for an IP or CI policy is covered in addition to the main benefit provided by the policy in the event of disability. It is possible that waiver of premium can work in different ways. For example: 1.        companies that refund premiums proportionately for the period during which a claim is paid, or 2.        premium payments that fall due whilst the benefit is being paid could be waived.

Wearable technology is often in the form of devices worn by a health and care insurance company’s policyholders, which allows the insurer to monitor the policyholder’s physical activity. For example, some devices allow the tracking of the number of steps taken by an individual on a given day. Some health and care insurers offer incentives and premium discounts for consumers that wear this type of technology.

This is an autonomous health organisation set up in 1948, with the aim of assisting the population in the attainment of the highest possible level of health. WHO proposes conventions, agreements, regulations and makes recommendations about international nomenclature of diseases, causes of death and public health practices. It also develops, establishes, and promotes international standards concerning foods and biological, pharmaceutical and similar substances

In the case of regular premium business, written premiums are defined as the annualised amount of premiums for all policies commencing or renewing in a given period. Single premiums are deemed wholly written.

subsections.title: Chapter Overview
bullets: Solving complex problems and analysing complex issues are the key tasks in the SA1 exam.
Section 1 reminds candidates of the big issues covered earlier in the SA1 course (and in Subject SP1), indicating likely topics for discussion in long exam-style questions.
The remaining sections discuss the technique of answering complex questions in more detail.

The ability to look at a problem, solve it, and then communicate the results clearly is a very important skill that actuaries need in their day-to-day work, whatever field they work in; the examiners will test this ability.
The higher-level skills needed to pass a Specialist Applications Subject are: analysis, synthesis, critical judgement, and communication.

The aims of this chapter are to: (1) help develop these higher-level skills; (2) explain how to apply the skills when answering an exam question; (3) demonstrate the application with an example question; (4) give hints and tips on how to develop the skills further.

subsections.title: Overview
bullets: There is no explicit syllabus objective for this chapter.
Candidates must demonstrate knowledge and understanding of the subject as a whole, in order to apply and produce solutions to problems relating to health and care.

Specifically, candidates must be able to: recommend coherent solutions and courses of action in relation to the overall financial management of health and care insurance companies; analyse complex problems in terms of actuarial, economic and financial factors to a level where appropriate analytical techniques may be used; assess the implications and relevance of such factors, integrating the results into a coherent whole; evaluate the results critically in a wider context, drawing appropriate conclusions; propose solutions and actions, or a range of possible solutions and actions, based on this evaluation.

Examiners expect candidates to apply knowledge and understanding developed through study of the Core Reading to produce coherent advice and recommendations for the overall management of a health and care insurance company.

subsections.title: Examples of Likely Exam Topics
bullets: Discussion of advantages and disadvantages of current or new product structures — different product structures were described in Chapters 2, 4 and 7.
Discussion of the implications of different regulatory structures — regulatory regimes were discussed in Chapters 10 and 13.
Analysis of proposed product concepts — candidates may be given a complex proposed health and care product and asked to comment on its suitability or problems.

Recommendations regarding the various investigations that an actuary might undertake to assess appropriate assumption levels underlying a new basis, or to judge the continuing validity of existing assumptions — setting a basis was discussed in Chapters 8 and 19 and also in Subject SP1.

Demonstration of understanding of the appropriate regulatory valuation of assets, liabilities and capital requirements — the rules and regulations concerning the valuation of assets and liabilities and capital requirements were covered in Chapters 11 to 13.
Comparison of various investment strategies and their appropriateness for a particular health insurer under given circumstances — investment was covered in Chapter 18 and in Subject SP1.
Demonstration of how the long-term finances of a health insurer would be measured and monitored — the need for regular monitoring was covered in Chapter 19 and also in more detail in Subject SP1.

Assessment of risks involved in a given situation and recommendation of strategies to mitigate those risks — the consideration of risk when setting strategies under a number of scenarios was covered in Chapters 22 and 23.

subsections.title: Nature of the Exam
bullets: The Subject SA1 exam is a test of the ability to analyse problems, make judgements, and communicate clearly — so analysis, judgement and communication are far more important than simply learning and recalling.
The exam consists of up to four questions; some bookwork knowledge may still be required.
Candidates should be aware of the health insurance and healthcare scene in the UK, including current issues.

Guard against the temptation to write about a current topic when it is not especially relevant to the question — by exam time, candidates should know so much about health and care insurance that they do not have a pet topic.
A large part of the answer could well be fairly straightforward — make sure this is given. However, the answer must also be relevant to the specific situation in the question.

The skill of the actuary (and hence the candidate in the exam) is to recognise the standard 80% and give it, but then to give the 20% that is relevant to the specific situation in hand (or apply the standard 80% to the specific problem).

Most marks are for the basic theoretical and technical aspects (the cake), with relatively less credit for the fancy current aspects (the icing). A common reason why candidates fail is that they give too much icing and not enough cake — state the obvious and try not to be too clever.

Answers should not be confined to theory — they should also show a practical outlook. If traditional actuarial methods do not work for a given situation, say so and suggest other approaches that may give a practical solution.

key_terms.definition: A metaphor for exam technique: the cake refers to the standard theoretical and technical content (worth most marks), while the icing refers to fancy current or novel aspects (worth relatively fewer marks). Candidates often fail by giving too much icing and not enough cake.

subsections.title: Overview of Exam Process
bullets: The exam allows 3 hours and 20 minutes to answer up to four questions.

To maximise the chance of passing, each of the following steps in the process must be approached carefully: reading the question; planning your time; generating ideas; filtering and prioritising your ideas; deciding upon a structure for the answer; writing out your answer.

subsections.title: Reading the Question
bullets: One of the most common reasons for candidates failing the exam is that they do not read the question properly — it cannot be emphasised enough how important it is to read the question accurately.
The initial step should be a quick read through to get an idea of the question being asked, then a second careful read to pick up all the detail.

Make sure to pick up on: whether the company is mutual or proprietary; whether the company is large or small; what products it is selling; how many marks are on offer for each section; what the instruction words are.
Use the number of marks and instruction words when deciding upon the number of points to make, the level of detail, the tone of the answer, and time allocation.
As well as picking up on the detail, show the examiners that you have done so — e.g., by writing since it is a small company or since it is a proprietary company.
Any unusual features or clues in the question are likely to be relevant (but will not always be relevant) — it is the job of the actuary to decide what is important and what is not.
A final read of the question should then be used to make sure you are answering the question that has been set and that you have picked up on all the clues.

subsections.title: Planning Your Time
bullets: Time allocation should be basically pro-rata based on the number of marks on offer.
Some parts of questions will inevitably be easier than others — spend less time on these.
Calculation questions always look better if they are finished; there may also be a couple of marks for getting to the finish — so spend slightly more time on these (but do not get carried away).
The key question(s) in the pass/fail decisions are likely to be the tricky ones — spend more time on these.
The key message is that it is unwise to go materially over time on any part.

subsections.title: Generating Ideas
bullets: The next step is to generate as many ideas as possible without worrying at this stage about prioritising or relevance.

Ways to generate ideas include: role playing (consider what shareholders, sales managers, policyholders, actuarial function holders, regulators, underwriters etc all want); use the Core Reading and any relevant professional guidance; consider each of the areas of the course or use a mega-list of all the key words in the course; consider the steps involved in a process; consider the actuarial control cycle; consider the product control cycle; use the clues in the question; use ideas from your own experience; use ideas from similar questions (but tailor them to the question); negate the question (e.g., if the question asks for advantages, also think of disadvantages to generate areas for discussion); ask why, when, who, how, what for, etc.

key_terms.definition: A comprehensive list of all key words and topics in the SA1 course, used as a brainstorming prompt to ensure no major areas are overlooked when generating ideas for an exam answer.

A technique for generating exam ideas by considering the perspective of each stakeholder — shareholders, sales managers, policyholders, regulators, underwriters, etc. — and thinking about what each would want in the scenario described.

subsections.title: Filtering and Prioritising Your Ideas
bullets: After generating ideas, filter and arrange them using the following principles: make sure the answer has a clear logical structure; prioritise so that the key ideas come first; if a question is for fifty marks, think about what you would say if it was for only five — this gives the headings and key ideas; look at the instruction words and the number of marks on offer; look at all the parts of the question, not just the part being answered, to help filter ideas for each part; expand the key ideas to give an appropriate level of detail; ensure a wide range of relevant, clearly explained points rather than focussing on one or two issues; make sure to give the cake, not just the icing.

subsections.title: Structuring Your Answer
bullets: If the idea generation, filtering and arranging have been successful then the structure should follow naturally.
For a process, start at the beginning and finish at the end.
Consider using the actuarial control cycle (or the product control cycle) as a structure.
If the question has a logical structure, then use that structure.

subsections.title: Writing Out Your Answer
bullets: For a fifty-mark question it does not take a long time to write down an answer if you know exactly what you are going to write — however, you will be thinking as you write, so it will take longer. The planning process alone should take at least half an hour.
When writing out the answer: make sure to explain your points; use headings, bullet points and blank lines to break up your answer and make it easy to mark; avoid repetition and avoid waffle.

subsections.title: The Car Manufacturer Question
bullets: A deliberately non-actuarial example question is used so that candidates concentrate on the process of solving the problem rather than reading a standard ActEd solution.

The scenario: you are the strategy director for a large multinational car manufacturer, concerned about the marketing and sales approach of the whole range of vehicles in one particular country. The CEO has noted that sales of the X456, which were good when the car was originally launched three years ago, have now started to fall. The main reason appears to be adverse publicity regarding the performance of the car's engine. The CEO requests a report recommending how to take this forward.
The task: outline the points you will make in your report.

subsections.title: Reading (Applied to the Example)
bullets: A quick read of the question should pick up on who you are (strategy director with interest in marketing, i.e. sales, product design, market research, advertising, brand-building, pricing and profitability), a brief description of the problem, and an idea of your objective.

Key clues from careful reading: it is a multinational company — probably a big household name, large product range, lots of assistance available from other countries; you are responsible for the full range of vehicles but the problem only concerns sales of the X456 in one country; the car was launched three years ago and sales were initially good; sales have now fallen — the main reason appears to be the performance of the engine, but there might be other factors (better models offered by competitors, cheaper models, the whole market may have fallen, better advertising by competitors, distribution problems, fashion change over three years); the question asks what we mean by re-launching and whether there would be other solutions such as a change of price or advertising agency; a report with recommendations is required.
The final read checks that you are answering the question set and have picked up on all the clues.

subsections.title: Generating Ideas (Applied to the Example)
bullets: Role playing: the customer wants performance, safety features, economy, reliability, appearance, gimmicks, reasonable price, status symbol, size — price must be weighed against perceived value. The dealer wants an easy sale, lots of commission, good service from the company, fast delivery. Shareholders want profit now and in the future and growth in share price. Regulators have influence over safety features and advertising controls. Competitors' car, performance, appearance, image, brand, price and features must all be considered, as well as how they will react to future changes.
Core Reading and professional guidance: although not about cars, the Core Reading covers monitoring and redesign of insurance products, and many of those ideas are still relevant to redesigning cars.

Mega-list and areas of the course: factors for insurance such as rating factors are still relevant for cars — for example, competitors may have two models (a luxury and an economy) while our single product sits between the two and sells little as a result.

Process: think through the process of driving a car — what features does it need? This triggers ideas about engine mechanics, steering, cooling systems, comfort, safety devices, anti-theft devices, gauges, etc.

Actuarial control cycle: consider background information, commercial and economic environment, what the aims are, what the risks are, developing the optimal solution, continually monitoring, allowing for professionalism and regulatory constraints.
Negate the question: ask why we must redesign the car, how the best way of doing so is, and why we might not need to.

subsections.title: Filtering and Arranging Ideas to Plan an Answer
bullets: The actuarial control cycle provides a good basis to structure the answer.

Background information section of the report should cover: competitors information (analysis of features, prices, reasons why they are selling, market share of key companies, press comments); market research of customers and/or dealers; economic climate and social factors and their impact on price, sales and desirability of different models; estimated impact on costs, sales and hence profit.

Specify the problem: objective is profit; constraints are risk and capital. Risks include: excessive time and money spent developing the project; money not properly directed so that sales do not result; the product being delivered late or not being a significant improvement; insufficient resources being put on the project so it fails.

Develop the solution: consider changes and estimate likely impact upon sales, consider prices and costs, hence project to quantify impact on profits; consider the sensitivity of demand to price reduction; consider advertising and marketing of the launch; consider testing prototypes.

Monitoring and reviewing: this will happen after the launch — look at all aspects mentioned above and take appropriate action; bear in mind time scales for change (changing the price will be much easier than redeveloping the engine).

subsections.title: Introduction (of the Report)
bullets: The purpose of the report is to identify the reasons why sales of the X456 in Ruritania have fallen over the last two years and to recommend a strategy to rectify the problem.

subsections.title: Market Information
bullets: Appendix A sets out the market information regarding the X456 and its key competitors.

Appendix A1 shows the market size, sales and hence market share of the X456 alongside key competitors (the Charaski 123, the Euro XYZ and the Exma 789). Sales have fallen despite an increase in the size of the market, giving a sharp reduction in the company's market share.

Appendix A2 shows the features that the X456 offers compared with those of key competitors, ranked by the following categories after market research: legal requirement; essential to the sale; very nice to have; unimportant. For optional features, the cost is shown for both the X456 and its competitors, and for features not currently offered, an estimate of the cost of introducing them is shown.

Appendix A3 covers press commentary and response of interviews with the dealers — it is clear that the performance of the engine, its reliability and efficiency are critical and have received considerable negative press comment; this is the key reason for the fall in sales.

Appendix A4 looks at future sales — including an overview of Ruritania's economic prospects, the impact of new sales methods (e.g. internet), the likelihood of cheaper imports in future and possible competitors' reactions to changes.

subsections.title: Objective When Redesigning the Car
bullets: The purpose of the redesign of the car is to address the problems with the current model in order to increase future sales.
The aim is to find changes that will maximise the company's profits over an acceptable time period for an acceptable level of capital investment and risk.

Risks include: excessive time and money spent developing the project; money not properly directed so that sales do not result; the product being delivered late or not being a significant improvement, resulting in negative publicity; insufficient resources being put into the project so it fails; competitors reacting quickly so that the model is out of date when it is launched.
The impact on the company if things go wrong and the steps that can be taken to minimise the impact of these risks will also be considered.

subsections.title: Possible Strategies
bullets: Given the aim, the following strategies have all been considered as the most realistic possibilities: (1) Improve engine performance, new sales campaign; (2) Keep car as it is, big reduction in price to appeal to a new market niche; (3) Keep car as it is for same price, add some extra features, new sales campaign; (4) Stop marketing cars entirely, concentrate on commercial vehicles.

In the exam, many problems will lead to one obvious solution and so the idea of considering various alternatives may not be necessary. However, some problems will require a choice to be made between alternative courses of action.

When a choice is required, clearly present the relative advantages and disadvantages of these courses of action, in particular the extent to which they help meet the aim and with what risks — then weigh up these pros and cons and reach a conclusion.
Recommendation: plan 1 as the long-term strategy along with plan 3 in the short term.

subsections.title: Addressing the Problem of Engine Performance
bullets: The key issue is the performance of the engine. Issues to investigate include: the positioning of the spark plug within the combustion chamber (including the possibility of two spark plugs per chamber); the shape of the combustion chamber to ensure smooth burning; the possibility of increasing the number of valves to increase the flow of inlet and outlet gases; the balance of the engine by considering other possible cylinder configurations; the possibility of fuel injection as a standard feature.

For each option, consider: the cost of implementing the change (on realistic, optimistic and pessimistic bases); the impact upon engine performance and fuel consumption; the risks to the company; the amount by which the car's current price can be increased.

Note: in the exam, questions will be about healthcare — candidates are expected to know the equivalent level of detail in that domain. This example is deliberately non-actuarial to make candidates focus on the problem-solving process.

subsections.title: Other Factors
bullets: Other factors to consider improving include: the comfort of the vehicle for both driver and passengers; the car's braking system and other safety features; optional additional features; the appearance of the car.

subsections.title: Short-term Strategies
bullets: Look at the costs and benefits (and risks) of advertising including a change of advertising media and a change of advertising agency.
Also consider short-term changes such as special offers or adding features that are currently optional as part of a standard package.

subsections.title: Recommendations
bullets: Engine performance: assemble a development team to look at the factors in Section 6.5, especially: the capital cost of the development and ongoing costs; the amount by which the engine will improve; the impact on sales (and the likely variability); the timeframe within which delivery can be confidently achieved. The optimal solution — which may involve upgrading the standard model or offering improved engine performance as an optional extra — should be found by looking at capital outlay, profits and risks.
Other systems of the car: review other features (braking systems, comfort and appearance) — for each, examine the costs, risks and impact on sales, again with the aim of maximising profit.

Additional features: by looking at the desirability, cost and price that can be charged for the features set out in Appendix A2, derive a strategy that maximises future profits with very low development costs. Also consider the number of features included as part of the standard model and developing a sports package with appropriate features.
Short-term strategy: examine the factors in Sections 6.6 and 6.7 to derive an optimal strategy to at least maintain the current level of profits for the next 18 months (in accordance with plan 3).

Time scales: aim to achieve the change as quickly as possible to halt the slide in market share, but it is more important to get it right than to do something too quickly — expect the new model to be launched within two to three years.
The future: continually monitor and review the car's performance on a regular basis so as not to be in this position again — aim to be leading the market, not following it.
